THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES:
(OFFICIAL REPORT.)
IN THE
THIRD SESSION OF THE FIRST PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
APPOINTED TO MEET THE TWENTY-SEVENTH DAY OF FEBRUARY, IN THE
THIRTEENTH YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS MAJESTY
KING
GEORGE V.
FIRST SERIES-VOLUME III.
THE SENATE.

---

Tuesday, 27th February, 1923.
The Third Session of the First Parliament
of Northern Ireland, appointed to meet 27th
February, 1923, in the thirteenth year in the
Reign of King George V., was opened by the
Governor (His Grace the Duke of Abercorn,
K.P.) in person.
The House met at a Quarter before Twelve
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER (Right Hon. the
Marquess of Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O., D.L.)
in the Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace, the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher
of the Black Rod to let the Commons know
"It is His Grace's pleasure that they attend
him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with their
Speaker, His Grace delivered the following
Speech from the Throne, and then retired :-
--
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
On this the first occasion on which I
have the privilege to address you in my
present capacity, I desire to express my
appreciation of the high honour conferred
upon me by our Sovereign, and my sense of
humble duty to Him and His Loyal subjects
throughout Northern Ireland, and my congratulations to all concerned that this, the
third Session of your Parliament, opens
under brighter auspices than might have
been hoped for even six months ago.
Hitherto you have been mainly occupied
with matters incidental to the setting up
of your Parliament and Government Departments, and with measures necessary
for safeguarding life and property. Owing
to wise administration, and with a Judiciary
of which any State might be proud,
strengthened by Acts passed for the special
purpose, order has been restored and repect for the Law established. For this satisfactory condition I feel a tribute should
be paid to the Inspector-General and the
officers and men of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, and of the Special Constabulary
in which Forces discipline and a high
sense of duty throughout all ranks have
called forth universal and well-merited
praise, which is also due to officers and
men in your Prison service.
The time has now happily arrived when
you are in a position to enter upon the
consideration of affairs of a more domestic
and remedial nature.
A
THE SENATE.
The King's Speech.

The King's Speech,
A Committee has been appointed to inquire
into Reformatory and Industrial Schools,
and steps have been taken to appoint a
Committee to investigate the Jury system.
The Principal Bills of the new Session
will deal with Education and Temperance
Reform. The first of these, which will be
laid before you at an early date, will be
framed to co-ordinate and reform the several services of public education and to promote the general welfare of children and
young persons. The latter cannot be dealt
with, at the earliest until after Easter,
owing to complicated financial and other
questions involved. I earnestly trust that
the discussion on these two primary
measures will be conducted with a spirit of
helpfulness, toleration and goodwill, to the
end that there may be placed upon the
Statute Book Acts conferring lasting benefits
upon the people as a whole.
an
Other important measures which will engage your attention will include the application to Northern Ireland of the Criminal
Evidence Act, 1898, which enables
accused person to give evidence in his own
defence, and a Bill dealing with the Amendment of Criminal Law in the interests of
young persons. Bills to amend the Trade
Boards Acts, the Unemployment Insurance
Acts, and the Petty Sessions Acts will be
introduced, and also legislation having for
its object the reconstruction of records of
Joint Stock Companies whose registered
offices are situate in Northern Ireland. A
Bill will be introduced making it obligatory
upon all Civil Servants and other persons
employed by the Government of Northern
Ireland to take the Oath of Allegiance to
His Majesty the King and His Government
of Northern Ireland.
There are in addition, a certain number
of other administrative measures which will
claim your attention.
It is intended at an early date to appoint
a Royal Commission-
To consider and report as to how the
natural and industrial resources of Northern Ireland can be best developed, with
a view to increasing general prosperity
and reducing the extent of unemployment."
The Government proposes to set up a Committee to consider the form legislation should
take to deal with the question of Rent Restriction, in view of the expiry of control in
June next.
I am particularly gratified to learn that
voluntary machinery for negotiation is being
set up by the Railway Companies and the
Railway Trade Unions, the result of which,
I trust, will be that a larger measure of
harmony will in future prevail throughout
the railway systems of Northern Ireland.
The problem of unemployment is still
serious, and I appreciate the patient way
in which the people have borne the trials
which they have been called upon to endure
in common with their fellow subjects
throughout the United Kingdom. The prevailing indication of a revival of trade and
shipbuilding encouraged in certain directions by the operation of the Loans
Guarantee Act of last Session causes me
great satisfaction; my Ministers are giving
their most anxious consideration to the
many problems inseparable from such a
revival, so that as quickly as possible steady
employment may be provided as a natural
consequence of trade prosperity, and local
authorities and other public bodies throughout the country are heartily co-operating
with the Government in the promotion of
relief schemes, many of lasting utility,
providing much-needed employment and
absorbing over £1,500,000. Work on the
preparation of the site for permanent
Parliamentary and Ministerial Buildings
has been begun, and I am assured that there
will be no interruption until the whole
undertaking is completed.
Members of the House of Commons.
By agreement with the British Government a Special Committee has been
established under the Chairmanship of
Lord Colwyn to inquire into and report
upon the financial relations between that
Government and the Government of
Northern Ireland as affected by the setting
up of the Free State. Your Minister of
Finance is laying before that Committee
the various matters as to which Northern
Ireland is concerned.
The estimates of expenditure on the
services for the ensuing financial year will
The King's Speech.

27 FEBRUARY 1923
Debate on the Address.
be laid before you in due course, but until
Lord Colwyn's Committee has completed its
labour it will be only possible to present
partial and tentative estimates.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
His Gracious Majesty, when he addressed
you in Person on the 22nd June, 1921, said,
"I am confident that the important
to
matters entrusted
the control and
guidance of the Northern Parliament will
be managed with wisdom and with
moderation with fairness and due regard
to every faith and interest, and with no
abatement of that patriotic devotion to the
Empire which you proved so gallantly in
the Great War." I am proud to testify that
the faith entertained by His Majesty on
that occasion was abundantly justified.
And I pray that Almighty God may bestow His abundant blessing upon your
deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire, and
the Commons withdrew
The House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
NEW MEMBERS SWORN.
Alderman William George Turner, sworn,
and took his seat as the Right Hon. the
Lord Mayor of Belfast.
Alderman M. S. Moore, sworn, and took his
seat as Mayor of Derry.
PAPERS PRESENTED.
Ministry of Labour: Interim Report of the
Departmental Committee on the Cost of
Living.
Presented (by command) and ordered to
lie on the Table.
Ministry of Commerce. Census of Production (1924) Order (Northern Ireland), 1922.
Ministry of Home Affairs (1) Royal Ulster
Constabulary Pay Order, 1922. (2) Road
Vehicles (Trade Licences) (Northern Ireland).
Regulations, 1923.
Ministry of Labour: (1) National Health
Insurance (Approved Societies) Amendment
Regulations (No. 2), 1922. (2) National
Health Insurance and Unemployment Insurance ((Inspectors' Certificates) Regulations
(Northern Ireland), 1922. (3) National Health
Insurance (Arrears) Amendment Regulations,
1923.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
LOCAL BILLS.
Mr. SPEAKER acquainted the House that
the Clerk of the Senate had laid upon the
Table the certificates from the Examiner of
the Standing Orders applicable to the following Bills have been complied with:-
Basil McCrea Endowments.
Belfast Corporation.
Belfast Water.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
ADDRESS IN REPLY TO HIS MAJESTY'S
MOST GRACIOUS SPEECH.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL (in Military Uniform): I beg to move: That a humble
Address be presented to His Grace in the
following words :-
Your Grace,
We, Your Majesty's most dutiful and loyal
subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, beg leave to offer our
humble thanks to your Grace for the Speech
which your Grace has addressed to both
Houses of Parliament.
My Lord, I must appeal to right hon.
and hon Members of this House to
extend to me the utmost limit of
that indulgence which thev so uniformly
concede to the Mover of an Address
to the Throne. I can assure you, My
Lord, that it is no light task to express,
however inadequately, the feelings of loyalty
to His Majesty which animate our minds
and the sense of pride and satisfaction that
we all feel in the fact that he has chosen the
head of a great Ulster family to be his first
representative in Northern Ireland. I can
fully realise that our Government, in deputing me to carry out the duty of moving this
Address, is conferring a great honour, which
I feel is placed on me as a compliment to
that very large class of citizens in Northern
Ireland who have served His Majesty in the
Navy, Army or Air Force. The honour, I
may say, is doubly appreciated by me as
having at one time served in the same regiment as His Grace. I can assure His Grace
that he has no more loyal supporters than
those who served under him in the North
Irish Imperial Yeomanry, which later,
under the title of the North Irish Horse,
gave such splendid service to His Majesty
and to the Empire in the Great War.
I am sure that all of us here were deeply
impressed by the magnificent reception and
Debate on the Address. 8
Debate on the Address.
THE SENATE.
[Colonel Perceval-Maxwell]
warm welcome received by His Grace and
the Duchess, who has proved herself such a
sincere admirer and warm friend of
Northern Ireland, as they passed through
the streets of Belfast yesterday, and I feel
certain that hon. Members will agree with
me when I say that the country districts
will be in no way behind this great city in
testifying their loyalty to His Majesty's
representative whenever and wherever His
Grace and the Duchess find an opportunity
of visiting other parts of this loyal
Province. It is with feelings of profound
thankfulness that we note that His Grace
is able to congratulate all concerned on the
brighter prospects which seem to be dawning for this portion of our beloved country,
and we pray that the blessings of peace
which we now enjoy may continue with us
and may extend to the whole Island.
Rt. hon. and hon. Members will have
heard with gratification the warm tribute
which His Grace has paid to the Judiciary,
the Inspector-General, officers and men of
the R.U.C. and Special Constabulary, as
well as the officers and men of the Prison
Service. I think we all realise the very
great difficulties that the police had to
contend with, and I believe the country
owes a great debt to the Inspector-General
and those officers who served under him
for moulding the splendid material which
Northern Ireland produced into the increasingly efficient and disciplined force of to-day.
I also think that the Government is to be
congratulated on having acquired the services of an officer as Inspector-General who
has given such signal proofs of his administrative ability and clear insight into
police work and organisation. Turning to
future legislation, I notice that the
principal Bills to be introduced this session
refer to Education and Temperance
Reform.
With regard to the former which is
under the special charge of the Noble
Marquess, I feel that there is no division
of opinion in this country as to the desirability of placing within the reach of all
citizens the best and soundest education
that it is possible to give to them, in order
that, both for their own sakes and for the
sake of the State, they may be in a position
to discharge with balanced judgment the
ever-increasing duties and responsibilities
which modern conditions of government
place on the individual. There may be
financial considerations, perhaps more properly discussed in another place, which
may necessitate some curtailment or postponement of the educational benefits which
we should wish to confer. The second
Bill of great importance to be introduced
is one which I hope will be discussed in
that spirit of helpfulness, toleration and
good-will which it will be our duty to
cultivate, as suggested in the Gracious
Speech from the Throne.
Temperance measures are very seldom
discussed in a temperate atmosphere, but
personally I believe that the considered
decision of an Ulster Parliament representative of self-controlled, moderate, libertyloving men and women, for which Ulster
has always been famous, will devise a Meaure which will go as far as the State can
usefully go towards protecting individuals
from their own weaknesses without impairing their power of self-control or sacrificing the reasonable liberty of the individual or the legitimate interests which
are involved. I will not take up the time
of the House by referring to the other important Bills and the Royal Commission to
which His Grace has alluded, but I am
sure that right hon. and hon. Members
will share in His Grace's satisfaction at
the voluntary machinery which has been
set up for negotiations between Railway
Companies and Railway Trade Unions. If
the result of those negotiations should
settle, at any rate for a considerable time,
the vexed question of wages and conditions
of service, I feel certain that a great impetus would be given to the general revival of trade, which would have a farreaching effect on the problem of unemployment to which His Grace alludes in
terms which I am sure will be appreciated
throughout Northern Ireland.
The Gracious Speech concludes by quoting
the words of His Majesty at the opening
of our first Session. I believe hon. Members will feel deeply grateful to his Grace
for bringing those words to our memory
and for his own gracious testimony that
the faith entertained by his Majesty has
been justified. Wisdom and moderation,
even-handed justice and fairness to all
creeds and classes are the mainstays of all
good government, and in no country I
believe are they more essential and more
appreciated than in Ireland. I beg to
move.
Mr. WOODS: I beg to second the Address in
reply to the Gracious Speech from the Throne
which has been proposed in suitable and
eloquent terms by the right hon. and gallant
Member, and like the right hon. and gallant
Member, I would claim the indulgence of the
House for a short time. I wish to say how
much I appreciate the compliment of being
asked to second the Address, a compliment
not only to myself but to the great industrial
class of whom I am a representative, and I
feel sure that the industrial class throughout
the Six Counties will appreciate the treat
ment their representatives have received since
becoming members of the Senate.
Debate on the Address.
Debate on the Address.
27 FEBRUARY 1923
This is a very important occasion for l'ritish
North Ireland, and we fully appreciate the
high honour that has been conferred upon us
in having His Gracious Majesty's Speech
delivered to us by His Grace the Governor.
All the Members of the Senate will, I am sure,
join in expressing our grateful thanks to His
Grace the Governor and Her Grace the
Duchess for their presence here to-day. We
Ulstermen are proud of the fact that a true
Ulsterman has been chosen to fill the
important position of the first Governor of
our Imporial Province, and His Grace is well
aware that in no part of the British Empire
are more loyal men to be found than within
the borders of the Six Counties.
I know that I am expressing the feelings of
all our Ulster people when I congratulate the
Prime Minister and the Government on
having so completely restored law and order
throughout Northern Ireland.
With regard to proposed legislation for
Education and Temperance, educational reform is much needed. The working man is
looking forward to the time when he will be
able to give a better education to his children
than he himself received, and so equip them
better for the battle of life. If this can be
accomplished, and I think it can, knowing
the Minister of Education as we all do, we
feel assured that when the Education Bill is
introduced it will be received with satisfaction
by the people of this country. I think it
would be premature for me to say much about
Temperance Reform. When the Bill is introduced I feel sure it will be discussed in this
House in a very temperate manner. I note
with feelings of great satisfaction that a
Royal Commission is to be set up to consider
the Industrial situation. I earnestly hope
that the findings of that Commission will result in the removal of the exceptional causes
that are hampering our industries at the
present time, and lead to the development of
much-needed new industries, and, by so doing.
reduce unemployment to a minimum.
Another important matter is the Rent Restriction Act, and I observe that it is the
intention of the Government to set up a Committee to consider the form of legislation required, in view of the expiry of control in
June next. I hope this Committee will advise
the Government to retain control. Not only
that, but I hope it will extend control so that
landlords will not in future be able to hold
up houses for sale for months and even years
while families are being crushed into little
rooms, which is not conducive to the health
of the community.
Therefore, reviewing the proposals of the
Government as indicated in His Majesty's
Gracious Speech, it is patent to all that they
intend to do all in their power to promote
peace and good will throughout our land. I
beg to second.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I am sure the occasion
on which the King's speech is delivered
through the medium of the Governor of
Northern Ireland is an event which will not
be lost upon the community which we represent here. It is a great event of which we
have every reason to be justly proud, and I
feel sure that the impressive ceremony of
yesterday has made us all consider the
position in which we stand at the present
time. In my judgment that important
occasion sets the seal upon the Government
of Northern Ireland in accordance with the
Act of 1920. None of us can look upon that
important event without feelings of great
satisfaction. It seems to me that the
establishment of a Governor for this Province
furnishes one more buttress and one more support of the position which we want to maintain,
The position that we desire to maintain in
no sense destroys any of those links with the
United Kingdom which we value, and it places
us in a position of security from those dangers
which we have had every reason to apprehend.
Those dangers are in the attitude which one
political party for its own object or perhaps
with the idea of benefiting the State has
brought into being. Hitherto we have always
felt that in some degree we have been a pawn
in party politics, and that we have been
sacrificed for reasons which have not been
beneficial to ourselves. But we do feel that,
with the establishment of this Province as a
self-governing unit presided over by a
Governor, while our desire for union, while
our desire for co-operation with the United
Kingdom, while our loyalty to the
Crown are in no sense diminished or
impaired, yet we are in a more independent
position to maintain our rights and to maintain the status which we believe we have
every reason to hold.
I should like to congratulate the British
Government upon the choice of the person
to hold the high office of first Governor of
Northern Ireland. It is perhaps superfluous
for me to say anything about His Grace the
Duke of Abercorn. We all know him personally very well. He bears an honoured and
respected name, and his family have always
been foremost in supporting the institutions
of this great Province and doing their share
in the public life of the Empire. I need
hardly say we extend a very hearty welcome
to the Duke of Abercorn. We had the honour
of His Grace being a Member of this House.
In fact he moved the first Address in response
to the Speech from the Throne upon the
opening of the first session of the Parliament
of Northern Ireland. His eloquent speech
is still well within our memories. But the
loss to the Senate of his presence amongst us
as a Member is certainly a gain to the Province at large. I think we would be indeed
11 Debate on the Address.
Debate on the Address. 12
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
small-minded if we grudged to the Province
an
event which constitutes a loss to the
Senate. May I also on your behalf pay a
tribute to the gracious lady who will share
with the Duke of Abercorn the arduous social
and other duties of the high position to which
he has been called by His Majesty.
naturally hope for them a very happy and
prosperous term of office, and we can assure
them that they are secure in the loyal cooperation of all classes of the population of
the Six Counties.
We
With your permission I will now turn to
the Gracious Speech from the Throne.
May I be allowed to congratulate the mover
and seconder upon the singularly felicitous
phrases in which they have couched their
speeches in moving and seconding that response? Colonel Perceval-Maxwell is a very
familiar figure in this neighbourhood. He
holds the high honour of a Privy Councillor
of Ireland, and has certainly played his part,
as have all his family and forbears, in the
public service and in showing their loyalty
to the country to which we are all so proud
to belong. The right hon. Gentleman was
not making his maiden speech to-day.
We
have had the benefit of his speeches already
in this House, and although perhaps some of
his sentiments have not always been altogether in accordance with those I have
ventured to bring forward, still we welcome
any criticism not only from the right hon.
Gentleman, but from any Member of this
House, because I feel that no Government can
progress without that healthy criticism which
is the life-blood of progress, and certainly is
welcome in this House.
With regard to the seconder of the Address,
Senator Woods, may I also be allowed to congratulate him on the speech which he has
just delivered? We have the good fortune in
the Senate of Northern Ireland, which the
House of Lords on the other side of the water
cannot claim-we are privileged in having
direct representation of Labour in this House,
and whatever alterations are made in the
Second Chamber in England, I am sure that
that will be one of the improvements-that
that Assembly should have on its roll those
who are directly connected with the great
Labour institutions, and can speak directly
with the voice of Labour. Senator Woods is
one who is highly respected. He has reached
the prime of a very arduous life. He passed.
his apprenticeship and his working days in
that firm to which we all pay great honourthe firm of Harland & Wolff, and of which we
have so eminent a representative in Lord
Pirrie, who is sitting opposite to me at this
moment. We are glad to have heard Senator
Wood's speech, and I only hope he will lose
no opportunity of putting before us, and
consequently before all the people of Ulster,
the sentiments which he may hold and the
opinions which he desires to put forward for
the benefit of those whom he represents in
this House.
There is a change in the composition of
the Senate on this our first meeting this
year, owing to the change of person
in the Lord Mayor of Belfast, and also
in the Mayor of Derry While I feel that I am
voicing the sentiments of everyone present today when I say how much we shall miss the
genial presence of our friend Sir William
Coates, still we no less welcome the presence
of the present Lord Mayor at our debates, and
I hope he will take part in these debates as
the opportunity arises which he may select.
The retiring Lord Mayor has also had the
honour of being Deputy Speaker of the Senate.
May I also on your behalf extend a welcome
to Alderman Moore, the Mayor of Derry. I
sincerely hope he has established a precedent
for the civic head of the second city of this
Province by being present at our debates.
We had not the privilege of the presence of
his predecessor. I regret it very much indeed
because we always hope that those who are
associated with this great Province will do
their utmost, whether it is in their own
neighbourhoods or whether through the
medium of a public body like the Senate of
Northern Ireland, to carry on the work in
the interests of the people of the Six Counties
whom we endeavour to represent.
The Speech to which we have had the
honour of listening does not perhaps contain
what some might term a very ambitious programme of legislation. But I think you will
agree with me when I say that it is far better
that this programme should be confined to
measures which the Government hopes to be
able to bring into force, rather than raising
hopes which they are under no circumstances
able to fulfil. The Speech contains mention
of finance, education, and temperance, of unemployment, criminal law amendment, and
also minor legislative measures, and in the
reference which I propose to make in the few
minutes in which I shall venture to trespass
on your indulgence, I will only mention the
two measures which, I think you will agree
with me, are the measures of major interest--
those dealing with education and temperance. I hardly feel that with regard to the
latter I can improve on the very happy
phraseology of my right hon. Friend Colonel
Perceval-Maxwell, in which he spoke of
temperance. In his final words he said he was
certain that the question would receive
temperate handling at the hands of the
Senators. It will be what we call the second
great measure of the session, and the Government are bringing forward a measure which,
while it will not pander to the faddists, will
be drawn on a basis in consonance with the
advance and evolution of public opinion
upon a great subject and a subject which is
13 Debate on the Address. 27 FEBRUARY 1923

Debate on the Address.
uppermost in the minds of most people, the
subject of temperance reform.
The Education Bill is a matter which will
perhaps occupy more of your time, and as
Minister of Education I will endeavour to
do my utmost to enlist your sympathy for
that measure. After the fullest consideration
the Government intend to introduce their proposals for educational reform early next
month, and I sincerely hope that this longexpected measure will realise the hopes of
those who are considering all these aspects of
education. The success of the measure
naturally depends on the sympathetic consideration and co-operation of all classes of
I
the community and of all creeds.
suppose the question of education does, in
many of its aspects, excite more controversy than perhaps any other question
that could be brought forward. The
intention of the Government is to re-model
the system which has been in operation for
many years past, to remove many of the
anomalies which exist, and to establish on a
sound and lasting basis, a basis
sistent with the ideas of educationists and
of the great community we represent, a system
which will provide an education for the
rising generation and will enable them to
face and to shoulder with success the great
responsibilities that are bound to fall upon
them in their pathway through life.
•
con-
I do not feel that I should be entitled on
this occasion to review the provisions I have
in my mind, and which are being embodied
in the Bill I hope to introduce in the middle
of March. I should like to say now that the
Bill is not in its final form We are still
considering every clause and every line in
every clause, and I should welcome any
assistance which can be forthcoming from
those who are taking an interest in this
measure. We have been fortunate in having
a Committee sitting for some considerable
time inquiring into this question, and both the
men and women on that Committee have devoted many precious hours to going through
the various points, discussing amongst themselves details of a system of education, and
calling before them individuals from various
walks of life and various interests who could
bring to bear on this great subject the knowledge they possess. I need hardly say that I
am deeply indebted to that Committee and to
the Chairman of that Committee, Mr. Lynn.
They have had a very difficult task, and I
cannot sufficiently express my gratitude to
them, because on their recommendations
has been based the Bill which I am
bringing forward. The Committee have
had an opportunity of looking at the
question in a different manner from that
in which I and my advisers can look at it.
It is possible to comprise in a report a great
many ideas and a great many hopes
which it is well-nigh impossible to embody
in an Act of Parliament. They are guided
by what ought to be, and we have to be
guided by what can be, but I can say that I
am not anticipating there will be any wide
difference of opinion between us, although
while they are fortunate in being able to give
full expression to their opinions. I am naturally controlled by financial considerations and
perhaps not able, owing to these considerations, to go as far as I should like to go in
various directions.
There are the systems of Primary education,
Secondary education, and Technical education,
which we have to consider and to
endeavour to co-ordinate $0 that there
shall be a continuous system of education
without any overlapping and without any gaps.
I should like to make some particular mention
of Secondary education. It has perhaps not
been on the same satisfactory basis as Primary
education. The teachers in Secondary schools
have passed through a very hard time, but I
can assure them they have not been forgotten
by the Government, and I can promise them
this for what it is worth, that at least the
interim grant at its present increased rate
will be continued pending a solution of the
problem of their adequate remuneration, and
I may add that their adequate remuneration
is a matter that is continually before us and
of which I can assure them I shall under
no circumstances lose sight.
I think I shall be expected to make a review of the general situation. In doing so my
mind naturally turns to the Prime Minister.
I feel that in the Prime Minister we have a
tremendous asset. I believe that our Prime
Minister has achieved more in a short space
of time than any other man could have done.
He has carried an enormous burden of work
on his shoulders, and I feel we all owe him a
debt of gratitude for his self sacrifice and for
the manner in which he has devoted himself
heart and soul to the duties of the Prime
Minister of this Province. I have had many
opportunities of being associated with the
Prime Minister in his negotiations on the
other side. and I can assure you he has secured concessions which no one else could have
secured. We all owe him a debt of gratitude
for the manner in which he has discharged
the task given to him when he became Prime
Minister of Ulster.
As to the state of the Province, I do not
feel that I should be entitled to indulge in an
optimistic panegyric. I think that would be
unnecessary and unsuitable, but I cannot help
saying that I think we have every reason to
congratulate ourselves on the diminution of
crime in the territory under the jurisdiction
of the Northern Government. In my judyment the co-operation of all classes has produced that healthy state of public opinion
which after all is the first requisite for the
establishment of law and order upon which the
Debate on the Address.
Debate on the Address.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
whole stability of our public life and our
institutions depends. We see the immediate
results of the increased security of life and
property in the revival of confidence and
renewed interest in business enterprise. As
I have said, I do not want to be too optimistic,
but I do consider, and I am sure hon. Members
will agree with me, that all those signs are
here that the worst of the storm has passed,
and I repeat that our thanks for that are due
to our Prime Minister, the pilot who weathered the storm. Trade is improving in common
with the trade of the rest of the United
Kingdom. The shipyards are busier, and hope
of a corresponding advance in the great flax
industry is naturally before us. There is the
prospect of a coal field in County Tyrone, and
we feel and I speak with a certain amount
of intimate knowledge-that when we do have
a thriving coal field in our midst we shall be
in possession of one of the most valuable and
most helpful assets for the progress of this
community in the industrial world.
May I be allowed to pay a tribute to our
police force. We have every reason to be
proud of our police force, and of the work
they have done in re-establishing law and
order in our midst. It has not been an easy
task, but I think we can say that from the
highest to the lowest rank all have devoted
themselves whole-heartedly to re-establishing
this Province in the position in which we
wanted to see it two years ago, and in which
it is now. The police force have naturally
been aided by the calm and courageous attitude of the population, and I think we may
congratulate those who reside on the Border,
or close to the Border, and have been so
calm and courageous and realised that it was
by co-operation and by watching events rather
than by rushing to conclusions that the steadying atmosphere which prevails at the present
moment was to be brought about. The hon.
Gentlemen who seconded the Address ascribed
the credit for this change in the state of the
Province to the Prime Minister and the
Government. Perhaps the Prime Minister
and the Government are entitled to a certain
amount of credit, but the major portion of
the credit belongs to the population of the
Six Counties, who, realising their responsibilities, responded to the call which was made
upon them and have established public
opinion in the state in which it is possible to
successfully carry on the Government of a
great community.
When we look across the border to the Free
State, it is not possible to view the
situation there with any satisfaction. From
what we read in the newspapers
conditions in the Free State seem almost
chaotic. We read of the destruction of life
and of the destruction of property, and we
wonder when these clouds will be removed,
and when we shall see the South of Ireland a
prosperous and self-contented unit. Our
sympathies naturally go out to any party in
the South of Ireland who are desirous of
establishing and maintaining law and order,
and we feel an element of sympathy with the
Free State officials. Although I cannot say
we see eye to eye with them on many of those
great questions which exercise our minds, I
feel that it is my duty on this occasion to say
or do nothing that will hamper them in
their work of establishing law and order. I
need hardly say that I regret the suggestion
of the erection of a tariff wall That suggestion, curiously enough, was made upon the
very day when I was urging, to the best of
my ability, the case for the freedom of the
import of cattle into Great Britain, the cattle
of the Free State as well as the cattle of
Northern Ireland. Such a tariff wall is bound
to be provocative and cannot be in the true
interests of either North or South, singly or
together. I sincerely hope that saner councils
will prevail, and that a tariff wall, like the
boycott, will be found to be a very two-edged
weapon.
The generally sympathetic spirit and
attitude of the British Government is a matter
to which I should like to draw attention. We
see in office a Conservative and Unionist
Government, and I feel sure that they are
willing to recognise, as we do, that Ulster is
an integral part of the United Kingdom. I
need hardly say that I was somewhat disappointed at the unsympathetic reply I
received from the Imperial Minister of Agriculture when I ventured to bring forward in
the House of Lords the question of the
restriction of the importation of cattle. I
should like to add that I very much regret that
the speech which Lord Salisbury delivered on
that occasion did not receive the full
publicity here to which I think it was
entitled. The course of that debate is well
within the recollection of hon. Members. I
endeavoured to the best of my ability to have
the six days' detention removed on cattle
which are exported from this country into
England. It was a restriction we all
agreed to when there were outbreaks of Foot
and Mouth Disease which entailed the danger
of the spread of that disease. That is a restriction which we are always willing to admit
whenever there is a danger of that disease
becoming prevalent in England, but this restriction, which is a statutory restriction
rather than the result of administrative
action, is something to which we cannot agree
without very strong protest.
I do not wish to go into that debate again,
because I feel it is fresh in your minds, and
whereas the reply which we received from the
Ministry of Agriculture was not sympathetic,
I should like to take this opportunity of reading to you the words which occurred at the
end of Lord Salisbury's speech. He said this:
I can only repeat what I said on the
previous ocacsion, that we are profoundly
17 Debate on the Address

27 FEBRUARY 1923
Adjournment.
sympathetic with Ulster in all its interests
and all its desires, and in so far as the
safety of our herds will permit we should
be only too anxious to do all that we could
to accept what is dictated by the wishes of
Ulster. But, may I repeat, I make no
pledge whatever on the part of the Government. The Government must remain in
this matter absolutely unpledged. If and
when circumstances alter then we shall, of
course, find it our duty to consider the
changed situation in the light of the
changed circumstances. In the meantime,
I can only appeal to my Noble Friend not
to put us to the trouble of a division, which
would give the impression that we were
opposed either by conviction or sentiment
to the wishes of Ulster.
So that I think we can take courage from
the words which fell from Lord Salisbury,
who has been over here, and I hope will come
over again. I feel sure we shall tell him as
we did on a previous occasion that we value
his services to our cause. I have ventured,
perhaps, to detain the House too long at the
beginning of an
sure I am most grateful to the House for the
indulgence they have allowed me. I only
hope that this session which has been inaugurated in so satisfactory and so happy a
manner by his Grace the Duke of Abercorn
will be the forerunner of many more, in
which this Province will increase in prosperity for a long time to come.
arduous session. I am
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Address be presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors of
Ireland.
ADJOURNMENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move: "That this House do now
adjourn."
On Question Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
Minutes after Four of the Clock
B
19 Message from the Governor.

THE SENATE.

Adjournment.
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 28th February, 1923.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPERS PRESENTED.
Ministry of Commerce: (1) Report of proceedings under the General Pier and Harbour Act, 1861, and the General Pier and
Harbour Amendment Act, 1861. (2) Gas
(Carbon Monoxide) Order (Northern Ireland),
1923, laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
MESSAGE FROM THE GOVERNOR.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to read the
Message which has been received from His
Grace, the Governor:
I have received with great satisfaction
the expression of your thanks for the
Speech with which I have opened the
present session.
ABERCORN, Governor of
Northern Ireland.
STANDING ORDERS RELATIVE TO
"
LOCAL BILLS.
AND
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE
FERRARD: I beg to move the appointment
of the Joint Standing Orders Committee to
consider Standing Orders in reference to Local
Bills, and I move that the Senators following,
with the Chairman of Committees, be named
of the Committee:-
Mr. James H. Dickson.
Mr. T. M. Greer.
Mr. J. A. Woods.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
DEPUTY SPEAKERS.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that the Senators following be
named Deputy Speakers of the Senate during
the present session of Parliament:-
The Right Honourable Sir James Johnston.
The Right Honourable Samuel Cunningham.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
BILLS PRESENTED.
PUBLIC RECORDS BILL [S]
"to establish a Public Record Office of
Northern Ireland for the reception and preservation of certain public Records appertaining to Northern Ireland, and for purposes
connected therewith," presented by the
Marquess of Londonderry, read the First time,
ordered to be printed, and to be read the
Second time on Thursday, 15th March next.
PROMISSORY OATHS BILL [S.]
"to amend the law relating to Promissory
Oaths in its application to Northern Ireland,"
presented by the Marquess of Londonderry,
read the First time, ordered to be printed,
and to be read the Second time on Thursday,
15th March.
COMPANIES (RECONSTITUTION OF
RECORDS) BILL [S.]
"to provide for the Reconstitution of Records relating to certain companies having
their registered offices in Northern Ireland,"
presented by Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard, read the First time, ordered to be
printed, and to be read the Second time on
Thursday, 15th March.
LAND TRUST (POWERS) BILL [S.]
"to enable the Irish Sailors' and Soldiers'
Land Trust to carry out in Northern Ireland
the purposes for which it has been established
by law," presented by Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard, read a First time, ordered to be
printed, and to be read the Second time on
Thursday, 15th March.
ADJOURNMENT.
CONGRATULATIONS TO MR. SPEAKER.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn,
and in doing so I feel that I should be voicing
the opinion of everybody in this House if I
congratulate you, Sir, on the fact that His
Majesty has conferred upon you the high
office of Vice-Admiral of Ulster, an office
which was held by your illustrious father before you. I beg to move that this House do
now adjourn, and the date on which I think
it would be convenient for Members to reassemble would be 15th March.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before putting the Motion,
I beg to thank the Noble Marquess for his
kind words of congratulation. I feel
particularly pleased on receiving this honour,
not only for the honour itself, but because it
was held by my father. I thank you very
much
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Five Minutes after Three of the

Public Records Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): It is desirable for the
present to defer the progress of this Bill, and
I would venture to put before the House the
reason for taking that course. I think it can
be very briefly stated. The Government of Ireland Act contemplates the setting up, by
legislation of the Northern Ireland Parliament, of a Public Records Office of Northern
Ireland, and that this Public Records Office
shal!, when set up, not only afford a place
for the reception of Records relating to matters within the jurisdiction of the Northern
23 Promissory Oaths Bill.
Land Trust Powers Bill. 24
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
Ireland Government, but also, if the Imperial Government are willing, for the reception of Northern Ireland Records which relate to Imperial matters, such as the Records
of the Supreme Court of Judicature.
The Bill will require to provide machinery
for the collection and deposit in the new Records Office of Records under the jurisdiction
of both Governments. In order to make sure
that this machinery will be suitable for the
purposes of both Governments, the actual details for the Public Records Office are still
under consideration by the parties concerned.
That is the reason I ask the House to forgive
me if I do not proceed to-day with the Second
reading of this Measure.
Ordered: "That the Second reading of said
Bill be put off sine die."
Promissory Oaths Bill.
Order for the Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that the Promissory Oaths Bill
be read a Second time. The Bill is to amend
the law relating to Promissory Oaths in its
application to Northern Ireland. I do not
feel that there is any necessity for me to
trespass on the indulgence of the House over
the details of this Measure. The Promissory
Oaths Act, 1868, is the general authority
governing the taking of the oath which is
known as the Oath of Allegiance. This Act
is in force in Northern Ireland by virtue of
Section 61 of the Government of Ireland Act.
It specifies certain office-holders as being required to take the Oath of Allegiance, but
does not include Civil Servants. By a Defence of the Realm Regulation No. 45 E.E.,
clerical or administrative officers serving in
an established capacity in His Majesty's Civil
Service were required to take the Oath of
Allegiance, and this Regulation expired at
the termination of the War, namely 31st
August, 1921. A similar Regulation. No. 45
E.E. of the Restoration of Order in Ireland
Regulations, is legally still in force in
Northern Ireland, but as it also relates to
His Majesty's Civil Service in Great Britain,
it has no application to His Majesty's new
Civil Service in Northern Ireland.
It will be remembered that under the
Local Government Act which we passed last
year, a Declaration similar to the Oath of
Allegiance, but including a reference to the
King's Government of Northern Ireland as
well as to His Majesty himself was required
to be taken by members and officers of local
authorities in the Province, and the present
amendment of the law relating to Promissory
Oaths in this country is merely the logical
sequence to Section 5 of the Local Government Act to which I have referred. It would
be quite illogical were we to insist on public
servants under the local authorities making
a Declaration of Allegiance as a condition
of holding office if, at the same time, we
did not require a similar declaration from
public servants of the central administration.
There is a requirement now that public servants under the local authorities should
make a Declaration of Allegiance and therefore it will be perfectly obvious to this House
that a similar Declaration must also be required of the central administration, which
is His Majesty's Civil Service in Northern
Ireland.
In regard to this Bill, I am certain of two
things: Firstly, that it will meet with no
objection on the part of our Civil Service,
and Secondly, that it will nowhere be interpreted as a sign of any lack of confidence
on our part in the loyalty of a body which
has already rendered such signal service to
the State, service which I do not think this
House or the country generally has been slow
to recognise.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that the House
and the public generally will accept this B
with the utmost alacrity. It is, if I may
say so, not only the sort of Bill which the
people of Ulster want; it is exactly the Bill
which they mean to have. The only point
I would raise upon it is in the nature of
asking a question I make out—at least I do
so from a first reading of the Bill-that
the intention is that any person temporarily
employed for a period exceeding one month
would be required to take the Oath.
That
is how at first sight I read Sub-section (b) of
Section 1. It is not a matter on which we
could possibly raise the smallest objection, because our intention is undoubtedly that any
person who takes public money shall conform
to our Province and subscribe the Oath of
Allegiance both to His Majesty and to the
established Government.
On Question, Motion ("That the Promissory Oaths Bill be read the Second time ")
agreed to.
Bill read the Second time accordingly and
committed to a Committee of the whole
House, the House to be in Committee on
Wednesday next.
Land Trust Powers Bill.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD :
I owe an apology to this House about this
Bill. I very much regret to inform this
25 Land Trust Powers Bill-

15 MARCH 1923.
Second Reading Deferred.
House that this Bill has not yet left the
printers' hands, and has not been circulated
to Members of this House. Consequently it
will not be possible to take the Second Reading of this Bill to-day. I propose that the
Second Reading be taken at the next sitting
of the Senate.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the noble Viscount
has, to a certain extent, the sympathy of us
all in the announcement which he has just
been under the obligation of making. I am
equally sure that nobody would presume, or
wish to presume, to suggest for a moment
any lack of consideration on the part of the
noble Viscount or any Member of the Government. I would not suggest any lack of
consideration on the part of any Member of
this House. Neither do my remarks convey
anything in the nature of criticism as regards
the diligence of the officers of this House.
But the fact remains that the Bill to which
the noble Viscount referred was read a First
time by this House about 14 days ago. Members of this House have been summoned from
more or less distant parts of the Province to
attend this House to-day, inter alia, for the
consideration of this Bill, and we
are informed that the Bill has not yet left
think
the printers' hands I venture to
that
shall not be considered as
pressing unduly the rights which we possess
if we ask that steps may be taken to make the
printers and those immediately responsible for
their control see that this House is not trifled
with. If this House is summoned to consider
a Bill on Second Reading this House expects
that the Bill will be ready for their consideration. If I may say so, I do not for a
moment criticise any Member of this House
in regard to this Bill. I know perfectly well
this unfortunate incident is not one for which
they are in reality responsible, but I do feel
that out of consideration for Members of
this House, particularly for those who live
at a distance-at a greater distance than I do
myself-steps should be taken to prevent a
recurrence of this incident.
we
Mr. GREER: I am entirely in accord with
the observations which the hon. Member
has just made. I certainly do not wish to
fasten the blame on anyone in particular and
most assuredly not upon the Government. I
am quite certain it is not their fault, but
undoubtedly someone has blundered. I object
most strongly to Bills being brought into this
House at the last moment as has been the
case before now. I think that we all ought
to have sufficient time to read the Bill and
consider the Bill before we vote upon it and
approve or disapprove of it. After all we are
legislating for Northern Ireland, which is
rather an important matter. I think the
least we can claim is that we should receive
the Bills that come before the House in pro-
We who take the matter, seriously
per time.
-I include myself among that number and I
may say every other member of the Househave to read over a Bill and, in addition to
that, have very frequently to refer to Acts
of Parliament that are mentioned in the Bill.
These Acts of Parliament we cannot possibly
carry in our minds, and therefore we ought
to have sufficient time at least to refer to
these Acts of Parliament mentioned in the
Bill. I protest most strongly against Bills
being brought up on the morning of the Session in which we are asked to vote upon them.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
should be the last person to make any complaint of the remarks which came from hon.
Members of this House. Naturally on such
an occasion as this, when I can offer no excuse on behalf of the Government, I feel that
an apology is owing to all the Members of this
House for the most unfortunate circumstances
which have necessitated the postponement of
the two Bills which were on the paper for
Second Reading and for discussion to-day.
It is indeed very unfortunate that this has
occurred, and I should like, on behalf of
the Government to offer an apology to the
House for this occurrence. It is quite true,
what the two hon. Gentlemen who have
spoken have said, that Members, at inconvenience to themselves, have to come very
long distances to attend the sittings of this
House. Naturally they have every right to
expect and to see that the Measures for discussion which are on the papers which they
have received will be ready for them to discuss and to vote upon if necessary. I am one
of those who heartily agree with Mr. Senator
Greer, who is averse to measures being
forced into people's hands, if I may say so,
without their having had an opportunity of
considering them and comparing them with
other Acts of Parliament, and coming to this
House fully acquainted with the Measures.
I see that the hon. Gentleman foreshadows
that these Bills will be hastily brought into
this House, and that we shall be called upon
to pass them with as great expedition as
possible. I hope that may not be the case.
As regards the actual facts. The Bills are
not printed. That is the difficulty with
which we are faced at the present moment.
These matters are dealt with on the other
side of the water by departments who have
been occupied with this business for very
many years past. I am not trying to claim
that we are an entirely new organisation
and that that is the reason. But I do feel
that, perhaps in the interests of economy, we
have not the scope that is enjoyed on the
other side of the water.
Criminal Law Bill.
Criminal Law Bill.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
Perhaps when I come down to more precise matters in dealing with the subject that
has been raised, I am not sure that I am not
virtually the culprit. (An hon. Member: No,
no.) Perhaps the hon. Gentleman who in his
kindness absolves me, would allow me to finish
my sentence. There has been an Education
Bill before us, and I have endeavoured metaphorically to lash the printers into celerity in
producing that Education Bill, and I have been
successful. But I am not sure when I consider the matter closely, but perhaps that
tyrannical disposition is responsible
directly for the fact that these Bills are not
printed. I should like to offer an apology.
There has been no negligence on the part of
the Government or of the Officers of the
House. We fully realise the difficulties as regards the time which Members get to consider these Bills, and also their inconvenience
in coming from long distances to attend this
House. I assure them that whatever steps
can be taken to obviate a recurrence of this
incident shall be taken by the Government.
Hon MEMBERS: Hear, hear.

Criminal Law Amendment Bill.
Mr. GREER rose to ask the Leader of the
House;
"Whether, in view of the fact that the
Government propose to introduce in the
present Session a Criminal Law Amendment Bill for the protection of young girls,
they will consider the desirability of inserting in the Bill a clause to enable unmarried mothers to take legal proceedings
against the putative fathers such as they
have power to take in England and Scotland."
The hon. Member said: When I gave notice of the question on the paper I had not
a copy of the Bill or I might have worded it
somewhat differently. I hope it has sufficiently raised the question on which I desire
to obtain the views of the Government. The
Bill which is to come before the House
shortly refers to the criminal side of the
transaction, but I wish to call the attention
of the House to what appears to me to be a
serious difficulty-the civil side of the transaction in the hope that the Government may
see its way to set that right. As the law
stands at present, a woman who has been
wronged in that way has no direct legal remedy against the other party to the transaction. She has to bear the odium and disgrace and also the expense connected with it,
and the other party rides off scot free.
As the law stands in Northern Ireland, she
cannot bring any action against the man, because the act of seduction in itself is not
regarded as an actionable wrong, giving one
party a remedy against the other, and the
only length the Courts will go is to assume
that an action may be founded on loss of service, that is to say, that if the woman is
in the employment as servant of some man
he may bring an action for loss of service
connected with the indecent act. The father
in certain instances may also have a right of
action, that is to say, if the woman was living
with him and if she was under age the law
presumes loss of service on the part of the
father. But if the woman is over age the
father has to prove that she was actually
acting as servant to him at the time of the
indecent act.
Now everyone can understand how difficult
it is to get the ordinary master, say, of a
domestic servant to take proceedings and appear in Court. to court the publicity which is
attendant to that in a case in which he has
no personal interest. Sometimes, indeed, he
happens to be personally interested, and he
is not likely to bring an action against himself, or the proposed defendant may be a
friend of his and he does not like to take proceedings against him. There is no law compelling the master to take proceedings. Even
the father very often is so much scandalised
by the disgrace of the family caused by his
daughter that he simply tells her to leave the
house and make the best of it. I have not
much experience personally of these cases, but
I actually know cases of both these kinds.
The only other remedy that there is, so far
as I know, is that a poor woman may take
her child into the workhouse and the guardians
have a right of action against the putative
father for the expense of maintenance in the
workhouse. I consider that is a very poor
remedy for the woman who has to undertake
the maintenance, education, and perhaps medical expense of rearing this child.
15 MARCH 1923.
Criminal Law Bill.

Criminal Law Bill.
summons
corporated in the present Session in the
Criminal Law Amendment Bill for the protection of young girls. I venture to suggest to
the hon. gentleman that that really is not the
place for the provision which he seeks to enforce.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: Hear, hear.
What I desire to-day is to have the law in
this country assimilated to the law prevailing
in England and Scotland. If the House will
bear with me for a moment I will say, if I
may, in a very few words, what that is. In
England under an Act of Parliament, which
I need not trouble you with, any single woman
who may be with child or who may be delivered of a bastard child may apply to the
justices in Petty Sessions for a
against the putative father. Such justices may
make an order for maintenance against the
putative father, and the money payable under
that order has to be paid either to the mother
or to the person appointed by the justices,
if they think it not desirable that the mother
should receive it. They can by a subsequent
Act order or provide for a sum of money not
exceeding 5s a week for the maintenance of
the child. The order is to have no effect if
the child has died or attained the age of
13 years.
In certain cases the justices may
extend that period to 16. That is the law
in England. Then in Scotland jurisdiction is
given to the Sheriff's Court to order a decree
adjudging a person within its jurisdiction to
pay for the maintenance and education of a
bastard child of which he is the putative
father and of the expenses incident to the
birth or funeral expenses of such child, in addition to maintenance.
It appears to me that where a woman has
been in this unfortunate position there must
be two parties in the case, and it is very
unfair to fasten all the odium, disgrace and
expense on one and let the other off free.
In many cases the poor woman has not the
means to pay for the maintenance of the
child. She loses her situation. She cannot
get another situation. Her father casts her
off. I desire to put an end to that state of
affairs. I do hope the Government will add
a clause to the present Bill assimilating the
law with that of England, or better still,
bring in a short Bill to deal with the matter.
I beg to ask the question which stands in my
name.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY.
I have listened very carefully to the remarks
made by the hon. Senator who has just
spoken, and I note that he has asked a question with a view to his suggestion being in-
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The proceedings which are contemplated are
not criminal but civil. The hon. Gentleman
has cited certain Acts and I think he must
be referring to the Affiliation Orders Act of
1914, which I think is the last Act which
has been enacted dealing with these special
cases. But be that as it may, what I should
like to say to the hon. Gentleman is, that the
question of introducing a separate Bill to
bring the law in Northern Ireland into con
formity with that in Great Britain is under
the consideration of the Government at the
present time. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
It is impossible to arrive at a final dicision
yet. As the hon. Gentleman is aware there
is at the present moment a Bill before the Imperial House which was ordered to be printed
as recently as the 23rd February, 1923. The
Bill is a Private Member's Bill, and the Government are at the present moment desirous
of seeing what the fate of that Bill will be
in the Imperial Parliament. I should like to
assure the hon. Gentleman that this is a
matter which the Government are considering
very closely, and I feel sure that before this
Session ends probably we shall be able to
bring forward some legislation in which I
have no doubt the hon. Member will be able
to take part and make these suggestions
which he thinks will be in accordance with
the principles he has put forward.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn
until Wednesday next.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten minutes before Four of the Clock, until

Wednesday, 21st March, 1923, at
a Quarter after Three of the Clock.

Promissory Oaths [8] Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
""
Moved: That this House do now resolve

Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.)
Clause 1-
(1) Every person who is at the passing of
this Act either-
(a) an officer serving in an established
capacity in the civil service of the Government of Northern Ireland; or
(b) otherwise employed on full time
duties in or by any department or authority of that Government
shall before the first day of July, nineteen
hundred and twenty-three, or before such
later date as may in any special case be
allowed by the Ministry of Finance for Northern Ireland, and every person who after
the passing of this Act is admitted to serve
as such an officer, or is taken into such employment as aforesaid, shall, within
month after he is so admitted or taken into
employment, take an oath in the form prescribed by the Schedule to this Act.
one
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to move an
Amendment to Clause 1, page 1, line 11.
After "or" (" any department or authority")
insert" by any." This is purely of a drafting nature. The word "in," which occurs
in line 10, can be appropriately used with
reference to a Department, but not with reference to an Authority, as the latter word
implies, as a rule, some single Executive
Officer. It is purely a drafting amendment
which I ask the House to accept.
On Question, Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The next Amendment which I desire to move
is on page 2, line 18. At end add
"6
(5) The said Ministry of Finance shall
have power to apply the provisions of this
Section to such persons employed on parttime duties in or by any Department, or by
any Authority, of the said Government as
that Ministry thinks fit."
This Amendment is intended to meet the
representations which have been made to the
Government, urging them to include within
the scope of the Bill certain cases of parttime employment. The Bill, as drafted and
read a Second time, deals with two cases-
(1) Permanent pensionable Civil Servants;
and (2) all other full-time employees of the
Government. That is to say. it only dealt
with cases of full-time employment.
The effect of this Amendment will be to
extend its provisions to part-time employees,
but it has not been considered necessary to
do this unconditionally, because on examination of the various cases it has been found
that the administration of the Oath would
often either render the procedure an absurdity or be ultra vires or impracticable. Parttime employees include charwomen, scavengers employed on odd jobs, occasional
labourers employed on harbour works, and
other persons engaged in this class of employment. There are, besides, Imperial Civil Servants doing occasional work for the Northern
Government, occasional consultants such as
bacteriologists, arbitrators and referees occasionally employed, and certain marketreporters, and occasional agricultural
inspectors.
It is recognised that it is desirable that
there should be a power to require the taking
of an oath by persons coming within some
of these classes, but according to the circumstances of each particular case. Accordingly,
this Amendment is brought forward in order
to give power to the Ministry of Finance, as
the Civil Service Authority, to apply the provisions of the Bill in suitable cases of parttime employment. I beg to move.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Clause 2 (Short Title) ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
