•
I have suggested that the easiest way would
be to put them all in the third-class roads for
the present. It would be easier, I think, to
turn third-class roads into first-class roads
than the other way about, and, therefore, I
should myself propose, if you think
it desirable to get rid of the halfand-half class, to put them
into the
third-class for the
to
present, subject
revision. But I am told by friends-and
the hon. Senator opposite (Mr. Dickson) has
emphasised the matter, and given instances
where some of these roads are undoubtedly
really through roads-that very considerable
objection indeed is felt, not unnaturally, to
classing these roads as third-class roads, even
temporarily. Therefore, the amendment that
I have put down seems to be as objectionable
to many people as the amendment just
moved. The conclusion to which this drives
me is that the authors of this Bill knew very
well what they were about when they drafted
it in putting these roads into a class by
themselves for the present till they were
sorted out by revision and classification; and
I hope the Senate will pass the Bill as
stands. rather than, as proposed to be
amended by the hon. Senator.
it
The objections to this class of half-and-half
roads are very obvious, but they are not
really very serious, because the difficulties
in account-keeping and so forth are no other
than what the Counties have in the past,
under Section 8 of the Local Government
Act, been in the habit of dealing with, so
that there is really no very strong objection
to the arrangement-if I may call it a compromise arrangement to be found in the text
of the Bill as drafted and laid on the Table.
Therefore, I shall feel obliged to vote against
this amendment.
I only say, before sitting down, that if the
revision is to correct grievances under the
Bill both as it stands and as it should be
amended, that revision will have to be done
with very great care, and carried out by
persons thoroughly acquainted with the
circumstances of this country. The directions under the Transport Act for classifying
roads are English directions. They are good
enough directions in their way, and they can
be made applicable to Northern Ireland
conditions by persons thoroughly acquainted
be
There can
with Northern Ireland.
thoroughly satisfactory revision from our
point of view without controverting any of
the instructions, but if these instructions are
used by any official who is not thoroughly
acquainted with the circumstances of this
country the revision will be a failure. I
a
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I regret that I cannot agree entirely with my hon. Friend
opposite (Mr. Montgomery). The circumstances of each county differ very much in
this matter, and I can quite understand
that in his particular county there may be
great difficulties in accepting this amendment. But I have given the best consideration I can to the circumstances of another
county, and there is nothing I can discover
to be said against the amendment. Some of
the second-class roads are of exactly the
same character as the first-class roads.
They start from centres of importance, and
though they may not lead to centres of
exactly the same importance, they lead to
other counties and are virtually arteries of
traffic. In the four Rural Districts the difference between the rate in pence as proposed
in the Bill, and as proposed in the amendment, does not exceed a fraction of a penny.
Therefore, I think there is no grievance on that score. I also find that
some of the first-class roads are not
continuous. For instance, part of the
road from Armagh to Newry as far as
Markethill is second-class, but from Markethill to Newry it is first-class, yet there is no
other road that I am aware of to lead to
Newry from Armagh. I think the simplification that would arise from combining these
roads would be of very great advantage in the
direction of economy and efficiency, and
having given the best consideration I can to
the matter I feel bound to support the
amendment.
Mr. GREER: I think all the Members of
the House are agreed that at any rate the
condition of the roads is deplorable, and we
all welcome the fact that a Bill is now before
the House which may lead to a very great improvement in the condition of the roads. I
am going to speak in support of the amendment. As the Leader of the House pointed
out when moving the Second Reading, in the
original classification of roads as main roads
and district roads, all roads in the county
on which there was considerable traffic from
one town to another were included in the
definition of main roads. The second classification Was based rather on the idea of
having first-class roads, which are arteries of
traffic from one county to another, forming a
network of roads all over the country, kept
in first-class order. A good many of the
roads in the county which were main roads
did not therefore appear in the definition of
first-class roads, and appeared practically in
the definition of second-class roads or district
roads. I know of many roads which used to
be kept up when they were main roads, and
which are now in such a condition that it is
impossible to motor on them. This whole
system has somehow or other broken down,
G
Roads Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Greer].
and surely we ought to be able to devise some
plan by which the roads can be properly
maintained. I know from experience that
the difficulty generally is that the Rural
Councils will not provide sufficient money to
enable proper repairs to be carried out to
roads. They have been working at this for a
quarter of a century. It is a quarter of a
century since the duty was cast upon them of
maintaining these roads in conjunction with
the County Council. That system has completely broken down, and personally I have
no confidence in the public spirit and enlightenment of these Rural District Councils
and I very gladly welcome the idea that
second-class roads should be dealt with in a
manner similar to the first-class roads. 1
shall therefore vote for the amendment.
Lieut.-Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL-I
would appeal to my hon. Friend opposite (Mr.
Hill Dickson) not to press his amendment. I
quite understand that the hon. Senator, having served so long on a County Council, and
having done so much towards improving the
roads in Co. Down-which I am glad to be
able to say are not at all bad, although of
course they might be better-desiring to get
greater control, which his amendment would
undoubtedly give to the County Surveyor and
the County Council. I am not opposed to
that provided I am convinced that there is a
real public necessity for it. But whatever
improvement it may effect in the roads, there
is one thing perfectly certain, and that is
that if this amendment is passed it will increase the rates in certain districts. Everybody is in favour of keeping down the rates
until a concrete case crops up, and then
people say, "Oh, it is only a penny," or It
is only twopence-that is nothing." We must
remember that these rates are paid, not entirely by Senators, or rich men in Belfast, but
they affect the District Councils and the
people who elect the District Councils, and
an increase of a penny in the rates is a serious
matter to a great many of those electors.

Let us
I am more in favour of the main roads
being under a single authority, and I think
the County Council is the proper authority to
control them. We have already got in these
"A" roads practically all the main roads,
and until we see what the result of the revision of the classification is, I would ask the
Senate, and I would ask my hon. Friend opposite (Mr. Hill Dickson) to go slow.
await the result of one year's experience with
the present first-class roads, leaving the Bill
as it stands. Let us see what the effect of the
revision of classification on the roads will be.
If that revision is satisfactory, I do not think
there will require to be very much alteration,
but if a small amending Bill is necessary, I
will support my hon. Friend opposite another
year, even if it should mean a little extra in
the rates.
Taking my own County, there are at
present 650 miles of main road, half County
and half District charges. Under the Bill as
it stands 250 miles will come under the
County Council, and if this amendment is
passed 145 more miles will come under the
Council. If you add those two mileages together it makes 425 miles, and if you take
that from the 650 miles, now paid, half by
the County and half by the District, it leaves
a balance of 225 miles which you are going
to throw entirely on the District rates. It
may be necessary, but I think it will be
quite sufficient for us now to pass the Bill as
it stands, and after we have had some experience we can then consider whether we will
put this extra taxation on the ratepayers. I
believe that any revision of the classification
would make a great change in the roads. I
know roads now marked as first-class or “A”
roads that ought certainly to be in the "C"
class. I know that the road on which 1
should have travelled to-day if I had motored
is a first-class road for a short distance out
of Belfast. It then becomes a "B" or seconu.
class road. Anyone would imagine that the
chief communication between Belfast and a
County town would be entirely a first-class
road, but a great part of it is not. There are
very many other roads which show that the
classification as at present existing is certainly not up-to-date. I believe that the
Government will agree to carry out a reclassification of the roads within the next
year. Why not leave this Bill as it comes to
far
from another place, so
as this
amendment is concerned, and then, if it is
necessary to amend it after the lapse of a
year we will do so even if we have to put an
extra rate on the District Councils. My hon.
Friend opposite said that while in the old
days the roads were used for local purposes
they were now used by the world at large.
but the way to meet that is by increased
taxation of heavy motors using the roads and
not by increasing the taxes of the ratepayers
who live in these rural districts. I would
appeal to the mover of the amendment not to
press it at the moment.
us
Mr. BARBOUR: I rise to speak in favour
of the amendment. My main point is proved
by the discussion we have had. I think we
all agree that it would defy human ingenuity
to think out any legislation which would
effect these changes that would be acceptable
to everyone. Seeing that that is the case, and
that from every point of view it would be
desirable that any change that is brought
about by legislation should be carried out
loyally by the County Councils with the
determination that they will make it a
success, I think that we and the Government
must pay some heed to the feelings of the
locality. There have been one or two things
mentioned that I think have attracted an
undue measure of attention on the part of the
speakers who have referred to them. One is
the question of the accountancy work. I think
the accountants in our County Councils to-day
tackle far more difficult subjects than the
Committee.

Roads Bill-
24 APRIL 1923
mere cost of the upkeep of roads, and I do
not anticipate that any of the extra work
involved by this legislation will embarrass
them in any way.
As to the passing of control, I think that
under this Bill County Councils will still
have the final say as to the amount of expenditure on any particular road, and we
must remember that on the County Councils
there are representatives of the rural districts. Consequently, I think the amendment
is one which, if any change is made, will
bring about that change with the smallest
amount of friction and feeling of hardship
amongst the people who are perhaps directly
affected. There are districts in the County,
which, if this measure were confined to firstclass roads, would have a real grievance in
that the mileage of first-class roads in their
area is comparatively small. That grievance
is removed to a great extent when it is
proposed to treat second-class roads in that
way. Consequently, I hope that my hon.
Friend who introduced this amendment will
let it go to a vote, and I sincerely trust that
the Senate will see its way to support it.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE and FER-
RARD: We have had an interesting discussion on the amendment moved by Senator
Hill Dickson, and I think 1 may say the
consensus of opinion of the House seems to
be in favour of the amendment. The effect
of it is to put second-class roads on the same
footing as first-class roads-that is, to take
them out of the hands of Rural District
Councils, and put them into the hands of the
County Councils. Though perhaps in some
counties this may entail a slight increase in
the rates, yet on the whole I think the House
will agree with me that it will be for the
benefit of the county and the community as
a whole if these second-class roads are merged
with the first-class roads. On behalf of my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, I am prepared to accept the Amendment, and to accept the principle that secondclass roads are of county rather than district importance, and should be merged into
first-class roads. If my hon. Friend goes to
a division I shall support him.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I am obliged for the
support the Government are prepared to give
to this amendment. The amendment came
from the members of the Association of
County Councils of Ulster and from my own
County and Rural District Council, of which
I am also a member. It was through these
bodies, all of them representative bodies; that
I was asked to bring forward this amendment, which I desire to press. I thank the
Government for its support.
Question put.
Mr. SPEAKER: I might explain the procedure, as we do not often have a division.
After three minutes have elapsed I will put
the question again, and if it is challenged I
will then ask the Clerk to read out the names
of the Senators, and they can record their
votes by saying whether they are content or
not content.
Question: Line 14, after ("road ") insert

will be deleted in consequence of Mr. Hill

Mr. MONTGOMERY: I do not move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-

to
Mr. GREER: I rise to move the amendment
which appears on the Paper in my name. I
hope that the passing of this Bill will lead
the reconstruction and substantial
improvement of many roads throughout the
Northern area, and I want to point out that
the present system of repair and reconstruction is generally condemned by experts and
Roads Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Greer].
that it is not by any means the best system
that could be devised. At present, when
considerable repairs are being done on a road
surface the road is divided into two parts.
One side of the road is taken up and repaired,
and the total traffic is bound to go on the
other side. Then when that strip has been
repaired the entire traffic must be brought on
to that strip and it is generally brought on
before that strip is properly consolidated. The
result is that the new road shows signs of
deterioration before the other strip has been
completed. It is absolutely necessary, unless
the amendment I suggest is made in the Bill,
that repairs should always be done in that
way, because there is no power to close a
road for any purpose whatever other than to
close it absolutely when a new road has been
constructed in substitution for it. I think
we passed an Act by which a road can be
temporarily closed for motor races.
are the only occasions on which
road can be closed.
Those
a county
I am informed that the proper way to deal
with these repairs would be to repair the
whole surface of the road at the same time.
Then the junction between the two sections
of road would disappear. At present it is a
serious blemish that two strips of road do
not thoroughly unite, but under the proper
method the road could be kept closed entirely
until the new one was properly consolidated.
In my opinion, that would be a very much
better way of dealing with these repairs. On
the ground of inconvenience to the public,
I do not think it would be particularly
inconvenient. I have seen it done in London,
and the traffic there is certainly much greater
than on the country roads in the Northern Area. In Oxford Street and other
streets in London, where they can divert
the traffic they repair the whole width
of road, and in other places where they
cannot divert the traffic they have to
repair the surface in strips. I would like to
have the same system introduced in the
Where it is possible to
North of Ireland.
find another way of allowing the general
public to get from place to place the road
under repair should be stopped by the county
council and the repairs made in the manner
I have suggested.
There is another kind of repair that I
In some cases
think is very badly needed.
roads have been made across bogs. The bogs
have been cut away and the road remains as
a sort of elevated strip sometimes six feet
and sometimes sixteen feet across the height
of land adjoining, which is not only a
danger-
Mr. SPEAKER: I do not think that has
got anything to do with your amendment.
Your amendment deals with the closing of
roads, and not as to how roads should be
repaired.
Mr. GREER: I bow to your ruling, but I
wanted to show that the only way those roads
can be dealt with is by closing the road, but
I will not proceed any further with the argument. The amendment I move is to place
the power in the hands of the Minister of
Home Affairs to close a road temporarily
after making due inquiries and seeing that
the public would not be injured, and to do
that at the request of the county council.
I beg to move.
Moved: After Clause 2, insert as a new
Clause-
The Ministry of Home Affairs may, on
application from a County Council, by
order direct that, for the purposes of repairing or reconstructing a road, that road
may be closed for public traffic within such
limits and for such period and subject to
such conditions as may be specified in the
Order.
Provided that no such application shall
be made to the Ministry unless the County
Council concerned shall have given public
notice of intention to make the application
by public advertisement in some newspaper
or newspapers circulating in the county in
two successive weeks previous to the date
of application, and by causing notice of the
intention to apply to be posted throughout
the county in places in which notices are
usually posted. (Mr. Greer.)
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The amendment proposed by the
hon. Senator is entirely a new proposal, and
is a drastic one, although it is a valuable
and I think a very necessary one. I am prepared on behalf of my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Home Affairs, to accept the
principle that these roads should be closed
entirely for the purpose of the roads being.
properly repaired, but I propose to substitute
for the hon. Member's amendment an
amendment of my own-an alternative
amendment, which I will read to the House.
I do not know whether the hon. Senator has
a copy of it. I think it meets our case, and
it will be more acceptable to the Government
than the hon. Member's amendment. It is
as follows:
The Ministry of Home Affairs may, if
application is made to it by the Council
of any County after such public notice as
may be prescribed by regulations under
this Act, make an Order after such local
inquiry as that Ministry deems necessary,
directing that, for the purpose of repairs
or reconstruction, a road may be closed to
public traffic within fixed limits, for such
period, and subject to such conditions, as
may be specified in the Order.

"That this House do now adjourn until

Mr. LESLIE: I take this opportunity of
suggesting to the noble Lord, the Leader of
the House, that that when the Minister of
Home Affairs draws up any Order that might
be necessary in connection with the new
Clause of the Roads Bill the House has
passed, he should bear in mind that if a main
road is closed to traffic that traffic has to go
by some other road, and it may well be that
if one of our great traffic roads is temporarily closed you will be diverting an amount
of traffic over one of these district roads
which are not getting any contribution from
the county-at-large. I think it will be quite
open to the Minister to deal with that point,
because I think it is one that ought not to
be lost sight of.
I would like also to take this opportunity
of asking when it is proposed to take the Report stage of the Criminal Evidence Bill?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member has raised a very
important point, but I think it could have
been more properly raised on the Committe
stage. It may be raised on the Report stage,
and I can assure the hon. Member that if
you, Mr. Speaker, are willing that he should
catch your eye he will have an opportunity
of discussing this matter. At the moment I
do not feel in a position to say anything on
the point. As far as I know, the Report stage
of the Criminal Evidence Bill will not be
taken until Tuesday next.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

1 MAY 1923
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 1st May, 1923.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
BASIL M'CREA ENDOWMENTS BILL.
BELFAST WATER BILL.
The following reports were made from the
Joint Committee on the said Bills, viz. :-
"That they had considered the said Bills and
examined the allegations thereof, which were
found to be true, and that the Committee had
gone through the Bills and made some amendments thereto.'
Ordered: That the reports do lie on the
Table.
PAPERS PRESENTED.
Orders in Council:
(a) Irish Free State (Consequential Adaptation of Taxing Acts) Order, 1923, dated
16th day of April, 1923.
(b) Irish Free State (Consequential Adaptation of Enactments) Supplemental Order,
1923, dated 21st day of April, 1923.
Presented (by Command), and ordered to
lie on the Table.
Criminal Law Amendment Bill.
Order for the Report to be received read.
Moved: That this Report be now received.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Criminal Evidence Bill.
Order for the Report to be received read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I have an amendment to move on
Report. I am not sure whether I should first
move that this Report be now received.
The SPEAKER: The noble Viscount should
move the Report first.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Report be
now received.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
FER-
Viscount MASSEREENE AND
RARD: I have an amendment standing in my
name which I beg to move. It is as follows:
First Schedule, page 6, insert:—
Unemployment
10 & 11 Geo. 5,
c. 30.
Insurance Act,
1920.
The whole Act
as amended by
any subsequent
enactment.
The object of this amendment is to enable
the wife or husband of an offender against the
Unemployment Insurance Act, 1920, to be a
competent witness in legal proceedings under
the Act. The class of offence to be dealt with
would be that of wrongfully drawing unemployment benefit, making false representations, etc. In England wives or husbands
were made competent witnesses in such cases
by Section 11 of the Unemployment Insurance
Act, 1922. It was not considered desirable to
introduce such a provision into the corresponding Northern Ireland statute until the
general law of criminal evidence was assimilated to that of England, which is now done
by the present Bill. The amendment, therefore, places the Unemployment Insurance Act,
1920, in the first Schedule of the Bill, thus
bringing offences under that Act within Subsection (1) of Clause 4. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: There is another amendment standing in my name, which reads as follows:
Second Schedule, before line 1, insert :-
47 & 48 Vict., c.
14.
This
The Married
Women's
Property Act,
1884.
The Whole
Act.
amendment is really consequential
upon the inclusion of the Married Women's
Property Act, 1882, which is mentioned in
the First Schedule, within Sub-section (1) of
Clause 4 of the Bill. Now that this is donethe Married Women's Property Act put in
the Schedule-there is no longer any reason
for the special provisions of the Act of 1884,
whose sole purpose was to remove doubts as
to whether a husband is admissible as a witness against his wife in criminal proceedings
under the Act of 1882. Possibly Sub-section
(1) of Clause 5 of the Bill would be held to
supersede the Act of 1884, but the repeal of
the Act as proposed will place the matter
beyond doubt. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I think as regards the
amendment moved by the noble Viscount, the
only thing to be done is to congratulate him
on having uprooted another weed from the
wilderness of single instances. The particular Act was necessary at the time, but it has
served its purpose. But as this is the last
amendment and the last opportunity we shall
have of dealing with the Schedules, I think
I might perhaps, with your permission, take
the opportunity of asking two questions
which arise on the Report stage. I would like
Roads Bill-
Report.
THE SENATE.
"
[Mr. Leslie]
to ask the noble Viscount whether it is the
intention of the Government to issue something in the nature of an explanatory circular
for the benefit of magistrates and police officers and others who would have to work the
new Act, of which they would have but little
experience? There are one or two points
under the Act which might cause some little
difficulty. I have a particular Section in my
mind on which I spoke on the Second Reading, but I learn from those with practical
acquaintance with that particular Section that
there is now no danger of misunderstanding.
If I may, I would go further and ask the
noble Viscount whether it is proposed to adhere to the two months mentioned in the
second Sub-section of Section 5? The fact is
that time has gone on, and it might be
thought that it would be better to name a
fixed date in order to avoid the possibility of
the Bill not being in operation by the Summer
Assizes.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member, Senator Leslie,
asked two separate questions which I will
answer now. As regards the first point that
there should be some circular issued explanatory of the effect of this Bill as regards
criminal evidence, I can only tell him that the
Government will consider very seriously the
advisability of issuing some sort of circular to
those concerned.
As regards the second question asked by
the hon. Senator with reference to whether
the Government intend to stick to the time
mentioned in the Sub-section to Clause 5,
which reads: "This Act shall come into operation on the expiration of two months from
the passing thereof," I propose, by leave of
the House, on the Third Reading, to move an
amendment to alter that, and to state a fixed
date on which this Act shall come into force.
Petty Sessions Fees and Administration Bill

Local Government (Roads) Bill.
Order for Report to be received read,
Moved: That this Report be now received.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to..
FER-
Viscount MASSEREENE AND
RARD: I should like, if I may, by leave of
the House, to add somewhat to the answer
that I gave to the House a few days ago
when this Bill was in the Committee stage.
We had an amendment by my hon. Friend on
my right, Mr. Dickson, on which we had a
lengthy debate. The amendment was to the
effect that second-class roads in the Northern
area were to be put in the same category as
first-class roads, and certain hon. Senators objected to this being done. Mr. Montgomery
and the right hon. and gallant Senator,
Colonel Maxwell, both spoke against this
amendment, but on rather different grounds.
Mr. Montgomery's view was that the effect of
putting second-class roads into the same category as first-class roads would be to raise the
rate that some Rural District Councils would
be called upon to pay, and, as hon. Members
are aware, he proposed that second-class roads
should be put into the third-class roads,
which the Government considered would be a
retrograde step. Colonel Maxwell, on the
other hand, though he did not absolutely disapprove of the amendment, thought it would
be much better to go slowly in this matter,
and that we should wait for a year, at anyrate, to see how it would work. He and Mr.
Montgomery also expressed the hope that
there would be a reclassification of the roads.
In my reply I unwittingly made no mention
of the points raised by the hon. Senator, and
so now, to avoid lengthy discussion, I think
it would be as well if I were to enlarge upon
what I said in my reply the other day. I can
assure this House that my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Home Affairs, has every intention that the roads should be reclassified
during the course of the present year. This
reclassification is, of course, a serious and
lengthy matter, and will have to be done in
consultation with the local authorities. I can
give the House the assurance that this reclassification will take place during the current year.
Of course hon. Members are
aware a reclassification of the roads does not
necessarily last for all time, but is liable to
revision as and when it is considered necessary. I thought it well to inform the House
of this fact, so as to avoid any questions and
that I might save time and trouble to hon.
Members.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the House has listened with satisfaction to the statement of
the noble Viscount, that we may look forward
to a reclassification of the roads, for the
original classification did not certainly receive universal approval. There is another
point to which I would like to direct attention. It arises out of the amendment moved
by the noble Viscount in Committee to the
Roads Bill.
Land Trust Bill.
1 MAY 1923
effect it is Sub-section (3), Clause 2:-The
Ministry of Home Affairs may, after such
public notice, and SO on, make an Order
directing that, for the purpose of repair or
reconstruction, a road may be closed to such
traffic, within such limits, for such period, and
subject to such conditions as may be specified
in the Order.
On
The point which I made the other day was
that if you closed the first-class road the
traffic that would normally go upon that road
must necessarily be diverted over some other
road. It may well be that the other road, the
temporary road, may be a third-class road,
rather unsuited for heavy traffic, and in consequence the third-class road may suffer very
severely. In order to put that road back
again into proper repair a good deal of expense might be caused to the rural district
responsible for the upkeep of that road.
the face of it, that would be unfair to the
Rural District Council. I would like to ask
the noble Viscount whether he is satisfied
that the powers conferred upon the Ministry
by this Sub-section are sufficient to prevent injustice? It might be held that the Ministry
could declare the temporary road, for such
and such period, to be regarded as a firstclass road and get over the difficulty in that
way, or the resources of the Ministry might
find another way out of the difficulty. I
submit that the point should be placed beyond
doubt. If the Government is not satisfied, we
should introduce a Clause on Third Reading.
Mr. GREER: I do not think that there is
much likelihood of any injustice of the kind
arising, and for this reason. No action can be
taken by the Minister of Home Affairs unless
the County Council requests him to make an
Order. The County Council consists not only
of members who are elected directly, but of
members of the Rural Council. The Chairman
of every Rural Council is a member of the
County Council. This closing of the road is a
matter which would necessarily come before
the County Council, who will have an opportunity of considering what damage it might
do to other roads, and the member who represents the particular Rural Council will
therefore have an opportunity of expressing
his view and of bringing the matter before
the Minister of Home Affairs. If he does so,
the Minister will take that matter into consideration, as well as all the other matters
that affect the question, and he can lay down
some condition with regard to the user of the
third-class roads which will prevent any injustice being done.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think the hon. Senator who has
just sat down has really answered the question of the hon. Senator, Mr. Leslie. I fully
appreciate the fact that where a road is closed
for repairs a third-class road may have heavy
traffic thrown upon it for a certain period,
and may deteriorate very rapidly, and the expense would be thrown on the Rural District
Council. He also asked if I am satisfied that
under Clause 3, the Clause which I moved in
Committee, no injustice will occur. I can
assure the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) that my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, has ample powers at present to deal
with such a problem by administrative action,
and that he does not consider that any new
legislation is necessary. I can give that assurance, and I trust that the hon. Member will
be satisfied with it, and with the further assurance that my right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs, will naturally deal very
sympathetically with any case brought before
him.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time on Thursday next.

Massereene and Ferrard.)

In Committee accordingly.

four of the Irish Land (Provision for Sailors

THE SENATE.
subject to any liabilities mentioned in the
Order.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I have an amendment to move, which
is as follows:
Clause 1, page 2, line 21, leave out from
"1919" ( ("Provision for Sailors and Soldiers)
Act, 1919") to end of line 34 (" mentioned in
the Order "), and insert:
"including power to carry out and complete
any scheme made by the said Board before
the sixth day of December, Nineteen
Hundred and Twenty-two, so far as respects the compulsory acquisition of land
thereunder, in like manner as if the proceedings taken by the Board before the said
date had been taken by the said Ministry
of Home Affairs in the exercise of the
powers conferred upon it by this Act.

(2) Any land, property or rights acquired
by the Local Government Board for Ireland
under Section four of the Irish Land (Provision for Sailors and Soldiers) Act, 1919, or
by the said Ministry of Home Affairs under
the said Section, as applied by this Section,
shall be transferred to and vest in the Trust
on such respective dates as may be specified
in Orders made by the said Ministry of
Home Affairs, without the necessity of any
transfer, assignment or other instrument."
This amendment is really to amplify Subsection (2) of Clause 1 of the Bill and to add
a new Sub-section to that Clause. The amendment does not in any way alter the principles
of the Bill; its real effect is to enable the
acquisition of land for pending schemes to be
completed by the Ministry of Home Affairs,
so that the schemes may then be taken over
by the Land Trust. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.

117 Civil Service Committee.

3 MAY 1923
Civil Service Committee
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 3rd May, 1923.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Unemployment
Insurance Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
That they have agreed to the Promissory
Oaths Bill, with an Amendment, to which
they desire the concurrence of this House.
That they have agreed to the Companies
(Reconstitution of Records) Bill, with Amendment, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time." (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Civil Service Committee.
Mr. BARBOUR: I beg to ask His Majesty's
Government for what reason the Civil Service Committee for Northern Ireland has
been reconstituted, since it was understood
that it was appointed temporarily on the
dissolution of the Local Government Board
for Ireland; and whether the Government of
Northern Ireland does not propose at an
early date to put in the hands of the
Ministry of Home Affairs the duties now
performed by the Civil Service Committee.
I see by the "Gazette" of April 6th that
this Committee has been re-appointed, and
as far as one can gather from the notice in
the "Gazette" it is to enjoy all the powers
and privileges which it has enjoyed heretofore. I think everyone will recognise that
the object of this Committee at the time it
was formed was to bridge over the hiatus
between the passing of the old Local Government Board and the coming into full
functioning of our Ministry of Home Affairs.
But our Ministry of Home Affairs, I think
we may all agree, is well in the saddle now
and quite capable of carrying out all the
duties which usually fall to be carried out by
the Minister of Home Affairs, and of these
duties I think a full measure of Local
Government is the most important. In the
opinion of some of us, the Minister of Home
Affairs does not enjoy that full measure of
Local Government that one would expect.
The position is really most anomalous. There
are certain officers to-day whom the County
Council have to select. After we have selected
them the Minister of Home Affairs confirms
our selection and it becomes an appointment.
With the appointment comes the settling of
the salary, which is also approved by the
Minister of Home Affairs. Time passes, and
this officer wishes to retire, and instead of
going to the Minister of Home Affairs, as we
would like to and as I hope we eventually
will, and very soon, he has to go the Civil
Service Committee which sits in London. We
think this is an anomaly and is bound to be
so, and we sincerely trust that the Government of Northern Ireland will be able to tell
us that this change will come about quickly.
I can assure the House that some Councils
are being extraordinarily embarrassed at the
moment by the necessity of having to go to
this Civil Service Committee in London, and
we feel it will be desirable in every way to
relieve the Local Authorities who are so embarrassed of that embarrassment. We think
it will be better for the discipline of certain
officers if they realised that they had to be
dealt with by their own Council at home and
after that by our own Northern Minister of
Home Affairs.
Mr. LESLIE: I must admit that until my
hon. Friend (Mr. Barbour) directed my attention to the paragraph in the "Gazette" I
was unaware of the re-appointment of the
Civil Service Committee. I only rise to support my hon. Friend, and to remind the
House that he speaks as one with many years'
experience not only of County Council administration but also of practical work on
the Finance Committee of the County Council.
Other hon. Senators no doubt in their different counties also sit on their Councils and
and on their Finance Committees and I think
they will one and all agree with my hon.
Friend that it is essential that the Council
should be master in its own house, subject
to appeal only to the Minister responsible for
the Local Government Department, and that
no County Council in the Six Counties should
be required to bring an appeal before a
tribunal sitting in England. I join with my
hon. Friend in the hope that the Government
will be able to give us some satisfaction in
this matter.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I fully appreciate the reason which made my
hon. Friend put down his question. He spoke
about the inconvenience caused to Local
Boards by an officer having to go over to this
Committee which sits in London, and the
other hon. Member who spoke (Mr. Leslie)
spoke about the County Council being re-
119 Criminal Evidence Bill.

THE SENATE.
Land Trust Bill.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
sponsible only to the Minister of Home
Affairs. I am afraid the answer I will give
him may not satisfy him. The Civil Service
Committee for Ireland was necessarily reconstituted when the Southern representative
ceased to be a member of the Committee on
the establishment of the Irish Free State.
The Committee is now known as the Civil
Service Committee for Northern Ireland.
The Committee was not appointed temporarily. Under Section 68 of the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, it was given certain
functions to enable it to safeguard the interests of officers of Local Authorities under
previous Local Government Acts, in order
that such officers might not be prejudiced by
subsequent Acts under the new Parliaments.
The Government of Northern Ireland does
not propose to transfer the duties now performed by the Committee to the Ministry of
Home Affairs for Northern Ireland. To do
so would be a breach of the Government of
Ireland Act, 1920, and would be outside the
jurisdiction of the Northern Government.
Criminal Law Amendment Bill.

Criminal Evidence Bill.

CLAUSE 5 (Application of Act, repeal, and
construction).
5.-(1) This Act shall apply to all Criminal
proceedings, notwithstanding any enactment
in force at the commencement of this Act,
except that nothing in this Act shall affect
the Evidence Act, 1877.
(2) This Act shall come into operation on
the expiration of two months from the passing thereof.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move the Amendment which stands
on the Paper in my name as follows:-Page
5, line 25, leave out from "the" to the end of
line 26 ("the expiration of two months from
the passing thereof") and insert, "thirtieth
day of June, nineteen hundred and twentythree."
The object of this Amendment is to ensure
that this Act shall come into operation before
the Summer Assizes. It will obviously be
highly undesirable if this Act were to come
into force while the Assizes were sitting, and
therefore to avoid that I beg to move the
Amendment.
On Question. Motion, "That these words be
there inserted," agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass." (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.

to return the said Bill and to acquaint them
that the Senate have agreed to the same,
with some Amendments, to which they desire

Adjournment-
Crime Statistics.
3 MAY 1923
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move the Amendment which stands
on the Paper in my name as follows:-
Clause 1, page 2, line 43, at end insert :-
"(4) Paragraph (d) of Sub-section (4), and
Sub-sections (7) and (8), of Section four of
the Irish Land (Provision for Sailors and
Soldiers) Act, 1919, shall not apply to the
powers conferred on the said Ministry of
Home Affairs by this Section, and the power
of making orders adapting enactments conferred on the Local Government Board for
Ireland by Sub-section (6) of the said Section
four may be exercised by means of orders
made by the Trust and the said Ministry of
Home Affairs jointly."
Perhaps I may be allowed to inform the
House that this Amendment meets the point
raised by Senator Greer who expressed a
doubt as to whether, in the absence of further provision, the powers of the Act of 1919,
so far as they are given to the Ministry of
Home Affairs, are governed by the time limit
mentioned in paragraph (d) of Section 4 (4).
If this were so, the Ministry of Home Affairs
would in effect be unable to carry out the
purposes of the Bill. The Amendment also
makes it clear that the provisions of the Act
of 1919 relating to the expenses of the Irish
Local Government Board and the audit of
their accounts are not to be applied, by corporation, to the Ministry of Home Affairs.
On Question, Motion, "That these words be
there inserted," agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD
I beg to move, ""That Standing Order XXV.
be considered in order to its being dispensed
with in respect of the said Bill." The reason
I ask the House to pass this Motion is that
the Bill may go through all its stages
at the present sitting. I think it would be
for the convenience of the House if that were
done.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: "That the said Standing Order be
dispensed with in respect of the said Bill."
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass." (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the House of
Commons to carry down the said Bill and
desire their concurrence thereto.
Adjournment.
STATISTICS OF CRIME IN COUNTY
ANTRIM.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, "That this House do now adjourn," and I think it will suit the convenience of hon. Members if I propose that the
House should not meet again until Wednesday of next week. The noble Marquess, the
Leader of the House, will be occupied all next
week in another place on the Committee
stage of another Bill, and I have to inform
the House that I cannot possibly attend here
next Tuesday. I hope it will meet the convenience of hon. Members if we adjourn until
Wednesday of next week.
Mr. LESLIE, who had given private notice
of the following question:
"With reference to the official statement
made last Tuesday in another place to the
effect that indictable offences in Belfast and
Antrim during the past year were mine hundred and eighty-five in number, whether it
is practicable to apportion the total figure,
and, if so, whether the Leader of the House
will state the result of the apportionment."
said:
I am sure that the arrangement the noble
Viscount has suggested is one we shall be
very ready to fall in with. I desire to take
the opportunity of asking a question of which
I have given the noble Viscount private
notice. On Tuesday last some statistics were
given in another place in support of one of
the ordinary Motions for a Vote of Supply.
The statistics related to the number of criminal offences brought before a judge and jury
in the Six Counties during last year. In the
case of two counties, Londonderry and Armagh no figures were given. In the case
of other counties, Fermanagh, Down and
Tyrone, the actual number was given for
each county, Fermanagh having a very good
record. I think the total was only 57 cases.
But as regards County Antrim, in which I
am more especially interested, it happened
for some reason that County Antrim and the
City of Belfast were brought in together.
Now, Belfast has its own cases, inseparable,
as I have heard many a Judge of Assize
saying when charging the jury-probably
inseparable from the size of a great city, and
it makes it almost necessary that the number
of offences which come to light in a great city
is somewhat large. It is rather unfortunate
perhaps that any figures should be given at
the moment because figures sometimes convey
a misleading impression. Anybody seeing the
figures suddenly and looking at them without
consideration might reflect only on the size of
the figures for this last year-the number of
offences-and might forget the circumstances
of them, particularly the provocation which
gave rise to a good many of the offences. At
the same time I do not think that the County
Antrim would desire to have such offences as
it has to its discredit amalgamated with those
of the City of Belfast. Therefore, I should
like to ask, would it not be possible to sepa-
Adjournment-
Crime Statistics.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
rate the figures so that such crimes as may
have been brought to light in the County of
Antrim may be seen in their true proportion
and that Antrim may take its fair place in
the Six Counties. Perhaps I may be permitted to say that I expect to find in the noble
Viscount an ally in this particular point,
because he is interested in County Antrim
and his responsibility for County Antrim is
far greater than my own.
Mr. ARMSTRONG : The hon. Member
mentioned that County Armagh statistics
were not presented. I do not know who was
responsible, but I can assure the House that
the statistics were exceedingly favourable to
County Armagh. The Judge at the late
Assizes commented on the much improved
condition of the county and the small number
of cases that came before him.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The question of which the hon. Senator
Leslie gave me private notice is one which
I also have very much at heart as he has.
I think I can satisfy the hon. Member. My
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, has furnished me with information
which will, I think, allay my hon. Friend's
reasonable anxiety for the honour of County
Antrim-a county of which he is so distinguished and honourable an ornament. I
need hardly assure the House that no reflection upon that county was intended by my
right hon. Friend, the Attorney-General,
when he made his statement. Perhaps I may
be allowed to quote the figures. They are as
follow:
Total number of Bills for indictable offences in Belfast, eight hundred and sixty-four.
Total number for County Antrim, one hundred and twenty-one.
It is unnecessary for me to add that I
personally share in the satisfaction and pleasure which I am sure these figures will bring
to my hon. Friend.
In justice both to Belfast and County
Antrim I must call the attention of the
House to the fact that these figures are the
numbers of Bills, not the numbers of cases
or persons indicted. I believe the figures
given by my right hon. Friend, the Attorney-
General, in another place for the other
counties were those of the numbers of cases
or persons indicted. Therefore, in order to
arrive at figures for Belfast and County
Antrim to correspond fully with those for the
other counties, we may make a subtraction
of one and a half to two per cent. to meet
those occasions upon which more than one
Bill was issued in a single case.
As regards the latter part of the questionwhether it is practicable to apportion the
total figure and whether the Leader of the
House will state the result of the apportionment-there need be no apprehension in my
hon. Friend's mind that when the statistics
are published there will be any likelihood of
his county being placed in an unfavourable
light. In these statistics each administrative
county is invariably shown separately, and
so assumes to itself the due meed of honour
or odium which attaches to it from its own
record. I hope in this case it will be a meed
of honour.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty minutes before Four of the Clock, until
Wednesday, 9th May, at a Quarter
after Three of the Clock.

Wednesday, 9th May, 1923.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.

BELFAST CORPORATION BILL.
Moved: That the Senators following be
named of the Joint Opposed Committee on
the said Bill:
Mr. H. B. Armstrong.
Mr. T. M. Greer,
Mr. W. J. McDowell,
-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Loans Guarantee (Amendment) Bill.
Message from the House of Commons:
That they have passed the Loans Guarantee
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
First time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill-the Unemployment Insurance Bill-be read the Second
time. The House is aware that throughout
the period of abnormal depression both types
of benefit have been available, but the object
of Parliament has been that, as and when
trade should improve, we should get back
to the covenanted benefit only. The emergency Acts of 1921 and also of 1922, in
addition to providing uncovenanted benefit,
increased the total number of weeks' benefit
whether that benefit was covenanted or uncovenanted, which could be paid by making
it possible to receive a certain specified maximum number of weeks' benefit in periods of
less than twelve months, which are called
special periods. We have had four special
periods, and we are now in the fourth period.
By the legislation of 1922 the current fourth
special period was to run from the 2nd
November, 1922 to 1st July in 1923, with a
maximum of 22 weeks' benefit whether
covenanted or uncovenanted. Unfortunately
although employment has improved, we are
still far from normal, and the circumstances
which then necessitated emergency legislation still continue at the present moment.
Accordingly the British Parliament has
passed an Act which extends the fourth
special period as far as 17th October in this
present year and increasing the maximum
number of weeks' benefit, both covenanted
and uncovenanted, to as many as 44. The
British Parliament has also made further
provision for the future, which I will venture to explain later when I come to indicate
the terms of the Bill which is now before
this House.
I feel sure that hon. Members will recognise that if special provision is necessary in
Great Britain it is still more necessary with
us here, and for two reasons-First of all,
because our unemployment is higher than in
Great Britain, and, secondly, because no outdoor relief is given in Northern Ireland. I
do not think that it is necessary for me to
give the unemployment figures since June,
1921, but I have them here, and if hon.
Members would like to hear them I can give
them. In the meantime the Government has
passed four measures which deal with this
matter. In the first place there was the
Loans Guarantee Act, the results of which
have abundantly justified the enactment of
that Act. Secondly, relief funds have been
promoted to the value of no less a sum than
£960,000. Thirdly, there has been and is in
in existence a Commission of Development,
and, fourthly, unemployment insurance legislation, of which the Bill now before the
House represents the latest provision which
is proposed by the Government.
The Bill which we are now discussing
follows out our policy of keeping on parallel
lines with Great Britain. Briefly it contains
the following provisions: There is an extension of the fourth special period, as I have
said, until 17th October, 1923. The period
as extended consists of 50 weeks, during
Unemployment Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
It
which 44 weeks' benefit may be drawn. That
is provided for in Clause 1 of the Bill.
enacts the continuance of the uncovenanted
benefit during the ensuing 12 months, subject to an over-riding maximum of both
covenanted and uncovenanted benefit during
the twelve months of 26 weeks. At the end
of the fourth special period in October the
benefit year is to run from mid-October to
mid-October, instead of from July to July,
as was provided in the original Act of 1920.
The object of this is to ensure that benefit
may be available as far as possible during
the winter months, when it is obvious that
benefit is mostly required. It enacts also the
making permanent of the additional benefits
which are payable in respect of wife or invalid husband, and also of dependent children, which under the existing legislation are
only temporary enactments. It enacts also
an improvement of the rule for preserving
continuity of employment for the purpose,
first of all, of removing the difficulties which
certain classes of claimants have found in
putting in the necessary six waiting days
before they are entitled to benefit, and,
secondly, to remove temptation to refuse
casual employment for a day or so for fear
that by so doing a person will have to wait
longer before he can receive unemployment
benefit.
And lastly, the Bill enacts an extension of
the borrowing powers of the Unemployment
Fund. The financial state of the Unemployment Fund for the 12 months ending 1st
March, 1923, is-I have the chart before methat there is a deficit which has accrued
during the year of £807,000. I do not feel
that the House will desire we to go into these
figures on the occasion of the Second reading.
These are more matter for the Committee
stage. So I will venture to move the Second
reading.
Mr. WOODS: I am sure we all regret that
the condition of unemployment remains such
as to necessitate the introduction of another
Unemployment Insurance Bill. I am sure we
agree that the Government have done all that
they possibly can to assist the people who are
unemployed. Still there are more things
that I think they could look into. For
instance there are a number of men-young
men-I propose to call them single men.
They may range from 21 to 40, and simply
because they are living with their parents,
and the parent is working, they are not
entitled to receive uncovenanted benefit. Now
the young
men feel that this is a very
serious grievance so far as they are concerned. They have been paying contributions towards unemployment benefit right
from the commencement of insurance, and
still when they are thrown out and receive
the period of covenanted benefit and become
entitled, as they think, to the uncovenanted
benefit, they are refused because there is a
certain amount of income coming into the
house.
Now the reason I mention this matter is
that I consider that to a working class family
this is a very serious grievance. The
majority of working men that put their sons
to serve their time to a trade have got to
support them during an apprenticeship on
a very small wage. Therefore when they
come to be men they think they should be
self-supporting. But the result is that there
they are, and because the father or brother
is bringing in a small income they have
to support them. These boys feel this matter
very much. I come into contact with a
considerable number of them, and they are
complaining to me day in and day out in
regard to this particular grievance.
Furthermore, it is the object of this
Government and of the local committee
appointed by the Government to popularise
the Labour Exchanges so far as they possibly
can, both with employers and with employees. Well, this is one of the things to
my mind that are going a considerable way
to make the Labour Exchanges unpopular.
Besides, the Government expect in future to
have all this money repaid by contributions.
Now these men ranging from 20 to 40-the
single men are the men who will pay a
great deal more of this refund than the men
who are the fathers of these boys, because
they will, perhaps, live a little longer.
Therefore they feel that they are entitled to
receive this uncovenanted benefit just as well
as the other men, simply because they have
paid contributions. I hope the Government
will consider this matter and see if something can be done to alleviate this grievance.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am sure we have listened with great
interest to the remarks which have fallen
from the hon. Member. There is no one
more entitled to speak on this subject or
more capable of eloquently putting his views
forward. I can assure him that I shall most
certainly mention the matter to my right
hon Friend (the Minister of Labour), and
between now and the Committee stage I may
be able to have some answer that will satisfy
him. But the matter which he has referred
to is one which comes properly up on tho
Committee stage; and I am sure that if he
thinks it advisable to put down an amendment in respect of the views he has put
forward, we can have a very beneficial discussion on the subject which affects such a
large proportion of the population of the Six
Counties. I will leave it at that stage to-day
Promissory Oaths Bill.
Companies' Records Bill.
9 MAY 1923
and undertake to represent the case to my
right hon. Friend.

read the Second time.) agreed to.

COMMONS' AMENDMENT.
(1) This Act shall apply to every person
who, on or after such date as may be determined by the said Ministry of Finance, after
consultation with the Ministry of Education
for Northern Ireland, is employed or taken
into employment as a school teacher, in like
manner as it applies to a person employed
in a department of the Government of
Northern Ireland, except that the time
within which the requirements of this Act
are to be complied with by a school teacher
shall be such time as may be determined as
aforesaid.
(2) For the purposes of this section, the
expression" school teacher" means a teacher
in any national, public elementary, or other
school in Northern Ireland to or in respect
of which any grant is payable out of moneys
provided by Parliament or any aid is given
from funds raised by means of local rates,
whether by way of salary to such teacher
or otherwise.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The two amendments to the Promissory
Oaths Bill which have come down from
another place have been made in order to
apply the provisions of the Bill to school
teachers. It will probably be within the
knowledge of hon. Members that the Local
Authorities Act of 1922 provided that servants of local authorities are bound to take
the Oath of Allegiance, and, therefore, if
and when the Education Bill becomes law
such teachers as are under the new local
education authorities will be bound to take
the Oath, whereas the other teachers will
not be amenable unless teachers as a whole
are included in the present Bill. I think
hon. Members will appreciate the necessity
for the amendment. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

and shall be payable subject to such conditions as may be prescribed by those

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The only material alterations which have
been made by the Commons in this Bill since
it passed through the Senate are, first, the
introduction of Clause No. 6 in the present
print of the Bill, and secondly, an amendment to Clause 5, Section 4, of the Bill,
contained in lines 10 to 14, page 5.
I would venture to explain that representations were made by a deputation of
chartered accountants and solicitors to the
Ministry of Commerce that some provision
should be made in the Bill whereby reasonable expenses incurred by companies for
professional assistance in complying with the
provisions contained in the Bill might be
borne by the State, and this claim was
certainly felt to be reasonable. Accordingly
this Clause, No. 6, was added to the Bill in
the Committee stage in the Commons.
The other amendment, as I have said, is
contained in Clause 5, Section 4, of the Bill.
The lines are numbers 10 to 15, and this
amendment is intended to re-impose the
requirements of this Act upon such companies as, after having been dissolved, are
restored to the register. Hon. Members will
realise that this provision is necessary as
otherwise companies which are restored to
the register might continue in existence
without attempting any compliance with the
provisions of this Act with respect to filing
copies of their Memorandum and Articles of
Association and other records. This bring
them within the scope of the Act.
The remainder of the amendments and the
alterations which have been made in the Bill
in the Committee stage in the House of
Commons are merely drafting amendments
for which I feel hon. Members will not require any explanation. I beg to move: That
the Senate do concur in the amendments that
have been made by the Commons.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Four of the Clock
until To-morrow at a Quarter
after Three of the Clock.

Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Third Reading of this
Bill I would like, if I may by leave of the
House, to make some reply to the question
raised by the hon. Senator, Mr. Woods, on
the Second Reading of this Bill with reference to the payment of uncovenanted
benefit to single persons. The hon. Member
asked the noble Marquess, the Leader of this
House, whether single men living with their
parents would not be entitled to uncovenanted benefit, and the noble Marquess said he
would represent the matter to my right hon.
Friend the Minister of Labour. I can assure
the hon. Member (Mr. Woods) that this has
been discussed with my right hon. Friend the
Minister of Labour.
As the hon. Member is no doubt aware,
there is no restriction on the grant of
covenanted benefit, and every insured person,
whether single or married, who fulfils the
statutory conditions is entitled to the full
statutory rates irrespective of family circumstances. The Acts have been extended to
enable benefit to be paid in certain circumstances to people even after they have exhausted all the covenanted benefit to which
they are entitled on their contributions. But
the income of the Unemployment Fund has
been seriously diminished by the grave
unemployment which is unfortunately so
prevalent, and it is therefore obviously impossible to pay this kind of free or uncovenanted benefit to every one out of work after
he has exhausted his contributions. Accordingly, uniform conditions have been attached to the payment of this uncovenanted
benefit in Northern Ireland and in Great
Britain, the underlying principle of the
conditions being that the diminished resources of the fund must be conserved for
those who need it most. In the circumstances
the hon. Member will understand that the
claims of the married men with dependents
Franchise Bill.
Adjournment.
16 MAY 1923
be allowed to ask the noble Viscount to into this benefit are obviously greater than
form the House what is the intention as to
those of the majority of single men.
the adjournment for the recess?
The whole question of the payment of uncovenanted benefit to single persons, however,
is at present under consideration both in
Great Britain and here. My right hon.
Friend the Minister of Labour assures me
that any modification of the present rule
which may be introduced in Great Britain
will, as far as practicable, be adopted in
Northern Ireland. I trust that this will go
a long way to meet the cases of hardship to
single persons to which the hon. Member
referred. I beg to move.
Mr. WOODS: I would just like to express
my satisfaction with the answer I have received to the question I put on this day week.
I was perfectly satisfied with the promise I
received from the noble Marquess, the Leader
of the House, to have the matter looked into.
I was perfectly satisfied he would have it
attended to. It might interest this House to
know that at the same time the same question, or something similar, was asked in the
Imperial House, and a promise was given to
have the matter looked into. I am quite
satisfied that the Minister of Labour we have
in Northern Ireland will see that whatever
is done by the Imperial Parliament will be
done also in Northern Ireland.
now pass.

LOCAL GOVERNMENT FRANCHISE BILL
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill was introduced by the
Government of Northern Ireland as the result of repeated representations made to my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, regarding the urgent necessity for
some legislation to remedy abuses in connection with the Local Government
Franchise. Hon. Members are aware that
under the existing law the Parliamentary
and Local Government Franchise is
governed by the Representation of the People
Act, 1918. The Government of Northern Ireland are precluded, under the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, from altering the law
regarding the Parliamentary Franchise until
three years have elapsed from the date of the
setting up of their Parliament, but there is
no restriction on the Parliament in regard
to remedying defects in relation to Local
Government franchise.
Under the Act that I have quoted above
occupancy of land or premises in a Local
Government electoral area by a man entitles
him to the Local Government franchise
irrespective of the character or value of the
land in question. The result has been that
a considerable number of persons have been
admitted to the Local Government franchise
whose claim to the occupancy of land or premises arose out of the occupation of land of
practically no value at all. I may mention
that numerous cases of this kind have been
brought to the notice of the Government.
No doubt Members of this House are themselves acquainted with many such cases, SO
I will not weary the House with a recital of
them. I think it will be sufficient if I
merely mention a few glaring examples. For
instance, in one urban district it
established that the local Council had let
potato patches to a considerable number of
people; and on the ground of this occupancy
the people in question were admitted to the
franchise. In the same district stalls in the
fair ground were let to various persons which
qualified them to vote at Local Government
elections. Another instance was brought to
the notice of the Ministry of Home Affairs
was
141 Loans Guarantee Bill.

29 MAY 1923
Adjournment.
in which over 50 votes were obtained out of
small bog plots of practically negligible
value, the owners of these bog plots actually
residing and having votes in other areas.
Votes have been manufactured wholesale;
fictitious lettings have been effected in order
to create what I may call these bogus votes.
The Bill which is now before the House
purports to remedy these abuses in that it
prescribes for the Local Government vote
that the person claiming the vote must
be in actual occupation of land of the yearly
value of not less than £5. This Bill does
not in any way affect the Parliamentary
franchise, but at any rate it has this advantage, that it requires a man to have the
same qualification that a woman is required
to have in respect of the occupation of premises, that is to say, that on the last day
of the qualifying period he must occupy, as
owner or tenant, land or premises of the
yearly value of not less than £5.
The general effect of this Bill will, it is
hoped, obviate the difficulties I have already
referred to. It will also reduce considerably the enormous burden of work that has
been thrown on the registration officer during
the last few years in dealing with the
numerous claims that have been made,
based on very doubtful qualifications.
Furthermore it will ensure that the Local
Government register of electors will comprise
persons with a certain financial interest in
Local Government affairs.
As regards the form of the Bill itself I
have very little to say. It is really a oneclause Bill, of which the first sub-section
gives effect to the objects I have already
enumerated, and the second sub-section
enables the Governor of Northern Ireland
by an Order in Council to make such alteration in the registration forms as may be
necessary to give effect to the desired change.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment
Moved: That this House do now adjourn
till Thursday. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Might I
ask, as a good many of us are anxious on
account of next week as you know, if you
have any information as to when the Education Bill is likely to be brought before the
House?
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In reply to the right hon. and gallant Gentleman, I understand that it is just possible that
this House may receive the Education Bill
next Thursday. It is hoped we shall have the
First Reading of the Bill on that day, in
which case I take it that the Second Reading
will be on either the Monday or the Tuesday
following. The noble Marquess, the Leader
of the House, is away, but I take it the
Second Reading will probably be on Monday
or Tuesday of next week.
Mr. LESLIE: Might I ask whether it is
proposed to give two days for the Second
Reading of the Bill?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Member, I
cannot say definitely. It depends on this
House and upon how the debate proceeds.
If necessary no doubt two days will be given
for the Second Reading of this Bill. It
entirely rests on the discretion of the House.
I trust, however, that not more than one day
will be necessary.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Half
after Three of the Clock till
Thursday next at a Quarter
after Three of the Clock.
Education Bill.
Franchise Bill.

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 31st May, 1923.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
WEATHER FORECASTS.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
ask whether the Government will endeavour
to arrange with the Imperial Authorities that
telephone subscribers in Northern Ireland
shall have the same facilities of receiving
the weather prospects as are given to subscribers in Great Britain, seeing the advantage of such information to farmers and others.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The answer to the question asked by
the right hon. Gentleman is as follows: No
evidence of a general desire on the part of
Northern Ireland telephone subscribers to
participate in the facilities mentioned has
yet reached the Northern Government, but
if such evidence is forthcoming the Government will be prepared to approach the Postmaster-General with a view to securing the
extension of those facilities to Northern Ireland.

First time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I think
before the Motion is passed-I may be out
of order-but I think on this occasion in
the absence of the Minister of Education,
perhaps it might be the right thing for
this Senate, as it was mentioned in the
other House, to say how we regret the cause
of the Minister of Education's absence on
the introduction of this Bill. I had some
remarks to make, but for this reason I defer
them until the Second Reading of the Bill.
In the meantime, if in order, I would move
that we tender our sympathy to the noble
Marquess on the great loss he has sustained.
Mr. DUFFIN: I would second that if it
requires a seconder. I am sure we all deeply
sympathise with the Marquess of Londonderry. We regret the absence of the noble
Lord, the Leader of the House, on this
occasion, and the cause of it. I think I
might take this opportunity of saying as
one deeply interested in the subject that I
welcome the introduction of this Bill to this
House. As it has not been introduced with
any observations or with any ceremony on
the part of the Government I think really
should avoid any discussion or any
intimation of any alterations or amendments
that may be found to be necessary in its
passage through the Senate.
we
On Question, Motion ("That this Bill be
now read the First time") agreed to.

itself into Committee on the said Bill.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

In Committee accordingly.
(The Marquess of DUFFERIN AND AVA
in the Chair.)
