the
Mr. GREER: We all congratulate the
Minister of Education on having piloted this
Bill safely through the somewhat stormy
conditions in the Lower House, and
on having introduced it into the somewhat serener atmosphere of this House
in such an interesting and lucid manner.
I believe it to be an admirable attempt
to deal with complicated and difficult question, and I have no doubt it will
prove to be very successful. I want to point
out that there is one thing we can take some
comfort from, that is, that if not perfect,
it can be altered. The Bill will soon pass
into law, and will become the law of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, but it will
not be by any means the law of the Medes
and Persians that cannot be altered, and we
know that in somewhat similar conditions in
Canada they produce an Amending Bill
almost every year. We can do it quite
easily; so if as we gain experience, and after
the scheme is fairly launched on the community, we find that it can be improved in
any manner, why it is quite easy to pass
an Amending Bill, and if any difficulties
arise, set them right.
I am not quite sure that the noble
Marquess adhered altogether to his policy of
decentralisation in the Bill. He has
arranged that the appointment of teachers
will not rest with the local committees, but
will be reserved for the education authority.
I am not quite satisfied in my own mind that
that is a wise policy, but time will show. I
am afraid it may lead to a much smaller
number of schools being transferred than if
the appointments had been left to the School
Committees. On the other hand, I am not
at all sure that it would be desirable to have
practically every school transferred at once.
I think the policy will be for the education
authorities to go slowly and gradually. If
they have to pay the entire cost of maintenance of the transferred schools they have no
idea of what it may amount to.
It may
amount to a very large sum, whereas if the
schools are not transferred, as I understand
the Bill, they have only to pay one-half of
that cost, and it might not be a bad thing
if at first a large number of schools would
not be transferred. I do not really know
whether that is the policy of the Ministry
or not upon this question.
There is one matter to which I wish to
call the attention of the House, and that is,
that there is no power given to the county
councils to contribute anything to the maintenance of public county libraries. I look
upon libraries as a not inconsiderable means
of education. They take the place to some
extent of continuation schools, and if, when
children are at school the teachers can only
get them to acquire what I may call
the reading habit, if they begin to
read for themselves and to enjoy reading, I think they will derive very great
benefit from those county circulating
libraries. We have got one in County Antrim.
It is established under the Carnegie Trust.
Under that Trust the Trustees have take
rooms for the central repository? They have
appointed a librarian and an assistant. They
have placed there some 9,000 volumes of
books. They have undertaken the expense of
sending those books all over the county free
of charge wherever they are wanted. I am
very glad to say that these books are very
widely read over the county, and are going to
be an excellent thing for disseminating information of various kinds. They are not by any
means all novels and light literature. I can
give particulars of the kind of books that are
being read. Amongst the number are 100
books of philosophy, 200 of religion, 300
sociology, 400 language, 500 natural science,
600 useful arts, 700 fine arts and recreation,
800 literature, 900 history, and geography and
travel 1,374; fiction, 6,454; juvenile fiction,
3,650; and the total number of issues of books
during the first year amounted to 12,888.
Now that library has only been working
one year, I think that is a wonderful record
for one year's work. Under the Carnegie
Trust the Trustees will only be responsible for
the maintenance of this library for two years.
It was established in May, 1922, so that in
May, 1924, they will cease to help, and the expenditure then it comes to something between
d and 1d in the pound-will have to be raised
by rate. Some County Councils I know in the
Northern area are anxious to be in a position
to strike a rate for the support of a library
of this kind, and I think it would be a very
desirable thing in the interests of education
that the County Council should be empowered
to contribute a rate say, not exceeding a penny
in the pound, which would never amount to
a very large sum, for the purpose of supporting a library of this kind.
It may be said this would be for the education of adults, but it need not necessarily be
So. I observed in the report of an annual
meeting that was held in England, some time
ago, that Mr. Clynes, the celebrated Labour
Member, made a speech, and he stated that
he was obliged to leave school at the eary age
of twelve years. He attributed his success in
life very largely to being able to avail himself
of a circulating library, and he said that these
libraries could not be too much encouraged.
He said also that this was a democratic age;
that the democracy might be a dangerous
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
4 JUNE 1923
thing if it were not properly informed, and
that it was the duty of everyone to do everything he possibly could to inform adults as
well as younger people..
I have ventured to make a longer statement
on this subject than I originally meant, but
I look upon it as a very important subject.
I hope the Government will see their way to
introduce a clause in the Bill to empower
County Councils to strike a rate. At the
present time they are not able to strike a rate
for such a purpose. There might be some way
of doing it under the Libraries Act, if there
was any objection to doing it under this Bill;
but I think as this is an Education Bill, and
as the libraries would really help the education of young people and old people it would
come very well under a Bill of this kind. But
if it can only be brought under the Libraries
Acts I hope the Government will see their
way to introduce a Bill for that purpose.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I may say
it is with great pleasure I have listened to
the introduction of this Bill by the noble Lord,
the Leader of this House. There were several
points that struck me, but one struck me
most; and that was where he referred to the
welfare of the children as being the primary
object of this Bill. Now as far as I can see
under the Bill children have to go to school,
and there is no protection for these children
against any petty tyranny that may be going
on. I do not profess to be an authority on
education, but I know in the days when I was
at school I met now and again with a class
of man who, when he could, would tyrannise
over the boys. I would be very glad to see
introduced into this Bill some protection for
children in case of punishment. I understand
in the army no man can be punished without
its being logged. I do not see in a case of
this kind why a schoolmaster should not
have to log any punishment that takes place.
The information, of course, would be confidential, but it would enable those who look
after the schoolmasters to see whether oneperhaps orphan boy, or one child was being
"sat upon," and whether or not the fault was
altogether that of the child. Therefore I
would be very pleased if the noble Lord would
introduce some clause into the Bill that would
protect children in a case like that, and allow
those who are responsible to see something of
what happens.
I would just say that as far as I can see
the Bill is an effort to solve a most complicated and difficult problem, which has been
found almost impossible to solve in all other
countries. I think we may take credit that
one of the Members of this Senate has introduced into this Parliament a Bill which I
hope will be worthy of that Member, and of
all honour in the years to come.
Hon. MEMBERS: Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: It is quite impossible for any
of us to approach the consideration of this
Bill without experiencing, and without endeavouring to express our deep sense of the
gratitude which is due from all the people of
Northern Ireland to the noble Marquess for
the exhaustive exposition of the Bill, which
he has given this afternoon, and still more
to him--and indeed I include, as he did himself-the staff that has worked with him-
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: Gratitude is due for the unremitting attention that has been given to
this matter, since the responsibility for education in the Northern area was confided to
the noble Marquess. And I think we must
also record our thanks to the hon. Gentleman who represents West Belfast elsewhere,
and to the members of his Committee, for
the self-sacrificing devction which they displayed in giving up so much of their time to
hearing evidence, and subsequently to the
compilation of a report remarkable for its
exceptional value. The report was very remarkable, partly because it had a historical
introduction which was very much wanted,
and partly perhaps because of the appendices, which were extremely useful. I could
not help noting with great satisfaction the
tribute which Mr. Lynn and his Committee
paid to their secretary for the very great
assistance he had given them. I also note
the fact that the noble Marquess recorded his
great appreciation of that same official. Very
much of the success of the Lynn Report, I
think, must be due to that secretary.
The noble Marquess claims that the Bill
which he has laid before us follows in the
main the recommendations of the Interim
Report of the Committee, that it gives to us
a system of education second to none in the
world, that it is based on broad principles of
decentralisation, and decentralisation working on the modern lines of taking the parent
and the teacher into partnership with the
Ministry rather than on the lines of anything
approaching autocratic control. He asks that
we should read every clause and every line
of the Bill with a view to considering what
is in the best interests of the child. He says
he has recognised parental responsibility, and
has given full weight to the feeling that is
entertained, and deeply entertained, in
Ulster that secular education is not all, but
that opportunity must be given for instruction in other matters; and finally, that the
Bill as submitted to us is a consolidated Bill,
complete in itself, and almost entirely free
from the vice of legislation by reference to
which the noble Marquess has more than
once, both here and elsewhere, declared uncompromising opposition.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Hear, hear.
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
Mr. LESLIE: When we come to examine
the Bill in detail-and I may say that inexperienced Parliamentarians like myself are
at a disadvantage in trying to take part in a
Second Reading discussion on a Bill of such
length and such complexity when they know
that they must, as far as possible, keep away
from detail-when we come to look into the
details of this Bill and follow the educational
history of the child of the future, I think we
shal! all agree that every claim that the
noble Marquess has put up is being made
good by him. The child, I think by the option only of its parents, begins by going into
the nursery school, and staying there from
two to five. From six he goes to the public
elementary schools. He is entirely protected
from premature employment before the age
of twelve. That was a point which was not
mentioned, but I cannot regard the provisions that are made in this Bill for restraining the employment of children as a detail.
I think it is one of the principles of the Bill.
It is a provision perhaps, in a way, subordinate to education, but it is a very important
one. And this Bill does give absolute protection from employment before the age of
twelve, and makes other restrictions. And
finally the child is given the advantage of
a system of advice when it comes to the consideration of the choice of his career. Meantime he has had opportunities of passing
through the intermediate and secondary
schools which are included in the Bill,
and of obtaining scholarships, and his
health and SO on have been looked
into, and
looked
his general interests
after. When we come to the details,
or rather the detailed method of decentralisation, we get first of all the Advisory
Council, of which it is certainly unnecessary
to say anything, except perhaps to congratulate the Ministry on having steadfastly
adhered to the constitutional principle that
there must be no body between the Ministry
and control, and that the responsibility of
the Ministry to Parliament for control must
remain, and not be delegated to any council
whatever.
In regard to decentralisation, which I
think was described as the long step, in the
case of our two county boroughs the Lynn
Report appears to be exactly followed, but
when we get to the counties an apparent
departure has been made in the institution
of what it was at one time intended to
describe as Regional Committees. I now
understand it is under consideration to remove the word regional," and certainly I
shall not regret its disappearance. I think
the word, if I remember an earlier speech
correctly, was only adopted for want of a
better, and perhaps because the other words
were all taken possession of. The original
proposal of the Lynn Committee was that
the subordinate committee under the County
Council should be in each case either an
Urban District Council or a Rural District
Council. That would, as was pointed out in
the resolution in the Reservations to Report,
have entailed a multiplicity of bodies,
and would not have secured a breadth
of view which usually comes from a
body consisting of representatives drawn
from a larger area. I do not wish
to be in any way disrespectful to the
Lynn Report, for which I have a great respect, but I do think that the proposals that
the authorities should be the Urban District
Councils on the one hand and the Rural
District Councils on the other, were to some
extent, if I may use the expression, mutually
distructive, because in your urban areas,
certainly in the 24 or 25 small areas-outside
the two county boroughs we have only five
towns with a population exceeding 10,000you may be quite certain that the schools are
very greatly resorted to by scholars who belong to the adjacent rural areas. Therefore,
if you make separate authorities in your
urban and rural areas you get the position
that you are taxing the urban area and you
are sending your rural child into the urban
centre for education, where the rural district
has no voice whatever. That is what I
describe as a mutually distructive proposi
tion, but I do not think from the Report
that the proposal of the new education committes will be really objected to by those
who desire to adhere word for word to the
Lynn Report. After all, that Report dealt
very favourably with the question of possible
combinations of rural and urban areas into
one district, and also laid great stress on the
breadth of view which it found in the representatives from the urban small areas.
I am rather inclined to suspect that the
germ of the proposal for the Regional Committee is to be found in the reservation
under the signature of the hon. Senator
opposite (Mr. Duffin). These education
committees will have great responsibilities. Unless there is some further
change in the Bill it would still remain possible for these committees to be.
composed partly or wholly of people who are
not members of the County Council. That
will operate as a great protection to the
County Councils from the rather heavy
strain of overwork to which my hon Friend
(Mr. Greer) on my left alluded to-day, as the
hon. Senator the Chairman of the Londonderry County Council (Mr. Long) did a few
days ago while speaking on another matter.
The fact that your Education Committee
need not be composed exclusively of members
of the County Council will not only broaden
very advantageously the area of selection,
but will also limit the labours of the County
Council. While I welcome very sincerely the help of the County Council in
preparing schemes for the
these
areas
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
4 JUNE 1923
Education Committees are to operate in, I
am very glad the last word will remain with
the Ministry, for, I think, it is important
that there should be an outside view of the
distribution of these areas, and the Ministry
must necessarily be in a position through
the reports of its Inspectors, who, after all,
have many of them been travelling these
areas for a good many years, to know all
there is to be known about their geographical and other possibilities. I think the outside view will exercise a moderating and
certainly valuable influence on the suggestions of the County Councils.
We have to remember that if it is possible,
as it will now be possible, for a County
Council to suggest, and for the Minister to
approve, a scheme whereby there is in any
one county only one education committee, we
shall have to consider then the question of a
flat rate over that county. As the Bill stood
a few days ago before it left the other House
there was certainly no necessity for a flat
rate throughout a county. It was clearly provided that the education rate would be raised
by means of the poor rate for the original
area affected, and this point will require to
be watched. My hon. Friend (Mr. Greer) has
just spoken of the power which will rest with
the Minister to vary the areas of these committees, and, of course, that is so, and it may
be of assistance. I am not so sanguine as to
suppose these changes will be brought into
effect without a certain amount of early difficulty, but I have every confidence in the ultimate success of the proposal to work in the
boroughs through the committee appointed
and in the counties through these new committees. and I rely very much on the help
which those committees will receive from the
school committees which they are able to appoint, and which in some instances they are
required to appoint.
The scheme of education itself as set forth
in Part 2 of the Bill appears to me to be admirable in the way in which it avoids the
waste of any existing facilities It seems to
use all the material there is, and it provides
amply for consultation between the appropriate parties. Part 3 gives us the foundation
of the Bill, and it starts at once with words
which would be sufficient to repel any suggestion that the Government do not propose to
include moral instruction. It adopts, in defining elementary education, the words "both
literary and moral," and those are the words
which stand at the head of the old "Rules
and Regulations" of the National Commissioners. They were taken from the Act of
1831, and I am glad the noble Marquess has
preserved them. Also, in the same Part we
get a hint that practical instruction will be
continued. Of course, we get elsewhere a detinition of what practical instruction is. We
get our nursery schools and our vacation
schools, and then we come to the part of the
Bill which has given rise, perhaps, to something in the nature of controversy, but it has
rather died down, and when we get it here
we will be able to consider with it cther parts
of the Bill. It comes before us at the moment, so to speak, in connection with the
transfer of existing schools from private or
semi-private control to the newly-appointed
public bodies The intention of the Ministry
in regard to these schools appears to me to be
perfectly plain. When a school has been
transferred it is to be conducted in all respects as though it were a provided school.
There are left loopholes which are, I think,
not intended to be permanent. The main
principle is that a school when transferred
becomes a provided school; that means to say.
that as those schools will now be entirely supported by public funds, public control must
go hand in hand with that. Attacks have
been made-I think rather unfortunately
made-on this policy of complete transfer, and
perhaps those who take the other view would
have preferred something approaching the
Scottish system. I have not looked very
deeply into the Scottish system, because
it seems to me to be entirely out
of court, SO far as we are concerned,
because of the wording of the Government of Ireland Act. It seems to me that if
the Bill attempted to go further in the way
of enabling private persons or bodies of persons mostly representing one or other chade
of religious thought, that is to say, to give
them more help financially than you give
them at present for the maintenance of their
schools-I do not mean actually more pounds,
shillings and pence, although that would result, but help based on some wider scale of
alteration-without insisting on public control you would have been directly or
indirectly showing preference in respect
of a particular religion, and it would
not have made your position any better
if you did the same thing for a school
of one shade of opinion on this side of the
street and for another of a different
opinion on the other side of the street; you
would only have multiplied your transgressions. The limits of what can be done are provided for us. We have strict limitations, and
I think we have gone to the limit of them.
Every fairness has everywhere been shown to
every class of school, and I can detect nothing
approaching undue preference.
I want now to ask one particular question,
although I should perhaps not mention it today, but put it down for a subsequent occasion. I want to know what is to happen
under this scheme to the Ballymoney Technical School and any other technical school that
may be run on the same lines-there may not
be more than one or two. The particular
school is under the County Council of Antrim.
but is maintained principally by a rate on the
town and on the adjacent rural district. It
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
serves, in fact, both the urban and rural
district. The rates for both those districts
are mortgaged for the next forty years
to pay for the building. But I am not
sure that the position of schools cf that
description is made sufficiently clear. I have
nothing to say as regards religious instruction
because in the Bill liberty of conscience
appears to be preserved. I think the provisions for school attendance will require some
consideration. It is a very difficult and
dangerous subject for anybody to meddle with
because it is one of the very cardinals of this
Bill. School attendance must be put on more
satisfactory lines. The old obligation was 75
per cent. of attendance in the half-year, and
I think we ought to have something better
than that. But there will be cases-I am
thinking more of the country districts-where
there will be bona-fide difficulties. Perhaps
there will be such a case as an epidemic prevailing in the locality of a school, and a
parent does not like to send his child there.
The Minister has no power that I can find
for closing the school in such circumstances.
That is one of the points that I think does
require attention. I have already spoken of
the restrictions on premature employment.
So far as street trading is concerned, they
have not gone so far as I would have liked,
but if public opinion goes further we can go
further later on. The provisions on health
follow naturally, and then we come to what
I regard as one of the most attractive portions of the Bill, and a part of the Bill which
really ought to have a second reading to itself. I mean the part that deals with the
question of afflicted children. We all adopt
the Clause in the Lynn Report. which I
think the noble Marquess quoted in one of his
speeches in the other Chamber. The passage
is to the effect that they, the members of the
Committee, see no reason why the education
of the afflicted child should be less a matter
of public concern than the education of the
child that is so fortunate as to be normal.
That policy has certainly been carried out
throughout Part IX. of the Bill. In fact, the
noble Marquess has gone a little beyond the
English Act, because while introducing
cripples he has abstained from a definition,
so that he has a very free hand. Reformatories and teachers are dealt with, and then
we come to finance.
I have read the section which deals with
finance, and I recognise that it is all we can
ask for. Having regard to what we know is
the position as regards the finances of
Northern Ireland we cannot, and I think we
ought not, to try to get the Government to
give us any more definite assurance. I have
every hope that the proportion of £2 to £1
which has been adumbrated will be maintained. We must leave it at that. Particularly is it necessary from the point of view of
the agricultural districts that we should get
all the help we can. The right hon. and
gallant Gentleman in front of me (Colonel
Sharman Crawford) has dealt with the position of the agricultural districts. They do
feel the burden of the present rates very
heavily. Some of us are inclined to recall
that we are still waiting for the liquidation
of that debt due from personality to realty.
on which was laid stress in the introduction of the Imperial Budget in April,
1914. Owing, perhaps, to the war, we have
not yet had that pledge fulfilled, but we hope
it will be in the near future. However,
finance is not particularly appropriate to this
House, and there is so much in the financial
arrangements of the Bill for which we have
to be grateful that I do not think we ought
to be unduly grasping. Α most valuable provision is that to assist by loan
in the building of any school that may
prove necessary. That brings us to the
end of the Bill except for the administrative
sections. Most of them. I think, are taken
from the English Act. I think it is on them
there will be the most ground for discussion
and suggestions for amendments. But those
are the details, and we need not waste time
on them to-day. We ought not to put any
thing in the shape of a little shrubbery it
the foreground of the Bill, because we might
obscure the view of the forest which the noble
Marquess has put in the middle distance. As
a whole the system of education foreshadowed
in the Bill is, I venture to state, complete.
The responsibility of the parent has never
been taken from him, the best interest of the
child throughout has been studied, and the
enactment is complete in itself. If we take
this Bill in conjunction with the Children
Act. 1908, as I think we must, we shall see
that the child of the future in Northern
Ireland has nothing of which to complain;
in fact, we have reason to hope he will be
grateful for what is being done for him, and
that he will perhaps remember that he is the
trustee of posterity, and we hope he will conduct himself accordingly. I have pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading.
Mr. MOORE: I should like first of all to
congratulate the noble. Marquess, the Leader
of the House, on the very excellent Bill he
has produced and on the able and lucid manner in which he has introduced it. There is
only one point to which I should like to refer,
and in my ignorance I am not sure if this is
the proper time to bring it forward. It is in
regard to Technical Schools. At present the
Act seems to be quite silent on the manner
in which those schools are to be financially
supported and I would like to have that
point elucidated.
Mr. McDOWELL: I felt rather amused
when Senator Samuel Cunningham said he
hoped something would be inserted in the
Bill to protect the children. The thought
came very forcibly to my mind that if the
Education Bill-
4 JUNE 1923
Second Reading.
school-children were all like me the schoolmaster would need protection, because on one
occasion I lifted an inkpot and threw it at
him. (Laughter). I congratulate the noble
Marquess on the very fluent statement he has
made, but I notice that a great deal of what
he said on the First Reading of the Bill in
another place-I will not say he evaded, but
he seemed to leave out. I refer particularly
to the sectarian question. In introducing the
Bill in the other House the right hon.
Gentleman emphasised that the Government
could not be sectarian. We quite agree with
that, but I am strongly inclined to believe
that this teaching of dogma in our schools
has been the curse of the individual and of
the community. What is religion? In my
opinion religion is the sole possession of God,
the beautifying of the moral life. But sectarian teaching has created nothing but
suspicion and hatred, and if you call that
You
religion I cannot recognise it as such.
may build whatever sort of edifice you like
on this foundation, but in my opinion the
Bill will never have the opportunity of consolidating the people of this Province in the
way it would have done if this sectarian question had been left out of it altogether. I am in
favour of religion. Not very long ago I read
a statement by the Moderator of the General
Assembly to the effect that the people of
Northern Ireland are fond of their Bible. 1
quite appreciate that, and I say the Bible
should be read in our schools. It should be
brought into the curriculum of the school,
but there should be no sectarian teaching.
sus-
The teaching we are imbibing in our schools
at present is carried with us right through
life. It is taken into the trade union world
and into other spheres, and there is a
picion, a distrust, yea, and a hatred that
should not exist in the breast of humanity.
Consequently, I am not in favour of this
teaching of dogma in our schools, and in fact
it was my intention on the Second Reading of
the Bill to move its rejection on this ground.
I am sure you will be surprised at that statement. Sectarian teaching should not be
tolerated for one moment in this Province,
but we should introduce the Bible as we know
it into the school. So far as the appointment
of the Education Committees is concerned,
I think they would work far more successfully
if they were elected in the usual way by the
ratepayers. You are going to strike a rate and
the ratepayers should have the right to say
whom they want to represent them in the
management of their schools. Apart from
these two points I have no complaint
to
make with regard to the general
principles of the Bill. A great edifice
is bound to be built on this Bill,
but I believe the foundation is wrong,
and where you have a wrong foundation it is
very bad for the permanent structure you wish
to build on it. I am sorry that it should have
been necessary for me to speak in this way,
but I feel strongly that it is time this question
we would have far more cohesion and a far
more consolidated State than we can possibly
have under the present system of sectarian
teaching in our schools. As far as I am concerned I will vote against that principle.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
There are various points upon which,
perhaps, the House wou'd like me to offer
some reply. My hon. Friend Senator Leslie
touched with unerring accuracy most of
those points which have given us most
trouble in framing the Bill, but with that
Parliamentary knowledge which he invariably displays I think he knows well that
the best stage on which to reply to the
points which he raised is on the Committee
stage. The hon. Member raised some very
valuable Committee points, and I feel that
I can give him more or less a satisfactory
answer on most of them and, if I cannot
do that, we may be able to arrange on the
Committee stage for some amendments which
he may desire to insert in the Bill.
I should like to congratulate my hon.
Friend (Mr. Moore) on making his maiden
speech in this House. I am sure we are all
gratified to hear him speak with the knowledge that he represents the great city of
Derry. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) The
question he raised about technical schools
is also a Committee point. If the technical
schools come under the education authority
the rate will be raised over the whole of the
educational district. I will give him a full
answer to his question when it comes up in
its proper place on the Committee stage.
My hon. Friend (Mr. McDowell) who has
just sat down has impressed the House with
the sincerity of his remarks, but I need
hardly say that I regret a certain amount of
what he has said. The question of religious
teaching and religious instruction is one on
which those who desire to see it excluded
entirely from our schools can be as dogmatic
as those who profess the religions themselves.
I cannot help feeling that the great majority of those whom we represent here
are desirous and anxious that facilities
should be afforded for the imparting of those
doctrines which are not universally held, but
one or the other of which most members of
the community hold. I should feel that I
was wanting in my duty to the community
if I had not included in the Bill a provision
which gives opportunities for that instruction
to be imparted. The hon. Gentleman speaks
of Bible reading in the schools. No one
agrees with him more than I do thit the
Bible should be read daily in every one of
the schools-as one hopes it is read in every
household-but I am not sure if he is aware
that Bible reading from one point of view
is sectarian, and that a large proportion of
the children in this community would have
to be withdrawn if what he is desirous of
M
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
was wiped away altogether. I maintain that
seeing were put into force. These are all
difficulties that come before a Minister of
Education in his desire in the interests of
the children to smooth out of the way those
things which mi'itate against the ambitions
and the desires he has in his heart. I feel
that in the compromise which we have made
we have done what we could to keep-as I
have said more than once in my speechesparamount before us the educational and
moral welfare of the children. I remember in
the early stages of the Bill I was cesed of
banning moral teaching from the schools
because in my First reading speech I made
no mention of it beyond saying that the State
was non-sectarian. I do not believe that the
great majority of the people in the Six
Counties desire that the State should dissociate itself entirely from moral teaching
in the bringing up of the children. I do not
know if the hon. Gentleman wishes to go
further into this controversy. Nobody knows
more than I do the difficulties which sectarianism and perhaps dogma have brought about
in our midst, but I say with all sincerity
that I far prefer dogma to no religion at
all, and I would far sooner encourage and
help those who profess some dogma with
opportunities to impart what they believe to
be right to the children of their community
than ban religion altogether and bring up
a Godless community. Perhaps I am digressing on a subject on which we all feel very
strongly, no less than the hon. Member who
made a very eloquent and very sincere
speech.
I
The hon. Gentleman (Mr. Joseph Cunningham), who spoke first, spoke of the responsibility that would fall upon the parents.
should not like him to think that I have the
idea in my mind that there are many parents
who are not fully alive to their responsibilities in connection with their children.
will recognise, however, that there are black
sheep in every fold, and when it comes to
legislating we have to legislate for the black
sheep, and not only for those who are going
to carry out the wishes he has in his heart
and I have in mine.
He
In his very interesting speech the right hen.
Member (Colonel Sharman-Crawford), representing, as he does, the County Councils, regretted that the representatives of the Ministry had been omitted from the Regional Committees. I think he inadvertently said that
the County Councils had no power to put
their representatives on the Regional Committees. That is not so. The County Councils will have the power and the opportunity
of doing so. I shall be very glad to take him
into my confidence as to the representatives
of the Ministry. When I first considered the
question I agreed with him that we should
The
who
have a leavening of the Ministry on these
Committees, but when we think of it very
carefully I am not sure that it is a very practical or a very good project to follow.
County Council would place their representatives on the Regional Committee. The arba
and district councils would have their repre
sentatives. The Ministry would then come in
the third place and discover someone
would be put on to represent the Ministerial
point of view. I think the right hon. Gentle
man sees the difficulty that if the County
Councils did not put on those Committees persous who are interested in education and who
would be an asset to the Committees thenselves they would be wanting in their duties. I
cannot think that in any County Council over
which my hon. Friend presides will in any
case be ever wanting in their duties. That is
the difficulty in which the Ministry was
placed. Also I think it would have been a
very invidious position for the Ministerial representatives to be on those Committees. I
think they would have occupied a difficult
position in relation to their colleagues and
would not have known exactly what line to
take, whether they should carry out their
duties as mere nominees of the Ministry or be
independent members of the Committee. After
very mature consideration we felt that the
object we all have in view was attained by the
Ministry retaining in their hands certain
powers of control, which I think would effect
the object which the Ministerial representation on the Committees themselves was designed to carry out.
The hon. Member (Mr. Barbour) spoke of
the Technical Committees, and made two
suggestions. One was with reference to the
transfer, but I do not think it would be
possible to carry that out. If the property
is not transferred. neither can the liability
which falls upon the Council be transferred.
There are difficulties in these points which
perhaps when we come to the Committee
stage we can consider. As to the matter of
representation, that is a matter which will
come up in the schemes which the County
Councils will put before the Ministry for the
operation of the Act.
The hon. Member (Mr. Greer) spoke about
the question of the libraries. I am sure we
all agree with a great deal of what he said,
but I feel that this is a matter which would
come more appropriately under a Libraries
Act rather than an Education Act. I am not
sure really whether libraries do not bear
more of a recreational character than an
educational character; or, rather, they are
both educational and recreational, and we,
as the stern administrators of education, I
think, must confine ourselves to the educational services and leave the services
rendered by the public libraries to the
Libraries Acts. There is also the point,
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
4 JUNE 1923
which I cannot believe has escaped the hon.
Member, and that is that the County
Councils would have a very strong inducement to hand over their libraries to themselves in their new capacity as Education
Authority, and by doing so would be able to
claim relief of local expenditure on the
libraries by calling upon the State to pay
two-thirds of the grant. I do not know if
that point has struck the hon. Gentleman, but it is a very forcible one which I
think an overburdened taxpayer might raise
in the interests of his fellow sufferers.
The right hon Gentleman (Mr. Samuel
Cunningham), whom I do not see in his place,
spoke of the records of punishments, and
appeared to regret that those records have
been done away with. This was a matter
that came up when I first became Minister
of Education, and I may say that when it
was put before me that no records should
be kept of these offences I accepted the suggestion with alacrity. The hon. Member
seems to think that there might be some injustice, that a child might be ill-treated or
ill-used, or that there might be a cabal
against him. I am not sure that I feel that
there is very much substance in the suggestion or in the fears of the right hon. Gentleman, but when I considered the matter I felt
I should most certainly be very unwilling
to think that when I have arrived at the
July of my existence there should still be a
record of my childish misdemeanours. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
I do not feel that there is anything more
that the House would wish me to go into at
the moment, as we have still before us an
opportunity on the Committee stage, and
also on the Report stage. There is one
matter which I see on my notes connected
with the appointment of the teacher which
I think was mentioned by my hon. Friend
(Mr. Greer). The question of the transfer,
of course, is a very important one, and I
need hardly say that I hope sincerely that in
time to come all the schools will be transferred to the local authority, and that they
will operate as provided and transferred
schools. I cannot say, however, that I am
anxious to press schools into adopting this
new form of management, and I am quite
willing that they should be able to see for
themselves, in the time that will elapse before this Bill is really in proper operation,
that the best plan in the education system is
that schools should be transferred.
Hon. Members will see that whereas I
would welcome transfer it is impossible for
me to submit to such conditions of transfer
as would make the transfer nugatory, and in
fact not be a transfer at all. A transfer to
the people, which is what it comes to, must
be satisfactory for the people, and they must
gain advantages for accepting the transfer
and increased burden which would rest on
their shoulders. The matter of the appointment of teachers naturally has come up in
many forms. It came up in the other House
all through the debates, and it has been the
subject of correspondence in the Press, but 1
think the point that will really strike hon.
Members in connection with the appointment
of teachers is that the local authority becomes
the owner of the school. The people become
the owners of the school, and on these principles of ownership. which we all understand,
the owner of the school, whether the people
or the managers, should have the appointment of the teacher. Controversy has raged
round this question. It is said that although
managers are prepared to hand over schools,
still they should retain practically in their
own hands the power to appoint the teacher.
This point came up in another place, and on
the very principle of the measure I was unable to concede. I will not venture to weary
the House with a longer exposition of the
Bill. I should like to take the opportunity
of thanking hon. Members for the very kindly
consideration which they have given me and
also for the reception which they have accorded to this Bill.
On Question, Motion "That this Bill be
now read the Second time" agreed to. Committed to a Committee of the whole House
on Friday next.

the community. And this increase will
ensure our obtaining the services of a
thoroughly competent and thoroughly qualified officer if and when the time comes-and
I feel sure that we all sincerely trust that
that time is very far distant-for us to find

office.
The second purpose of the Bill is to provide

Office of Attorney-General Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to move the
Second Reading of the Office of Attorney-
General Bill. The purpose of this Bill is
two-fold. In the first place it is designed to
increase the salary which is attached to the
office of Attorney-General for Northern Ireland by the sum of £1,000, that is to say,
that our Attorney-General in future will
draw a salary of £2,500 instead of £1,500
which was the figure originally attached to
his office.
In explanation of this increase, I may
inform the House that it was anticipated
when the salary was first fixed that the
holder of the office of Attorney-General
would have ample opportunity for engaging
in private practice. But the experience of
the last two years has proved that such
opportunities are extremely limited by
reason of the constant demand upon the
services of the Attorney-General that his
public office makes. I am sure there will be
no question in regard to the payment of such
a salary to the present holder of this important office, who certainly has deserved well
of the Government and has deserved well of
Public Records Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This Bill is introduced in accordance with a
power conferred by Section 9 of the Government of Ireland Act which authorised the
Northern Government to establish a Public
Record Office of Northern Ireland for the
reception and preservation of public records
appertaining to the Province, which would
otherwise have been deposited in the Public
Record Office, Dublin. The Act allowed the
option of either continuing to use the public
repository in Dublin, which was maintained
as a Reserved Service, for Ulster records or
of setting up a local repository.
The Record building in Dublin with its
contents was destroyed in the Four Courts
fire, and subsequently with the setting up of
the Irish Free State the remains of the
Record service in Dublin ceased to be reserved to the Imperial Government and
became a purely Free State matter over
which Northern Ireland had no power of
control. Thus there was no Record Department to take charge of Northern Ireland
documents, and the specially equipped
building to which they had been formerly
sent has been destroyed, as I have said, by
fire. Meanwhile these documents have been
accumulating in various offices in the Province awaiting the customary transfer to a
permanent repository, in some cases causing
a great deal of congestion and a great deal of
inconvenience. Accordingly, as it becomes
necessary to provide some central storehouse
in Belfast for the accommodation of those
documents whose preservation is considered
essential in the public interest.
The Government therefore has decided to
set up in Northern Ireland a Public Record
Office, and this office should be placed under
the control of the Minister of Finance. The
organisation of the proposed Department
will be modelled very closely on existing
Public Records Bill--
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
precedents and almost all the provisions of
the Bill will be found in the present Record
legislation of the United Kingdom.
There is, however, one exception. It must
be remembered that the Northern Parliament has only power to make laws with
regard to certain services and therefore in
'setting up a Public Record Office it can only
legislate as to records concerning those
services. These the Bill designates "Northern
Ireland records." But there is also an extensive and important class of records which
relate to what are known "Reserved
as
Services." As to these, which are called in
the Bill "Imperial records," it is obvious
that the Northern Parliament cannot legislate, but the Bill makes provision for the
adoption of the Act by the Imperial Government, should it so choose, which course
would permit the deposit of Imperial records
relating to Northern Ireland in the Record
Office set up by the Bill. Amongst these
Imperial records are included wills and
other testimentary papers which technically
are records of a reserved service, the
Supreme Court of Northern Ireland. These
documents are very important for proving
title and succession to property.
Power has also been taken for the reception
from private custody of documents which
may be considered of sufficient historical
or other value to merit their preservation.
Following the precedent of the English
Record Office, provision has been made for
the disposal of valueless documents under
stringent safeguards, which include the submission of a schedule of documents whose
destruction is proposed to both Houses of
Northern Ireland. Hon. Members will
realise that this power is taken in order
to prevent overcrowding of limited space
with comparatively worthless documents. It
is not expected that need will arise for its
exercise until the Record Office has been
long in existence; but it is the fate of all
such repositories to become overcrowded
eventually and it is generally found that
with valuable matter some useless matter
has also crept in. Therefore it is advisable
to retain the power to discriminate and not
be obliged to preserve anything that has
once passed the portals of the repository.
Finally it may be mentioned that certain
Acts of Parliament which relate to the preservation of the pre-disestablishment records
of the Church of Ireland are adapted so that
the powers therein contained may be exercised so far as they relate to Parishes in
Northern Ireland by the proposed Record
Office. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the establishment
of such an office as has been suggested will
not meet with opposition from anybody, but
the Bill does give rise perhaps to one or
two inquiries. I did not appreciate from the
remarks of the noble Marquess at whose
expense this office is to be erected, whether
the cost is to fall on the Imperial Government or on the Government of Northern
Ireland. I am not quite sure at the moment
as the Bill has only been circulated. Nor
am I sure whether the establishment of such
an office comes within the general cost of
starting the new system of Government in
this country or whether the cost of providing
a storehouse will fall upon Northern Ireland.
That is one point. The second point that
occurs to us is whether the rights of what
are generally known as statutory libraries
are sufficiently preserved. I expect they are,
because there is power in the Minister of
Finance to present copies of these books
gratuitously. There are, as the House will
remember, certain liabilities incurred of
sending one copy to the British Museum and
copies to certain universities, one of which
is Trinity College, Dublin, if the University
authorities apply for such a copy, and it
is also usual to have something in the way
of an exchange between libraries, which
would be of great value to the library of
our own Parliament in the future. I do not
quite understand the distinction which is
apparently drawn towards the end of the
Bill between those documents of public
interest to which the noble Marquess referred, documents of historical interest
which are deposited in this Record Office.
It would appear that Northern Ireland
records can be seen and copied by the public.
They can get certified copies of records, but
I do not quite make out whether it is
intended that the public should have the
same facilities as regards other historical
documents. That is a point which I think
ought to be considered. In general the Bill
will provide, as the noble Marquess said, for
the relief of congestion both now and hereafter, because the provision for the
destruction of documents in due course has
always been followed in London Government
offices. I think documents are collected from
such offices as the Colonial Office at reason.
able intervals, and sent to the Public Record
Office, where they are again examined at reasonable intervals, and the necessary destruc
tion takes place. So far as I can observe, in
that matter the Bill is entirely following
Imperial lines to which we are accustomed. I
think we shall require to look into the Bill a
little more closely later on, though I do not
think we shall have to deal with it in any
very violent way. Certainly, nothing will be
done to interfere with the main purpose of
the Bill.
Public Records Bill.

6 JUNE, 1923.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member has raised some very
interesting points which I am not altogether
prepared to reply to off-hand. The question
of whether the expenditure falls on this
Government or on the British Government
is important. I should say the cost of our
Record Office would fall on the finances of
the Northern Government. The other matters to which he referred are not altogether
covered by the Bill, and with his permission
I shall delay the answers to them until the
further stages of the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this House do now adjourn."
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive minutes before Four of the
Clock,

THE SENATE.

FRIDAY, 8th June, 1923.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
BASIL MCCREA
ENDOWMENTS BILL.
Order for Third Reading read.
Ordered: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.
BALLYMONEY TECHNICAL SCHOOL.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to ask the Leader of
the House for information as to the status,
financial and otherwise, of the Ballymoney
Joint Urban and Rural Technical School,
when the Education Bill now before Parliament becomes law.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): The Ballymoney
Technical School is managed by a Joint Committee of the Ballymoney Urban and Rural
District Councils. The premises were erected
in 1905 and extended in 1914, the expenditure
being mainly met by loans from the Office of
Works in Dublin. The amount of the loans
was £2,900, of which £2,100 was secured on
the Rural District rates, and £800 on the
Urban District rate for Technical Instruction.
Provision is made in the Bill to meet such
cases as this of the Ballymoney Technical
School. In the first place, where the County
Council and Urban Council come to an
agreement, Clause 23, sub-section (2), paragraph (b), and sub-section (3) of the same
clause operate. In the second place, where
a deadlock occurred on the failure of agreement between the two authorities, the
Ministry would take action under section 23.
sub-section (2), paragraph (a), and utilise
their compulsory powers to ensure a working
agreement.
Business of the House.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that the remaining questions
on the paper be taken in preference to the
Education Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

(3) For the purposes of this Act the expression "records" means rolls, records,
writs, books, proceedings, decrees, bills, warrants, accounts, papers and documents of a
public nature.
Mr. LESLIE: It occurs to me as we are
going into considerable detail, following the
precedent of 1838, in defining the expression
records in sub-section (3) of Clause 1, it
would be better to include maps, because
the making of maps has advanced a great
deal in the last 80 years or so. It would seem
rather incongruous if they were left out. I,
therefore, suggest that between the words
"accounts" and papers" ("accounts.
papers, and documents") in line 19, page 2.
the word maps should be inserted. I beg
to move.

""

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The amendment which the hon. Member
has moved is in my judgment covered by the
Public Records Bill-

words "
papers and documents of a public

CLAUSE 2 (Charge and superintendence of
records and appointment of deputy-keeper
and staff) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Removal of accumulating records
to Record Office).
The Minister of Finance shall by warrant
under his hand from time to time appoint a
fit person to attend all courts, Government
Departments or offices in Northern Ireland,
and in his name to receive and take charge
of all Northern Ireland records of which the
charge and superintendence are by this Act
vested in the Minister of Finance, and the
judge of the court or principal officer of the
department or office to which any Northern
Ireland records belong, upon sight of the
warrant of the person thereby appointed to
take charge of the Northern Ireland records
to be delivered from that court, department,
or office into the custody of the Minister of
Finance, shall give the necessary orders to
the proper officer of his court, department, or
office for the delivery thereof. As soon as
such Northern Ireland records have been so
delivered to the person appointed to receive
them in pursuance of such warrant, such
Northern Ireland records shall be deemed
to be in the custody of the Minister of
Finance, and shall forthwith be removed to
and deposited in the Public Record Office of
Northern Ireland, and shall be subject to the
rules made under this Act; and the person
receiving any Northern Ireland records shal!
thereupon deliver the schedule thereof and
receipt for the same under his hand to the
judge or officer from whom he receives them,
and shall deliver a copy of such schedule to
the Minister of Finance or the officer appointed by the Minister of Finance to receive
the same into the said Public Record Office.
""
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move in page 3,
line 36, after any" ("receiving any Northern Ireland records") to insert " such." I
think there has been a slip in the printing
press. The word "such" appears to be
required after the word "any." I beg to
move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am willing to accept the amendment of the
hon. Gentleman.

Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move on page 3, line 40, after the word
"or" ("Finance or the officer appointed ")
to insert "to." This is merely a drafting
amendment.
Ordered: That Clause 3 as amended stand
part of the Bill.
Ordered: That Clause 4 (Validity of records
after removal) stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Deposit of documents in
Record Office by trustees or other persons).
It shall be lawful for any trustee or other
person having the custody of any deeds or
documents, which in the opinion of the
Minister of Finance are fit to be deposited
in the Public Record Office of Northern
Ireland, so to deposit the same with the
permission of the Minister of Finance, and
subject to any rules to be made under this
Act, in a repository which may be provided
for that purpose in the said Public Record
Office, and any deeds or documents SO
deposited shall be deemed to be public
records in the custody of the Minister of
Finance.
The following amendment
name of Mr. Leslie:
stood in the

Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I

and insert Northern Ireland." This will

received in the Record Office on account of

It is a necessary amendment. I beg to move.

CLAUSE 8 (Power to make rules as to disposal of valueless documents).

a schedule to be prepared of the documents

Public Records Bill-
THE SENATE.
to
ing of such documents in the proposed
manner. Where there are several
documents of the same class or
description, it shall be sufficient

according to their nature and contents,
instead of specifying each document separately. The power of disposal given by this
section shall not be exercised in respect of

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 8,
page 7, line 16, leave out from the word
"separately" ("each document separately")
to the end of line 20, and insert-
"such schedule shall be laid before both
Houses of Parliament, and if, during, the

requiring the preservation of any scheduled
record, such record shall not be disposed
of."

The real point of the amendment is to get
rid of the expression, submitted to both
Houses of Parliament for a period of not less
than four weeks," which, I understand, is not
the customary phrase.
The CHAIRMAN: Does the Noble Marquess
agree?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am ready to accept the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move, on page 7, line 18, to leave out

to insert instead thereof the word "of."
The CHAIRMAN: These words have

(4) Every rule made in pursuance of this
section shall be forthwith laid before each
House of Parliament, if Parliament he sitting, and, if not, then within three weeks
after the commencement of the next
easuing Session of Parliament, and if
neither House of Parliament within
twenty-one days, exclusive of any period
of prorogation, after the rule has been laid
before it, presents an Address praying the
Governor of Northern Ireland to withhold
his assent from the rule, the Governor of
Northern Ireland may, by Order in
Council, approve of the rule. Every such
rule, when approved by Order of the
Governor in Council, shall, subject to revocation or alteration by a subsequent rule,
have effect as if it were enacted in this Act.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, in Clause 8, page 7, line 25, to
leave out from the word "be" to the word
"and" in line (28 ("forthwith laid before
each House of Parliament, if Parliament be
sitting, and, if not, then within three weeks
after the conLencement of the next ensuing
Session of Parliament, and "), and insert
instead thereof--
"laid before both Houses of Parliament
within twenty-one days after it is made, if
Parliament be then sitting, and if not sitting, within twenty-one days after the next
meeting of Parliament."
The object of this and the following amendment is to make the procedure in regard to
laying before Parliament the matter of the
disposal of valueless documents the same as
in the case which is now covered by Clause 9,
sub-section 4.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, in Clause 8, page 7, lines 29 and
30, to leave out the words, “exclusive of any
period of prorogation."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9.-(Rules for management of
Record Office).
The Minister of Finance shall have
power to make rules for carrying into
execution the provisions of this Act (other
than the provisions with respect to the disposal of valueless documents) and, in particular, with respect to all or any of the
following matters :-
(1) The management of the Public Record
Office of Northern Ireland;
(2) The admission of persons to the use of
the Northern Ireland records, calendars.
catalogues and indexes, in his custody;
(3) The amounts of the fees which he
shall think proper to be paid in connection
with the use of the Northern Ireland
records under his charge and superintendence, and the cases in which the payment
of fees may be dispensed with;
(4) Any other matter (other than the
disposal of valueless documents) with respect to which rules may be made under
this Act.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 9, page 7, line 38, to
leave out from the word "shall" to the word
"and" in line 41 (have power to make rules
Public Records Bill-
Committee.
8 JUNE 1923
for carrying into execution the provisions of
this Act other than the provisions with
respect to the disposal of valueless documents, and), and to insert instead thereof the
words,
"(without prejudice to the provisions of
this Act with respect to the disposal of
valueless documents) have power to make
rules for carrying this Act into execution."
I think the meaning of that amendment is
obvious.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, in Clause 9, page 8, lines 9 and
10, to leave out the words 'other than the
disposal of valueless documents."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSES 10 (Annual Report) and 11 (Modification of 38 and 39 Vict. c. 59 and 39 and 40
Viet. c. 58) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 12.-(Application of Act to Imperial Records).
If provision is made by or in pursuance of
any Act of the Parliament of the United
Kingdom for the removal to, and the reception and preservation in, the Public Record
Office of Northern Ireland of any Imperial
Records within the meaning of this Act, the
Governor of Northern Ireland may, by Order
in Council, apply the provisions of this Act,
with such modifications as may be specified
in the Order in Council, to the purposes of
such removal, reception and preservation.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 12, page 9, line 22, after
the word "Kingdom ("Parliament of the
United Kingdom") to insert the words ["or by
any authority of the Government thereof."]
This amendment is intended to govern those
cases where the Imperial Government may be
empowered to deposit records by administrative action apart from any statutory authority.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 13 (Short Title) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
PREAMBLE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, in the Preamble, to leave out the
words, "any such office," and to insert instead
thereof the words ["a Public Record Office of
Northern Ireland."]
This is merely a drafting amendment, which
I think is in the nature of an improvement in
the Bill.
Amendment agreed to.
Preamble, as amended, agreed to.
Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with amendment to the House.
Moved: "That Standing Order No. XXV. be
considered in order to its being dispensed with
in respect of the said Bill."-(The Marquess of
Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That the Report be now received."
-(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that the Bill be now read the
Third time, and in doing so I should like to
point out to the House that we are governed
by the privileges of the Commons, and that
consequently we are unable to initiate a
clause which contains matter of a financial
character. The procedure under which we
are governed means that we send this Bill to
another place, and Section 5 of Clause 2 is left
in blank, and then the House of Commons
themselves put in this amendment, because
the financial provisions of this Bill are solely
within the scope of the powers of the House
of Commons. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that the following sub-section be
deleted from Clause 2:-
(5) There shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament to the said Deputy
Keeper of the Records and to the officers,
clerks and servants appointed in pursuance
of this section, such annual salaries or
remuneration as the Minister of Finance
may determine.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That the Bill do now pass."-(The
Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Education Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill. (The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Education Bill-
Committee.

In Committee accordingly.
CLAUSE 1.--(Advisory Council on Educational Matters) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2.-(Local Education Authorities
and Committees).
*
