CLAUSE 92 (Proof of age).
Where a child or young person is apparently of age alleged for the purposes of
any proceeding under this Act or a by-law,
order or other instrument made thereunder, it shall lie on the defendant to prove
that the child or young person is not of
that age.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 92,
page 60, line 32, after "where" ("where a
child or young person is apparently of the
age") to insert "it appears to a court of
summary jurisdiction that" and to leave out
"apparently." This clause is taken from
the English Act but it does not appear to
me to be altogether satisfactory, because we
are not told who it is that is to behold the
apparition, and we are not told when or
where we may expect it to manifest itself.
This clause seems to go back to the Cruelty
to Children Act of 1894, and it appeared
again in 1904, but in that case it was
definitely stated that it appears to the court,
and it appeared part of the Act with a
sub-heading, "evidence and procedure." It
was made quite clear that the "apparently"
related to the view taken by the court considering the case. In 1908 and in the Children Act again there was a long section
about apparent age, giving the court power
to say.
Unfortunately also in the Children
Act-unfortunately for this purpose-there
are two or three other sections where the
word "apparently" is used. One of them,
relating to juvenile smoking, gives to the
park-keepers power in case a child is apparently, etc. In another section power is
given to other people when the age 18
apparently, etc. Therefore, though I think
it is pretty plain that the clause here refers
to proceedings in a police-court, especially as
it follows 91 in the same order as the English
Act-although in the English Education Act
there is a sub-heading, "Legal Proceedings”—
I think it would be better to have it quite
right and insert after "Where," "it appears
to a court of summary jurisdiction that" and
leave out "apparently."
The CHAIRMAN: That will be a separate
amendment.
Amendment (to insert "it appears to
court of summary jurisdiction that")
agreed to.
a
Amendment (to leave out "apparently")
agreed to.
Clause 93, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 93 (Proof of attendance at school).
Where an education authority are, by.
reason of the default of the managers or
proprietor of a school, unable to ascertain
whether a child or young person, who is
resident within the education area of that
authority and attends that school, attends
school in conformity with this Act or any
by-laws, orders or other instruments made
thereunder, it shall lie on the defendant in
any proceeding under this Act to show that
the child or young person has attended
school in conformity with this Act or with
those by-laws, orders or instruments.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave out
Clause 93. I suggest that this clause is not
strictly necessary. It is pretty well covered
by 97, and in any case it is not a clause that
is very attractive. On the face of things it
is putting on the parent the responsibility
for the default of those who are not his
servants the managers and keepers of
schools. Although I am quite willing to
consent to anything in this way that will
really assist in school attendance and things
of that sort, still there are, I think, some
things we ought not to do. We ought not
to insert in this Bill a clause of this stamp,
especially as all the good of it is, I think,
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie.]
given to us by Section 97. Therefore, I beg
to move the omission of Clause 93.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The last thing I would like to do is to
impose any hardship on parents or to have
some wording in the Bill that might offend
their susceptibilities in any way. The hon.
Member considers that Clause 93 is covered
by Clause 97 and that it may inflict hardship
on parents. I cannot say that I agree with
him in his suggestion.
Clause 93 relates to "Proof of attendance
at school" and the onus of proof is placed on
the defendant, as it should be. Clause 97 goes
further and puts a limitation on right
to certain defences. The two things are
different and I must oppose the amendment.
There can be no question of hardship on
parents. It must be borne in mind that
these two clauses become operative at the
stage when "legal proceedings" are taken.
Before that stage is reached the parent must
have appeared before the education authority; he must have been warned; a school
attendance order must have been issued and
he must have failed to comply with the order.
If the hon. Member will consider these points
I think he will see that Clause 93 is to a
very large extent necessary, and I feel that
the Bill might suffer if we followed his
suggestion and omitted the clause. I hope
he will allow the clause to stand.
Mr. LESLIE: I withdraw the amendment,
as the noble Marquess is satisfied that the
retention of the clause will serve a useful
purpose.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 94 (Certificate of teacher as to
attendance) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 95 (Appearance of education
authority in legal proceedings).
An education authority may appear in all
legal proceedings by their clerk, or by some
member of the authority authorised by
resolution of the authority, and every such
resolution shall appear upon the minutes
of the proceedings of the Authority, but
every such resolution shall, until the contrary is proved, be deemed in any legal
proceedings to appear upon those minutes.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 95,
page 61, line 16, to leave out the word "legal"
("appear in all legal proceedings ") and in
line 17, after the word "proceedings" to insert the words ("before a court of summary
jurisdiction")
This again is a section copied from the
English Act, but I think it has gone too far.
The object is to enable the education authority
to save expense by sending their clerk to the
local police court instead of employing a
lawyer. I suggest that we should leave out
the word "legal" and put in the words
"before a court of summary jurisdiction." I
think that is the intention of the section, and
that is as far as we ought to allow the education authority to go. I do not think we ought
to encourage the education authority to send
its own officers to represent the ratepayers
before any superior court of jurisdiction to the
magistrates.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think the hon. Member has not fully appreciated the words of the clause.
“An education authority may appear in
all legal proceedings by their clerk.”
This does not interfere with appearance by
counsel in the ordinary way.
Mr. LESLIE: I must point out that this
gives the clerk to the education authority
power to appear in the High Court.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
There is no harm in that.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not understand how the
section got in the English Act, but I do not
think it can be disputed that the section as
it is drawn would entitle the clerk of an
education authority, if he had got his resolution, to appear in just the same way as any
ordinary counsel.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think the hon. Member is rather addressing
himself to the very minor point, and he is
not observing that there is nothing in the
clause which interferes with appearance by
counsel in the ordinary way. When we do
proceed to the higher courts it is conceivable
that we should be represented as an education
authority by eminent counsel. There is no
point of disagreement between the hon. Member and myself, and I shall be glad to consider
the matter between now and the Report stage.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I will withdraw the
amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 96 (Appearance by member of
family), 97 (Limitation or right to certain defences), and 98 (Power to enforce duties of
education authority), ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 99 (Regulations of Ministry under
the Act).
*
*
Education Bill-

(b) with respect to the giving of religious
instruction in any school to or in respect
of which grants are so payable
come into force, they shall be laid before
each House of Parliament for a period of
not less than twenty-one days during which
the House is sitting, and, if either of those
Houses, before the expiration of those
twenty-one days, presents an address to the
Governor of Northern Ireland against the
regulations or any part thereof, no further
proceedings shall be taken thereon, but
without prejudice to the making of new
regulations.
(3) Regulations made by the Ministry,
other than the regulations to which the last
preceding sub-section applies, shall be laid
before both Houses of Parliament as soon as
may be after they are made, and, if an
address is presented to the Governor of
Northern Ireland by either of those Houses
within twenty-one days on which that
House has sat next after any such regulations are laid before it; praying that the
regulations may be annulled, the said
Governor may thereupon by Order in
Council annul the same, and the regulations
so annulled shall forthwith become void,
but without prejudice to the validity of
anything which in the meantime may have
been done thereunder.
*
*
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 99, page 63, line 8, to
leave out from the word "before
"to the word

V presents" in line 12, and to insert instead
thereof (both Houses of Parliament and if
during the next ten days on which either
House shall sit, either House ").
This and the following consequential amendment is merely technical, and effects the procedure of laying schemes and regulations
before Parliament. The amendments bring
the procedure into line with the practice in
other cases where Papers have to be laid before Parliament.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 99, page 63, line 21, to
leave out the word "twenty-one" (within
twenty-one days) and to insert instead thereof
the word "ten."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSES 100 (General Returns), 101
(Public inquiries by Ministry and
Ministry of Home Affairs, 102 (Service
of notices etc.), 103 (Evidences of
certificates, etc., issued by education
authorities), and 104 (Forging of certificates), ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 105 (Publication of notices).
(1) Notices and other matters required to
be published under this Act shall, when no
particular method is provided or indicated,
be published either by advertisement, or by
affixing the same on the doors of churches
and chapels, and other public places, or in
such other manner as the Ministry may
either generally or with respect to any particular area, place, or notice, or class of
area, place, or notices, by regulations determine, as being in their opinion sufficient for
giving information to all parties interested.
(2) If any person wilfully tears down, injures, or defaces any notice affixed in pursuance of this Act, or any order or regulation of the Ministry made thereunder, he
shall be liable in respect of each offence to
a fine not exceeding forty shillings.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 105,
page 65, line 42, to leave out from the word
"or" to the word "in" on page 66, line 1
(by affixing the same on the doors of churches
and chapels, and other public places, or). I
think it would be better if we omitted these
words. Although it is the practice in England-and this section again is taken from the
English Act-to deface church doors with
notices of a public character, in Ireland it is
not the custom, and I am not aware that the
State has any right now to affix notices on the
doors of churches. It will not seriously impede the operation of the statute because it
says that notices will be published by advertisement or in such other manner as the
Ministry may provide.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
accept the amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 105,
page 66, line 7, to leave out the words "tears
down" (wilfully tears down) and to insert
instead thereof the word "removes." In this
suggested alteration I am thinking of bills,
etc., that are hanging loose.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 105,
page 66, line 8, to leave out the word
"affixed " (any notice affixed) and to insert
instead thereof the word "exhibited."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
CLAUSES 106 (Annual Report), 107 (Power
of education authority to accept gifts for education purposes), and 108 (Definitions),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 109 (Commencement of Act and
appointed day).
This Act shall come into operation on the
appointed day, and the appointed day shall
be such day, not being earlier than the first
day of July, nineteen hundred and twentythree, as the Governor of Northern Ireland
may by Order in Council appoint, and different days may be appointed for different
purposes and for different provisions of this
Act, for different education areas or parts
of education areas, and for different persons
or classes of persons.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 109, page 68, line 19,
to leave out the word "July" (first day of
July) and to insert instead thereof the word
"October." This amendment refers to the
time when the Act will come into force. I
think hon. Members will see that this is a
necessary amendment, giving longer time.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
SECOND SCHEDULE (Transfer of schools
and institutions to the education authority).

4. Any arrangement under this Schedule
may also provide for the transfer or application, subject to the approval of the
Ministry of Finance, of any endowment
belonging to the school or institution, or for
an education authority undertaking to discharge any debt charged on the school not
exceeding the value of the interest in the
premises or endowment transferred to them.
*

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in the Second Schedule, page 70,
line 18, at end to add: "transfer to the
education authority of a teacher's residence
held or used in connection with a school or
for the."
This amendment is consequential upon the
amendment on Clause 14 which I moved in
reference to teachers' residences.
Amendment agreed to.

5. Where any arrangement is made under
this Schedule, the managers may, whether
the legal interest in the premises or endowment is vested in them or in some person
as trustee for them or the school or
institution, convey to the education authority all such interest in the premises and
endowment as is vested in them or in the
trustee, or such smaller interest as may

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in the Second Schedule, page
70, line 24 ("Where any arrangement is made
under this Schedule, the ") to leave out from

"it shall be one of the terms of such

in pursuance of such arrangement, execute

arrangement."

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in the Second Schedule, page
71. line 6, at end to add the following article:
"11. Where an arrangement under this
Schedule provides for the transfer of a
teacher's residence to the education authority, this Schedule shall apply for the
purposes of such transfer in like manner
as it applies for the purposes of the transfer of a school."
This amendment is also consequential on
the amendment to Clause 14.
Amendment agreed to.
Second Schedule, as amended, agreed to.
THIRD SCHEDULE (Provisions as to transfer
of property, etc., to education authorities,
and as to existing local authorities and
officers).
1. Where any powers and duties are
transferred by or under this Act from a
council or committee (not being an
education authority for the purposes of this
Education Bill-
Committee.
8 JUNE 1923
Act) to an education authority, the following provisions shall have effect, namely:-
(a) any property or rights acquired and
any liabilities incurred by the council or
committee for the purpose of the performance of the powers and duties transferred from them to the education
authority (including any property or
rights vested or arising or any liabilities
incurred under any local Act or trust
deed) shall be transferred to the
education authority.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
2. Where any powers and duties are
transferred by or under this Act from any
such council or committee as aforesaid,
any existing officer of that council or committee employed immediately before the
appointed day in or about the execution
of those powers or duties. may, with the
consent of such officer, be transferred to
the education authority, and if any such
officer is so transferred the prior service
of the officer under the council or
mittee from which he is transferred shall,
for the purpose of the grant of a superannuation allowance to such officer by the
education authority on his ultimate retirement, be reckoned as service under the
education authority.
com-
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in the Third Schedule, page
71, line 19, after "shall" ("shall be transferred to the education ") to insert “ except
where the Ministry otherwise directs." As
hon. Members are aware the Third Schedule
deals with the transfer to the education
authority of property from councils or
committees that are not themselves education
authorities. It does not follow that in each
and every case where powers and duties are
transferred it would be necessary or desirable that the buildings and property should
be handed over. That, of course, will be
the general rule, but the proposed amendment
allows the Ministry to decide each case on
its merits. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
There is another amendment to Schedule 3,
page 72, line 10 ("as service under the
education authority ") at the end to add
"3 (1) Any person who is at the appointed
day employed as an officer of a school
attendance committee under the Irish
Education Act, 1892, shall, unless the
Ministry otherwise directs, be transferred
to the employment of the education
authority of the education area in which
he has been employed as such officer, and
shall perform under that authority duties
analogous to those which he performed in
his capacity as such officer, and the employment of any such officer under the
education authority shall be subject to such
conditions, as to remuneration and otherwise, as may be determined by the
Ministry.
(2) The provisions of Section eighty-seven
of the Local Government Ireland Act, 1898,
shall have effect as if the reference to an
officer of a school attendance committee
under the Irish Education Act, 1892, included a reference to an officer of an
education authority under this Act."
This amendment is designed to meet the
special case of officers of school attendance
committees. The school attendance committees were set up under the Irish Education
Act of 1892, one half of the members being
appointed by the local authority and the
remainder by the National Education Commissioners. The school attendance committees
were empowered to appoint and also to remove
officers and to prescribe their duties and,
with the approval of the local authority, to
fix their remuneration. The salaries and
expenses of these officers are defrayed out of
the local rates. But the Act of 1892 disappears, and with it also disappear the school
attendance committees. The duty of enforcing
school attendance is now placed on the new
education authorities. Some of the officers
of the school attendance committees are
whole-time employees. Others of them are
part-time officers only, and some of them
hold other public offices, such as registrars
of births, clerks to unions, and also clerks
to district councils and other appointments.
The proposed amendment is designed to safeguard the interests of these officers, and it
provides that unless the Ministry otherwise
directs the officers shall be transferred subject
to such conditions as to remuneration and
otherwise as may be determined by the
Ministry. The reference to Section 87 of the
Local Government Act, 1898, in the amendment means that in so far as any such
officer has pension rights those rights shall
not be affected by his having to act as school
attendance officer under the education
authority. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
*
*
*
*
*
*
3. If any question arises as to the application of any provision of this Schedule
in any particular case, that question shall
be determined by the Ministry of Home
Affairs.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in the Third Schedule, page
72, line 13, after "by" (" by the Ministry ")

Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry].
to insert "the Ministry, after consultation
with." It is necessary to bring in the
Ministry in view of the duties placed upon
the Ministry by the foregoing amendment in
connection with the school attendance officers.
I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Schedule, as amended, agreed to.
FOURTH
SCHEDULE (Constitution,
duties, procedure, etc., of committees).
Part I. Regional Education Committees.
The constitution, powers, duties and
procedure of a regional education committee of a county and the term of
office of the members of such regional
committee shall, subject to the provisions
of this Act, be such as may be determined
by a scheme framed by the council of the
county and approved by the Ministry.
Provided that the rules hereinafter set
out shall, except to such extent as the
Ministry may otherwise direct, be incorporated in every such scheme, that is
to say-
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
3. A member of a regional education
committee may resign on giving to the
appointing authority or council
month's notice in writing, of his intention
so to do.
*
*
*
one
*
7. The quorum of a regional education
committee shall be one-third of the total
number of members of the committee.
*
* *
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the Fourth
Schedule, page 72, line 38, to leave out from
"resign" ("may resign on giving") to the
end of line 40 ("writing, of his intention
to do so") and insert "by giving written
notice to the appointing authority or
council." I think it would be better that a
member of the regional committee should be
able to resign simply by giving written
notice to the appointing authority. If we
follow Scotland and provide a month's notice
it may perhaps frighten them from joining the
committee at all, or the member will simply
stay away during his final month. I do not
think this provision about a month's notice
is of any practical service. Therefore, I
suggest this change. I think that that is
the practice on some of the local committees,
but of course the county council is a more
serious thing. A man has to pay a fine if
he resigns. We do not want anything of
that sort, because it would discourage people
from joining.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This is an amendment which I am not sure
is altogether necessary, and if the hon.
Member will allow me I will consider between
now and Report. During the month that the
notice of resignation would be before the
committee the member would probably absent
himself from the meetings. Moreover, by
the amendments made in this Schedule in
the House of Commons these rules may be
varied with the consent of the Ministry. I
will consider the amendment between now
and Report.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: There is another one on this
schedule on page 73, line 11, dealing with the
quorúm of the committee. I suggest that
the committee itself should fix its own
quorum, but the number must not be less
than three. I think if you put a compulsory
quorum of one-third the total number you
are likely to have several meetings of the
committee without any result. I have found
that in the war pension sub-committees and
even in the finance committee of the county
council. It is remarkably difficult to get even
three people together. I have also observed
that when people turn up and find that there
is no quorum they are extremely loth to come
back. I therefore think it would be better
to take out the words "one-third of the
total," etc., and insert "such number not less
than three as that committee may appoint."
Perhaps the noble Marquess will consider
this also. I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The amendment which the hon. Member has
moved is not one to which I am particularly
favourable. I believe that it is a deplorable
circumstance that even in these large com.
mittees it does happen on occasions that there
are very few members present. A quorum
of three members in a committee of 15 or 21
is very small indeed. A quorum of one-third
may be perhaps fairly large. I do not feel
somehow that it is too large for the objects
we desire to obtain. I feel that the hon.
Member will agree with me that it is
particularly undesirable that the work of the
regional committee, especially if there is
only one committee in the county, should
devolve into the hands of very few people.
The object which I have had in this Bill is
really to associate the people with the requirements of education; and while education
has been faithfully served in the past by a
great many individuals working very often
single-handed still the practice is not one
we want to encourage. If a great many advantages may accrue, there are a great many
disadvantages which may also accrue, from
the whole jurisdiction of education residing
in the hands of one or two or
even three
Education Bill-
Committee.
8 JUNE 1923
people in the locality. Nevertheless I am
not going to say that I do not see the force
of the hon. Member's arguments where this
matter of practice is concerned, and it is
better to rely on three people to carry out
the duties which are comprised in the Education Act than not to get that higher
measure of a larger quorum. I accept the
invitation to consider this between now and
Report, and to go into the matter of the
pros and also of the cons.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much obliged to the
noble Marquess and withdraw the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Fourth Schedule agreed to.
FIFTH SCHEDULE (Provisions as to the
compulsory acquisition of land by the education authority).
*
*
*
*
*
6. On confirming an order the Ministry
shall forthwith publish notice of the confirmation, and, if within one month after
such publication, a petifion against the order
is received by the Ministry signed by not
less than two persons interested in the land,
then (unless the petition is withdrawn within the prescribed period) the order shall be
provisional only and shall not have effect
unless confirmed by Parliament.
Mr. LESLIE: There is a small point here.
I beg to move in the Fifth Schedule, page 75,
line 13, to leave out "not less than two"
("signed by not less than two persons") and
insert "any." It deals with difficulties that
may arise after an order has been confirmed
by the Ministry, and gives the right to two
people interested in land to petition. But I
do not see why you should limit the right of
petition to cases of two people interested.
What would happen might be that you would
get a piece of land from two people and they
would have a chance of further hearing. If
there is only one single owner he also might
have something to say. Therefore it is perhaps a little hard to close him out. I beg
to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
After having accepted so many and such
helpful amendments from the hon. Member I
regret exceedingly that I should feel bound
to refuse the last one he should move. I
think the hon. Member will see that if this
amendment were accepted it means that every
order might be taken to Parliament. It is
contrary to the intention of the Schedule
itself. I think also he will recognise that
there are numerous precedents for Departmental orders becoming final without the
necessity of Parliamentary confirmation, and
I feel that when he considers his amendment
in this light he will see that to carry out the
Fifth Schedule, compulsory acquisition of
land by the education authority, there is
really no necessity for the requirements embodied in his amendment. I hope I shall be
able to prevail upon him to withdraw his
amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I hope I shall never be implacable, especially after receiving so much
at the hands of the noble Marquess. I have
pleasure in asking leave to withdraw the
amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Fifth Schedule agreed to.
SIXTH SCHEDULE agreed to.
PREAMBLE and TITLE agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill, as amended, be
reported to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with amendments.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, "That this House do now
adjourn during pleasure." We are expecting
to receive from another place Bills to which
we propose to give a First Reading.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Increase of Rent
and Mortgage Interest Restrictions (Continuance) Bill, the Scutch Mills Bill, and the
Intoxicating Liquor Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
INCREASE OF RENT AND MORTGAGE
INTEREST RESTRICTIONS (CONTINU-
ANCE) BILL.

Intoxicating Liquor Bill.

THE SENATE.
INTOXICATING LIQUOR BILL.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving that this Bill be now read
the First time, I should like to take this
opportunity of informing the House that the
Prime Minister proposes to take the Second
Reading of this Bill himself in this House.
The Prime Minister, as hon. Members are
aware, is the promoter of this Bill, and has
taken an immense interest in it, and he proposes to move the Second Reading in this
House.
On Question, Motion: "That the Bill be
now read the First time," agreed to.

Tuesday, 12th June, 1923.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three

BANGOR GAS PROVISIONAL ORDER
CONFIRMATION BILL.
Order for Third Reading read.
Ordered: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to the said Bill without any amendment.

Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
