Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in the Schedule, Page 5, to leave out lines 8 to 10
inclusive (The Summer Time Act, 1922). This
Amendment which I have the honour to propose this afternoon deals with a matter
which I mentioned on the Second Reading.
The object of the Amendment is to leave out
of the present Expiring Laws Bill the reference to the extension of the summer time to
Northern Ireland. When I spoke the other
day I alluded to the previous history of the
Summer Time measure, and in reply the
noble Marquess who was supporting the application of summer time to Northern Ireland
referred to summer time as an accepted principle across the water. He said that it had
come to stay. There are various kinds of
visits. There is the week-end visit and there
is the annual visit. I do not think that it
is the general desire in Northern Ireland-in
the country districts at all events-that
summer time should become an annual infliction. Also, in the course of his speech, the
noble Marquess quoted the right hon. Gentleman the Prime Minister as having stated
elsewhere that the majority of the inhabitants of Northern Ireland live in the towns
rather than in the rural districts. I confess
that I felt rather disquieted at hearing that
statement on such an authority, because it
pointed to a large transference of population
within comparatively recent times. According to the figures of the Census of 1911 the
preponderance was in favour of the rural
districts, and the preponderance amounted to
75.000. I take these figures-the figures as
between town and country-from the figures
appended to the Lynn Report on Education,
which happened to be the means of reference
I had nearest at hand. Therefore the statistics which the right hon. Gentleman quoted
must be of recent date. I can only say that,
for I do not for a moment challenge their
accuracy. Still I do not think that it is
desired that the population should shift from
the country to the towns.
The right hon. Gentleman, as quoted,
pointed out that the prosperity of the country
Expiring Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE,
[Mr. Leslie]
is dependent on the prosperity of the towns.
He said that if the one is prosperous the
other would be prosperous. I did not observe
from the speech which the noble Marquess
quoted any indication of how the prosperity
of the towns was dependent upon the use of
summer time in the towns. The noble Marquess spoke of the inconveniences resulting
from the old system-the old system of having
our time five and twenty minutes behind the
time of Great Britain; and again I must say
that the roble Marquess did not indicate very
clearly what these inconveniences actually
were. The noble Marquess was a Member of
the House of Commons at Westminster, I
think, during the whole of the period when
Mr. Willet's Daylight Saving proposals were
under discussion. The proposals were derided
in Mr. Willett's lifetime but they were
accepted after his decease. Therefore the
noble Marquess will be fully equipped with
all the arguments for and against, and in
fact he will know more about it than the
majority of us.
The great objection to summer time--the
only formulated obiection really-comes from
the agricultural and rural districts. They
drew attention to the fact that though Parliament may alter the time of the clock by directing that the hands he moved backwards
or forwards. Parliament can do that, but
Parliament cannot control the sun. Parliament cannot arrange that the dew shall be
off the grass or off the hay or the moisture
off the corn stacks, and the agriculturists
also points out that the cow, one of the most
methodical of creatures, obiects to any interference with her natural habits. The general
agriculturist puts up the case that summer
time is actually of no benefit to him, and is a
positive nuisance. His men, as a rule, still work
by what is locally called God's time. It is a
distinct interference with the rural districts.
that they should work by one time while everything else is done by another. Their case in
fact is that we are too far west of Great
Britain for it to be reasonably practicable for
us in this instance to conform to the English
practice. They point out that Ireland is an
island west of Great Britain, some of it a
good deal west, and in doing that they are
not oblivious, I think, of the fact that there
are small districts of Ireland that are no more
west han parts of Scotland. and as you get
further west so does the difference in time
get more and more oppressive.
As regards the inconveniences that existed
in the days when we had to alter our
watches by twenty-five minutes when we
crossed the Channel, I do not think the inconvenience was so very great. The railway
time tables were printed in the ordinary
way. There was usually a foot-note saying
that this was Irish time or English time,
as the case may be. There was also the
necessity imposed on the Post Office of recording the despatch of telegrams in accordance with English time. I think that was
all. Regarding the telegrams, it made no
difference to the public. I do not think the
alteration of the watch made any difference,
for indeed the alteration as between England and Ireland is no more, if I remember
aright, than between French time and Central European time. The alteration of the
watch seems to be a necessary concomitant of
travel. The inconvenience of the watches
and telegrams is no more than that of the
difference in the tides. The tides will be
ebbing and flowing as usual. The tides have
more influence on us than railway timetables. Also, I remember during the old
period the train which connects with the
English service at the station near where I
live started 20 minutes later than it does at
the present time. I cannot agree that there
was any serious inconvenience under the old
system.
The agriculturist would, I think, prefer if
summer time is to be imposed that it should
come before Parliament as a separate measure, which could, of course, be done in the
spring of next year. He thinks that that
would give him more time by himself, or by
his associations, to approach members elsewhere of both Houses generally, and put his
side of the question plainly before them. I
think the agriculturist has some right to ask
for a separate Bill, because this question has
been raised before. It was raised elsewhere
in March of last year by means of a question addressed to the right hon. Gentleman,
the Minister of Agriculture. The Minister of
Agriculture stated-I will quote from page
75 of "Hansard of that date:-
وو
While (he himself was) in entire sympathy
with the whole agricultural community in
their antagonism to summer time.
That was the statement of the Minister of
Agriculture, and as the Minister of Commerce did not express any disagreement with
it, I think you may conclude that the Minister of Agriculture and the Minister of Commerce were at one.
Now as regards the towns, it is claimed
for the towns that summer time increases
the period available for leisure and exercise
in the open air, and that that is a great advantage to the dwellers in the towns. Nobody
would wish to deprive them of such opportunities for open-air exercise as they can get.
but I must point out that it has never yet
been established that the entire population
of the town makes use of the facilities
afforded. Clerks and shop-boys, and indeed
to some extent the workers in the shipyards
are able to exercise themselves in the open
329 Expiring Laws Bill-

13 NOVEMBER 1923.
Committee.
air more conveniently than before, but I do
not think that the women living in the
smaller back streets of Belfast find summer
time at all to their convenience. I think it
simply lengthens the period of their toils,
and increases their difficulty in getting the
children to bed and up again in the morning.
I do not think it can be contended that the
alteration is good for the children. I very
much doubt whether summer time is really
for the physical advantage of the bulk of the
inhabitants of the towns.
There is one advantage which the towns
might expect to reap if we returned to prewar practice and did not adopt here the
time which is adopted in Great Britain.
Commercial letters for Belfast would arrive
apparently one hour earlier from London.
The time of transit, of course, would not be
affected, but according to our time letters
would be delivered one hour earlier, because
trains in London could not be altered without losing such advantage as there may be
in summer time in that town. I do not say
of course, that the Belfast man would have
any longer time in which to answer his
letters, because the time of departure would
necessarily have to be adjusted. But the
Belfastman would apparently get his letters
one hour earlier--in fact an hour and twenty
five minutes if you do not lose the 25 minutes
in addition to the hour.
On the whole, I think it would be better
if any alteration is to be made in order
that there may be more time for open-air
exercise for the dwellers in towns, that the
alteration should be effected artificially
rather than in this way. It is perfectly
possible if the will is there, for the businesses
to open earlier and to close earlier. We are
told, of course, that it would be impossible.
We were told exactly the same thing when
the early closing of shops was first mooted,
and when the weekly half-holiday was first
suggested. We were told all these things.
Both the early closing of shops and the
weekly half holiday have come to have
general acceptance. I do not think there is
any insurmountable difficulty in varying
artificially hours in towns, whereas it is
absolutely impossible to change the actual
facts of real time in the country. I hope
that there will be a free expression of
opinion on this matter, and in fact that in
the end the House will consent to leave this
particular Bill out of the ambit of the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill. That will leave
the hands of the Government perfectly free
to introduce a proposal next year as a
separate measure.
Mr. LONG: I should like to express the
very strong dissatisfaction of the agricultural
community at the proposal of the Government to continue summer time for another
term. We considered that it was only a
war time measure. In the interests of agriculture the Government should have some consideration for their position in the matter
and their views. As the hon. Senator who
has just sat down pointed out they form a
very large majority of the population, and
a very considerable proportion of the remaining population are dependent upon agriculture. I think, therefore, the least the
Government could do would be to give some
consideration to their views in the matter.
It seems to me to be a case of the tail wagging the dog for the towns to dictate to the
country in this matter.
I had intended to speak on the question
of the children, but as the hon. Senator has
exhaustively dealt with the matter, it is
not necessary. I would just wish to protest
against slavishly following everything
British. I think in minor matters like this
it is not necessary at all that we should
follow in every particular. Our loyalty
would not be in the least questioned, I am
sure, if we acted differently in the matter of
summer time from what they do in England.
Our hearts are not regulated by our watches.
I think the loyalty of Ulster has been
sufficiently demonstrated to prove to all that
we are heart and soul with the Empire. I
should like if the Government could see
their way to accede to the wishes of the
hon. Senator in this matter. He has reminded the House that Mr. Willett did not
live to see the reform for which he pleaded
carried into effect. I trust a better fate, as
far as Northern Ireland is concerned, will
befall the hon. Senator, and that he will
live to see the change for which he so eloquently pleaded carried into effect. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Amendment
of the hon. Senator.
no
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I have
doubt the attitude adopted by the
Government on this question in another place
is prompted by what they consider is the
wishes of the majority of the people of
Northern Ireland. But I cannot help feeling
that the opponents of my hon. Friend's (Mr.
Leslie) amendment, whether they are in or
whether they are outside of the Government,
do not realize the heavy additional burden
they are placing on agriculture, which is oneperhaps the most important one-of
our industries. The Northern Irish
farmer suffers from some disabilities
which his English and Scotch competitors do not suffer from. He is further
from his market, because our market is really
the large industrial centres in England. He
is continually subject to have his-the whole
of his-export trade in cattle and sheep held
up for uncertain periods by any supposed case
Expiring Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Colonel Perceval-Maxwell]
of infectious disease, perhaps 200 miles from
his farm or 300 miles, whereas in England,
unless you are unfortunate enough to be
within 15 miles of it, you are all right. The
Northern agriculturist has a climate to deal
with which makes the harvesting of crops
always an anxious, late and uncertain proceeding. Providence has made Ireland 25 minutes
behind Great Britain by the sun. No Act of
Parliament can alter this, but we have, I
think, a right to expect that our own Parliament will not add to our anxieties and difficulties by putting another hour to our disadvantage. Arbitrarily making us call it six
o'clock when it is really about 4-30 forces us
to lose the best hour and a half in the day.
For every farmer knows that on many days
in the late autumn it is impossible to do anything in the way of saving crops until after
the dinner hour on account of the dew.
They cannot
I notice that the noble Marquess the Leader
of this House, speaking on the Second reading, said it was a passion with us to place
ourselves in the same position as Great
Britain. That is a sentiment which all the
loyal and industrious farmers of the Six
Counties will thoroughly endorse. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) There are some disabilities in which it is not in the power of
the Government to help us.
change the fact that we are surrounded by
sea, but I would ask the noble Marquess to
do something towards placing us in the same
position as Britain by using his influence with
the Cabinet to re-consider their attitude on
this question, and place us, as far as possible,
in the same position as the British farmer.
I think that in doing so we will not be asking
the Government to depart from a policy which
they have already adopted. This Parliament
was received by the people of Ulster on the
advice of their leaders, many of them in the
present Government. I do not think it was
ever meant merely as a machine for registering what had happened in Westminster,
whether it was good or bad for Ulster. The
noble Marquess has himself brought in an
Education Bill somewhat different, and I am
sure, in spite of criticisms, he at any rate
considers it a better Bill and a better system
than that introduced at Westminster. We
have had a Licensing Bill not identical with
the Licensing Law in England. If none of
these Acts disturb the close attachment which
we have to Great Britain, and which I hope
will always continue, why on earth should we
cripple this great industry for the sake of
conformity with the law of time in England?
There is another argument which I have seen
used. It is said the majority are in the
towns, and the towns want summer time. I
am not quite sure about that. I heard only
last week complaints on the part of working
men, that it kept the children up too late.
But supposing there is a majority in favour
of a long summer evening, we realise that
minorities must suffer when serious material
interests clash. But you are placing a very
great burden on an industry vital to the
whole community in order that the townsman
may have an hour's more amusement. That
is really what you are doing. The townsman,
the shopkeeper, or the manufacturer can close
his works and say, "I will begin at such and
such a time to-morrow." The farmer's business never ceases, day or night. The farmer
does not have half the holidays, his business
is always running; nature is always running;
his anxieties are always running on. I would
very strongly appeal to the Government to
reconsider this question, and accept my hon.
Friend's amendment, and if they think, after
careful consideration, that it is necessary to
bring in this summer time, let them bring in
a separate Bll next year. I heartily support
the amendment proposed by my hon. Friend.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to consider this amendment so far as it
affects the majority of the people of the Six
Counties. It appears to me that those who
have already spoken are considering the
interests of those among whom they are
situated.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: No, no.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The farmer
may have some little disadvantage, but at
the same time there seems to be considerable
advantage to the citizen, even in the small
towns as well as in the large cities. I think
there is a considerable amount of imagination
about the grievances that the Summer Time
Act has brought to the farmer We are told
that the cow would be affected by the change.
Well, I have not heard it said whether the
hen lays as regularly or not. But how the
cow, or even the laying of the hen, could be
interfered with is more than I can understand. There are many more things to be
taken into account in this matter. It has
been stated repeatedly by one or two hon.
Senators that children cannot be got in at
night or got to bed. I quite believe that,
even a child knows what is really good for it
and when it is happy. Undoubtedly the
lengthened evening and good sunshine enable
the child to have a longer time to enjoy
itself before bedtime. It is quite true that
it is impossible almost to get children into
their homes at a reasonable hour in summer
time. It is not only the children-they must
not be neglected-but you must take into
consideration those people who are tied up in
thousands in the works in the City of Belfast
and in towns such as Lisburn, Portadown and
Expiring Laws Bill-
Committee.
13 NOVEMBER 1923.
other places-women who are closed up inside
four walls from a quarter to eight in the
morning probably, and from a quarter to one
or two o'clock until six o'clock in the evening.
Is it not necessary to give these people as
much daylight as possible and as much leisure
as possible? This Daylight Saving Bill has
undoubtedly brought about a tremendous
change for the benefit of the worker. These
men in the shipyards value the lengthened
evening. It enables them to get out into the
country to see the green fields. I must say
that so far as I am concerned I cannot vote
for the amendment to have this Daylight
Saving Bill deleted. I think while I would
be in favour of making some little sacrifice
in the interests of the farming class, the
sacrifice would be too great in a case such
as this. I must certainly vote against the
amendment and in favour of the maintenance
of the Dayling Saving Bill.
I
Mr. GREER: I do not often find myself in'
disagreement with my hon. Friend who has
moved this Amendment (Mr. Leslie), but on
the present occasion I certainly differ very
strongly. He was professedly speaking on
behalf of the agricultural community.
take issue with him there. I think he is only
entitled to speak on behalf of the farmers
themselves. I live within some 15 miles of
the sea, and I am often delighted to see
hundreds of farm labourers going on their
bicycles down to the sea in the evenings,
delighted to have the opportunity. I think
it is a splendid thing for them to have a
few hours to enjoy the fine sea breeze. It
must be good for them and enable them to
do the work on the farm much better than
if they had nowhere to go, but just to lounge
about their homes.
My Friend also spoke in the interests of
the cows. I do not understand how he was
instructed by them. I think the cows very
readily conform to the new system.
I should not be very much guided
in my opinion or judge the merits of this
Act by its effect upon the cows. He also referred to the children in the slums. Well,
after all, there are some naughty children
who will not go to sleep when they ought
to. I suppose their parents have some way
of dealing with them. I do not think we
should hang up a Bill of this importance
because there are some children in the slums
who cannot sleep.
I cannot help feeling that the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) perhaps omitted one of the
most important considerations in regard to
his Bill, and that is that it might require
one to rise one hour earlier in the morning.
That matter might possibly-I do not know
whether it does or not-affect his judgment
to some extent. I can only say for my part
that with the exception of very few people
everyone I have spoken to is delighted with
the long evenings and the arrangements for
the observance of summer time. I cannot
see why the farmers if it is too early to deal
with the labour at the earlier hour, they
cannot change the hour and let their
labourers come an hour later and stay an
hour longer. We have heard in general
terms that it is crippling the great agricultural industry. I would like some of my
friends to give a concrete example of how
it cripples the industry. It seems to me
that the farmer can very easily arrange so
that it would not do them any harm at all.
Apart from the farmers, who I believe are
few in number, their families, the labourers,
and certainly all the dwellers in the towns
and villages would be very much in favour
of this Bill being continued, and for that
reason I would be very sorry to see it dropped.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: May I
give my hon. Friend an example of how it
cripples agriculture? I make arrangements
personally as he suggests that you should
work late, and I have had men leave me
because they wanted to get away before the
shops closed. That is one way it cripples
us. Also he seems to think that there is
not a general demand. I have a resolution.
here which I will not trouble you by reading
unless you wish it. It is from the Executive
Committee of the Farmers' Union, representing all the farmers in the whole of the
Six Counties, and expressing their opposition
to summer time.
Mr. WOODS: I would just like to add my
voice against the amendment, because I
believe the Summer Time Bill is a very useful
Bill in industrial centres, not only in allowing us to have an hour extra at night for
amusement but for the great saving it is
in industrial cities and towns in light. That
is a considerable advantage to the working
people for saving in taxation. I quite agree
with my hon. Friend, Senator Cunningham,
that there is a great deal of imagination
amongst farmers in regard to this great
disability. I go sometimes into the country
-I cannot afford to live there-and I find
farmers raising objections to the Summer
Time Bill, but when you really get down
to argument with them and ask for concrete
facts about it you will find that they can
give you nothing concrete. I have found
that in some parts of the country the farmers
do not use summer time at all. They change
one clock specially to keep the time of trains,
but the other clocks on the farm are kept
at the usual time and they carry on their
work in the usual way. I do not see any
great disadvantage and I certainly see very
great advantage to the industrial centres.
That is the reason I am in favour of the
Summer Time Bill passing again.
Committee.

Expiring Laws Bill-
THE SENATE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I think that based on the amendment proposed by my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) we
have had a very interesting debate, and I
am sure that no one will quarrel with him
for having raised this question again. It
seems to me that the arguments we have
heard for and against are certainly arguments which anyone has a right to put
forward. The matter which we have to
consider as a Senate is, what is best for the
community as a whole. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) I was anxious at one moment
of the debate lest it might develop into an
argument between the agricultural interest
and the industrial interest, and that is a
controversy which, like all my hon. Friends
here, we expressly deprecate. The community
of Ulster is made up of a splendid agricultural population and an equally efficient and
courageous industrial population, and one
without the other cannot exist. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) It seems to me of
the highest importance that both these great
wheels should work together so as to make
our Province the place which we desire it
to be. The particular question of summer
time is one which my hon. Friend rightly
says came up in the House of Commons at
Westminister, and when he spoke of the
debates which used to occur in that assembly
I began rather to tremble and to wonder
whether he might not have unearthed some
speech which I might have delivered on a
Friday afternoon in the House of Commons.
Mr. LESLIE: Oh, no.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As he assures me he did not I will not
pursue the matter further. In respect of
that I think we should recognise that we
are a very conservative people. Probably
the agricultural community are more conservative than other sections of the population,
and when an innovation is brought in they
will believe longer than perhaps any other
section of the community that it is not conducive to the national welfare. I do feel
that this perhaps is a case in point, and that
when summer time has been exploited for
some years the agricultural community no
doubt reluctantly will settle down to the
changed state of affairs and will find that
their prophecies and prognostications as to
the disasters that would ensue have not
actually taken place. I think my hon. Friend
will agree with me that this practically
occurred across the water. I may be wrong,
but I have not heard, recently at all events,
that there is a strong body of opinion in
Great Britain against the re-enactment every
year of summer time. I somehow feel that
this state of affairs will come about in
Ulster, and that the agricultural population
will realise that on the whole the advantages
accruing from summer time in the direction
of assisting the industrial population are on
the whole best for the community. My hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie) knows more about the
gyrations of the sun and probably about the
idiosyncracies of the cow than I do, but 1
hardly feel that those arguments are sufficiently cogent to induce the Government to
reconsider the position they have taken up.
I am willing, however, to say this to the
hon. Member (Mr. Leslie), that there is no
reason why if he does not agree with me,
if he thinks my arguments are not as cogent
as his-there is no reason why he should
not in Our next Session follow in the
steps of Mr. Willett on the other side and
introduce a private Member's Bill for the
purpose of reverting to the time that he
desires, or propose another time than the
one ordained by the sun. The point which I
would venture to make particularly is the
advantages accruing as a whole to the community. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I
believe that when we consider it from that
point of view, the difficulties in this direction
which surround the farmer are really less
than the difficulties which, if we change,
would surround the industrial community.
My hon. Friend has made a point of what
I said and what the Prime Minister said in
another place as regards the actual figures
of the two populations. I feel unwilling to
speak of two populations as if they were
entirely separate. It is quite true that we
were originally an agricultural people, but
with the passing of time and the march of
centuries we have developed industry, and
while we can certainly say that the agricultural community is of longer antiquity than
the industrial community 1 think we
must realise that the prosperity of the great
industrial centres is perhaps the bed-rock of
the prosperity of the country. The more the
industrial centres prosper the greater is the
demand which they will make upon the
agricultural community for the purpose of
supplying them with their requirements. So
that it does seem to me that we should concentrate on making the industrial centres
additionally successful, and I feel that it can
be argued that summer time gives them many
advantages. My right hon. and gallant
Friend (Colonel Perceval-Maxwell) spoke of
the leisure which it gave to the townsman.
Now leisure is not a thing to be decried, and
it is of the greatest importance to those who
work in our shipyards and in our factories
that they should have an ample amount of
leisure, because we know quite well that no
good work can be performed unless there is
leisure also. The present shortness of hours
as compared with working hours formerly is
not really a concession extracted by militant
trade unionists out of unwilling employers.
Not a bit. Employers have realised that the
best work is obtained by observing these con-
13 NOVEMBER 1923.

Committee.
Province would be served by bringing this
matter forward in the early days of next
Session I am willing to undertake that if
he introduces a private Member's Bill I will
use all the influence that I possess to see that
the Bill is discussed in entirety in the Ulster
Houses of Parliament.
ditions of humanity; that there is a certain
amount of time men should work and that
they should not work more than that, and
that combined with work they should have
ample time for leisure. Summer time gives
greater opportunity for those who work in
the towns. That is not a matter which we
should decry in any way. We should feel
that it is an advantage arising from summer
time.

I do not know there is much I can add
to the arguments so ably brought forward
in defence of the Bill by my hon. Friend
(Mr. Greer). The point which I ventured to
make on a previous occasion was that I do
feel it is of vital importance that we should
keep in line, if I may use the expression,
and synchronise with Great Britain. My
hon. Friend behind me (Mr. Long) spoke
-I will not say in disparaging terms,
but in somewhat vehement terms as
to our slavishly following Great Britain.

the expression

used. I quite understand the argument

spirit of our people that we should slavishly

But we certainly have a great desire to remain in line with Great Britain and keep
as close to Great Britain as we can, and

surely that is an argument for keeping as

Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: Nobody
wants to get away from them.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
He spoke of the Education Act and also the
Licensing Act. Those are two measures
which touch Ulster and touch Ulster alone.
But when we are speaking of having a
common time with Great Britain I am sure
my right hon. and gallant Friend will see
that there is a distinction between a measure
of summer time for the purpose of synchronising railway times and other matters with
the industrial centres of Great Britain-that
there is a distinction between that and Acts
which solely touch the Province of Ulster.

However, I do not
be

entitled to trespass longer on the indulgence

should be retained rather than that we should

(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) If I am wrong
there is nothing, as I said before, to prevent
the hon. Member from raising this matter
again and bringing to his support all that
body of opinion which he tells us exists

Member feels that the best interests of the
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid that I must say
that the earlier speeches which I have heard
this afternoon in opposition to my proposal
rather strengthen my views that it would be
desirable to postpone this matter until next
Session, because I think there is urgent need
for a little clear thinking on the part of
We
those who are opposing my amendment.
have been told how easy it would be for
the farmer to alter the hours and begin a
little later and keep his men a little longer
at work. We have also been told what a
charming thing it is to see the agricultural
labourer bolting off on his bicycle to the
seaside, rejoicing for an hour in the sunshine
which has been obtained by the Daylight
It is impossible to reconcile
Saving Bill.
these two propositions. I take due note of the
-shall I say-rather light manner in which
the cow has been treated. I can only hope
that the cow in bulk will not retort by
decreasing the supply of milk to the towns,
because after all that is what it comes to.
If the arrangements are not satisfactory the
milk will not get to the towns at the time
it is wanted. The cow has a good deal to
do with that unless the town population are
disposed to rely entirely on tinned milk. I
hope they are not. I do take note of what
my hon. Friends on my left have said as to
the extent the manual workers in Belfast
make use of this extra hour. That really is
a very important matter. I also take note
of the offer of the noble Marquess to consider
a private Bill next Session. I think that the
offer will receive consideration on the part
of the Ulster Farmers' Union, who have
addressed some of us very pointedly on this
subject. I think, too, that the farmers in
Northern Ireland will be pleased at the
amount of interest which has been shown on
their behalf this afternoon. I do not think
from the speeches I have heard or from the
silence of those who have not spoken that
there is any desire to record opinions on
this matter in the Lobby. We have heard
several speeches both for and against, and
I am inclined to think that it will be in
general accord with the opinion of the House
that the amendment should not be pressed
to its logical conclusion. Therefore, I think
the interests of business would be best served
by asking leave to withdraw the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Oral Answer to Question.
AMENDMENT OF STANDING ORDERS.
Mr. LESLIE: I asked a question the other
day in the course of a discussion which arose
out of the necessity to amend to some extent
the Joint Standing Orders relative to Local
Bills. The fact was that although there was
not the slightest reason to anticipate the
smallest difference as between the two Houses,
the machinery for arriving at a joint decision
did not seem very clear, and it occurred to
me that it might be desirable to get something on the records of this House. Therefore I decided to trouble you, as Chairman
of Committees, with the question that stands
in my name. That is to say, to ask whether
you can give any information as to the
procedure in regard to the amendment of the
Joint Standing Orders relative to Local
Bills.
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member has
asked me whether I can give him any information as to the procedure in regard to the
amendment of the Joint Standing Orders
relative to Local Bills.
I may say at the outset that We have
modelled this procedure as closely as we can
on that of Westminster. At Westminster
the Counsel to the Chairman of Committees
in the House of Lords and to the Chairman
of Ways and Means in the House of Commons
consider all suggestions for amendment of
the Standing Orders which come in from time
to time from the various Government Departments, or from Parliamentary Agents, etc.
They then draw up the amendments in consultation together and submit them for
approval to the two Chairmen and finally to
each House once every year. By this procedure the Standing Orders of the two Houses
at Westminster as far as regards the Standing
Orders which affect the public, i.e., promoters
and opponents are practically identical. The
Standing Orders differ only where the procedure on the Bills themselves during their
course through either House is different.
We have followed Westminster completely
as to the method of putting the proposed
amendments before each House for its
approval, and this was carried out last
Session. But owing to the fact that it was
considered simpler to have one set of
Standing Orders for both Houses, due to the
principle of sending all Bills to Joint Committees, I quite agree that a difficulty may
arise in the event of one House not agreeing
to amendments which the other House has
already agreed to. In such an event, which
I hope will never arise, the Standing Orders
would have to be separately printed for each
House.
In order to lessen the chance of any divergence of opinion, it is proposed to put down
these amendments for the consideration of one
House before they are considered by the
other, in other words they will not be considered simultaneously by both Houses.,
Should the House which considers them first
make any alterations, then the other House
will have the opportunity of considering the
amendments as altered.
I have considered alternatives to this procedure, but I cannot see that they make for
improvement. We might have a Joint Committee appointed to consider the proposed
amendments, but there is no certainty that
both Houses would support the views of that
Joint Committee, and I would point out that
in effect the present procedure is by an
informal Joint Committee composed of the
Chairman of Ways and Means and myself.
Or again we might pass the amendments in
one House and send them to the other
House, but here again if one House had
different views from the other either one
House must give way or we must have two
different sets of Standing Orders.
I suggest that although there is a possibility of divergence of opinion, it is not very
likely to arise, and that the present procedure
is really the simplest that can be devised.
I do not propose that the House shall
consider these amendments until next week,
by which time I hope that the other House
will have agreed to such amendments as I
am ready to submit to this House.
Special Order.
Business of the House.
13 NOVEMBER 1923.
Finance Bill.
I was asked on Thursday last by Senator
Greer, I think, whether these amended
Standing Orders would take effect for the
introduction of next Session's Bills. I think
it would be more convenient that they should
take effect immediately they are agreed to
by the House. No dates are changed in the
Standing Orders, and I do not think Parliamentary Agents will find it inconvenient to
act on the amendments which are to be
proposed.
Should any difficulty arise owing to the
fact that these Standing Orders have been
amended rather late in the Session I am
sure that the House will not be insistent
if there is any non-compliance with the
In
Standing Orders owing to this fact.
future I hope it will be found possible to
bring forward the amendments earlier in
the Session.
AND DISTRICT
ELEC-
MARKETHILL
TRICITY SPECIAL ORDER, 1923.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move to resolve
"That the Markethill and District Electricity Special Order, 1923, made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland, under the Electricity (Supply)
Acts, 1882 to 1919, and confirmed by the
Ministry of Commerce, under the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, in respect of the
Townlands of Coolmillish, Edenykennedy,
Crunaght, and Gosford Demesne, in the
Rural District of Armagh, in the County of
Armagh, and which was presented to the
Senate on the 7th day of November, 1923,
is hereby approved.
In moving this Resolution I should like
to inform the House very briefly what this
Order is. It is an Order to grant statutory
powers to a company to supply electricity
for light and power in Markethill and the
adjoining district. The Company in question
has been formed by local residents who desire to have the benefit of electric light in
their homes and places of business and in
the streets of the town, and they are providing the necessary capital. The Rules of
Procedure for giving notice to interested parties were duly observed before the Order was
made and confirmed and the Order as granted
follows the usual form. I would, therefore,
ask the House to approve the Order.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Finance Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Moved: That this Bill be read the First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move - That this House do now adjourn. I understand that there will be no
business before Friday, and I would suggest
that the House should now adjourn until
Friday. I hope this will suit the convenience
of hon. Members.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like the noble Marquess, if he can, to give us some indication
as to the days next week on which it is
proposed the House should sit. Some of us
were rather hoping we should have been able
to sit to-morrow and Thursday, and that we
should

like to know if we will sit next Tuesday. Before I sit down I should like, if I may, to
thank you, Mr. Speaker, for the reply you
gave the House

Standing Orders.
on
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am very glad the hon. Member has raised
this particular point. I know he is well aware
of the difficulty under which we labour and
that Bills come before us as they come from
the Commons. There is no business for this
House until next Friday, which will be
occupied in taking the First readings of Bills
sent down to us from the Commons. The hon.
Member will thus realise that the work of the
Senate comes next week rather than this
week, and after we have taken the First
readings on Friday it will be possible-and
I am not sure that it will not be necessary,
and I hope convenient to members-to meet
on Monday, and perhaps Tuesday, Wednesday
and Thursday. So far as I can tell, the First
readings on Friday will be of the Trade
Boards Bill, the Housing Bill, and other
Bills which are all very important. We shall
be able to take the Second reading on
Monday and the remaining stages on other
days of the week. It is hoped that the
prorogation will be the week after that.
I am sure hon. Members realise that it is
the intention of the Government not to rush
these Bills through the Senate, and in my
position as Leader of this House, I feel that
is a responsibility which I shall endeavour
to discharge to the best of my ability. As
far as it is possible I shall not encourage
the suspension of Standing Orders for the
purpose of passing Bills through all their
Business of the House.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
stages on a particular day when hon. Members have not had an opportunity of considering those Bills and bringing forward
amendments they desire to be discussed.
Consequently I feel that the programme I
have endeavoured as far as I can to map out
will be agreeable to hon. Members, having
regard to the merits of the legislation and
the manner in which it should pass through
this House. If hon. Members will come to
see me or my hon. Friend (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard) with respect to particular
days allotted for the various Bills we shall
be very glad to do all we can to facilitate
them.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am not
sure whether I am in order or not, but I
would like to refer to the statement made
by Senator Leslie that he came to Belfast
to attend the Senate, and unfortunately there
is only sufficient business for one day. It
means that he will have to go home again
and return on Friday, and again on Monday
or Tuesday. What I would like to know is,
does the Government not think it is time
that something should be done to relieve the
expense and inconvenience that Members are
put to in travelling to Belfast to attend the
Senate. I think it is rather strange that
nothing has yet been done for the Members
of the Senate, who are put to greater expense than the Members of the other House
and they are allowed expenses. I would
like some information on the matter.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Gentleman has raised a matter
which I can assure him has not been lost
sight of. The difficulty has been the manner
in which it would be possible to carry out
the suggestion which the hon. Member makes,
and I will ask him to leave it for a short
time longer until it is possible to be put
before the House a scheme which will be
suitable to the Parliament of Northern Ireland and also agreeable to the Members of
the Senate.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Half
after Four of the Clock
Friday next.
until

Finance Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The Bill to which I have the honour to ask
the House to give a Second reading this
afternoon deals principally with the question
of Finance administration. Many of its
clauses follow closely the Imperial Finance
Act, 1923, and I do not think there is any
really controversial matter to be found in this
Bill. I think it would meet the convenience
of the House if I were, in asking the House
to give this Bill a Second reading, to take
the different clauses of the Bill and touch
briefly on them.
Clauses 1, 2, 3 of Part I of this Bill are
all based on the Imperial Finance Act, 1923.
Clause 1, which deals with the relief from
Entertainments Duty, is one, I think, which
will commend itself to the House, because
up till now an agricultural show or a show
promoted in the interest of some particular
industry, or an industrial exhibition, would,
if a band attended at that show or
exhibition, be liable to this Entertainments Tax. This Clause now obviates that.
In future bands can attend at these shows
without rendering them liable to Entertainment Duty.
The same thing applies to Clause 2. That
is, relief of Entertainments Duty in respect
of school entertainments. Up till now if a
school gave a concert and an old boy over the
age of 18 years came down and sang a song
or gave some theatrical sketch, the fact of
that person being over the age of 18 years
and giving that entertainment rendered that
school liable to Entertainment Duty. This
clause does away with that.
Clause 3 deals with Licence Duty on sixwheeled vehicles. This also is taken from
the Imperial Finance Act, 1923, and is due
to the fact that lately there has come into
existence a new type of motor vehicle
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
which
know,
is six-wheeled. So far as I
there are very few of these
vehicles at the present moment in
in operation in Northern Ireland, but there
are a considerable number in use across the
water. No doubt it is only a question of
time until they will be in considerable use
over here. This vehicle is, as I said, a sixwheeled one, and instead of having a goodscarrying body, is fitted with a trailer, and is
so constructed that this trailer is partly superimposed on the motor vehicle, so that at all
times a substantial portion of the weight of
the load of the trailer is borne by the vehicle.
Doubt was expressed as to how this vehicle
was to be taxed for motor licence purposes.
This clause makes it quite clear that these
vehicles are to be treated as if they were
single vehicles used for drawing trailers,
Clause 4 deals with the exemption from
stamp duty of instruments relating to
Savings Certificates. This clause refers to the
Ulster Savings Certificates. Some doubt
arose as to whether certain instruments relating to Ulster Savings Certificates were to
be exempt from stamp duty. This clause
makes it perfectly clear that they are exempt.
Hon. Members will notice that this clause is
retrospective, and dates back to 12th October,
1922, the date of the issue of the regulations
with regard to the Ulster Savings
Certificates.
Clause 5 exempts from stamp duty wholetime Civil Servants of the Government acting
as solicitors. At the present time these
solicitors are servants of the Government,
and the effect is that the stamp duty has to
be paid in reality by the Government. At
the present moment it is entirely a matter
of book-keeping entry. It is felt that it
would be better and more direct if these servants were exempt from duty. This clause
effects that.
Clause 6 relates to Section 2 of the
Finance Act of 1894, the Act which was introduced by the late Sir William Harcourt, and
which made a great change in what are
commonly known as the death duties. This
clause is due to a recent legal decision in
England which challenged the interpretation of that Act. Briefly, the effect of this
Clause is that Estate Duty can be charged on
each occasion on which property passes, so
that there is no distinction between the case
where legacy succession duty has been paid
in one sum, and the case where a sum has
been paid on each passing of the property.
This clause follows the Imperial Finance Act
of this Session.
Clause 7 repeals the last of the famous
land clauses of the 1909 Finance Bill, and is
the only one which has not been repealed by
the Imperial Finance Act of 1920. I do not
think I need say any more on that clause.
Clause 8 is merely for administrative purposes, and it enables the Ministry of Home
Affairs to refund certain rebates in motor
licence duties. There are certain motor
licence holders having a car over a certain
age who are entitled to rebate. It was not
provided in the Act exactly how this was to
be paid. This clause obviates that.
Clause 9 deals with one of our own Acts,
the Loans Guarantee Act, 1922. That Act
provided that no guarantee should be given
after the expiration of twelve months from
the commencement of that Act, which would
mean that no guarantee can be given after
14th day of December of the current year.
Before the summer adjournment this year
an Amending Act was passed, which increased
the limit of the Government guarantee to
somewhere about 3 millions. So it has been
considered desirable to extend the period of
this Act and to allow that a guarantee can
be given for another twelve months.
Clause 10 deals with another one of the
Acts of our own Parliament. The first part
of this clause is drawn up to amend Section
2 of the Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Act (Northern Ireland), 1923, which was
passed last Session. This Act provided for
the creation of a fund which was to be applied to the maintenance and depreciation of
public buildings. That Act was passed in
another place, and came up to us, and while
it was being considered by this House the
Government came to the conclusion that
maintenance and depreciation was a revenue
charge. As hon. Members are aware, this
House is very limited in its powers to deal
with money Bills, and it was impossible to
move amendments to this Act. So the
earliest opportunity was taken of bringing
in an amendment, so this clause was introduced into this Bill.
Section 2 of Clause 10 deals with thee
granting of loans to Civil servants, and is an
extension which enables loans to be given to
Civil servants, not only for the purpose of
building houses, but also for the purpose of
purchasing houses, and it is limited in fact
to five years from the 29th March, 1923.
Clause 11 is merely the construction clause
and does not need any comment from me. I
beg to move.
On Question, Motion (That this Bill be
now read the Second time) agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time on Tuesday next.
Business of the House.
Adjournment.
16 NOVEMBER 1923.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving that this House do now adjourn,
I think, perhaps, for the convenience of the
House it would be best if I were to foreshadow the programme for the remainder of
the Session. Next week, as far as we are concerned, will be a somewhat full week. It
will be necessary to sit on Monday, Tuesday,
Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday. On Monday there will be the Second reading of the
Trade Boards Bill, the Local Government
Bill, and the Housing Bill. On Tuesday
there will be the amendment of the Local
Bills Standing Orders, the Third reading of
Finance Bill, the Second reading of the
Appropriation, Superannuation, and Constabulary Bills. On Wednesday there will be
the Committee stages of the Trade Boards,
Local Government, and Housing, and on
Thursday there will be the Third reading of
the Appropriation Bill, the Committee stages
of the Constabulary and Superannuation
Bills, and the Report stages of the Trade
Boards, Local Government, and Housing.
On Friday, the 23rd, there will be the final
stages of all the Bills. At this moment I am
moving that we adjourn during pleasure until
we receive from the other House business
which they are sending to us.
House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Housing Bill,
the Local Government Bill, and the Constabulary Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Housing Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
First time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read the First time accordingly,
ordered to be printed and to be read the
Second time on Monday next.

Moved: That this Bill be now read the
First time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

Bill read the First time accordingly,
and ordered to be read the Second time on

First time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

Bill read the First time accordingly,
ordered to be read the Second time on Tuesday next.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at a quarter
past Four of the clock.
351 Reconstitution of Records.
Reconstitution of Records. 352
THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Monday, 19th November, 1923.
The Senate met at a Quarter past Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
NEW MEMBER SWORN.
Sir Emerson Crawford Herdman, Knight of
the British Empire, Sworn, and took his
seat in the room of His Grace the Duke of
Abercorn.
RECONSTRUCTION OF RECORDS AND
REGISTRATION OF TITLE.
Mr. GREER had the following question on
the Order Paper:
1. What steps have been taken and what
progress has been made toand
(a) Reconstruct the documents
maps destroyed in the Four Courts,
Dublin.
(b) Expedite the dealing with documents lodged for Registration in the
Central and Local Registration of Title
Offices.
(c) Provide the accommodation for the
Central Office Staff as already approved
by the Treasury; and
(d) To provide for the proper staffing
of the Local Offices.
2. Whether he can state what progress
has been made with the completion of the
25-inch Ordnance Survey Maps.
3. Whether having regard to the great
delay that periodically arises in obtaining
directions from London in urgent matters
owing to the Land Registry being a reserved
Service, he will endeavour, in the coming
Irish Land Bill or otherwise, to have the
control of the Land Registry transferred to
the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
He said, The question on the Paper in my
name raises a matter of very great importance to every land owner, and especially to
every tenant-purchaser in the Northern area,
and I must crave the indulgence of this
House for I think it is necessary to make a
few remarks by way of introduction. When
property is of such a character that ownership
does not pass by mere delivery as, for example, a table, or a chair, or a banknote,
or a bearer bond does, but requires some
document to be signed in order to transfer
it, it is usual and indeed necessary to have a
register to show who is the owner for the
time being of such property, and every Member of the House is familiar with registration as it applies to stocks and shares.
By far the most important class of property
requiring registration is that of land owing
to the variety of interests a person may
possess in land. He may hold in feesimple, that is, absolutely, or in tail, or for
life, or for a term of years or he may be a
mere caretaker, and the mere fact of his
being in possession would not show to which
of these classes he belonged. Accordingly we
find that certain systems of registration are
to be found in operation in practically
every civilised country in the world.
There are two systems of registration in
Ireland-first, the registration of deeds which
has been in operation since the year 1708,
and, secondly, registration of titles which
was started in 1891 under the Local Registration of Titles (Ireland) Act, 1891. The
first of these systems, the registration of
deeds was established under a statute in the
reign of Queen Anne, the Preamble of which
is:-
"For securing purchasers and preventing forgeries and fraudulent gifts and
conveyances of land which have been
frequently practised in this Kingdom, and
for settling especially a certain method
of registration,"
and so on.
Under this method a memorial or abstract
of every document that was signed affecting
land with a few very trifling exceptions must
be verified and that abstract must be lodged
in the Registry of Deeds Office. Abstracts
or memorials are written on parchment and
also signed by the parties who signed the
deed, and the Registrar of Deeds has this
filed, put into books, and filed on the shelves
of the Registry of Deeds Office. There are
thousands of these abstracts filed there in
that office. I have got a deed here. I just
want to explain that an abstract of this
document, signed by the signatories of this
deed, has to be lodged at the Registry of
Deeds Office. They then put this note on
the deed:-
"Registered in the Registry of Deeds,
Dublin, at 37 minutes after Two o'clock,
on the 29th day of July, 1920, Book 59,
Number 83."
They have to be very particular as to the
date and hour of the registry, because a
good deal depends on priority of registration,
particularly in the case of mortgages.
Now one advantage of that system is that
it preserves a record of the document even if
the document itself has disappeared, and it
shows also, as I have said, priority of regis
tration. The great disadvantage is that it
is very expensive. If one wishes to find
what documents there are affecting a plot
of land, the title of which is being inquired
into, one must have a search made at the
353 Reconstitution of Records.
Reconstitution of Records. 354
19 NOVEMBER 1923.
Registry of Deeds Office and see what abstracts have been lodged. That search may
cost £50 or £60 in the case of a large estate.
Another objection is that the documents are
handed back and form part of the documents
of title relating to the particular piece of
land. A professional man has to be called
in who has to examine the documents, read
them and see what the effect of them is,
see that they are properly stamped with the
stamp duty obtaining at the date, and to
see that they are properly signed, and if
one of the signatories resides abroad, that
his signature is properly attested. It is a
very expensive matter dealing with lands
that are registered in the Registry of Deeds
Office.
The second method, the registry of title,
is much simpler and cheaper. It is to this
method that I specially wish to refer on
the present occasion. Instead of registering
every document the Registrar registers the
effect of the document and retains the
document. Instead of handing it back with
the note I have just read upon it he registers
the effect of the document. The foundation
of this system of registration of title is a
map. There must be an official map. It is
a map made as accurately as possible. On
that map are plainly marked the boundaries
of this particular piece of land held by the
owner who is getting the title registered,
and for convenience we will call him A. The
title of A to this plot of land with this
identifying number put upon it is thus
investigated at the official Registration of
Title Office. A kind of ledger account called
a folio is opened in the name of A, setting
out his exact interest in the land, as for
example, owner in fee subject to a purchase
annuity, mentioning the amount, or subject
to mortgage. This statement is authoritative
and conclusive. Of course it has to be
amended officially every time there is a
transfer of ownership of the land. Anyone
on payment of a small fee of a shilling
or so can inspect that folio and see at a
glance how the title is held and the precise
manner in which A holds the land. A can
himself get a certificate of his own title-a
very useful thing to have. It is the best evidence he can give of his title. In case there is
any difficulty on the part of the Registrar
as to the proper construction of the document
that may be handed to him to be registeredif it is not quite precise what estate the
owner takes upon it-provision is made by
Act of Parliament for taking the opinion
of a Judge of the High Court on the question.
This system has been adopted in Germany
and France-indeed in a great part of Europe
and also America and in many of our
Colonies, and it may be regarded as an ideal
system so far as small land owners are concerned; and in a very short time, if the
coming Land Bill passes into law, every
owner of land in the Northern area will be
a small landowner. It would be a very
great boon in my opinion to them to have
this system of registration of title made as
simple as possible. In the Northern area
we have a central or head office in Belfast
under the charge of the registrar of title,
who is called the Central Registration
Authority. There is also a local registration
office in each county, generally in the county
town-only one local office in each county.
That is in charge of a local registrar, and
a suitable number of clerks are, or at any
rate ought to be, in central office and in
these local offices. Some persons think there
ought to be many more local offices in each
county. There is certainly some justification
for this opinion if it were not for the expense
it would entail. In each local registration
office there are kept ordnance maps of the
holdings showing the boundaries and ownerof all the land registered in
the county, also all the folios relating to such land are kept in
local office. Duplicates are sent
ship
the
to
the central office, so that both in the central
office and in the local office there are reliable,
authentic records of the exact position of
ownership.
Application for the registry of title in the
first instance or to alter the registration
on a change of ownership is made to the
nearest registering authority, accompanied
by all the necessary documents. It is then
referred to the central office. The registrar
satisfies himself as to the title, prepares a
note to be entered in the folio in regard
to the particular effect of the document
produced. When this is done it is sent down
to the local office and kept there.
I am afraid I have taken too much time
over this uninteresting subject, but it really
is necessary to go into it fully in order to
bring out the point I want to make, and 1
am glad that the hon. Member from Tyrone
(Mr. Montgomery) is here to-day, as I know
he has taken a great interest in this question for many years and has studied the
registration question on the Continent, in
France, and Germany. We know he was
Chairman of the Land Owners' Convention,
and I personally do not think there is anyone in the Northern area who knows the
land question better in all its ramifications.
So I am very glad to see him here to-day,
and I hope he will take an opportunity of
putting his views before us on this question.
The position in the Northern area is as
follows: We have this system of registration
of title which, as I said, is an ideal one, but
it is not as perfect as it might be at present. There is a considerable congestion of
Z
Reconstitution of Records. 356
Reconstitution of Records.
THE SENATE.
In
[Mr. Greer]
cases for first registration. There is certain
-this is my special point-to be a great rush
of cases of this kind for first registration
if the coming Land Purchase Bill passes
into law. It is absolutely necessary, in my
opinion, to make suitable provision in time,
so that the office will not be overwhelmed
when these cases come in. One. of the defects in the system of registration of title
is that it is optional except where there is
a tenant purchaser who has to pay the purchase annuity to the Land Commission. In
that case registration is compulsory.
other cases it is merely optional, and in my
opinion should be made compulsory in every
case. When the coming Land Purchase Bill
passes, every tenant will have become the
tenant - purchaser, willingly or unwillingly, of the land, and the landlord's interest which is vested in the
tenant will then be compulsorily registered,
but the tenant's interest will not be necessarily registered. I think that when the
matter is being dealt with it should be dealt
with finally, and the tenant's interest in the
land should be gone into and registered as
well as the landlord's. Under the previous
Land Purchase Acts in order to avoid the
expense and delay of making a complete
registration, the landlord's interest only was
registered, and in order to inform the public that the tenant's interest had not been
gone into, the Registrar made an entry in the
folio that the title was registered subject to
equities, and that avoided the important
principle of the whole registration of the
title code, because you could not at a glance
see the position of a man's title.
Here is a folio, and this folio sets out the
name and the lands, and says:
The said so-and-so is registered as owner
in fee-simple of the lands above described.
Then it states where the lands are, the
area, and so on. There have been many
transactions in this case. Another document
was sent to the Registrar, who records the
effect of it there. There are altogether six or
seven, but it ends up by certifying that the
last person who was registered is the owner
of the land. Now, the point I am going to
make is that any person reading that knows
the exact position of the man in occupation
of the land. If he wants to go to a bank
and raise some money, or if he wants to get
credit in other ways, the mere producing
of that document will show his position.
This folio contains this Clause which, I
think, is very unsatisfactory:
Note. These lands are registered subject to the rights or equities, if any, arising
from the interest vested in the tenant
being deemed to be
graft
a
on
his previous interest in the land or arising in any other manner from the existence of such previous interest.
That statement merely shows that the
tenant's title was not investigated or dealt
with by the Registrar at all. The tenant
might be holding the lands subject to a
mortgage or subject to the interest of some
other person.
That would not appear in
this document, and before anyone having
this document in his hand could understand
the position he would have to begin to go
into the title of the tenant. I think that
this is a very great mistake and that the
expense would be much less to have the
tenant's title registered in this way during
the progress of the purchase of the land
through the Land Commission, and I think it
ought to be gone into and that we ought to
see that if possible the full title will be
registered.
Another point I want to refer to is the
ordnance maps. These maps are, of course,
The
the foundation of the registration.
ordnance maps, I am afraid, are not brought
up to date, and it will take a considerable
I
time to bring them up to date. have,
therefore, asked the Question No. 2 about
these ordnance maps. As far as my information goes, they are complete for the city
of Belfast, and for some reason or other
they are complete in County Down, but I do
not think they are complete for any other
county. County Antrim is rather far behind, I am afraid, but I just want to know
accurately about that. We have had for
many years in Ireland an excellent set of
ordnance maps on the 6-ins. scale, and a
large survey on a 25-ins. scale is in progress,
and has been going on for many years. I
know it is not completed yet, and I am
anxious to know what progress has been
made with it. If
Act
the coming
takes the form, as it possibly may, of vesting
all agricultural land in the tenant's right
of way, immediately after the passing of the
Act the rush of work thrown upon the Registration office will be immense, and it is very
important that there will be no arrears of
work then outstanding, so that the office
will be in a position to start with a clean
sheet. So far as I can see, assuming the
present Government are returned to power
and that the new Land Bill is introduced in
the first Session, the rush could not arise
until the summer of 1925, so there is plenty
of time to get things ready in the meantime.
My object in moving in the matter now is to
see that things are taken in time and that
when this rush comes on the office will be prepared to deal with it.
A large number of the ordnance maps were
sent from the local Registration offices to
the central office, which was then in Dublin,
357 Reconstitution of Records.

19 NOVEMBER 1923.
Reconstitution of Records.
and were destroyed during the fire in the
Four Courts. It is absolutely necessary that
these maps should be reconstructed at the
earliest possible date if the dealings of the
lands included in those maps are not to be
brought to a standstill. Fortunately, this
can be done, as the Irish Land Commission
have duplicates of the maps. It is now a
considerable time since the destruction of
those documents in the Four Courts, and I am
anxious to know what progress has been
made in replacing the maps that
destroyed.
were
I understand that some additions have
been made to the excellent staff which is
employed in the central office at the county
courthouse here and that by working at
extra pressure they should be able to work
off the arrears before the rush of business
comes on, but with the present accommodation that they have at the county courthouse, one room here and another at a
considerable distance borrowed from the
various other departments who have got
offices there, it is impossible that they should
get through their work with the rapidity
that they otherwise might. For example, I
understand that a kitchen table has been
taken from the lower premises up to the
attic and that this is being used as a drawing board or drawing table for the maps.
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon. Member
will not go too far outside the question on
the Paper. It seems he is going beyond the
actual question.
Mr. GREER: The question was to ask the
Leader of the House what steps have been
taken to provide the accommodation for the
central office staff as already approved by
the Treasury. The accommodation is-
Mr. SPEAKER: I thought that the hon.
Member had passed that and got to the 25inch ordnance survey map.
Mr. GREER: I am referring now to the
accommodation that the Registrar in the
central office has, and for registering any dealings for land he requires a large office because
he must have a large staff. However, I will
not go on with that. The local offices, 1
happen to know some of them, also require
increases in the staffs, and my object in
mentioning these things is that I am anxious
to have the matter looked into carefully to
see whether there are proper staffs or not
and whether there is proper accommodation
or not. I believe there is rot.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I have visited the
Land Registry at the Antrim Courthouse,
and before touching on some other aspects
of the question I wish to confirm what
Senator Greer has told you. I will not take
up your time going into details. I can only
say I consider that the accommodation
there at present is scandalously inadequate
considering the immense importance of this
office for the welfare of that large portion
of the population of Northern Ireland who
are owners of agricultural land. I believe
that some additional accommodation has been
sanctioned by the Treasury I understand
that this is a reserved service, so that the
Northern Government is not directly responsible for this state of things I am objecting to. But at the same time they should
put on the utmost possible pressure to get
this office properly equipped. Some additional accommodation has been approved, but
apparently is not in immediate view, and
I doubt very much whether what has been
approved of is sufficient for such an important
office. At any rate it is not there. In the
same way I understand that an increase of
staff has been sanctioned. I am of opinion
from what I have seen that that increase
will not be sufficient to do the work that
ought to be done there properly, but such
as it is it has not come yet, and I hope
we may receive some assurance, when Mr.
Greer's questions are answered, that the
Northern Government will lose no opportunity of trying to get this matter remedied.
The immediate necessity is to deal with the
arrears that are lying there, and which
cannot possibly be adequately dealt with as
matters are now.
I do not think many people are fully aware
of the importance to the peasant proprietary
of this institution. The ignorance and
apathy that exist about it have had the effect
of there being no public opinion behind the
demands which the excellent officials who
have been entrusted with the administration
of the Local Registration of Title Act have
from time to time put forward. I wish
especially to draw the attention of the House
to one aspect that I do not think is sufficiently understood. Of course, with a large
body of very small owners it is perfectly
obvious that it is very important to make
the legal transfer of land and of small
parcels of land as simple and as inexpensive
as possible, but what is much more important
is to put within the reach of the owners
of these small farms-many of them very
small-a source of sound credit that the
ownership of land, if properly registered,
entitles them to. From the earliest times
the owners of agricultural land have had to
borrow money. I think one of the earliest
orators describes the plains of Attica as
being dotted with columns to show the land
mortgaged. That, I suppose, was the earliest
form of the Registration of Deeds. In every
country the history has been the same. The
profits of agriculture are so slow in coming
in and the business is so precarious in many
ways that it does not tempt capitalists who
359 Reconstitution of Records.
Reconstitution of Records. 360
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Montgomery]
employ capital directly, and therefore much
of the capital used in farming must always
be borrowed money. On the other hand, to
make up for the evil of this, the ownership
of land is the best possible security for
advances, and there
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt
the hon. Member, but I cannot see that that
has anything to do with the question on the
Paper.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: Of course I bow to
your authority, but it appears to me that
to show the importance of putting this
matter in order, it is necessary to prove
that the most important function of
registration of title is to provide the
very best and most accessible basis of
credit. However, I will not dwell upon that
point, as you think it should not be dealt
with. It may be possible to move for Papers
on another occasion, and to then have the
question thoroughly discussed, because I
think it is one of the most important
matters we have to deal with in view of the
increase in the number of small owners, and
I desire to draw attention to it. At any
rate, for whatever object the ritle has to be
made clear, it is of supreme importance that
it should be made clear and accessible. Mr.
Justice Madden, in bringing in the Local
Registration of Title Bill, in 1891, said he
hoped to have an office within moderate
distance of every holding. This is very far
from being the case. On the Continent.
where these things are thoroughly understood, the districts for the office are comparatively small, no bigger than a barony,
and the idea of controlling in one office all
the transactions in connection with the land
of a country the size of Ireland, is, I think,
unique. I do not think any other country
possesses such a cumbersome and obstructive
institution as that which was set up in this
country, and it is not astonishing to find it
is a relic of the penal laws and was the invention of a lawyer for preventing the penal
law against the purchase and inheritance of
land by Papists being evaded. Germany had
local registration of title in many provinces
for hundreds of years before we had it, and
owing to the decentralisation it works there
in an entirely different way to that in which
it works here. The county office here is
too far off, and when we go there we find
that nothing can be done without reference,
formerly to Dublin and now to Belfast.
Lastly, as Senator Greer has pointed out,
wt find that the titles are not clear. Messrs.
Browning & Smith, in their handbook, explain what this was due to.
I do not agree with Senator Greer that
all these titles should have been investigated
at the time of first registration. I believe
it would have caused such delay as would
have led to great confusion, and therefore
I think the provisions in the Registration
of Title Act, and in the Orders that were
made under it, enabling these titles to be
registered in the first place subject to equities in the terms Senator Greer has read
out, were perfectly justified, as a temporary
measure; and in order to get these titles on
the register in some shape or form, most
of them were registered subject to
"equities," but as soon as that was done
these titles ought to have been taken in hand
and these blots remedied. The result now
is that the register is not by itself sufficient
evidence of title to the lands, and before
a title subject to equities can be accepted
by purchasers or lenders an investigation
of the title to the earlier tenancy is necessary. I know that as early as 1903 Mr.
Justice Madden was engaged in devising a
system to get all these blots attended to and
these equities inquired into and put in
proper order, county by county, but it has
never been done, probably because the
British Treasury would never advance the
money. Now that we are going to have this
large increase of owners in Northern Ireland
it should be done at once. Messrs. Browning
& Smith, who are the best authorities we
have on registration of title, say these
matters should be investigated, and they
add-
"It is not advisable that the responsibility
of this investigation should be undertaken
by the parties themselves; it should be
thrown on the registering authorities, and
the note as to rights or equities should be
cancelled."
The House has a good deal of business
before it, and, therefore, I must refrain from
going into various matters I should otherwise
have wished to bring before the House. What
I want to point out particularly is that in
order to deal with the Register properly
adequate accommodation and an increased
staff are urgently necessary, and the greatest
pressure should be put on whatever
authority is responsible to have these matters
dealt with. A fact that should not be lost
sight of is, that as soon as these arrears
have been overtaken and the office put in
some sort of order the question of doing away
with the equities should be considered. I
am told at the office that applications from
the owners to clear equities are coming in
in considerable numbers but in a large
number of cases the blot on the title remains,
and as long as this is so no organisation
of the real credit of the occupying owners
can be accomplished. In the countries where
the system is best worked the ordinary
transaction for the sale of a small farm.
or for putting a mortgage on a farm, can
be done in one day at the local office if the
parties attend there. The longest period that
361 Reconstitution of Records.
Reconstitution of Records. 362
19 NOVEMBER 1923
any of these transactions occupy is three
days, and in the case of a large and complicated estate it may possibly take three weeks.
In this country, however, a solicitor will
tell you that he can get nothing done for
many weeks, and sometimes it even takes
twelve months to carry through a transaction.
That stands very much in the way of a farmer
being able to use the real credit he is entitled to, and drives him to have recourse
