Public Records Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): It is desirable for the
present to defer the progress of this Bill, and
I would venture to put before the House the
reason for taking that course. I think it can
be very briefly stated. The Government of Ireland Act contemplates the setting up, by
legislation of the Northern Ireland Parliament, of a Public Records Office of Northern
Ireland, and that this Public Records Office
shal!, when set up, not only afford a place
for the reception of Records relating to matters within the jurisdiction of the Northern
23 Promissory Oaths Bill.
Land Trust Powers Bill. 24
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry)
Ireland Government, but also, if the Imperial Government are willing, for the reception of Northern Ireland Records which relate to Imperial matters, such as the Records
of the Supreme Court of Judicature.
The Bill will require to provide machinery
for the collection and deposit in the new Records Office of Records under the jurisdiction
of both Governments. In order to make sure
that this machinery will be suitable for the
purposes of both Governments, the actual details for the Public Records Office are still
under consideration by the parties concerned.
That is the reason I ask the House to forgive
me if I do not proceed to-day with the Second
reading of this Measure.
Ordered: "That the Second reading of said
Bill be put off sine die."
Promissory Oaths Bill.
Order for the Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that the Promissory Oaths Bill
be read a Second time. The Bill is to amend
the law relating to Promissory Oaths in its
application to Northern Ireland. I do not
feel that there is any necessity for me to
trespass on the indulgence of the House over
the details of this Measure. The Promissory
Oaths Act, 1868, is the general authority
governing the taking of the oath which is
known as the Oath of Allegiance. This Act
is in force in Northern Ireland by virtue of
Section 61 of the Government of Ireland Act.
It specifies certain office-holders as being required to take the Oath of Allegiance, but
does not include Civil Servants. By a Defence of the Realm Regulation No. 45 Ε.Ε.,
clerical or administrative officers serving in
an established capacity in His Majesty's Civil
Service were required to take the Oath of
Allegiance, and this Regulation expired at
the termination of the War, namely 31st
August, 1921. A similar Regulation. No. 45
E.E. of the Restoration of Order in Ireland
Regulations, is legally still in force in
Northern Ireland, but as it also relates to
His Majesty's Civil Service in Great Britain,
it has no application to His Majesty's new
Civil Service in Northern Ireland.
It will be remembered that under the
Local Government Act which we passed last
year, a Declaration similar to the Oath of
Allegiance, but including a reference to the
King's Government of Northern Ireland as
well as to His Majesty himself was required
to be taken by members and officers of local
authorities in the Province, and the present
amendment of the law relating to Promissory
Oaths in this country is merely the logical
sequence to Section 5 of the Local Government Act to which I have referred. It would
be quite illogical were we to insist on public
servants under the local authorities making
a Declaration of Allegiance as a condition
of holding office if, at the same time, we
did not require a similar declaration from
public servants of the central administration.
There is a requirement now that public servants under the local authorities should
make a Declaration of Allegiance, and therefore it will be perfectly obvious to this House
that a similar Declaration must also be required of the central administration, which
is His Majesty's Civil Service in Northern
Ireland.
In regard to this Bill, I am certain of two
things: Firstly, that it will meet with no
objection on the part of our Civil Service,
and Secondly, that it will nowhere be interpreted as a sign of any lack of confidence
on our part in the loyalty of a body which
has already rendered such signal service to
the State, service which I do not think this
House or the country generally has been slow
to recognise.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that the House
and the public generally will accept this B
with the utmost alacrity. It is, if I may
say so, not only the sort of Bill which the
people of Ulster want; it is exactly the Bill
which they mean to have. The only point
I would raise upon it is in the nature of
asking a question I make out at least I do
SO from
first reading of the Bill-that
the intention is that any person temporarily
employed for a period exceeding one month
would be required to take the Oath.
is how at first sight I read Sub-section (b) of
Section 1. It is not a matter on which we
could possibly raise the smallest objection, because our intention is undoubtedly that any
person who takes public money shall conform
to our Province and subscribe the Oath of
Allegiance both to His Majesty and to the
established Government.
That
On Question, Motion ("That the Promissory Oaths Bill be read the Second time ")
agreed to.
a

committed to

House, the House to be in Committee on

Land Trust Powers Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I owe an apology to this House about this
Bill. I very much regret to inform this
25 Land Trust Powers Bill-

15 MARCH 1923.
Second Reading Deferred.
House that this Bill has not yet left the
printers' hands, and has not been circulated
to Members of this House. Consequently it
will not be possible to take the Second Reading of this Bill to-day. I propose that the
Second Reading be taken at the next sitting
of the Senate.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the noble Viscount
has, to a certain extent, the sympathy of us
all in the announcement which he has just
been under the obligation of making. I am
equally sure that nobody would presume, or
wish to presume, to suggest for a moment
any lack of consideration on the part of the
noble Viscount or any Member of the Government. I would not suggest any lack of
consideration on the part of any Member of
this House. Neither do my remarks convey
anything in the nature of criticism as regards
the diligence of the officers of this House.
But the fact remains that the Bill to which
the noble Viscount referred was read a First
time by this House about 14 days ago. Members of this House have been summoned from
more or less distant parts of the Province to
attend this House to-day, inter alia, for the
consideration of this Bill, and we are informed that the Bill has not yet left
the printers' hands I venture to think
that we shall not be considered
pressing unduly the rights which we possess
if we ask that steps may be taken to make the
printers and those immediately responsible for
their control see that this House is not trifled
with. If this House is summoned to consider
a Bill on Second Reading this House expects
that the Bill will be ready for their consideration. If I may say so, I do not for a
moment criticise any Member of this House
in regard to this Bill. I know perfectly well
this unfortunate incident is not one for which
they are in reality responsible, but I do feel
that out of consideration for Members of
this House, particularly for those who live
at a distance at a greater distance than I do
myself-steps should be taken to prevent a
recurrence of this incident.
as
Mr. GREER: I am entirely in accord with
the observation's which the hon. Member
has just made. I certainly do not wish to
fasten the blame on anyone in particular and
most assuredly not upon the Government. I
am quite certain it is not their fault, but
undoubtedly someone has blundered. I object
most strongly to Bills being brought into this
House at the last moment as has been the
case before now. I think that we all ought
to have sufficient time to read the Bill and
consider the Bill before we vote upon it and
approve or disapprove of it. After all we are
legislating for Northern Ireland, which is
rather an important matter. I think the
least we can claim is that we should receive
the Bills that come before the House in proper time. We who take the matter seriously
-I include myself among that number and I
may say every other member of the Househave to read over a Bill and, in addition to
that, have very frequently to refer to Acts
of Parliament that are mentioned in the Bill.
These Acts of Parliament we cannot possibly
carry in our minds, and therefore we ought
to have sufficient time at least to refer to
these Acts of Parliament mentioned in the
Bill. I protest most strongly against Bills
being brought up on the morning of the Session in which we are asked to vote upon them.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
should be the last person to make any complaint of the remarks which came from hon.
Members of this House. Naturally on such
an occasion as this, when I can offer no excuse on behalf of the Government, I feel that
an apology is owing to all the Members of this
House for the most unfortunate circumstances
which have necessitated the postponement of
the two Bills which were on the paper for
Second Reading and for discussion to-day.
It is indeed very unfortunate that this has
occurred, and I should like, on behalf of
the Government to offer an apology to the
House for this occurrence.
It is quite true,
what the two hon. Gentlemen who have
spoken have said that Members, at inconvenience to themselves, have to come very
long distances to attend the sittings of this
House. Naturally they have every right to
expect and to see that the Measures for discussion which are on the papers which they
have received will be ready for them to discuss and to vote upon if necessary. I am one
of those who heartily agree with Mr. Senator
Greer, who is averse to measures being
forced into people's hands, if I may say so,
without their having had an opportunity of
considering them and comparing them with
other Acts of Parliament, and coming to this
House fully acquainted with the Measures.
I see that the hon. Gentleman foreshadows
that these Bills will be hastily brought into
this House, and that we shall be called upon
to pass them with as great expedition as
possible. I hope that may not be the case.
As regards the actual facts. The Bills are
not printed. That is the difficulty with
which we are faced at the present moment.
These matters are dealt with on the other
side of the water by departments who have
been occupied with this business for very
many years past. I am not trying to claim
that we are an entirely new organisation
and that that is the reason. But I do feel
that, perhaps in the interests of economy, we
have not the scope that is enjoyed on the
other side of the water.
Criminal Low Bill.
Criminal Law Bill.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry)
Perhaps when I come down to more precise matters in dealing with the subject that
has been raised, I am not sure that I am not
virtually the culprit. (An hon. Member: No,
no.) Perhaps the hon. Gentleman who in his
kindness absolves me, would allow me to finish
my sentence. There has been an Education
Bill before us, and I have endeavoured metaphorically to lash the printers into celerity in
producing that Education Bill, and I have been
successful. But I am not sure when I consider the matter closely, but perhaps that
tyrannical disposition is responsible indirectly for the fact that these Bills are not
printed. I should like to offer an apology.
There has been no negligence on the part of
the Government or of the Officers of the
House. We fully realise the difficulties as regards the time which Members get to consider these Bills, and also their inconvenience
in coming from long distances to attend this
House. I assure them that whatever steps
can be taken to obviate a recurrence of this
incident. shall be taken by the Government.
Hon MEMBERS: Hear, hear.
Ordered: "That the said Bill be read the
Second time on Wednesday next."
Companies (Re-Constitution of
Records) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I very much regret that I have to inform the
House that this Bill also has not yet left the
printers' hands, and therefore cannot be taken
to-day. I propose that the Second Reading
shall be taken at next sitting of the House.

Criminal Law Amendment Bill.
Mr. GREER rose to ask the Leader of the
House:
a
"Whether, in view of the fact that the
Government propose to introduce in the
present Session
Criminal Law Amendment Bill for the protection of young girls,
they will consider the desirability of inserting in the Bill a clause to enable unmarried mothers to take legal proceedings
against the putative fathers such as they
have power to take in England and Scotland."
The hon. Member said: When I gave notice of the question on the paper I had not
a copy of the Bill or I might have worded it
somewhat differently. I hope it has sufficiently raised the question on which I desire
to obtain the views of the Government. The
Bill which is to come before the House
shortly refers to the criminal side of the
transaction, but I wish to call the attention
of the House to what appears to me to be a
serious difficulty-the civil side of the transaction-in the hope that the Government may
see its way to set that right. As the law
stands at present, a woman who has been
wronged in that way has no direct legal remedy against the other party to the transaction. She has to bear the odium and disgrace and also the expense connected with it,
and the other party rides off scot free.
As the law stands in Northern Ireland, she
cannot bring any action against the man, because the act of seduction in itself is not
regarded as an actionable wrong, giving one
party a remedy against the other, and the
only length the Courts will go is to assume
that an action may be founded on loss of service, that is to say, that if the woman is
in the employment as servant of some man
he may bring an action for loss of service
connected with the indecent act. The father
in certain instances may also have a right of
action, that is to say, if the woman was living
with him and if she was under age the law
presumes loss of service on the part of the
father. But if the woman is over age the
father has to prove that she was actually
acting as servant to him at the time of the
indecent act.
Now everyone can understand how difficult
it is to get the ordinary master, say, of a
domestic servant to take proceedings and appear in Court. to court the publicity which is
attendant to that in a case in which he has
no personal interest. Sometimes, indeed, he
happens to be personally interested, and he
is not likely to bring an action against himself, or the proposed defendant may be a
friend of his and he does not like to take proceedings against him. There is no law compelling the master to take proceedings. Even
the father very often is so much scandalised
by the disgrace of the family caused by his
daughter that he simply tells her to leave the
house and make the best of it. I have not
much experience personally of these cases, but
I actually know cases of both these kinds.
The only other remedy that there is, so far
as I know, is that a poor woman may take
her child into the workhouse and the guardians
have a right of action against the putative
father for the expense of maintenance in the
workhouse. I consider that is a very poor
remedy for the woman who has to undertake
the maintenance, education, and perhaps medical expense of rearing this child.
Criminal Law Bill.
Criminal Law Bill.

15 MARCH 1923.
S
What I desire to-day is to have the law in
this country assimilated to the law prevailing
in England and Scotland. If the House will
bear with me for a moment I will say, if I
may, in a very few words, what that is. In
England under an Act of Parliament, which
I need not trouble you with, any single woman
who may be with child or who may be delivered of a bastard child may apply to the
justices in Petty Sessions for a summons
against the putative father. Such justices may
make an order for maintenance against the
putative father, and the money payable under
that order has to be paid either to the mother
or to the person appointed by the justices,
if they think it not desirable that the mother
should receive it. They can by a subsequent
Act order or provide for a sum of money not
exceeding 5s. a week for the maintenance of
the child. The order is to have no effect if
the child has died or attained the age of
13 years. In certain cases the justices may
extend that period to 16. That is the law
in England. Then in Scotland jurisdiction is
given to the Sheriff's Court to order a decree
adjudging a person within its jurisdiction to
pay for the maintenance and education of a
bastard child of which he is the putative
father and of the expenses incident to the
birth or funeral expenses of such child, in addition to maintenance.
It appears to me that where a woman has
been in this unfortunate position there must
be two parties in the case, and it is very
unfair to fasten all the odium, disgrace and
expense on one and let the other off free.
In many cases the poor woman has not the
means to pay for the maintenance of the
child. She loses her situation. She cannot
get another situation. Her father casts her
off. I desire to put an end to that state of
affairs. I do hope the Government will add
a clause to the present Bill assimilating the
law with that of England, or better still,
bring in a short Bill to deal with the matter.
I beg to ask the question which stands in my
name.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY.
I have listened very carefully to the remarks
made by the hon. Senator who has just
spoken, and I note that he has asked a question with a view to his suggestion being incorporated in the present Session in the
Criminal Law Amendment Bill for the protection of young girls. I venture to suggest to
the hon. gentleman that that really is not the
place for the provision which he seeks to enforce.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: Hear, hear.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The proceedings which are contemplated are
not criminal but civil. The hon. Gentleman
has cited certain Acts and I think he must
be referring to the Affiliation Orders Act of
1914, which I think is the last Act which
has been enacted dealing with these special
cases. But be that as it may, what I should
like to say to the hon. Gentleman is, that the
question of introducing a separate Bill to
bring the law in Northern Ireland into con
formity with that in Great Britain is under
the consideration of the Government at the
present time. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
It is impossible to arrive at a final dicision
yet. As the hon. Gentleman is aware there
is at the present moment a Bill before the Imperial House which was ordered to be printed
as recently as the 23rd February, 1923. The
Bill is a Private Member's Bill, and the Government are at the present moment desirous
of seeing what the fate of that Bill will be
in the Imperial Parliament. I should like to
assure the hon. Gentleman that this is a
matter which the Government are considering
very closely, and I feel sure that before this
Session ends probably we shall be able to
bring forward some legislation in which I
have no doubt the hon. Member will be able
to take part and make these suggestions
which he thinks will be in accordance with
the principles he has put forward.
Adjournment.

Clause 1-
(1) Every person who is at the passing of
this Act either-
(a) an officer serving in an established
capacity in the civil service of the Government of Northern Ireland; or
(b) otherwise employed on full time
duties in or by any department or authority of that Government
shall before the first day of July, nineteen
hundred and twenty-three, or before such
later date as may in any special case be
allowed by the Ministry of Finance for Northern Ireland, and every person who after
the passing of this Act is admitted to serve
as such an officer, or is taken into such employment as aforesaid, shall, within
month after he is so admitted or taken into
employment, take an oath in the form prescribed by the Schedule to this Act.
one
دو
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to move an
Amendment to Clause 1, page 1, line 11.
After " or " (" any department or authority")
insert "by any." This is purely of a drafting nature. The word "in," which occurs
in line 10, can be appropriately used with
reference to a Department, but not with reference to an Authority, as the latter word
implies, as a rule, some single Executive
Officer. It is purely a drafting amendment
which I ask the House to accept.
On Question, Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The next Amendment which I desire to move
is on page 2, line 18. At end add
"(5) The said Ministry of Finance shall
have power to apply the provisions of this
Section to such persons employed on parttime duties in or by any Department, or by
any Authority, of the said Government as
that Ministry thinks fit."
This Amendment is intended to meet the
representations which have been made to the
Government, urging them to include within
the scope of the Bill certain cases of parttime employment. The Bill, as drafted and
read a Second time, deals with two cases-
(1) Permanent pensionable Civil Servants;
and (2) all other full-time employees of the
Government. That is to say. it only dealt
with cases of full-time employment.
The effect of this Amendment will be to
extend its provisions to part-time employees,
but it has not been considered necessary to
do this unconditionally, because on examination of the various cases it has been found
that the administration of the Oath would
often either render the procedure an absurdity or be ultra vires or impracticable. Parttime employees include charwomen, scavengers employed on odd jobs, occasional
labourers employed on harbour works, and
other persons engaged in this class of employment. There are, besides, Imperial Civil Servants doing occasional work for the Northern
Government, occasional consultants such as
bacteriologists, arbitrators and referees occasionally employed, and certain marketreporters, and occasional agricultural
inspectors.
It is recognised that it is desirable that
there should be a power to require the taking
of an oath by persons coming within some
of these classes, but according to the circumstances of each particular case. Accordingly,
this Amendment is brought forward in order
to give power to the Ministry of Finance, as
the Civil Service Authority, to apply the provisions of the Bill in suitable cases of parttime employment. I beg to move.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Clause 2 (Short Title) ordered to stand part
of the Bill.

Land Trust Powers Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
This Bill, as hon. Members will observe, is
a short Bill and a fairly simple one. The
necessity for this short Measure arises out
of the establishment of the Irish Free State.
In 1919 the Imperial Parliament passed an
Act, the Irish Land (Provision for Sailors
and Soldiers) Act, under Section 4 of which
the Local Government Board for Ireland was
granted powers to acquire land either compulsorily or by agreement for the purpose of
crecting thereon houses for ex-Servicemen.
The Act in question defined the various
powers and duties of the Local Government
Board, and laid down a limit of three years
within which land could be compulsorily
acquired for this purpose. This time limit
expired in December last. On the establishment of the Free State it became necessary
to make other provision for the continuance
of the work in connection with ex-Servicemen's cottages, which was then and is still
far from completion. During the past year
the Ministries of Finance and Home Affairs
have, pending the nomination of a successor
to the Local Government Board for Ireland,
carried on existing contracts for houses, but
have not under the limited powers delegated
to them, been able to enter into any fresh
commitments, either in regard to the acquisition of sites or in respect of the erection
of cottages not already contracted for by the
Local Government Board for Ireland.
In order to complete the authorised programme of housing schemes provision was
made in Section 3 of the Irish Free State
(Consequential Provisions) Act, 1922 (which
appears in the schedule to the Bill now under
consideration), for the establishment of a new
Trust to be designated the Irish Sailors and
Soldiers Land Trust. This body, as hon.
Members will see, is to consist of five members of whom three are to be appointed by
the Imperial Government, one by the President of the Executive Council of the Irish
Free State, and one by the Prime Minister
of Northern Ireland. To this body are to be
transferred all the powers vested in the Local
Government Board under the earlier Act
while the Imperial Government is placing a
sum of £1,500,000 at the disposal of the Trust
for the erection of cottages throughout the
whole of Ireland.
As there would have been objections to
giving such an independent body powers
under an Imperial Statute to acquire com.
pulsorily land within the jurisdiction of the
Northern Government the present Measure
provides that the power of compulsory acquisition of land for the provision of sites
for ex-Servicemen's cottages should be vested
in a Northern Ministry, namely, the Ministry of Home Affairs, who in turn will
transfer the lands so acquired to the new
Trust. Sub-section 7 of Section 3 of the
Irish Free State (Consequential Provisions)
Act, 1922, specifically provided that the
establishment of the Trust was dependent
upon complementary legislation enabling the
new body to acquire and hold lands being
passed by the Parliaments of Northern
Ireland and the Free State. The Measure
now under consideration is being submitted
to the Imperial Treasury for the certificate
required by the Sub-section in question. I
beg to move.
be
Moved: That the Land Trust (Powers) Bill
now read the Second time. (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
Mr. GREER: I think the noble Viscount
has made a very strong case for the passing
of this Bill, but there is one matter which I
think is of some importance to which I
should like to call attention. I suppose the
proper time to bring it before the House
would be when in Committee, but I wish at
this stage to mention the point in the hope
that perhaps the framers of the Bill will see
their way to alter it before it comes to Committee. As the noble Viscount has already
told us, the Local Government Board were
appointed by the original Act in 1919 to carry
out the object for which the Act was passed,
that is to say, to provide dwellings for the
Irish soldiers and sailors. They got the
powers of acquiring land by agreement or
compulsorily which the Local Government
Board had under the Labourers Act in Ireland, which gave them full power to acquire
land compulsorily, if it could not be acquired
by agreement. But as we have already heard,
Sub-section (d) of Section 4 confined the
powers of the Local Government Board to
three years from the passing of the Act. As
a matter of fact, these powers have now come
to an end. They expired on the 23rd of December last.
The next Act to which the noble Viscount
referred is one of which the third Section
is printed in the Schedule to this Bill. By

35 Land Trust Powers Bill.

THE SENATE.
Companies Bill.
[Mr. Greer].
that Act an Act of 1922, I think, a Trust was
established to carry out the objects for which
the original Act was passed, and the Trust
got the same powers of acquiring land compulsorily or by agreement as the Local Government Board had. The framers of this Act
apparently saw that these powers came to an
end on 23rd December, 1922, and they put in
a proviso. After giving the same powers as
the Local Government Board for Ireland had
they went on to say-
Provided that the provisions of the said
Section relating to the compulsory acquisition of land, limiting the time within which
the power to acquire land may be exercised
by the Board, and regulating the expenses
and receipts and audit of accounts of the
Board shall not apply to the Trust.
That is to say, the time limit does not apply
to the Trust constituted by the Act of 1922.
Then this Bill by Section 1, sub-Section 1,
provides that the Trust shall not have as
respects Northern Ireland any power of compulsory acquisition of land. I entirely agree
with the desirability of having that provision,
but by Sub-section 2 it gives to the Ministry
of Home Affairs for Northern Ireland all the
powers which were conferred upon the Local
Government Board for Ireland by virtue of
Section 4 of the Irish Land (Provision for
Sailors and Soldiers) Act, 1919, that is to
say, it gives them the powers of acquiring
land in the same way as the Local Government Board were enabled to do it under the
Labourers Act. But there is no Clause doing
away with Sub-section (d), which limits the
powers of the Local Government Board to
three years after the passing of the Act,
and as a matter of fact the Local Government
Board has no power
whatever of
acquiring land compulsorily. I propose,
unless the Government sees its way to meet
me in the matter, to move in Committee some
words to this effect at the end of the Clause:
now
but without the limitation of time for the
exercise of such powers prescribed by subsection (d) of said section." I think, perhaps,
upon consideration that this has been an
oversight and may be rectified, and that it
may not be necessary to mention the matter
again,
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator who
has just spoken, I venture to say that the
intention of Sub-section 2 of Clause 1 is to
describe the powers given to the Ministry
of Home Affairs, and I may say that if the
words used in that Section are non-effective
I am, on behalf of the Government, quite
prepared to bring up an amendment in the
Committee stage which I think will satisfy
the hon. Member and this House.
Mr. GREER: I am quite satisfied.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Companies (Reconstitution of
Records) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD :
The object of this Bill is to remedy as far
as possible the injury caused by the destruction by fire of the files of companies
belonging to Northern Ireland in the Companies Registration Office at the Custom
House, Dublin, on 25th May, 1921. As all
indexes of registered companies and all
records of the situation of their registered
offices were destroyed at the same time it has
been a matter of considerable difficulty to
identify the companies that are proper to
Northern Ireland, but up to the present
1,300 have been identified. I may here say
that the test of a Northern Ireland Company
is that its registered office at 31st December,
1921, was situated within the Six Counties
of Northern Ireland. It will be observed
that in Clause 1 the Bill lays down that
this Act shall apply to every company other
than the companies named in the First
Schedule of this Act. The reason for that
is that the files of the three companies
named in the First Schedule to this Act
were saved because they were with the
Board of Trade in London at the time of
the fire.
I may mention to the House that the file
of a company contains its constitution, that
is to say, memorandum and articles of
association, duplicate certificate of incorporation; copies of all special resolutions,
original contracts for the payment of shares
otherwise than in cash; orders of court
altering the objects of the company or reorganising its capital, register of directors,
returns of allotments, notice of situation of
registered office; annual returns and also
particulars of mortgages, debentures, and
charges registered and duplicate certificates
of registration thereof.
The problem of reconstituting or recon.
structing the companies' records in the
Registry Office and giving legal validity to
them is no light matter-(hon. Members:
Hear, hear) and is one that has received
very careful consideration on the part of
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of Commerce. It might have been dealt with either
by requiring the re-incorporation of the
companies or by requiring the complete
reconstruction of the files as they existed
prior to the fire, or by a limited reconstruction requiring copies of all documents and
instruments containing or evidencing the
company's constitution and rules together
with a summary that would disclose the
Second Reading.
Companies Bill.
21 MARCH 1923.

te

is

ON

-0
other material particulars of the company's
transactions from the standpoint of the
company's registration up to 25th May, 1921.
The latter course has been adopted by
the Ministry of Commerce and is the
method embodied in the Bill. It was realised that the task of rebuilding the whole
of the records that were destroyed would
have been altogether too onerous inasmuch
as it would have involved in some cases
compiling annual returns of shareholders for
upwards of forty years back. The course
that has been adopted by the Ministry of
Commerce and is embodied in this Bill is,
I may call it, a limited reconstruction.
The Bill requires a statement in the form
prescribed in the Second Schedule, to be
furnished to the registrar of companies at
the Ministry of Commerce, and also copies
of the company's memorandum and articles
of association, and other instruments which
contain the company's constitution, and
upon the registration of these documents the
Registrar will issue a certificate, which will
evidence that the company has complied
with the requirement of this Act, and that
the company was in fact incorporated under
the Companies Act. The particulars diled
with the Registrar are, by the provisions of
this Bill, to be accepted for all purposes as
being the constitution, regulations, or records, as the case may require, of the
company, and shall be received as such in
evidence in all Courts, but provision is made
in this Bill for the Court to order a rectification of the register upon proof that any
document filed by the company in pursuance
of this Act is false, misleading, or inaccurate
in any material respect.
to
a
Although this schedule may appear to
hon. Members to be
somewhat lengthy
one, I venture
say that in most
cases officials of the companies concerned
will not really have much difficulty in complying with the provisions of the Act. It is
very regrettable that the records should have
been destroyed, but at the time of the fire
they were not in the custody of the Govern.
ment of Northern Ireland, and no records
were in fact transferred to Northern Ireland
until 1st January, 1922, when the Ministry
of Commerce took over the duties. No action
was taken by the Board of Trade in London
as the responsible authority up to 31st December, 1921, towards reconstructing the
records, consequently the matter now falls
to be dealt with by the Government of
Northern Ireland. The proposals contained
in this Bill are, I believe, the simplest and
the safest that can be devised in the interests of the companies and in the interests of
their shareholders and creditors, and tall
other interested parties. I therefore beg to
move the Second reading of this Bill.
Mr. GREER: This Bill is really brought
forward in the interests of the companies
who are placed in a very perilous position
by reason of the destruction of their records. It will relieve the minds of the
directors of these companies immensely to
see how well the difficulty has been met by
the present Bill. In fact it would scarcely
have been possible to carry on properly
without having some measure of this sort
passed. The problem is a very difficult one
to deal with, and I think the framers of the
Bill deserve very great credit for the plan
that they have devised. I hope it will pass
without any question.
There is just one point I should like to
refer to arising under Clause 5. That is the
penalty clause. The Bill provides that the
companies are to furnish certain particulars
in regard to the companies, and Section 5
provides that-
"if the Company fails to furnish these
particulars within one month after requi.
sition in pursuance of this Act, or in the
absence of such requisition not later than
31st March, 1924, any statement or copy of
a
document required to be furnished to
him under this Act, or fails, for a period
of one month after the Registrar has
required the production of the certificate
of incorporation of the company, to produce such certificate, the company and
every director and manager thereof shall
be liable on summary conviction to a fine
not exceeding one pound for every day
during which the default continues.
a
My point with regard to that section is
that it is rather too drastic. If the company
fails to do these things the company and
every director and manager is under
penalty of a pound a day. Now, some of
these documents may be mislaid, may be
burned, or may be lost in some way or other.
It may not be possible
produce
the certificate of incorporation. It is quite
a small document, just a sheet of paper, and
it may very easily be mislaid and may not
be found within the time-limit of the Section,
to
Moreover it is quite possible that some of
the directors of the company may be enjoying
themselves in some distant part of the world
where the postal arrangements may not be very
satisfactory, and they may not know anything
about them until they return home and find
that they have been accumulating a bill of
a pound a day for a considerable time without
knowing anything about it. I would suggest
that the words "without reasonable excuse"
be inserted after the word "if" in the first
line of the Clause--" If without reasonable
excuse the company fails to furnish." And
then lower down, where it says "fails for
a period of one month" to produce the
certificate of incorporation, I would suggest
(
Companies Bill-
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Greer].
that the words should read "without reasonable excuse on the part of the company or
any director or manager thereof fails for a
period of one month after the registrar "-
That would leave it open for the director or
manager or company to make the case, when
the proceedings were taken against them for
the recovery of the penalty, that it was impossible to do it, that they were not able
to do it, that they were away, and that they
did not know anything about it.
I think the ends of justice could be met
by having these words inserted. I do not
know whether the Government is prepared
to meet me on this point, but if not I intend
to raise it in Committee. I can give notice,
but perhaps the Government will save me
the trouble.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we have all listened
with satisfaction and gratification to the account given by the noble Viscount of the
method which the Government proposes to
adopt in order to relieve companies in Northern Ireland from difficulties which were not
of their own making or of the making of the
Government of Northern Ireland. The Bill,
I am sure, will pass with great rapidity and
without any serious protest. There is only
one question I should like to ask. I ask it
because I am entirely unacquainted with any
precedent for a Bill of this sort, and, there
fore, one does not know where to look to
ascertain how the difficulties have been dealt
with. The question I want to ask is whether
it is intended to provide any appeal from
the decision of the registrar. We find in
sub-Section 4 of Clause 1 that the registrar
has power to dispense with the furnishing
of a copy of any document which it is impossible or impracticable for the company to
furnish. The question is whether there ought
not to be provision for appeal in the event
of the registrar and the company failing to
agree.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator, Mr.
Greer, in regard to Clause 5, I am prepared
to say on behalf of the Government that they
will consider the hon. Member's suggestion.
I think the hon. Member rather gives me
to understand that he is under the impression
that the company will not be given ample
time in which to furnish the documents they
are required to furnish. I can assure the
hon. Member that ample opportunity will
be given to the companies to furnish what
documents they are required to do prior to
any penalty being inflicted. However. I will
represent the matter to the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Commerce, and
see whether we cannot meet him in some
way at the Committee stage.
As regards the question asked by the hon.
Senator, Mr. Leslie, I cannot hold out any
hope that the Government will consider the
question of appeal.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read the Second time) agreed to.
Bill read the Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the whole House
on Tuesday next.

until to-morrow. To-morrow there will be

will have the remaining stages of the Bills

to move.

THE SENATE

Thursday, 22nd March, 1923.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
CARBON MONOXIDE GAS.
Moved: "That the Draft Special Order
proposed to be made by the Ministry of
Commerce for Northern Ireland under Subsection (4) of Section 2 of the Gas Regulation
Act. 1920 (10 and 11 Geo. 5, c. 28), limiting
the proportion of Carbon Monoxide which
may be supplied in Gas used for domestic
purposes, and which was presented to the
Senate on the 28th day of February, 1923, is
hereby approved." (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
DUNMURRY ELECTRICITY ORDER.
Moved: "That the Dunmurry and District
Draft Special Order, 1923, made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern Ire
land under the Electricity (Supply) Acts,
1882 to 1919, and confirmed by the Ministry
of Commerce under the Electricity (Supply)
Act, 1919. in respect of the Townlands of
Dunmurry, Kilmakee, Killeaton and Oldforge,
in the County of Antrim, and which was
presented to the Senate on the 15th day of
March, 1923, is hereby approved." (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.

(Leader of the House): I beg to move that
this House do now adjourn until Tuesday of
next week.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Belfast Water Bill.
Order for Second Reading Read.
Mr. GREER: I consider that Clause 10 of
this Bill is rather too wide. This is a Bill
giving the Commissioners power to borrow
on deposit and by issue of Bills and Notes,
and there is no provision in the Bill for
keeping a register of the amount so borrowed, although the total amount of the
borrowings must not exceed at any time the
total amount authorised to be borrowed by
the Belfast Water Commissioners. I merely
wish to call attention to the matter in the
meantime. I have reason to believe that my
difficulty will probably be met when the Bill
comes into Committee, and that when it
reaches the Report stage here I will be satisfied with the Amendment that will be made.
I merely mention this.
Mr. DUFFIN: I quite agree with the hon.
Senator, Mr. Greer, that there will have to
be at some stage some limitation of the
powers that are given for borrowing. One
has seen a great deal of late of temporary
borrowing not only by industrial and commercial companies under the Limited Companies Act, but also by public boards, which
owing to the very high rate of money, think
it undesirable to pledge their future by getting permanent loans on the terms on which
money can be borrowed. But in addition to
the absence of a provision for registration,
I think also something has to be done to
ensure that they are public loans that they
are open to the public. I do not think it is
desirable that any public body should be in
a position to make bargains with individuals,
either as regards terms or security. I think
all should be public and open to the public
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS
The Commons have passed the Inspectors
of Weights and Measures Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of this House.
INSPECTORS OF WEIGHTS AND
MEASURES BILL.
Brought from the Commons, read the First
time, and ordered to be read the Second
time to-morrow. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
Canals (Continuance of Charging
Powers) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move the
Second reading of the Canals (Continuance of
Charging Powers) Bill. This Bill applies to
such of our canals and inland navigation
undertakings as were taken over by the Minister of Transport under the provisions of
the Ministry of Transport Act, 1919. By that
Act the Minister of Transport was empowered
to take over the waterways and raise the
charges for their use in order to meet the
enormous increase in every direction of the
expenses of maintenance.
The Minister accordingly took over the
Newry Canal, the Lagan Canal, and the
Ulster and Coalisland Canals which are
united with the Lagan Canal, and ordered an
increase of 150 per cent. in their tolls. An
order of this nature under paragraph (e) of
Sub-section (1) of Section 3 of the Act was
to remain in force for a period of 18 months
after the expiration of the period for which
the Minister of Transport should retain
possession of the undertaking. Therefore,
although the control of the Minister of Com-
45 Companies Records Bill-
27 MARCH 1923.
Committee.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.)
merce ended in August, 1921, these charges
have been continued for the prescribed
period which has now expired. It expired a
few weeks ago
A fresh Act of Parlament is, therefore,
necessary in order to legalise a continuance
of such increased charges by the Canal Companies who are now operating their own
undertakings and, accordingly, in Great
Britain the Imperial Parliament has already
passed a measure of the same nature as the
one which I am asking the House to sancton
to-day.
As hon. Members are aware, we have by
no means yet returned to the old scales of
running expenditure for such undertakings
which obtained before the War, and though
the cost of upkeep, in common with most
other forms of expenditure, is fortunately
decreasing, it still remains necessary, if the
canals are to be self-supporting business propositions to maintain for them the power to
levy increased charges over and above those
that were fixed before the period of the War.
That figure has been as high as 150 per cent.
It will be noted that the Bill limits any
increase in these charges by fixing as a maximum those charges which Companies were
entitled to make on the 15th of February of
this year, and provision is made for the
movement upward or downward within this
limit of these charges by order of the Ministry of Commerce. The expenses of any inquiry under the Act will be borne, not by
the Ministry, but by the parties for whose
benefit presumably the inquiry has been held.
Power to continue these increased charges is
given by Sub-section (1) of Section 1 of the
Bill, which amends the Transport Act of
1919 in such a way as to extend the period
laid down by that Act until "the 15th day
of February, 1925." That is to say, we contemplate the continuation of the exercise of
these powers for a further two years. I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read the Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the whole
House-the House to be in Committee tomorrow.
Companies (Reconstitution of Records)

Clause 1 (Duty of certain Companies to furnish copies of documents). Clause 2 (Registration of copies of documents and issue of
certificate). Clause 3 (Provisions as to documents filed under the Act). Clause 4 (Saving
with respect to production of destroyed documents). Ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Clause 5.-(1) If the Company fails to furnish to the Registrar, within one month after
requisition in pursuance of this Act, or, in
absence of such requisition, not later than

hundred and twenty-four, any statement or

the default continues, either after the thirtyfirst day of March, nineteen hundred and
twenty-four, or after the expiration of one
month from the date of the Registrar's requisition (as the case may require).
Mr. GREER: I gave notice that I would
move an amendment to this Clause because
I thought it was of rather too drastic a
nature. Circumstances might arise when it
would be impossible for the company or the
director or manager to give the information
that was required, and the opportunity should
be given to him to show that he was unable
to produce this information. But I suggested
that perhaps the framers of the Bill might
meet me on this point and that it might not
be necessary to move the amendment. If the
Government is prepared to insert any words
that would meet my point I would not proceed with my amendment. I beg to move.
Amendment moved: Clause 5, page 4, line
7, after "if" ("If the Company") insert
"without reasonable excuse." (Mr. Greer).
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The amendment which the hon Member has
moved occurs in Clause 5, page 4, line 7.
His remaining amendments are consequential
to the first one. I can only say that I have
gone very carefully into this matter-the hon.
Gentleman has raised this point before and
I am advised by my right hon. Friend the
Minister of Commerce that he is unable to
accept the hon. Member's amendment in the
form in which he has moved it, but I am
authorised by him to go some way towards
Exchequer Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
meeting him. If the hon. Senator will agree
to it, I will introduce the followng amendments to this Clause upon the Third Reading:
Lines 15 and 16: Delete the words "and
every director and manager thereof";
Line 22: Add the words "and every director and manager of the Company who
knowingly and wilfully authorises or permits the default shall be liable to the like
penalty."
These alterations would bring the penal
provisions of the Clause into conformity with
the general penal provisions of the Companies
Acts; see for example Section 26 (5) and
Section 70 of the Companies (Consolidation)
Act, 1908, in both of which the words "every
director and manager of the Company who
knowingly and wilfully authorises or permits
the default" appear.
In practice it is not anticipated that any
prosecutions will be undertaken before April,
1924, as the Registrar's staff will no doubt
be fully occupied with the Returns that will
be furnished without the compulsion of a
prosecution.
I hope these words will meet
the hon. Gentleman's wishes and I will insert
them when the Bill comes up for Third
Reading.
Mr. GREER: I am quite prepared to accept the words as suggested by the Leader
of the House. In fact, I consider them
much better than my own, and they meet the
point I raised on the Section. I am glad I
will not have to press the matter. I therefore beg leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move the Second Reading of the
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill.
This Bill, as its title denotes, is almost entirely a money Bill, and though it is a very
important Measure I will not detain the
House with a detailed exposition of its provisions, inasmuch as they are so clearly expounded in the Memorandum to this Bill,
which has been issued as a White Paper, and
which I have in my hand at the present
moment. It gives the object of the Bill,
which is summarised under six heads. I think
hon. Members will find the details in the
White Paper are very comprehensive. Indeed
they are better explained than I could hope
to do to the House.
I am sure that hon. Members will agree
with me in congratulating my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, upon giving
this further evidence of his determination to
be guided by the principles of sound rather
than showy finance in the provisions which
he is making in this Bill for the establishment of Sinking Funds to perpetuate the revenues from Land Purchase and Church Temporalities, Annuities, and to create a Capital
Fund.
My right hon. Friend, like the prudent
financier he is, has decided-and I am sure
that in doing so he is following the wishes
of this House, and also of the other Houseto forego annually a certain amount of income from temporary investments in order to
make what would otherwise be a purely
transient source of State revenue into a permanent provision for the Exchequer. I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read the Second time accordingly, and
committed to
Committee of the whole
House the House to be in Committee tomorrow.
a

until to-morrow." (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Four of the Clock
until to-morrow at
quarter
after Three of the Clock.

of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
BELFAST CORPORATION BILL.

Second time. (Mr. Speaker).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a joint opposed Committee.

CONSOLIDATED FUND AND APPRO-
PRIATION BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the

On Question. Motion agreed to.

PROMISSORY OATHS BILL.
Order for receiving report of amendments

On Question, Motion agreed to,
Amendments were accordingly reported and

Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.
Criminal Law Amendment Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This Bill, the Criminal Law Amendment Bill,
Northern Ireland, is practically identical
with the Criminal Law Amendment Act
passed in the Imperial Parliament last year,
and for that reason, if for no other, it seems
desirable that we should pass a measure substantially similar, in order that the law here
may keep pace as far as possible with the law
on the other side of the water. But we have
a stronger reason than that for promoting
this Bill, inasmuch as its adoption has been
urged upon us in the interest of the younger
generation by many influential bodies, and I
do not anticipate that hon. Members will
oppose the principle of the measure, though I
have no doubt they may have criticisms to
make upon the details which are involved.
The first provision deals with the age of
consent for indecent assault, and raises it
from 13 to 16, thus making it identical with
the age of consent for carnal knowledge.
The second provision strengthens the law
in cases of unlawful carnal knowledge, by the
abolition of the defence of reasonable belief
on the part of the accused that the girl was
over the age of consent. In this Section 2 we
have departed from the English Act, which
adds a proviso affording the defence of reasonable belief on a first charge of this nature
against a man or young person of 23 or under.
The insertion of this proviso was apparently
the result of a compromise in the British
House of Commons, and my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs, is
advised that it may give rise to considerable
legal difficulties. For example, if a man
under the age of 23 be charged with this
offence and be debarred from pleading this
reasonable belief in his defence, it is quite
palpable to the jury that he is not a first
offender, and thus one of the main principles
of our Criminal Law procedure is violated,
and the prisoner's record is disclosed to the
jury before they have found their verdict.
In any case, we can see no valid grounds for
depriving a man a few days over 23 of a de
fence which may be available for a man a few
days under it. There appears to be no
particular virtue, either by nature or custom,
in the attainment of the age of 23. We have,
therefore, departed from the British Act and
have omitted this proviso. This Section also
extends the limit of time for prosecutions in
this class of offences to 9 months instead of 6
months only.
The provision increasing penalties for the
most detestable and despicable crime of
brothel-keeping will, I feel sure, meet with
no opposition here.
Hon. Members will notice that the Bill,
as amended in another place, contains no provision for the repeal of that Section of the
Incest Act, which requires that such cases
shall be tried in camera, such as the parallel
Act contains. This has been omitted by the
Government without any regret and in
D
Criminal Law Act-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
deference to the general opinion against the
repeal of the Section in question, expressed
during the Debate on the Bill in another
place. I beg to move
pass
Mr. LESLIE: The House is indebted to the
noble Marquess for the clarity of his exposition of a Bill dealing with a difficult and
disagreeable subject.
The Bill is certainly
one which may be expected to
this
House without difficulty, both, as the noble
Marquess mentioned, on account of the universal sympathy which everybody feels with the
object at which it aims and because of
the general desire-in this case it is practically a necessity to keep the law of Northern
Ireland in accordance, in matters of principle, with that of the United Kingdom.
were
necessary.
I must confess to some misgivings as to
whether the alterations in the law which
will be introduced by Section 1, and by the
second part of Section 2 regarding the time
limit, will be found to work efficiently and
equitably. I am not going to elaborate these
misgivings. Similar views were elaborated at
very great length across the water. I am
afraid I must confess that I think some of
the suggestions made
positively fantastic. I am not going to take up the time
of the House by referring to the possible
result of osculatory occurrences in railway carriages. I think all that has already been
done across the water. But I have some slight
misgivings. I am not at all afraid and I have
endeavoured to study the Bill-of any wrongful
convictions under any of its provisions.
There is nothing anywhere to take away the
discretion of the judge in inflicting the punishment which seems to him
There is nothing to take away from the power
of the Jury to acquit, if they feel so disposed.
I do not in any way regret the disappearance
of the particular kind of defence against one
particular charge. That defence was always
an unchivalrous one and I think it was very
often a fraudulent one, that is to say I think
many of the offenders never thought of the
defence when they committed the
offence itself, but it was afterwards
put into their heads. I am not in the least
afraid of the disappearance of that defence.
And as regards the suggestion of the privilege attaching to the age under 23, which is
in the English Act, I appreciate and humbly
applaud the decision of our own Government in
making the alteration. I do not see that the
special provision for a young man under 23
would have been of any practical service. My
hesitation as regards the two Sub-sections
particularly to which I have referred, arises
rather from some fear of the possibility that
in seeking to close one door we may be doing
something to open another.
As regards Section I, the question of a
time limit for prosecutions might perhaps be
considered, and I commend that line of
thought to the Government. But in doing 60,
I recognise-I fully recognise the very grave
difficulties in the way. It would be introducing
a differentiation from, instead of an assimilation to, the law of England. It would be
contrary to the general principle of our
criminal law, that there is no time limit for
offences, and I am not at all confident that
the balance of advantage is on the side of my
suggestion. I have a sort of thought that it
might possibly be of assistance in preventing
undesirable charges of the kind commonly
called blackmail. The revised time limit in
石
T

Section 2 for prosecution for the specific
offence named, owes its origin to a compromise arrived at at Westminster. I do not
think that it should be attacked to-day.
Originally, as the House will remember, the
time limit was three months. That was
under the old Act of 1885. It was extended to
six months under the Act of 1904. It is now
proposed to make it nine months. I do not
think it would be well to attack that provision to-day.
die
The noble Marquess alluded to the omission
from this Bill of the Clause which was originally inserted
repealing the provision
requiring
certain class of case to
a
be heard in camera. As the clause has not
come to us it is not necessary to discuss it
in detail. I will only say that if the Government had decided to retain that clause in the
Bill, out of deference to the views expressed
by His Majesty's Judges I would have supported the Government.
a
As regards the whole subject of offences
against women and girls I do not know of
any statistics that are available, but I am
going to ask the House, through you, Mr.
Speaker, my Lord, to listen to the recent
record of the County of Antrim and of the
whole of the Six Counties. At the last
Assize for the County of Antrim, with
population, according to the census of 1911,
of about 194,000 people, there were no cases
at all of offences against women or girls; at
the last Ballymena Quarter Sessions there was
one such case. At last Summer Assize for
the County of Antrim there were two cases;
in one of them it transpired that the parties
had since married, and the case did not go
on; the other was held over from the Spring
Assizes. At the Assize for the County of
Ulster Winter Assize-a fictitious county embracing for that purpose the whole Six
Counties, exclusive, of course, of the City of
Belfast, which was held last January and which
was a general jail delivery covering a period of
seven months, there were 129 bills for various
criminal offences sent before the Grand Jury.
Of these 129 bills only three dealt with offences against women. One case was
remarkable owing to the fact that the

I
There are two small matters of procedure,
which should perhaps be discussed in Committee. The first is whether we ought to take
this opportunity of bringing the law of Northern Ireland into conformity with that of
England, by specifically providing that all
misdemeanours under the Principal Act
should be tried at the Assizes. The old
Act of 1885 makes that provision as regards
ert England, but excludes Ireland. It is a point
on which a good deal could be said on the
contrary side. It is a matter for consideration. The second point is whether this is
the time to deal with the provision in Section
20 of the Principal Act, a provision which, it
will be remembered, gives power for the husband or wife to give evidence. That provision
as it stands apparently prevents the husband
or wife of the person charged from giving
evidence for the prosecution before grand
jury, but permits, though it does not compel,
them to give evidence in the case before the
magistrates and at the trial. That seems to
be an anomaly and I think it is an anomly
which ought to be cleared up in connection
with this Bill rather than with the other
more attractive measure. At all events we
need not spend time this afternoon in dealing
with these questions.
at

OL
ra
I sincerely hope that this Bill when
- it becomes an Act will prove to be not only
beneficial to the State, but also a help and
encouragement to those people specially interested in its success-I mean those ladies and
gentlemen who have, many of them for so long
a time, laboured so earnestly for the truest
19 welfare of so many of our younger citizens.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
congratulate the hon. Gentleman on his very
able and interesting speech on the Second
reading of this Bill. There is only one point
I should like to make, and that is that there
is no bar of time, as a rule, in criminal prosecutions, and there is none for indecent assault.
If a time limit is imposed in this Section to
which the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) is referring, the result will be that there will be a
e time limit for indecent assault on a person
under the age of 16, but there will be no time
limit where the assaulted person is over 16.
id I think that is the point the hon. Gentleman
wanted to make. The other point to which
he referred I think is dealt with in Section 20.
I have the Section before me:
Every person charged with an offence
under this Act or under Section 48 or Sections 52 to 55 of the Offences Against
Persons Act, 1861, or any of such Sections,
the husband or wife of the person so
charged shall be a competent but not a compellable witness on every hearing and every
stage of such charge except inquiry before
the Grand Jury.
I do not know exactly what point the hon.
Gentleman wanted to make on that Section
or if there is anything on which I can
enlighten him.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I may explain. My
difficulty is exactly what the Act says. The
Section prohibits evidence being given before
the Grand Jury, but it allows evidence to be
given at every other stage of the prosecution.
That seems to me to be an anomaly. I think
we should clear it up either on this Bill or
perhaps on the other. That is the difficulty.
I am much obliged to the noble Marquess.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
With the indulgence of the House I may perhaps say that his point is more a Committee
point than a point for Second reading. I am
not sure that I am not deserving of your
censure for embarking on a Committee point
in this way.
On Question, Motion (That this Bill be now
read the Second time) agreed to.

Bill read the Second time accordingly, and
committed to
Committee of the whole
House the House to be in Committee on
Tuesday, the 24th of April next.
Criminal Evidence Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
This is a Bill, as hon. Members are aware, for
assimilating the law in Northern Ireland to
the law as it exists in England in regard to
the competency of an accused person and the
husband and wife of an accused person as
witnesses upon trials for criminal offences.
This is a measure in which the hon. Member
who sits opposite me (Mr. Leslie) is taking a
great interest, and I hope the form in which
it now stands will meet with his approbation
and assent.
As the law at present stands, with very few
exceptions, an accused person is not a competent witness to give evidence on a criminal
charge in his or her own defence. Neither is
the husband or wife of such accused
person a competent witness either for the
prosecution or defence. This is the general
rule to which there are a few statutory exceptions. Some of these exceptions are contained in the Statutes which I have before
me, but which I do not feel there is any
need for me to go through for the benefit of
the house. In all these instances-the
Criminal Evidence Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
Licensing Act, the Food and Drugs Act, Section 156 of the Army Act, -in all these instances the defendant or the husband or wife
of the defendant is made a competent witness
for the defence,
The state of the law in England prior to
the year 1898 was the same as in Ireland,
but in 1898 the Criminal Evidence Act was
passed for England and Scotland but, chiefly
owing to the efforts of Nationalist representatives at Westminster for reasons which
there is no need for me to go into at present,
it does not extend to Ireland. The principal
provision of that Act is to make the person
accused of an offence, and the husband
wife, as the case may be, of such person a
competent witness for the defence at every
stage of proceedings in a criminal charge,
whether such person be charged solely or
jointly with any other person or persons.
While this Statute makes the accused person,
and the husband or wife as the case may be,
of the accused person, a competent witness
for the defence, they are not compellablethat is, the person charged cannot be called
as a witness except on his own application.
If the person so accused does not avail himself of the power to give evidence on his own
behalf his so declining to give evidence cannot
be made a matter of comment by the prosecution. The husband or wife of the accused
cannot be called as a witness for the defence
except on the application of the accused.
That is Section 1c.
The foregoing refers only to the competency
of the accused or the husband or wife of the
accused as a witness for the defence, but in
certain other charges mentioned in the Schedule to the Criminal Evidence Act, 1898, the
husband or wife of a person charged might
be called as witness either for the
prosecution or defence without the consent
of the person charged.
course
a
The present Bill contains similar provisions in all respects and almost in the
identical words of the British Statute above
mentioned. The only variations between this
Bill and the English Statute are certain
slight verbal changes rendered necessary
by the
of legislation since the
Act of 1898 was passed. The principal of these
is to be found in Sub-section (3) of Section 5
of the Bill providing that reference in this
Bill to any Acts of the Parliament of the
United Kingdom shall be construed as references to those Acts as they now apply to Northern Ireland under and subject to the provisions of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920,
and any enactments amending the same or
any Order of His Majesty in Council in pursuance of the last-mentioned Act. This modification became necessary in consequence of
the effect of recent legislation upon the Criminal Evidence Act of '98 in relation to Northern Ireland. The Bill differs in no essential or important particular from the Statute
of '98 except where such verbal alterations
were rendered necessary by the course of subsequent legislation.
The general effect of the new Bill is substantially to make the law as to the compe
tency of certain witnesses the same in criminal cases as it has always been in Civil
actions. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I ought to apologise both to
you, Mr. Speaker, and to the House, for inflicting myself upon you twice in so short a
space of time, but on this occasion it is not
only a pleasure, but a privilege and honour
to speak in support of a Bill of such vast
importance, and on a subject in which I can
claim to have taken a good deal of personal
interest; indeed, I remember appealing on a
former occasion for something in the nature of
a crumb in this connection, and I now enjoy
the satisfaction which a man feels when he
has been presented with the free run of a confectionery establishment. Speaking more
seriously, this Bill is really of an epochmaking description. It removes one of the
reproaches against our criminal system which
was insisted on by Bentham more than a
century ago. It is taking another step in the
direction of removing that exclusionary
character of our evidence which was so unfavourably commented upon. It is providing
for a distinction between competency and
credibility, and finally it is a recognition of
the great principle that innocence claims the
right of speech in the witness-box with
exactly as much force, and perhaps with more
justice, than guilt claims the privilege of
silence. That is really the broad principle
underlying the Bill.
a
All the great legal names of the past are
behind this Bill-Bramwell, Herschell, James,
Webster, and Halsbury. You have Lord
Brampton, better known as Sir Henry
Hawkins, at first an opponent of it, but completely converted after the trial of a criminal
case of some difficulty, and you have distinguished advocates like Sir Henry Poland.
It took fully a quarter of
century
to bring about the reform in England.
The first Bill apparently dealing with
it, or rather introducing the new principle, was an Act passed in 1872 relating
to the better regulation of Metalliferous Mines
and there was also the Licensing Bill of the
same year. Between those Bills, and 1898
there were, according to a list to which I have
obtained access, no fewer than 26 Statutes of
every conceivable variety in which the principle was adopted. For twenty-five years we
have had to wait for this Bill, but I rejoice
that we are going to have it now. As a consequence. I think we should get fewer
disagreements amongst our juries and in the
lesser matter of procedure at Petty Sessions
57 Criminal Evidence Bill-

28 MARCH 1923.
Second Reading.
I think we shall find there will be fewer crosssummonses, and therefore a considerable
saving of time.
Passing to the Bill itself, the first observation that occurs to me in regard to Subsection H, Clause 1, a clause which necessarily
substitutes a reference to the Petty Sessions
(Ireland) Act of 1848 for the Indictable Offences
Act which does not apply to Ireland. I hope
that some day in the not very distant future
the amendment of the Petty Sessions Act
itself will be undertaken. It is already
septuagenarian.
It contains provision for
cautioning a person, in which the words used
have to be :
Anything you do say will be taken down
in writing and may be given in evidence
against you on your trial."
The point cannot be discussed to-day, but
I think modern thought and the spirit of
this Bill would be in favour of leaving out
the final words in that sentence. We cannot
do it in this Bill, but it will come up when
the Petty Sessions Act has to be considered.
The next point which is of a little more importance, occurs in Section 4, which, as the
noble Marquess says, follows practically
identically the wording of the English Act.
The English Act was passed in 1898. It is 25
years old, and after it had been passed it
appears from the records, I am quoting Halisbury's encyclopædia of the Laws of England
-that the purport of this Section was at first
misunderstood. It was thought that the
words may be called as a witness" meant
that the person concerned could compulsorarily be called as a witness and required to
give evidence. The law of England as it
stands now, as appears from the supplement
to Halsbury and Archbald's Criminal Practice
of last year is perfectly established. A husband
or wife is a competent but not a compellable
witness, and, greatly daring, I am inclined
at a later stage to move to substitute those
words for "may be called," because I think
we ought, as I know we wish, to do everything
in our power, when a new Statute of this importance is being introduced, to make it perfectly easy of interpretation for all those into
whose hands it may come.
"
There is a further reason for the change I
propose, and it is to be found in the next Subsection dealing with the Common Law. The
same phrase, "may be called," is used, and on
the same authorities I have quoted before it is
well known that in cases to which the Common Law applies the law is that such evidence is compulsory, and I think we ought
not, if we can help it, to use the same phrase
in a different sense in two successive sections.
The provision that the Act does not come
into operation for two months follows the
English Act, and I think everyone will agree
that it is a very wise provision on the part
of the Government, because it gives time for
the fullest consideration. There may, perhaps, be little points arising later on in the
Schedule, but that does not matter now. The
Bill as a whole is, I venture to think, one
of the finest, if not the finest and I think
it is the finest that our comparatively new
Government has presented to us, and I should
like to humbly congratulate the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Home Affairs
and the noble Marquess, the Leader of the
House, for having introduced SO liberalminded a measure in our Parliament.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Inspectors of Weights and
Measures Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move the Second Reading of the
Inspectors of Weights and Measures Bill.
This Bill is designed to meet an emergency,
and its operations will only extend for the
limited period of the emergency. It is not
my desire to detain the House, but I will, as
briefly as I can, explain the provisions of
the Bill. The history of the measure shortly
is. that the duties of Inspectors under the
Weights and Measures Act of 1878 were performed in the past by members of the Royal
Irish Constabuary who qualified as Inspectors
by passing an examination conducted by the
Board of Trade and confined solely to members of the Force. We had expected that
when we formed our Royal Ulster Constabulary we should find that we had a sufficient
number of men qualified by examination to
conduct the necessary inspections of weights
and measures in the Six Counties. This, howover, has not proved to be the case, and we
are faced with the necessity of setting up a
course for training for the Board of Trade examination. Meanwhile, we are taking powers
by this measure to supply the tem.
porary deficiency by short-time appointments.
Hon. Members will observe that the operation of the Bill is retrospective to the 1st of
June of last year. The House will understand
that it is only the force of circumstances
that has compelled my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Home Affairs, with the consent
of the Government, to make appointments
before he has been empowered by Parliament
to do so. Owing to the war the inspection of
weights and measures had been neglected and
trade would have been considerably hampered had it not been for my right
hon. Friend's action in making these appointments forthwith as soon as he found that his
recruits to the Constabulary did not contain
a sufficient number of men qualified to inspect, and of such a rank that their employ-
Canals Bill.
Exchequer Bill.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
ment in the capacity of Inspectors of Weights
and Measures would be in the interests of
economy. It is obvious that to employ a
sergeant or head-constable who enjoys a
higher rate of pay than these inspectors command, would be the reverse of good policy,
and the reverse of the policy of economy
which we are all so desirous of pursuing.
I may inform the House that my right hon.
Friend has made 17 appointments, and that
the rate of pay is £3 a week with travelling
expenses. I feel sure that the House generally will commend my right hon. Friend's
courageous assumption of responsibility and
endorse his action by giving a Second reading to this measure. I beg to move.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
desire to move to leave out lines 25 to 28
inclusive, namely,
"The expression 'transferred civil servants' means persons who are either transferred Irish officers or specially appointed
officers within the meaning of the Superannuation Act, 1921."
These lines, which are a definition of the
expression "transferred civil servants," are
no longer necessary as an amendment in
another place removed the expression therein
defined from the Bill. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: Are the numbers of the lines
quoted quite right? In my copy of the Bill
the lines in question are 18 to 22. There
seems to be some difference in the copies.
The CHAIRMAN: I am afraid the wrong
copies of the Bill have been placed on the
table. They are prints of the Bill as introduced in the Commons, and the Bill in which
the lines are numbered 25 to 28 is the copy
as printed after it has been subject to the
Commons amendments. I think the best way
out of the difficulty would be for me to read
out the four lines proposed to be left out.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think that will meet the case.
