LOCAL GOVERNMENT FRANCHISE BILL
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill was introduced by the
Government of Northern Ireland as the result of repeated representations made to my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, regarding the urgent necessity for
some legislation to remedy abuses in connection with the Local Government
Franchise. Hon. Members are aware that
under the existing law the Parliamentary
and Local Government Franchise is
governed by the Representation of the People
Act, 1918. The Government of Northern Ireland are precluded, under the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, from altering the law
regarding the Parliamentary Franchise until
three years have elapsed from the date of the
setting up of their Parliament, but there is
no restriction on the Parliament in regard
to remedying defects in relation to Local
Government franchise.
Under the Act that I have quoted above
occupancy of land or premises in a Local
Government electoral area by a man entitles
him to the Local Government franchise
irrespective of the character or value of the
land in question. The result has been that
a considerable number of persons have been
admitted to the Local Government franchise
whose claim to the occupancy of land or premises arose out of the occupation of land of
practically no value at all. I may mention
that numerous cases of this kind have been
brought to the notice of the Government.
No doubt Members of this House are themselves acquainted with many such cases, so
I will not weary the House with a recital of
them. I think it will be sufficient if I
merely mention a few glaring examples. For
instance, in one urban district it was
established that the local Council had let
potato patches to a considerable number of
people; and on the ground of this occupancy
the people in question were admitted to the
franchise. In the same district stalls in the
fair ground were let to various persons which
qualified them to vote at Local Government
elections. Another instance was brought to
the notice of the Ministry of Home Affairs
141 Loans Guarantee Bill.

29 MAY 1923
Adjournment.

in which over 50 votes were obtained out of
small bog plots of practically negligible
value, the owners of these bog plots actually
residing and having votes in other areas.
Votes have been manufactured wholesale;
fictitious lettings have been effected in order
to create what I may call these bogus votes.
The Bill which is now before the House
purports to remedy these abuses in that it
prescribes for the Local Government vote
that the person claiming the vote must
be in actual occupation of land of the yearly
value of not less than £5. This Bill does
not in any way affect the Parliamentary
franchise, but at any rate it has this advantage, that it requires a man to have the
same qualification that a woman is required
to have in respect of the occupation of premises, that is to say, that on the last day
of the qualifying period he must occupy, as
owner or tenant, land or premises of the
yearly value of not less than £5.
The general effect of this Bill will, it is
hoped, obviate the difficulties I have already
referred to. It will also reduce considerably the enormous burden of work that has
been thrown on the registration officer during
the last few years in dealing with the
numerous claims that have been made,
based on very doubtful qualifications.
Furthermore it will ensure that the Local
Government register of electors will comprise
persons with a certain financial interest in
Local Government affairs.
As regards the form of the Bill itself I
have very little to say. It is really a oneclause Bill, of which the first sub-section
gives effect to the objects I have already
enumerated, and the second sub-section
enables the Governor of Northern Ireland
by an Order in Council to make such alteration in the registration forms as may be
necessary to give effect to the desired change.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment
Moved: That this House do now adjourn
till Thursday. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Might I
ask, as a good many of us are anxious on
account of next week as you know, if you
have any information as to when the Education Bill is likely to be brought before the
House?
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In reply to the right hon. and gallant Gentleman, I understand that it is just possible that
this House may receive the Education Bill
next Thursday. It is hoped we shall have the
First Reading of the Bill on that day, in
which case I take it that the Second Reading
will be on either the Monday or the Tuesday
following. The noble Marquess, the Leader
of the House, is away, but I take it the
Second Reading will probably be on Monday
or Tuesday of next week.
Mr. LESLIE: Might I ask whether it is
proposed to give two days for the Second
Reading of the Bill?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Member, I
cannot say definitely. It depends on this
House and upon how the debate proceeds.
If necessary no doubt two days will be given
for the Second Reading of this Bill. It
entirely rests on the discretion of the House.
I trust, however, that not more than one day
will be necessary.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Half
after Three of the Clock till
Thursday next at a Quarter
after Three of the Clock.

WEATHER FORECASTS.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
ask whether the Government will endeavour
to arrange with the Imperial Authorities that
telephone subscribers in Northern Ireland
shall have the same facilities of receiving
the weather prospects as are given to subscribers in Great Britain, seeing the advantage of such information to farmers and others.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The answer to the question asked by
the right hon. Gentleman is as follows: No
evidence of a general desire on the part of
Northern Ireland telephone subscribers to
participate in the facilities mentioned has
yet reached the Northern Government, but
if such evidence is forthcoming the Government will be prepared to approach the Postmaster-General with a view to securing the
extension of those facilities to Northern Ireland.

Double Taxation (Relief) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill is being introduced
in order to bring before the House the
arrangement, to which the consent of the
Government of Northern Ireland has already
Double Taxation Bill.

31 MAY 1923
Adjournment.

Σ
in
BE
5.
B


1-


been given, for affording relief in respect of
double taxation in the case of Estate Duty
and Stamp Duties as between Northern Ireland and the Irish Free State.
Hon. Members will remember that
the Government of Ireland Act, 1920
(Sections 28 and 9) provided against double
Death Duties and double Stamp Duties as
between Northern and Southern Ireland. The
arrangements were, broadly, that in the case
of a person dying domiciled in Northern Ireland and possessing property in Southern
Ireland, relief should be given in Northern
Ireland, in respect of the estate duty payable
in Southern Ireland, and vice versa. So far
as Stamp Duties were concerned, an instrument liable to Stamp Duty in both areas and
stamped in one area should, to the extent of
the duty it bore, be accepted as duly stamped,
in the other area.
On the establishment, however, of the Irish
Free State, Sections 28 and 29 of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, already referred
to, ceased to apply to Southern Ireland, but
by section 2 of the Irish Free State Constitution Act, 1922, the existing system of taxation
was continued, and these provisions remained
operative until the adoption by the Free State
of fiscal independence. Accordingly to provide relief from double taxation a section, in
the provisions of which the representatives of
Northern Ireland concurred, was introduced
in the Irish Free State (Consequential Provisions) Act. This provided that the necessary arrangements for granting relief were to
be made by the British and Free State Governments and effects to these arrangements
given on the British side by Order in Council.
In the case of transferred taxes, that is to
say, taxes imposed by the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, such as Estate and Stamp
Duties, the section was, with the consent of
the Government of Northern Ireland, to
apply to Northern Ireland as it applied to
Great Britain.
The declaration in Council was made on
the 29th March, 1923, and the Government of
Northern Ireland have signified their approval, in accordance with Section 5,
Sub-section (4) (b) of the Irish Free State
(Consequential Provisions) Act, so far as it
relates to Transferred Taxes. Now, as the
power of imposing Transferred Taxes and
making laws with respect thereto is the prerogative of the Parliament of Northern Ireland it is considered necessary that the consent given by the Government of Northern
Ireland should receive the authority of this
House.
Accordingly, the present Bill is brought in
in order to deal formally with the matter
of double taxation as regards the two transferred taxes-Estate Duty and Stamp Duties.
I should like honourable Members to realise
that the arrangements now made are precisely the same as the arrangements
contemplated by the Government of Ireland
Act, 1920, and the present legislation by this
House is rendered necessary solely by reason
of the political changes in Ireland since the
former Act was passed. The arrangement
is reciprocal and is only to continue so long
as each party to the arrangement does
not formally revoke its consent.
In Part III of the Schedule paragraph (c)
hon. Members will see it is stated that the
arrangement is reciprocal and shall apply as
between Northern Ireland and the Irish Free
State until the Government of Northern Ireland signify that they have withdrawn their
consent to such application. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this House do now
adjourn, and in doing so, I had intended to
say what has already been said by the right
hon, and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford) and the hon. Senator opposite
(Mr. Duffin) that our sympathy goes out to
the noble Lord, the Leader of the House, in
the great loss he has sustained and which
prevented his being present to move the introduction of the Education Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: May I
ask what is the probable date of the Second
Reading of the Education Bill?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: În reply to question asked by the
right hon. and gallant Gentleman, the Education Bill, to which a First Reading was
given by this House to-day, will be circulated
to hon. Members to-morrow, and it is proposed to have the Second Reading on Monday
next. I understand the Committee stage
will be taken on the following Friday-
Friday, the 8th June. Beyond that I cannot
say any more.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive Minutes before Four of the
Clock until Monday, 4th June,
at a Quarter after Three of the

THE DEATH OF LORD CHAPLIN.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
As a personal matter, before we commence
the proceedings to-day, I should like to express my sincere gratitude and the gratitude
of my family for the very sympathetic reference which was made to the loss which we
have sustained.

has been pleased to give his assent to the

First time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
NORTHERN IRELAND STATUTES.
Mr. LESLIE had given notice of the following question,
To ask whether it is proposed to publish
the Statutes of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland in volume form, and if so whether it
is intended to call attention, by a Schedule
inserted in the volume or otherwise, to any
repeals between the dates of the enactment
of the Statutes and of the issue of the
volume.
He said: The first part of my question explains itself; the second, perhaps, requires a
word or two of explanation. What was in my
mind was that we are now in the third year
of our existence as a legislature and we have
not yet produced a full volume of statutes.
I do not suggest that we should accelerate the
pace at all; but it will obviously be some time
before it becomes necessary to issue revised
statutes after the English manner. Therefore
it occurred to me it might be worth while for
us to issue with each volume a little appendix
showing what repeals had been made during
the currency of that volume. It is really a
simple matter of convenience. I think it
would be convenient to the public. That is
all I have to say.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
It is intended shortly to publish the Statutes of the Parliament of Northern Ireland
in volume form. The question of repeals
and other points affecting statute law revision will be considered in connection with
such publication.
BANGOR GAS PROVISIONAL ORDER
CONFIRMATION BILL.
Order for appointment of Joint Committee
read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving that two Senators be
appointed on the Joint Committee I have
to inform this House that a mistake has
been made and that the hon. Senator, Mr.
Barbour's name has been put down in error.
Accordingly, I beg to move that the
Senators following be named of the Joint
Unopposed Committee on the Bangor Provisional Order Confirmation Bill:
Mr. Joseph Cunningham.
Col. the Rt. Hon. R. G. Sharman-Crawford.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Education Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): The moment has
arrived when I have the honour and the
Education Bill.
Second Reading.

privilege of introducing into this House the
Education Bill for Northern Ireland. The
measure, we are all aware, is one of firstclass importance and one which is occupying
the minds of the great majority of the
people of the Six Counties. The measure,
as we are also well aware, has passed
through the other House, and it now has
reached the Senate for the confirmation of
the Members and its enactment, as we hope,
as a statute in a very short space of time
from now.
The provisions of the measure are very
well known to all whom I have the honour
of addressing to-day. It has been through
the various stages in the House of Commons, and I have had the privilege, which
is not accorded to Ministers in the United
Kingdom, of taking charge of the Bill in
another place through every stage of the
passage of the Bill and expressing the views
which the Government hold in regard to the
measure. Therefore, I feel that hon. Members
will not think it disrespectful of me to this
House if I do not dilate to the fullest extent
on all the provisions of the Bill, because I
know well that everyone of us here has taken
a deep interest in this Bill, and that the
great majority have been considering for a
long time past how they could improve it. I
know also that I am speaking to-if I may
use the expression-an instructed audience;
and so I will venture just to put briefly before
hon. Members the main provisions of the Bill
as they have eventually emerged from the
House of Commons.
It will not be out of place if I touch one
personal note. I can assure you that I esteem
it a great honour that it should fall to me to
move this important measure as the first
Education Minister in our Parliament, which
has only recently been set up. When the
Prime Minister conferred this great honour
on me, I was determined to do whatever I
could to establish education in the Six Counties on a basis which would be satisfactory to
the Six Counties and which would also be a
monument of the new administration which
the 1920 Act set up. I need hardly say that
during all this time I have received not only
co-operation but I have also received sympathy from everyone, and I do feel that unless
I had received this co-operation and this
sympathy my efforts would have been futile,
and that I could under no circumstances produce the Bill which I am now venturing to
put before you.
The first duty which I imposed upon myself
-a duty which I know everyone will agree
with me for undertaking-was the setting up
of a committee, an influential committee, to
consider the whole of this vast question in all
its aspects; and, as hon. Members are well
aware, that committee sat continuously for a
very long time under the very able chairmanship of Mr. Lynn. I feel that not only my
thanks but the thanks of the whole community are due to him and his colleagues for
the labours which they undertook and on
which I have been able to frame the measure
which is now in your hands.
No
I should like also to take this opportunity
of saying how fortunate indeed I have been
in the staff which is now what I may call
operating the Ministry of Education.
Minister in any Parliament could have received more whole-hearted and sympathetic
service than I have received from those with
whom I come closely into contact and from
those who have done the routine and ordinary
work of the Education Department.
The Education Bill is necessarily a very
complicated measure, and I have endeavoured
to keep before me the main object which we
have in view that is, the present and the
future welfare of the children of the community. My endeavour has been to place
education in the Six Counties on such a basis
that the best results can be obtained at the
minimum expenditure of money. My hope
has been-and I venture to say that, in a
degree at all events, I have achieved that hope
-to bring about co-ordination in education
from the earliest years to those years when
the child or young person as he or she will
be at that time is launched into the world
on his own resources. The next object which
I have kept in view is that opportunities of
education should be accorded to every child
in the Six Counties.
There are always difficulties of machinery
in this respect, and I do sincerely hope that
in this very comprehensive Bill we have set
up machinery which will be able to stand
the tests, whatever they may be, to which it
may be put. I have endeavoured, as I have
said, to keep the interests of the children
before my own mind and before the minds
of everyone else, and to endeavour to urge
them to realise that that is the paramount
necessity, and all sectional interests and all
sectional duties must give way to our one
object, that is to give to the children of the
Six Counties the best possible education. I
can assure you that we have carefully studied
all other systems which we have been able
to do in what has been the really short time
at our disposal. We have heard on all sides
the incessant clamour for education.
have listened to criticisms of all the existing
systems of education, but I have found that
some of these vehement educationists when
it comes to the question of paying for education are not quite so prominent in their
opinions in that respect as they are on
education itself.
We
We have hitherto existed under what may
be called a centralised system, and whereas
it is quite possible to discover advantages
in a centralised system, still I feel sure that
we are all in agreement that in the
Second Reading.
Education Bill-

THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
evolution of modern democratic institutions
the centralised system of controlling education: is entirely out of date. The great step
which this Bill is making is the long step
from the centralised system to a system of
democratic control. The centralised system
has been financed and supported by State
aid alone, but the system of democratic control is financed and supported by State aid
accompanied by local rating.
I shall have
something to say in a few moments about the
question of rates, but I would only venture
to say upon this at the present moment that
there is a fallacy, I would almost say a
delusion, which occupies a very large place
in the minds of very many people in regard
to what is called State aid. After all, the
State is made up of every one of us, and
when we speak of State aid it means that
a fraction of that State aid is coming out of
the pockets of every one of the taxpayers
in the community, but it is so bound up by
so many intricacies and the direct progress
from the pocket to the Exchequer is so
nebulous and sometimes SO well concealed
that there is the idea in the minds of a great
many people that State aid is a fountain of
gold which is limitless and which comes from
somewhere-no one quite explains where--
and that if State aid is not forthcoming the
Minister in charge of the Bill is either guilty
of very niggardly propensities or else is
thoroughly incompetent and not capable of
carrying out the duties which he is called
upon to discharge. So much for State aid,
but I would venture to impress on everyone
-I am not venturing to impress it on the
audience who are doing me the honour of
listening to me here to-day, but to the wide
audience of the general public outside this
House-that State aid is money that comes
from the taxpayer's pocket, and in these
days of economy when every Government is
called upon, and rightly called upon, to
exercise the strictest economy, even the
Minister of Education has to consider this
point very carefully from that point of view.
The great change which I have mentioned
is from the centralised system to the control
of education by the people themselves. This
policy marks the practical disappearance of
what has hitherto been known as the managerial system; it marks the disappearance of
the Commissioners for Education, of the
Intermediate Board, and of the Department
of Agriculture and Technical Instruction;
but I should not like anyone to imagine
that I was discarding these great forces of
education without saying, and saying from
my heart, what a great debt of gratitude
we owe them. It is not for me to go
into the great history of education in this
country. I may say that perhaps education
was on a stronger basis in his country some
centuries before it was in existence in other
parts of the world, and I feel that we owe
a great debt of gratitude to those pioneers
in the past and to their successors up to
very recent times who have done so much
to establish the foundations of education
amongst the community. There is the debt
of gratitude which we owe to the Churchesthey have done more, perhaps, than any
other section of the community in imparting
education to the youth of the country-and
to the Commissioners and the Intermediate
Board, and also to the Department of Agriculture and Technical Instruction. Let me,
on your behalf as well as on my own, offer
them the gratitude which they deserve for
the work which they have done on behalf
of education in Ireland. While we are dispensing with their services we are benefiting
by the work which they have done.
Our
hope in the future is in the direction of
co-ordination. We hope that we have coordinated those three branches of education,
the duties which fell to the Commissioners,
to the Intermediate Board, and to the
Department of Agriculture and Technical
Instruction. The administration of education has already been unified by the coming
into existence of the Ministry of Education.
I have already alluded to the paramount
object which we have in view, the welfare
of the child. We shall endeavour to carry
out this policy by establishing real compulsory attendance, by the setting up of medical
inspection, and the feeding of necessitous
children, and we are doing our utmost to
set up what I may call the educational
ladder from early years to the years of
adolescence by the establishment of scholarships and also of free places at secondary
schools.
May I say one word about the feeding of
school children? I have often heard this
policy criticised, and we are told that it is
atrophying the efforts of those who ought to
be responsible for feeding school children. I
agree that there is a certain amount to be
said for that argument, but I want hon.
Members to look at it from another, and
wider, point of view. We feel it is folly, if
I may use the expression, to endeavour to
impart education to the young when they
are not capable of assimilating the education
imparted to them. It is merely a waste of
time and a waste of the teacher's effort to
endeavour to inculcate education into starving children, and we have felt that in some
cases it is necessary before endeavouring to
educate children that they should be fed, and
that they should be in such a state of
physical health as to be capable of absorbing
the education which it is the teacher's endeavour to impart to them.
This brings me at once to parental responsibility, and I need hardly say there is
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
4 JUNE 1923
コ
:
as
nothing that I desire to maintain so much
the idea of parental responsibility.
It
may be said that the establishment of free
education has undermined to a large extent
the responsibilities which parents should
exercise towards their children.. I do most
sincerely hope that this indictment is illfounded and that parents as a whole, in this
country at all events, are as anxious and as
desirous of doing whatever they can for
their children as they have ever been in the
past, and I have certainly endeavoured in
this Bill to compel those parents who may
be wanting in this necessary idea to carry
out the responsibilities which should fall
upon their shoulders. Provision has been
made in this Bill for the payment by the
parent, wherever this is possible, for such
provision as feeding for school children, but
the point which I should like to emphasise,
and in which I know hon. Members will
agree with me, is that we cannot under any
circumstances allow the poverty of the
parent to be a bar to the child's enjoyment
of educational facilities. The great engine,
if I may use the expression, by which the
success of the Bill can be achieved is
operated by the teacher, and it is our hope
and our endeavour to establish in the Six
Counties a body of teachers who will be able
to carry on efficiently and well those great
requirements which we expect from them. I
think we always have been fortunate in the
teachers who have controlled the schools in
the Six Counties, and I feel that in the
teachers themselves there is not only a great
intellectual asset to the educational system
but there is also something deeper than that,
and that is the desire on their part to do
their duty to the utmost, not only what is
expected of them but also that additional
duty which teachers can discharge to children, and I feel, therefore, that in the teachers
of the Six Counties we have no cause to fear
that the cause of education will suffer.
I have already said that we are making a
great step from the centralised system to the
system of control by the people themselves,
and in this policy I am following the recommendations of the Lynn Committee. The
system which is set up in the Bill is first of
all the establishment of a local education
authority. As hon. Members are aware, this
was a question which the Lynn Committee
considered very carefully, and one which,
after the Lynn Committee had issued their
Interim Report, we in the Ministry of Education again considered very carefully. The
Lynn Committee came to the conclusion that
to negotiate this step from the centralised
system to the system of democratic control
the best plan to adopt was to set up throughout the Six Counties a large number of more
or less independent committees, with powers to
levy rates and in general to be in control of
the whole local educational system of the
Six Counties.
They did not disguise from
themselves or from anyone else the fact that
they considered that eventually the local control of education must be in the hands of the
county councils, but they felt that the plan
they suggested was the best method of bringing about the system which they hoped
would eventually be established. We considered this matter very carefully at the
Ministry of Education, and I came to the
conclusion that, because the county councils
are the actual rating authorities, and because
in the end they must have financial control,
it would be better if we established at once
the county councils as the education authority. That is how the Bill stands at the present moment. We are calling upon the county
councils, in addition to their manifold duties,
to undertake the responsibility of controlling the education of the Six Counties,
and I need hardly say I have no qualms on
this score. I feel that just exactly in the
same manner as these great public bodies,
giving their services voluntarily, have discharged their duties for the welfare of the
community in the past, so in connection with
education they will live up to their reputation. After having gone very carefully into
all the details, and having considered the
different schemes and the different methods
of management, I have thought that the best
way in which the county councils can operate
as the education authority would be through
what we call regional committees. We have
realised that the modern way in which we
deal with all these matters is to delegate
authority from the central and higher
authority to local committees, which carry
out these duties in the true system of decentralisation, and we in the Education
Ministry thought we could sub-divide the
counties into regional committees, which
would have the advantage of being large
enough to escape what I may call parochial
influences, and yet not be so large as not to
be sensible of and susceptible to local necessities.
I may say at once that I did not think
there would be a general acceptance of the
county council as the education authority,
and I felt that perhaps the best way was, first
of all, to establish the county councils as
the education authority, and secondly to
assist the county councils in the duties which
they will have to perform, by the estab
lishment of what we have called regional
committees. I may tell the House that in
the scheme of the Ministry of Education,
which we made out with great care and
trouble, we did set up one county as the
education authority under the county council,
with only one committee, which shows to
hon. Members that there is no necessity to
Second Reading.
Education Bill-

THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry].
rule out the idea of the county councils
operating through a single committee alone
in any county. Our Six Counties differ to a
very large extent in size, and while there are
small counties there are also very large
counties, and I am not yet sure that the
county councils in our large counties can
operate with one education committee as well
as they might operate with more than one.
However, this is a matter for discussion. It
is a matter for administration, and I have
left it in the Bill that the education authority should furnish schemes of control and
management to the Ministry. After all it is
a matter of arrangement between the county
councils and the Ministry, and I need hardly
say I anticipate no difficulty in the matter,
but I am confident that when the time comes
we shall be able to bring about an arrangement that will be in accordance with the
desires we have in the direction of education
and yet in accordance with the wishes of the
county councils themselves. Since the Bill
left the House of Commons there is a matter
I have been considering, and which I would
venture to put before the House now. That
is the deletion of the word "Regional." The
word "Regional" is a word manufactured by
the Ministry itself. It has no particular
virtue, and it has no hidden meaning.
was used to denote the committees which
would control the various regions under the
education authority, which is the county
council, but that is a matter which I shall
probably be able to raise again and put before hon. Members in the form in which I
think it should be put.
It
Now I come to the question of rates, and
this is a question which I am allowed to
discuss but over which, being a matter of
finance, the Senate has no power-or rather
it has a power which the Lower House is inclined to question at all times as one of the
rights that belong to them. The establishment of a general education rate in the Six
Counties is a new departure and one to which
we have not been accustomed, and about
which we know nothing. I am speaking of
an education rate, because as regards other
rates I think we know very we'l what they
are. The establishment of a system of democratic control postulates the establishment
of a system of raising the money from local
sources, and I do not think the community
of the Six Counties will be inclined to quarrel
with the establishment of a general rate for
education when we know the widespread desire there is for an adequate system of education-one that can produce full benefits for
the children of the Six Counties. I should
like to allay any fears that may exist at the
present moment that the rate may develop
in a very short space of time into a very
heavy burden on the community. I am quite
willing to say on my own authority that the
expenditure for the next few years under the
Bill cannot be expected to be higher than the
equivalent of a shilliing rate. That shilling
rate will not fall in its entirety on the people
of the Six Counties. Besides the expenditure
on additional teachers, which naturally falls
on the State alone, I may say here that the
Government hope to be able to make a grant
of £2 for every £1 of local expenditure. This
means that, for the time being, and for some
time to come, if my judgment is correct, the
education burden on the people of the Six
Counties cannot exceed the sum of 4d. This
is an infinitesimal rate, and it is one that
we can compare very much to our advantage
with the rates for education that are imposed
across the water. I am sure that the harrassed ratepayers of Great Britain would look
with great satisfaction and pleasure on their
Minister of Education if he announced to
them in the House of Commons at Westminster that he was only going to impose a
rate of 4d upon them. But, as I have said,
an education rate is a new departure here,
and I should be the last person to wish to
burden the ratepayers of the Province-the
great majority of whom are agricultural ratepayers with any great addition to the very
heavy burdens they already have to bear.
I would like to return to the paramount
consideration of this Bill, namely, the children themselves, and I may say that in the
Bill we are proposing to provide free places
for all children. There is a paramount need
in all parts of the Six Counties, but especially in Belfast, for decent schools, and we
have to look with misgiving on the fact that
at the present moment there are not sufficient elementary school places in the Six
Counties for the school children. This must
be remedied at the earliest possible date, and
I most sincerely hope, in a comparatively
short space of time from now, to be able to
state that every child in the Six Counties is
in a position to receive the education to
which he or she is entitled. We hope that
with the provision of central schools or
classes, practical instruction, including
cookery, wi'l be given to the children in the
later stages of the elementary course.

would venture to put before the House.
We have accepted the principle, which was
very specifically laid down by the Lynn
Committee, that the amount of local assistance
that comes from the rates should vary with
the amount of local control which is exercised
by the people themselves. We have, therefore,
divided the schools into three categories.
There are, first of all, what are known as
provided or transferred schools, which come
directly under the Education Authority;
there are, secondly, the voluntary schools,
Education Bill-

?
which consent to a Committee of Management
of six, of which two are appointed by the
Education Authority; and there are, in the
third category, voluntary schools under individual managers. The House is aware that
these two latter categories of schools receive
varying amounts of local assistance.
The
With regard to higher education, this brings
me to intermediate and secondary schools, to
technical schools, to continuation classes, and
also to the provision of scholarships.
question of secondary schools is one of very
great importance, and there is a great demand
on the part of the community that it should
be carefully considered and that these institutions should be further assisted from public
funds. The Lynn Committee made various
excellent recommendations on this particular
point, and I am glad to say we have been able
broadly to accept those recommendations.
The recommendations were that the owners
of a secondary school shall pay the minimum
rates of salary to the teachers, and that the
State should be responsible for all the increments which those teachers should receive by
reason of their length of service in those
schools. Hon. Members are aware that the
Parliamentary Secretary to the Ministry of
Education (Mr. McKeown) has been presiding
over a committee which has been considering
the scales of remuneration which these teachers should receive. That committee has
reported and a salary scale will be adopted
with effect from the beginning of the current
school year that is, 1922-1923. I am unable
at this moment to give the exact figures,
because we are naturally and necessarily
discussing with the Ministry of Finance the
subject of the scales which Mr. McKeown's
Committee has adopted.
The urban authorities have been left in
control of technical schools, and I may say
at once that I hope the time is not very far
distant when, in the interests of co-ordination,
they will realise the necessity for handing
over their schools to the Education Authority.
I am sure hon. Members will not feel inclined
to censure me for having created what may
appear to be somewhat of an exception, but I
do feel that we are deeply indebted to the
urban authorities for what they have done
in the past in regard to technical education,
and, while I have very little doubt that they
will very clearly see what is best for technical
education by coming under the county council
as the Education Authority, I do not feel
inclined to endeavour to coerce people who
have done so much in the past for the benefit
of the community. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
Now I come to the question of religious
instruction, and I do not know that there is
any great necessity for me to say very much
upon that point. I have always thought
that hitherto we have solved the question of
the religious difficulty in the Six Counties,
and the system which has been in operation
for many years past under the Commissicners is the system which we propose to
continue. We have put phraseology into the
Bill which, I regret to say, has caused a
certain amount of misgiving, but, while I do
not profess to be able with celerity or with
accuracy to read Acts of Parliament like a
lawyer, I feel that the phraseology that exists in the Bill at the present moment is
such that it should leave no occasion for
doubt whatever. We have established what
has always been the practice in the pastthat religious education should be given in
school hours, but not in compulsory school
hours. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) The
old system, with which we are all familiar,
was that religious instruction took place
usually at the beginning of the school hours,
and the roll was not called until that education had come to an end, which virtually
meant exactly what we are saying in the Bill
now-that religious instruction shall be
given in school hours but not in compulsory
school hours. We do feel that under the
education system which we are promoting,
and which the community is to control, the
best method of doing what the community
wants is to allow the denominations to give
their denominational instruction, and that
we should give an opportunity for that instruction, but that it should be instruction
in which the Education Authority as representing the people cannot take any part and
on which it cannot expend any money.
I should like to say one word about what
is called moral instruction. In case there is
any doubt upon that subject existing in the
mind of anyone I should like to say at once
that we are determined that moral instruction shall be associated with secular instruction, and we most sincerely hope that all the
children of these Six Counties will be
brought up with instruction in what we may
call civics, and that from the system which
we will inaugurate we will be able to produce as loyal citizens as have ever existed in
these Six Counties in the past.
There is one word I should like to say
about the teachers. It is a paramount necessity that we should have contented
teachers. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I
can assure the House that I shall leave no
stone unturned to remove from the mind of
the teacher those anxieties which we can
remove, and which, if they do exist in his
mind, naturally militate against the efficient
services which he can render to the State by
the teaching which he imparts to the children. We have taken power under the Bill
for the periodical revision of salaries.
bon. Members are aware the scale of salaries
for elementary teachers which exists now
is based upon the scale recommended by
the committee over which Lord Burnham
As
Second Reading.

Education Bill-
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry].
presided on the other side of the water. We
are well aware that those salaries were on
the high side. While none of us would venture or would wish to grudge the teacher the
salary to which he is entitled, one does feel
that, perhaps, in some cases the salaries
which teachers receive are somewhat
out of scale when we compare them
with the salaries which are received by
the other professional classes of the
community. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Across the water the teachers have made a
very patriotic move, and one which I think
does them a large amount of credit. They
have voluntarily suggested the curtailment of
their salaries in the interests of general
economy. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I
have made the suggestion to the teachers on
this side of the water, and, as I speak now, I
am not aware of what they are proposing to
do; but whatever they do I am perfectly convinced that they will look upon themselves as
public servants and as owing a debt to the
community, and I am sure that, if they feel
they can co-operate with us in the general
interests of economy, and in the general desire to get the very best we can out of education with the smallest burden on the community, they will follow that course.
There has been a Committee sitting under
the Minister of Finance (Mr. Pollock) which
has been considering the work of training of
teachers, and I think we owe a great debt of
gratitude to the right hon. Gentleman for the
work which he and his Committee have done.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I may say that
we hope to be able to supply teachers in sufficient numbers for all the denominations. As
hon. Members are aware, the question of
teachers and their denominations does not
really come under the Education Authority.
Hitherto a great deal of work has been done
by the teachers in denominational directions
entirely on their own initiative, and I am perfectly convinced that whatever the teachers
have done in the past for what they believed
to be for the benefit of the children and of
the community they will do in the future. But
I need hardly say that it is the desire of
the Ministry and the desire of the community
that in all these questions relating to denominational instruction the teacher should have
an absolutely free hand and be free from any
compulsion whatever.
I have endeavoured at certain length to
put before the House the main provisions
of the Bill which I have the honour to
introduce. I have purposely, as I said in my
opening remarks, not gone into the many
and varied details of the Bill on the present
occasion. I know hon. Members are almost
as familiar with the Bill as I am, and I
certainly shall have opportunities later on
of explaining any provision or any point
which at the moment may be in doubt. I
would like to say that I feel we have a great
duty before us. We have a great opportunity.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear. The opportunity has come to us from the grant of
Parliamentary institutions. It was a policy
for which we did not ask, and which we only
reluctantly accepted, but when we did accept
it we made up our minds that we would do
whatever lay in our power to benefit that
portion of the community which expressly
came under our jurisdiction, and also to prove
ourselves to be the loyal citizens of the
Empire which we have always claimed to be
in the past. I believe that in the introduction of this great measure we are showing
our own people and also the citizens of the
whole Empire that we are not wanting in
those qualities which have always been
uppermost in our race, and I most sincerely hope that when this measure is on
the Statute Book it will stand as a monument
to the Province of which we are so proud.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am very
much delighted on this occasion to congratulate the noble Marquess on the splendid
speech he has made in introducing the Bill on
the Second Reading. I am personally
very much indebted to him for the very
clear and lucid way in which he explained
it. He has certainly cut a great deal of the
ground from below our feet in matters we
may have objected to.
However, I will
simply deal with any objections I have, but
altogether I take a very favourable view of
the Bill. It certainly is working men's
legislation. It is intended specially, I
believe, for working class children. There
was never any handicap nor any barrier
hitherto in the way of better class children
getting proper education. They had the
means at their disposal, and, of course, could
easily obtain the necessary education. It has
been different with the working man's child.
I do hope, as has been said by the noble
Marquess, that a great change is going to
take place in the North of Ireland. I hope
that the picture we have seen so often drawn
of the poor man's child being able to climb
to the top of the ladder in the university
is going at last to be a reality, and that in
future the poor man's child will have every
opportunity, if he exhibits intelligence and
ability, to climb to the highest pinnacle of
education.
So far as I am concerned I must admit
that the recommendations made by the
Labour associations with which I am connected have almost all been embodied in the
Bill. That, to my mind, is very satisfactory.
There is only one exception, and that is in
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
4 JUNE 1923
Ba
We
regard to the composition of the local
education authorities or school comant mittees.
recommended that we
Tear were in favour of these committees being set
Dah up by direct election from the parents of
e the children. As it is provided in the Bill
now the county councils are to be the
authority for controlling and conducting the
afairs of education, the school and such
Eid like. If this had been left to the direct and
free election of the parents of the children
I maintain they would have taken a greater
interest in the education of their children
and in the government of the schools.
noble Marquess has pointed out this is only
an experiment, and I expect it will probably
work satisfactorily. That is one of the
points on which we differ with him.

ers
The
There is another matter which I am very
ant pleased to see has been retained in the Bill,
and that is that every opportunity is to be
mos given to the day school teacher to impart
religious instruction. A good number of the
working classes are more or less opposed to
that, but I believe I am speaking for the
large majority of the working classes of the
Six Counties when I say that they are in
favour of this instruction being given to
their children. It is something that is neglected in the working man's home. He either
has not the time, or the opportunity is not
there, to give the necessary religious instruction to his children, and it is in many cases
lefr to the teacher of the day school or
Sunday school to give that instruction.
Therefore, I am very pleased that this has
beer retained in the Bill, and that every
opportunity is to be given to teachers to give
the instruction. I think if there was more
religious instruction given it would have a
better effect, and there might not be such
need for so much other education. I believe
We would be educated to
considerable
extent to prevent ourselves being duped by
others who are educated, and if everyone was
religiously inclined and Christianlike I do
not think that would be altogether necessary.
ther
sp
al
atte

art

a
There is another thing in the Bill on which
I am pleased to commend the Minister of Education, and that is the feeding of school children. Lord Londonderry says there may be
parents who are negligent of their children.
There may be isolated cases of such a nature,
but I do know this, that most of the working
✓ class people make considerable sacrifices in
order to give their children a better education
than they received themselves (hon. Members: Hear, hear) - so that they may not have
the same hardships to contend with in this
world that they had. I believe that is almost
a rule. The father and mother endeavour to
the best of their ability to educate their
children better than they themselves were
educated, and I think the noble Marquess
will find that these children do not require to
be fed. I agree with him that it is very necessary that the child should be properly fed
before an attempt is made to give them educational instruction. I think he will find
many cases where the parents are in such
difficult circumstances that they are unable to
give food, and there may be isolated cases of
negligence or carelessness. In most cases,
however, he will find that considerable sacrifices are made by the working-class people.
I want to say that the people of the North
of Ireland are very fortunate in having secured one who has taken such considerable
interest in this question of education. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) The noble Marquess
will be for ever remembered, particularly by
the working classes, who may be benefited
considerably by this Measure. I hope he will
continue to take a great interest in this question. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I trust
in connection with the education to be freely
given to the children of the working classes
opportunities will also be afforded to these
children to enable them to get into the positions to which they are best suited. I am
very much afraid that there are many children who receive a very good education, and
who, after all, are not able to secure the influence necessary for them to obtain the positions they are well qualified to fill. I hope
the noble Marquess will be long spared to take
an interest in the question of education, and
further, that he will see that those who embrace that educational instruction and fit
themselves for positions are able to secure
those positions they are best suited for. I
look on this Bill with considerable favour, and
I am determined to give it all the support I
possibly can until I see it placed on the
Statute Book. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I desire to join in the congratulations to the noble
Leader of the House for the lucid explanation
he has given. Of course, it was not necessary, as he did it in the lower House, to give
details. There are several points in my mind,
but before I make my few remarks I would
like to have the permission of the House to
clear away what seems to be the impression
gained by what arose in the Committee and
also in the Lower House as to the action of
the County Councils towards the Education
Bill. The impression seems to have gone
abroad for some reason or another that the
witnesses before the Lynn Committee rather
frightened them, but I think this is a mistake, as there was only one point on which
the County Councils raised any opposition. I
also made some remarks myself, and I should
explain that these represented not only my
L
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Colonel Sharman-Crawford.]
own views, but they were in unison with the
views of the County Councils in the Six Counties who are associated in one body of which
I have the honour to be chairman. I will
read from the shorthand transcript of the proceedings of the Committee:
I think we are all agreed that we are not
here representing the County Councils. We
are members of what we call the Joint
County Councils, but we have had no opportunity of conferring with our County
Council on this matter. We are in entire
sympathy with the educational movement in
Northern Ireland, but we are not prepared
to do anything rashly until we know what
the scheme is.
Again I said:
Our only anxiety is to see that we have
proper control when it comes to spending
our own money. I do not think there would
be any objection to nominating two representatives.
I also said:
No doubt the County Council would be
prepared to strike a rate providing the
amount required was not too heavy and that
they had control of the scheme.
I again said:
If the County Education Committee or
the Minister of Education found the inspectors to do that kind of work, and if the
County Councils were given some kind of
control, then I am sure they would be prepared to do what you suggest.
I will only read one more extract:
It all comes back to the old point of how
much the County Councils will have to
spend. Until we know what the actual
amount is I would not be inclined to express any opinion.
At the conclusion I said:
It would be better if we could all pull
together. We are absolutely in sympathy
with the scheme.
I wish to point out that it was only in connection with the financial conditions of the
scheme that the County Council raised any
objection at all, but what has happened since
this Bill left the Lower House has made it
very different. We, in the first instance,
were opposed, rightly or wrongly, to the
number of Regional Committees being started.
There is no necessity for me to go into the
arguments I would have used if that had
stood as it did then. The noble Lord has
informed us that it will be up to the Regional
Committees to form their own scheme, their
own numbers, and so on. There is only one
thing I regret very much it appeared in the
first edition of the Bill and has been altered
since-and that is striking off the representatives of the Education Department on the
Education Committee. In the first edition of
the Bill there was provision made for a certain number of representatives, but they have
all been struck off. Of course, there may be
good reason for it, but I am voicing the view
of the County Council when I say they would
gladly welcome the representatives of the
County Council on the Education Committee.
It might simplify matters between them and
the Education Department.
There is another small point which perhaps
you would consider. Take my own county
(County Down), for instance. The regional
areas were broken up into bodies of about
40,000. From my own impression and what
I have heard--the matter has not yet been
before the County Council-the regional areas
will be considerably reduced. What I would
suggest is that the members on the committee
might be increased. The numbers that might
suit an area of 40,000 might not suit areas of
80,000, 150,000, or 200,000. That is all I wish
to say about the Regional Committees. We
are all satisfied now with the way in which
the Bill appears before the House. We are
not educational experts on the County Council.
The future of the Bill, which we all
heartily hope will be entirely successful, depends on how it is worked out.
I am not going to say anything in regard to
the working of the Bill, except on the one
part to which the noble Marquess has referred, and in which the county councils are
more interested than in anything else, that
is, the financial portion. As is well known
to the noble Lord, the people who pay the
rates in the Six Counties are mainly agriculturists, and as we all know agriculture
was never at a lower point so far as profit
is concerned than it is at the present time.
Further, we have, as the people of England
and Scotland have not, a terrible shadow
hanging over us-unless the matter is otherwise settled in the form of rates for
malicious injuries. That anestion, which has
never arisen before, will come up this year.
I do not think there will be any difficulty
about the rate, but there is one thing which
does not appear in the Bill, although it was
mentioned during the introduction of the
measure in the Lower House, and to-day by
the noble Marquess, that is, that of the 1s.
rate the Government will find eightpence and
the county fourpence. I quite admit that
that is very fair. Of course, the noble Lord
is optimistic, and we hope that for some
years at any rate the 1s. rate will be quite
sufficient to carry out the working of the
Act; but I am somewhat fearful that in

years to come-perhaps not so very far ahead,
when we look at the experience of England
and Scotland-it will be found that the 1s.
will not be sufficient to perform the duties
in the manner in which they ought to be
carried out. I am not talking now of hairbrained and extravagant schemes which, of
course, the Ministry of Education would not
listen to: I am talking of a time that may
come when the rate must be increased in
order to carry out the working of the Act
in a satisfactory manner. The agriculturist
like the ordinary man in the street always
likes to know what his responsibility is going
to be. I do not think the noble Lord hisself, or anybody else, knows what the
responsibility is going to be in the future.
I would like to see something in the Bill to
the effect that if a necessary increase in the
rate should take place the Ministry would
give the same proportion in the second shilling
as they propose to give in the first shilling.
I know there will be a tremendous outery
if the rate is ever increased to 2s. We hope
that will never come, but there is nothing
to prevent the county councils having to pay
1s. 4d. of the 2s. while the Government would
only contribute eightpence, and perhaps not
even that. There is no guarantee at all of
assistance from the Government in the
future.

These are small matters, perhaps, but it is
necessary to mention them. The county
councils will have this extra work thrown
upon them, and, speaking for my own county,
I can only say that we will give all the assistance we can in making this Bill a success.
think the noble Marquess and the Ministry 01
Education recognise that the only chance of
attaining complete success is for the Ministry
and the Committee to work in harmony. 1
see no reason why they should not do so. 1
have great pleasure in supporting the Bill.
Mr. BARBOUR: I think the Government
and the Minister are to be congratulated
upon the very successful way in which they
have accomplished one of the most difficult
tasks that any government or any minister
could set out to accomplish. I really think
that, in spite of the volume of criticism that
has been levelled against the Bill, there has
gone with that criticism a very sincere appreciation of the great amount of good that
has been accomplished. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) Personally I am in thorough
favour of the Bill, and I intend to support it;
but there are one or two points that I would
like to make, and I am very glad that the
noble Marquess himself mentioned that he felt
some apprehension that he was adding to the
labours of the county councils, which,
Heaven knows, are already heavy enough. I
would like to emphasise that point. I think
there has been a great tendency in legislation
recently to leave to the county councils the
execution of duties for the performance of
which those bodies were never elected.
In
this Bill the duties of the county councils
are added to. I believe, with the Leader of
the House, that the county councils will
make a strenuous effort to discharge those
duties to the full, but I think it is well
to let it go forth from here that there is
a limit. I think that if the duties of the
councils are to be added to much more it
will change the active personnel of those
council's, and will have a strong tendency to
create
bureaucracy that may not be
successful in interpreting the wishes of the
people in the different localities.
a
I
There is one thing that I like about the
Bill as I read it: that is, that there is a
certain latitude left either to the county
councils or to the Regional Committees.
think that is a splendid thing, and I think
that privilege has tended in a great degree
to the success of the technical committees in
the different areas of Ireland. I think there
is also a tendency in drawing up a complete
scheme to try and attain too great a degree
of uniformity. I think that danger has been
obviated, and I certainty believe that that
will tend to the successíul working of the
Bill.
There are just two more points that I wish
to mention. I quite agree with the desirability of getting a uniform scheme. I believe
steps can be taken which will attain uniformity more quickly than might otherwise
be the case, that is, in regard to the
Technical schemes. There are some of the
members of the Technical Committees who
do not feel inclined to transfer to the
education authority the property and the
buildings which the ratepayers they represent have acquired. I do suggest to the
Minister of Education that he might try to
meet that objection. I am not saying that
it is an objection of great importance, but
still it is an objection, and I do not believe
that it is an objection that cannot be bridged
over by sympathetic treatment. I believe
that if an attempt to solve that difficulty
were made it would tend to bring into the
scheme the Technical Committees in the
different urban districts, and I also think
that a further inducement might possibly be
added to that end, namely, that if before
the appointed day any Technical Committee
announced that it would hand over the duty
of technical instruction to the education
authority, possibly in the Regional Committee operating in that area, the Technical
Committee might be allowed slightly
additional representation. As I say, I am
in thorough support of the Bill, and I think
if those two measures of consideration could
be given to the Technical Committees it
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Barbour]
would bring about the uniformity which is
desirable and would encourage the different
Technical Committees to adhere to the
general scheme.

Mr. GREER: We all congratulate the
Minister of Education on having piloted this
Bill safely through the somewhat stormy
conditions in the Lower House, and
on having introduced it into the somewhat serener atmosphere of this House
in such an interesting and lucid manner.
I believe it to be
admirable attempt
to deal with
complicated and difficult question, and I have no doubt it will
prove to be very successful. I want to point
out that there is one thing we can take some
comfort from, that is, that if not perfect,
it can be altered. The Bill will soon pass
into law, and will become the law of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, but it will
not be by any means the law of the Medes
and Persians that cannot be altered, and we
know that in somewhat similar conditions in
Canada they produce an Amending Bill
almost every year.
can do it quite
easily; so if as we gain experience, and after
the scheme is fairly launched on the community, we find that it can be improved in
any manner, why it is quite easy to pass
an Amending Bill, and if any difficulties
arise, set them right.
I
am
We
not quite sure that the noble
Marquess adhered altogether to his policy of
decentralisation in the Bill. He has
arranged that the appointment of teachers
will not rest with the local committees, but
will be reserved for the education authority.
I am not quite satisfied in my own mind that
that is a wise policy, but time will show. I
am afraid it may lead to a much smaller
number of schools being transferred than if
the appointments had been left to the School
Committees. On the other hand, I am not
at all sure that it would be desirable to have
practically every school transferred at once.
I think the policy will be for the education
authorities to go slowly and gradually.
If
they have to pay the entire cost of maintenance of the transferred schools they have no
idea of what it may amount to. It may
amount to a very large sum, whereas if the
schools are not transferred, as I understand
the Bill, they have only to pay one-half of
that cost, and it might not be a bad thing
if at first a large number of schools would
not be transferred. I do not really know
whether that is the policy of the Ministry
or not upon this question.
There is one matter to which I wish to
call the attention of the House, and that is,
that there is no power given to the county
councils to contribute anything to the mainterance of public county libraries. I look
to
upon libraries as a not inconsiderable means
of education. They take the place to some
extent of continuation schools, and if, when
children are at school the teachers can only
get them to acquire what I may call
the reading habit, if they begin
read for themselves and to enjoy reading, I think they will derive very great
benefit from those county circulating
libraries. We have got one in County Antrim.
It is established under the Carnegie Trust.
Under that Trust the Trustees have taken
rooms for the central repository? They have
appointed a librarian and an assistant. They
have placed there some 9,000 volumes of
books. They have undertaken the expense of
sending those books all over the county free
of charge wherever they are wanted. I am
very glad to say that these books are very
widely read over the county, and are going to
be an excellent thing for disseminating information of various kinds. They are not by any
means all novels and light literature. I can
give particulars of the kind of books that are
being read. Amongst the number are 100
books of philosophy, 200 of religion, 300
sociology, 400 language, 500 natural science,
600 useful arts, 700 fine arts and recreation,
800 literature, 900 history, and geography and
travel 1,374; fiction, 6,454; juvenile fiction,
3,650; and the total number of issues of books
during the first year amounted to 12,888.
Now that library has only been working
one year, I think that is a wonderful record
for one year's work. Under the Carnegie
Trust the Trustees will only be responsible for
the maintenance of this library for two years.
It was established in May, 1922, so that in
May, 1924, they will cease to help, and the expenditure then it comes to something between
Ad and 1d in the pound-will have to be raised
by rate. Some County Councils I know in the
Northern area are anxious to be in a position
to strike a rate for the support of a library
of this kind, and I think it would be a very
desirable thing in the interests of education
that the County Council should be empowered
to contribute a rate say, not exceeding a penny
in the pound, which would never amount to
a very large sum, for the purpose of supporting a library of this kind.
It may be said this would be for the education of adults, but it need not necessarily be
SO. I observed in the report of an annual
meeting that was held in England, some time
ago, that Mr. Clynes, the celebrated Labour
Member, made a speech, and he stated that
he was obliged to leave school at the eary age
of twelve years. He attributed his success in
life very largely to being able to avail himself
of a circulating library, and he said that these
libraries could not be too much encouraged.
He said also that this was a democratic age;
that the democracy might be a dangerous
Second Reading. 170
Education Bill-
4 JUNE 1923
thing if it were not properly informed, and
that it was the duty of everyone to do everything he possibly could to inform adults as
well as younger people..
I have ventured to make a longer statement
on this subject than I originally meant, but
I look upon it as a very important subject.
I hope the Government will see their way to
introduce a clause in the Bill to empower
County Councils to strike a rate. At the
present time they are not able to strike a rate
for such a purpose. There might be some way
of doing it under the Libraries Act, if there
was any objection to doing it under this Bill;
but I think as this is an Education Bill, and
as the libraries would really help the education of young people and old people it would
come very well under a Bill of this kind. But
if it can only be brought under the Libraries
Acts I hope the Government will see their
way to introduce a Bill for that purpose.
me
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I may say
it is with great pleasure I have listened to
the introduction of this Bill by the noble Lord,
the Leader of this House. There were several
points that struck me, but one struck
most; and that was where he referred to the
welfare of the children as being the primary
object of this Bill. Now as far as I can see
under the Bill children have to go to school,
and there is no protection for these children
against any petty tyranny that may be going
on. I do not profess to be an authority on
education, but I know in the days when I was
at school I met now and again with a class
of man who, when he could, would tyrannise
over the boys. I would be very glad to see
introduced into this Bill some protection for
children in case of punishment. I understand
in the army no man can be punished without
its being logged. I do not see in a case of
this kind why a schoolmaster should not
have to log any punishment that takes place.
The information, of course, would be confidential, but it would enable those who look
after the schoolmasters to see whether oneperhaps orphan boy, or one child was being
"sat upon," and whether or not the fault was
altogether that of the child.
Therefore I
would be very pleased if the noble Lord would
introduce some clause into the Bill that would
protect children in a case like that, and allow
those who are responsible to see something of
what happens.
I would just say that as far as I can see
the Bill is an effort to solve a most complicated and difficult problem, which has been
found almost impossible to solve in all other
countries. I think we may take credit that
one of the Members of this Senate has introduced into this Parliament a Bill which I
hope will be worthy of that Member, and of
all honour in the years to come.
Hon. MEMBERS: Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: It is quite impossible for any
of us to approach the consideration of this
Bill without experiencing, and without endeavouring to express our deep sense of the
gratitude which is due from all the people of
Northern Ireland to the noble Marquess for
the exhaustive exposition of the Bill, which
he has given this afternoon, and still more
to him--and indeed I include, as he did himself-the staff that has worked with him-
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: Gratitude is due for the unremitting attention that has been given to
this matter, since the responsibility for education in the Northern area was confided to
the noble Marquess. And I think we must
also record our thanks to the hon. Gentleman who represents West Belfast elsewhere,
and to the members of Lis Committee, for
the self-sacrificing devotion which they displayed in giving up so much of their time to
hearing evidence, and subsequently to the
compilation of a report remarkable for its
exceptional value. The report was very remarkable, partly because it had a historical
introduction which was very much wanted,
and partly perhaps because of the appendices, which were extremely useful. I could
not help noting with great satisfaction the
tribute which Mr. Lynn and his Committee
paid to their secretary for the very great
assistance he had given them. I also note
the fact that the noble Marquess recorded his
great appreciation of that same official. Very
much of the success of the Lynn Report, I
think, must be due to that secretary.
The noble Marquess claims that the Bill
which he has laid before us follows in the
main the recommendations of the Interim
Report of the Committee, that it gives to us
a system of education second to none in the
world, that it is based on broad principles of
decentralisation, and decentralisation working on the modern lines of taking the parent
and the teacher into partnership with the
Ministry rather than on the lines of anything
approaching autocratic control. He asks that
we should read every clause and every line
of the Bill with a view to considering what
is in the best interests of the child. He says
he has recognised parental responsibility, and
has given full weight to the feeling that is
entertained, and deeply entertained,
in
Ulster that secular education is not all, but
that opportunity must be given for instruction in other matters; and finally, that the
Bill as submitted to us is a consolidated Bill,
complete in itself, and almost entirely free
from the vice of legislation by reference to
which the noble Marquess has more than
once, both here and elsewhere, declared uncompromising opposition.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
Hear, hear
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
Mr. LESLIE: When we come to examine
the Bill in detail and I may say that inexperienced Parliamentarians like myself are
at a disadvantage in trying to take part in a
Second Reading discussion on a Bill of such
length and such complexity when they know
that they must, as far as possible, keep away
from detail-when we come to look into the
details of this Bill and follow the educational
history of the child of the future, I think we
shall all agree that every claim that the
noble Marquess has put up is being made
good by him. The child, I think by the option only of its parents, begins by going into
the nursery school, and staying there from
two to five. From six he goes to the public
elementary schools. He is entirely protected
from premature employment before the age
of twelve. That was a point which was not
mentioned, but I cannot regard the provisions that are made in this Bill for restraining the employment of children as a detail.
I think it is one of the principles of the Bill.
It is a provision perhaps, in a way, subordinate to education, but it is a very important
one. And this Bill does give absolute protection from employment before the age of
twelve, and makes other restrictions. And
finally the child is given the advantage of
a system of advice when it comes to the consideration of the choice of his career. Meantime he has had opportunities of passing
through the intermediate and secondary
schools which are included in the Bill,
and of obtaining scholarships, and his
health and SO on have been looked
into, and his general interests
looked
after. When we come to the details,
or rather the detailed method of decentralisation, we get first of all the Advisory
Council, of which it is certainly unnecessary
to say anything, except perhaps to congratulate the Ministry on having steadfastly
adhered to the constitutional principle that
there must be no body between the Ministry
and control, and that the responsibility of
the Ministry to Parliament for control must
remain, and not be delegated to any council
whatever.
now
In regard to decentralisation, which I
think was described as the long step, in the
case of our two county boroughs the Lynn
Report appears to be exactly followed, but
when we get to the counties an apparent
departure has been made in the institution
of what it was at one time intended to
describe as Regional Committees.
I
understand it is under consideration to remove the word regional," and certainly I
shall not regret its disappearance. I think
the word, if I remember an earlier speech
correctly, was only adopted for want of a
better, and perhaps because the other words
were all taken possession of. The original
proposal of the Lynn Committee was that
the subordinate committee under the County
a
a
a
a
Council should be in each case either an
Urban District Council or a Rural District
Council. That would, as was pointed out in
the resolution in the Reservations to Report,
have entailed
multiplicity of bodies,
and would not have secured
breadth
of view which usually comes from
body consisting of representatives drawn
from
larger area. I do not wish
to be in any way disrespectful to the
Lynn Report, for which I have a great respect, but I do think that the proposals that
the authorities should be the Urban District
Councils on the one hand and the Rural
District Councils on the other, were to some
extent, if I may use the expression, mutually
distructive, because in your urban areas,
certainly in the 24 or 25 small areas-outside
the two county boroughs we have only Eve
towns with a population exceeding 10,000you may be quite certain that the schools are
very greatly resorted to by scholars who belong to the adiacent rural areas. Therefore,
if you make separate authorities in your
urban and rural areas you get the position
that you are taxing the urban area and you
are sending your rural child into the urban
centre for education, where the rural district
has no voice whatever. That is what I
describe as a mutually distructive proposition, but I do not think from the Report
that the proposal of the new education committes will be really objected to by those
who desire to adhere word for word to the
Lynn Report. After all, that Report dealt
very favourably with the question of possible
combinations of rural and urban areas into
one district, and also laid great stress on the
breadth of view which it found in the representatives from the urban small areas.
I am rather inclined to suspect that the
germ of the proposal for the Regional Committee is to be found in the reservation
under the signature of the hon. Senator
opposite (Mr. Duffin).
These education
committees will have great responsibilities. Unless there is some further
change in the Bill it would still remain possible for these committees to be
composed partly or wholly of people who are
not members of the County Council. That
will operate as a great protection to the
County Councils from the rather heavy
strain of overwork to which my hon Friend
(Mr. Greer) on my left alluded to-day, as the
hon. Senator the Chairman of the Londonderry County Council (Mr. Long) did a few
days ago while speaking on another matter.
The fact that your Education Committee
need not be composed exclusively of members
of the County Council will not only broaden
very advantageously the area of selection,
but will also limit the labours of the County
Council. While I welcome very sincerely the help of the County Council in
preparing schemes for the areas these
