(2) For the purpose of ascertaining
whether a child is an afflicted child within
the meaning of this Act, a certificate to that
effect by a duly qualified medical practitioner shall be required in each case, and
the duly qualified medical practioner shall,
if so directed by the education authority,
or if he is so requested by the parent of the
child, before giving a certificate under this
section, consult the head teacher of the
school (if any) which the child has been
attending, or such other person as the education authority may appoint for the purpose, and a copy of any report made by the
head teacher or such other person shall be
forwarded to the education authority.
The certificate shall be in such form as
may be prescribed by the Ministry.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 55,
page 42, line 37, leave out from the word "as"
to the word "and" in line 38 (the education
authority may appoint for the purpose), and
to insert instead thereof the words: "to the
education authority or to the qualified medical
practitioner may seem fit."
I would ask that sub-section 1 be deferred
until we have dealt with sub-section 4. If
that is agreed to I am afraid I must confess
that I am not sure I understand sub-section
2. The whole purport of this clause is to get
the best possible advice about an unfortunate
child and then to take steps to see that that
advice is acted upon, and for that purpose the
education authority naturally proposes to take
advantage of medical, and it may be, surgical
assistance. It is provided by Sub-section 2,
"that a duly qualified medical practitioner ''—
I hope he will be satisfactory to the authority
-"shall, if so directed by the education
authority, or if he is so requested by the
parent of the child, before giving a certificate
under this section, consult the head teacher
of the school (if any) which the child has
been attending, or such other person as the
education authority may appoint for the purpose." Those last words,
"consult the head teacher of the school (if
any) which the child has been attending, or
such other person as the education authority
may appoint for the purpose,"
are comprehensible if taken in connection
with the education authority, because the
education authority can tell the doctor to consult anybody he likes. There is to be a consultation by the doctor if he is so requested
by the parent of the child, but it becomes
difficult to connect the request of the parent
to some person appointed by the education
authority of whom the parent has no knowledge, and it struck me that we might meet
the whole case by leaving out the words as
on the Paper. That would fully preserve the
rights of the education authority and the
parents also, because if a doctor is going to
visit a child-I am thinking particularly of
a country district-I can quite understand
the parent saying to the doctor, "I wish you
would go and speak to Miss So and So, the
district nurse, or the health visitor, or to the
clergymen." My suggested alteration would
give the doctor power to do so, and take any
report from that district nurse or health
visitor or clergyman, and send it on to the
education authority. I am not sure whether
or not I am asking the Government to take
out words to which they attach value, but for
the moment I beg to move the amendment on
the Paper.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
have no objection to the amendment. There
is no special purpose in the words in the Bill.
The hon. Member may perhaps argue that the
clause as it stands rather points to the appointment by the education authority of
medical referee or some other expert, but I
can assure him this was not intended. Subsection (5) makes the Ministry the referee in
case of doubt. I shall be glad to accept the
amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 55,
page 43, lines 13 and 14, to leave out the
words "the examination" and insert instead
thereof the words ("any examination for
which the education authority has provided
facilities.")
It was for the sake of these words that I
asked the Committee to pass over Sub-section
(1.) If we refer back to Sub-section (1) we see
that the education authority, with the approval of the Ministry, is to provide facilities
for enabling any parent who thinks his child
ought to be dealt with to present his child for
examination. I think the words "provide
facilities" means that the education authority
will not only arrange for a doctor to be at a
certain place where the child's house is at a
given time, but will, if it is necessary, pay
the fare of the child and the parent to the
place of examination, or, it may be, will send
the workhouse ambulance for the child; but
at all events it is quite obvious the intention is to make the thing as easy as possible for the parent who has the intelligence
to have his afflicted child examined. Then
we pass to Sub-section 4, and there we have
got compulsory power. It says it shall be the
duty of the parent to have his child looked
after, but the sub-section goes some way to
bring that child to the doctor. It is quite
obvious that there must be cases where the
child must be removed from. home. It must
be remembered that they find it necessary to
take the child away from home to attend an
dexamination. What I think we should secure
a
e


here, if we can, is that any parent who is
required, under this Sub-section (4), to bring
his child somewhere or other to attend an
examination ought to have just the
facilities as a parent under Sub-section (1). It
thought not to be made any more difficult or
RI
same
But
any more expensive for him to do it. Thereefore I beg to move that we leave out the
words "the examination" in line 14 and
insert "any examination for which the educa-
$tion authority has provided facilities.”
although that is my formal motion, I am not
in the least wedded to my form of words, but
if it is the opinion of the Ministry-I am perfectly certain the noble Marquess can find
pr
some better form of words if it is the opinion
of the Ministry that some other form of words
would be better I am quite open. But as it
stands I think that this particular parent is
not getting quite such consideration as he is
entitled to. Therefore I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member, I need hardly say, always
has my sympathy but not always my concurrence. In this amendment I am not sure
that the words which he wants inserted are
necessary. It is, under Sub-section (1) of this
clause, the duty of the education authority
to provide facilities for enabling parents to
present their children to be examined, and I
feel that these words really are operative in
the sense that he desires the clause should be
operative. I have no actual objection to the
amendment, and if the hon. Member is desirous of pressing the amendment I can
accept it.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps it would be as well
to go on with the amendment. I am afraid
it is necessary.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.

(2) Where a teacher is employed in an
education area in any school to or in respect
of which aid is given out of local rates,

profit under or in the gift or disposal of

CLAUSE 71 (Approval and effect of
scheme).
(1) The scheme when framed shall be
forthwith laid before each house of Parliament, if Parliament be sitting, and, if not,
then within three weeks after the commencement of the next ensuing session of
Parliament, and if neither House of
Parliament, within twenty-one days, exclusive of any period of prorogation, after
the scheme has been laid before it, presents
an address praying the Governor of
Northern Ireland to withhold his assent
from the scheme, the Governor of Northern
Ireland may, by Order in Council, approve
of the scheme. The presentation of an
address as aforesaid shall be without prejudice to the making of a further superannuation scheme.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The amendment I desire to move occurs in
Clause 71, page 51. line 13. It is to leave
out from "be" ("framed shall be forthwith") to "presents" ("it, presents an
address") in line 19, and insert "laid before
both Houses of Parliament, and unless
during the next ten days on which either
House shall sit after the scheme has been
laid before it, either House"
This is merely a technical amendment which
affects procedure in laying schemes and
regulations before Parliament. The amendment brings the procedure into line with the
practice in other cases where papers have
to be laid. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Ordered: That CLAUSE 72 (Provisions in
regard to recorded services in certain cases)
stand part of the Bill,
CLAUSE 73 (Expenses of Education authorities).
(2) The expenses under this Act of an
education authority, not being the council
of a county borough, shall be defrayed out
of the county fund, and the amount required in respect of the combination of
county districts or single county district
assigned to a regional education committee
for the purposes of this act shall be raised
by means of the poor rate equally over so
much of the education area as comprises
the combination of districts or single district so assigned, and as a separate item
of that poor rate to be described as the
education rate.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I beg to
move in Clause 73, page 52, line 29, at end
("described as the education rate") to add
"Provided that for a period of five years
after the passing of this Act the education
rate shall not exceed fourpence." I have
considerable difficulty in moving this amendment, because apparently it is one rather
dealing with financial matters which this
House is not required to take cognisance.
But as it is not a point really out of order
I would like to make a few remarks. I may
point out at the beginning that nothing I
may say and nothing in the amendment is
directed against the actual rate that will be
imposed on the county boroughs or the
county councils. But the whole question of
increase really has been brought forward,
if I may say so, at an unfortunate time. If
it had been in normal times I am sure not
a word would have been said about the present proposed rating. In my opinion, and
in the opinion of all sensible people, the
demand is a most moderate one. But as we
know it is the question of the accumulative
taxes that are going to be imposed on the
community of which at the present moment
the proposed education rate is the smallest.
The malicious injuries rate is of course the
most important one.
I would just point out that in one division
of my county they have struck a rate of 5s.
in the pound. The Ministry of Agriculture
also is looking forward to increasing the
grant for agriculture, so that the ordinary
ratepayer does not look exactly at what he
is paying the money for. It is the total
amount that he will have to pay he looks at.
It is not the amount of the rate that the
county councils are questioning at all. As
far as it may be possible to do so they are
willing to do anything under the Bill in this
first year or any future year. Even in the
first year the county borough or the county
council might be called upon to pay the
whole rate, and then in the course of a few
years we may if we can judge by looking at
our neighbours in England and Scotlandprobably have a very large increase in the

demand for an education rate. My only aim
and object in mentioning the term of five
years during which the rate should not be
en increased beyond fourpence is simply for the
purpose of getting a breathing space for the
unfortunate agricultural ratepayer, who is at
the bottom of his pocket, and that we may
say to him that at any rate for these five
years he will not be called upon to pay more
than this rate. It may not be possible to do
that. Still it would be something.
I am encouraged by the opening remarks
Fate of the noble Leader of the House on the
d Second Reading of this Bill when he said that

This
for a considerable number of years, at any
I rate, it would not be increased beyond this

for him to bind himself in this kind of thing
-still, it would be something if he could, in

would subscribe 8d and the county council
subscribe 4d, that would relieve the situation
altogether. It is only for that reason I ask
that this amendment should be considered.

Mr. ARMSTRONG: If anything could be
done I think it would certainly serve to allay
the uneasiness almost amounting to alarm
which does prevail among the agricultural
ratepayers in the country. I notice the right
Shon, and gallant Member (Colonel Sharman
Crawford) said our expenses will be and are
Increasing. When I joined the county
council, when it was established, the rates
were half, or about half, lower than
they are now. New duties that have
been imposed upon us from time to time
have involved considerable increase in
Of course, as has been said, the 4d
is not the thing they think much about. It
is the chance of increase in future. They
know that in England the education rate
began at a low figure but mounted up
steadily. We do not know that they have
reached the limit there. This naturally
Pauses a great deal of uneasiness. I am aware
that in this House it is difficult to deal with
the matter as it might be dealt with in
another place, but I think it is desirable
-
that the feelings of the ratepayers should be
I laid before the Government on this occasion.
rates.
Mr. DICKSON: I would like to support the
amendment which has been proposed by the
✔ right hon. and gallant Gentleman opposite
(Colonel Sharman-Crawford) and supported by
my Friend on my left (Mr. Armstrong). I
know that the agricultural community are
anxious to have a good system of education,
and they are prepared to pay the rate of 4d
that is mentioned at the present time, but
they really cannot afford to pay a rate that
might go beyond that. I would hope that the
Leader of the House would be able to use his
influence with the Treasury in another place
to see that the rate of 4d does not increase, or
that, if it is increased, the Government's
contribution will go up in proportion. I beg
to support the amendment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The amendment which has been moved by the
right hon. and gallant Gentleman is one
which I would ask him not to press upon the
Government. In the first place it would raise
a very serious question of privilege if it were
passed through this House and went to
another place for consideration. I will quote,
if I may, from the chief authority on Parliamentary practice. Erskine May, who says:-
"The Lords may not amend the provisions in Bills which they receive from the
Commons, so as to alter, whether by increase or reduction, the amount of a rate,
its duration, mode of assessment, or the
limits within which it is leviable."
That is to say, the powers of this House are
limited so far as amendments are concerned
with moneys raised, not only by taxation, but
also by rates. Nevertheless I am certainly
not going to quarrel with the right hon.
Gentleman for having moved this amendment.
I think it is most important that a question
of this nature should be ventilated in both
Houses of Parliament, and it might be said
that the hon. Gentleman and those two hon.
Members who have also spoken were wanting
in their duties as county councillors and as
representing the ratepayers of the community
if they had not put the case forward in the
manner in which they have done.
The right hon. Gentleman's amendment is
one which raises more points than one. He
mentioned the over-burdened ratepayers, and
I think we are in agreement with him that
the burdens which are falling on the ratepayers now, owing to very exceptional circumstances which we all deplore but over
which we have no control, are very oppressive,
but this is not a burden that will be permanent. We sincerely hope that in years to
come the rates will resume their normal proportion, and that the ratepayers will be as
prepared to discharge the obligation which
rests upon their shoulders as are other members of the community in other directions.
I hope the right hon. Gentleman and his
friends who have supported him will not make
me feel a regret that the Prime Minister has
P
Education Bill-

THE SENATE.
Committee.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
consented to give a grant-in-aid in connection
with education. We can realise that the
Prime Minister had all these questions before
him, and that it was in justice to the ratepayers that the State, which after all is the
taxpayer of the Six Counties, should come
forward and relieve the local ratepayers of
certain of their obligations. But I cannot
help feeling that if the Government had not
made this suggestion the taxation might have
assumed a different form.
I do not for one moment think that the
great community of the Six Counties would
have said that sooner than pay a high rate
they would have liked to modify their desires
in regard to education, but they might have
said the scheme of education must be considered very carefully from the financial point
of view, and we must see what we can actually afford and what we cannot afford. But
it does put me in a difficult position when I
am urged by the whole community of the Six
Counties to produce a system of education
which will be adequate for the community we
represent, while at the same time I am told
that the community is so burdened by rates
that they cannot, as the right hon. Gentleman
said, pay more than a 4d rate. It is well that
the right hon. Gentleman makes that remark
in this Committee. If he ventured to say it
on the other side of the water I do not know
what the ratepayers of Great Britain would
say to him. The education rate in England
is something like 3s 6d and the limit we are
asking is 4d. The right hon. Gentleman asks
me if I will keep the rate at that amount,
and I reply that that is up to the county
councils. The county councils formulate the
education schemes which are approved of or
not approved of by the Government, and if
they feel it necessary to embark on a large
expenditure on education I do not think the
Government will quarrel with them if it is
wise expenditure. It is quite impossible for
the Government to pledge itself to subsidise
the rates for a number of years.
In my Second Reading speech on the Bill I
said:
"I should like to allay any fears that may
exist at the present moment that the rate
may develop in a very short space of time
into a heavy burden on the community. I
am quite willing to say on my own authority that the expenditure for the next few
years under the Bill cannot be expected to
be higher than the equivalent of a shilling
rate. That shilling rate will not fall in its
entirety on the people of the Six Counties.
Besides the expenditure on additional
teachers, which naturally falls on the State
alone, I may say here that the Government
hope to be able to make a grant of £2 for
the
the
every £1 of local expenditure. This means
that, for the time being, and for some time
to come, if my judgment is correct, the
education burden on the people of the Sir
Counties cannot exceed the sum of 4d."
If the right hon. Gentleman studies the Bill
and realises the length of time it will take to
bring all the provisions into effect and bringing them into effect is governed by the
financial position in which we find ourselveshe will be in a position to assure the rate
payers who look to him for guidance that they
need not anticipate their burden will be increased for some considerable time to come.
I will venture to trespass on the indulgence
of the Committee to quote the remarks I made
when I introduced the Bill in another place
on 14th March:-
et
h
and
in

"We have taken power under the Bill to
relieve the rates by annual grants from
moneys voted by Parliament. Out of consideration for the novel departure from the
old to the new system, and the difficulties S
under which agriculture especially is at
present labouring, the Minister of Finance,
with his usual willingness to recognise existing circumstances and his invariable desire
to help, has agreed that the Government, for
the present at any rate, may bear a share
amounting approximately to two-thirds of
the total expenditure estimated to be required from the rates. But I am not prepared to pledge the Government in any way
at the moment, and for this reason: the
Colwyn Committee, which is engaged in'
adjusting our financial relationship with
Great Britain, has not yet reported, and,
until we know exactly how we stand in
regard to the Imperial contribution, it is
impossible to say in what proportion the
State will be able to shoulder the burdens
involved by the improvement of education.
What I have said must, therefore, be taken
simply as an earnest of the Government
intention, the fulfilment of which depends
upon the final settlement of our financial
position."
Hon. Members are aware that there is a
Committee sitting which is going very closely
into our financial relations with Great Britain.
It has not yet reported, and it had not reported when I spoke on 14th March. Hon.
Members will see that in the administration
of our finances we are taking quite a different
line to the administration of the educational
finances in Great Britain. There is a provision in the English Education Act that 50
per cent. should be paid by the State and 50
per cent. should come out of local funds. I
was pressed in another place to put something

is
Education Bill-
Committee.
8 JUNE 1923
in the Bill that would stereotype a proportion
on the same lines, but when I asked if the
proportion laid down in the English Education Act would suit hon. Members they one
d. and all said "No," and if I suggested the
same thing to the right hon. Gentleman I
think he also would say "No." It is undesir-
__ able and impracticable to lay down a proportion in the Act or to give any pledge which
may not be fulfilled. I need hardly say that
I fully recognise that the burden imposed by
malicious injury claims is one that we cannot
possibly lose sight of, but I do think we ought
e
to look on the other side of the picture and
realise that instead of being called upon to
pay a rate of 3s 6d as in England, we are
only called upon to pay a rate of 4d. I think
there is a vast amount of consolation to be
derived from that fact.
Mr. DICKSON: In your reply you said we
O object to a 4d rate. We do not, but we hope
et it will not increase. What we do object to is
the 5s rate for malicious injuries. We would
not object to a 3s 6d rate for education if we
df get the 5s rate for malicious injuries wiped
out. We would rather pay for education
than for damage done by scoundrels.

Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: The last
thing I would like to do would be to embarrass the Government, unless they deserved
it. The last thing any ratepayer in the
county would like to do would be to embarrass
the Government, because it might tell against
his own pocket in the end. I am impressed
by the noble Lord's reference to the Colwyn
Committee, and I think I have done sufficient
to ventilate the question. Apparently the
ratepayers are going to get a certain time for
a breathing space, and I think we can give
the Government time for a breathing space
before we return to the charge again when
the circumstances may be quite different. I
know that as long as the noble Lord is Minister of Education and may he long remain so
--(hon. Members: Hear, hear)---we in the
county councils will have his sympathy in
keeping the expenditure as low as possible.
Under these circumstances I beg leave to
withdraw the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following amendment stood in the
names of Mr. MONTGOMERY and Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:-
After Clause 73 insert as a new clause,
"
Where the council of a county or of
a county borough decides to employ any of
its existing officers for the purposes of this
Act, such officer shall be bound to perform
these duties, and shall receive such increase
of remuneration as the Minister of Home
Affairs may determine, provided that such
council may, with the approval of the
Minister of Home Affairs, make a special
agreement with any such existing officer
respecting the terms and conditions on
which he shall discharge the new duties,
and the remuneration which he shall receive
therefor."
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Unfortunately, Mr. Montgomery is not able to be
here to-day, and I am not sure that what
he wishes to arrive at is not already covered
in the Bill. I think it would be better to
make it clear that what he means and what
we all mean is that if the Education Committee apply to the county council for the
use of any of their officials, those officials,
although they are officials of the county
councils, shall be remunerated for this extra
work the same as if they were not officials
of county councils. I do not know whether
county councils would lend their officers,
but if they do we want to make sure that
they will be paid as if they were officials
of outside bodies. I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I am not able to accept the amendment which
the hon. Member has proposed. An education
authority is empowered under Clause 84 of
the Bill to appoint, with the approval of
the Ministry, officers for any authorised
purposes and, with the like approval, it would
be competent under the Bill as it stands for
the education authority to make special
agreements, as suggested in the amendment
of my right hon. Friend, with any existing
officers for the discharge of new duties. To
compel an existing officer to perform certain
new duties is an entirely different proposition. The amendment proposes that
compulsion should be brought to bear upon
the "existing officers" at the option of the
county or county borough councils. I think
my right hon. Friend will agree that that
of itself is objectionable and it also must
be borne in mind that a good many officers
of the local authorities are fully protected
by the Government of Ireland Act. It would
not be possible to accept any amendment such
as this, which would be inoperative so far
as it might prejudice any rights conferred by
that Act, such as retirement on pension. I
think the right hon. Gentleman understands
what I mean that under the Government of
Ireland Act they have rights of pensions, and
if an existing officer is compelled to carry
out certain duties he would immediately take
advantage of those rights and retire on
pension, with the approval of the Civil
Service Committee. I venture to ask the
right hon. Gentleman (Colonel Sharman-
-232
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
Crawford) to withdraw the amendment,
because I am not sure that the clause which
stands in the name of my hon. Friend (Mr.
Montgomery)-who I am sorry is not able to
be present here to-day-would really carry
out the object he has in view, or, if it does
carry out that object, that it does not carry
with it corollaries which he does not
anticipate.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: If the
noble Lord is of the opinion that a county
council officer need not necessarily be employed I cannot agree with him. We do not
want these compulsory powers to create
difficulties. It is only to satisfy the minds
of officials that if they are employed, under
whatever name they are employed, the county
council will be justified in charging the
expense on the county council rate.
Mr. DICKSON: The education rate.
Colonel
education rate.
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD : The
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
That certainly would come under expenditure
on education; but all this would come up
in schemes which would be brought forward
by the education authority and would be
a matter for arrangement with the local
education authority and with the Ministry.
All these matters will certainly be subjects
for the schemes which we will have to decide
upon. I think the hon. Gentleman will find
that Clause 84, to which I have already
alluded, covers the point which the hon.
Member is making. Clause 84 says:
An education authority may appoint, with
the approval of the Ministry as to numbers
and remuneration, officers for any of the
purposes of this Act, and may from time
to time remove any of those officers. Two
or more education authorities may arrange
for the appointment of the same person
to be an officer to both or all those
authorities.
That relates to appointments.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I do not
quite understand whether that meets my
point. That may mean county council officers
as well as other people. The county council
officer will be considered as any other person,
and will have to get the same remuneration as
if he were not an official of the county council.
I think Mr. Montgomery was merely anxious
to have that matter cleared up and to see
that there would be no injustice. Therefore
I beg leave to withdraw the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

for the purpose of participation in any
grant out of moneys provided by Parliament
or in any aid from funds raised by
education authorities by means of local
rates.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
There are two amendments on this page 57
in Clause 82. The first is in line 2 to leave
out "with the consent of the Ministry," and

and confirmation of by-laws of education

CLAUSE 92 (Proof of age)..
Where a child or young person is apparently of age alleged for the purposes of
any proceeding under this Act or a by-law,
order or other instrument made thereunder, it shall lie on the defendant to prove
that the child or young person is not of
that age.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 92,
page 60, line 32, after "where" ("where a
child or young person is apparently of the
age") to insert "it appears to a court of
summary jurisdiction that" and to leave out
"apparently." This clause is taken from
the English Act but it does not appear to
me to be altogether satisfactory, because we
are not told who it is that is to behold the
apparition, and we are not told when or
where we may expect it to manifest itself.
This clause seems to go back to the Cruelty
to Children Act of 1894, and it appeared
again in 1904, but in that
it was
definitely stated that it appears to the court,
and it appeared part of the Act with a
sub-heading, "evidence and procedure."
case
It
was made quite clear that the "apparently"
related to the view taken by the court considering the case. In 1908 and in the Children Act again there was a long section
about apparent age, giving the court power
to say. Unfortunately also in the Children
Act-unfortunately for this purpose there
are two or three other sections where the
word "apparently" is used. One of them,
relating to juvenile smoking, gives to the
park-keepers power in case a child is apparently, etc. In another section power is
given to other people when the age is
apparently, etc.
Therefore, though I think
it is pretty plain that the clause here refers
to proceedings in a police-court, especially as
it follows 91 in the same order as the English
Act-although in the English Education Act
there is a sub-heading, "Legal Proceedings"-
I think it would be better to have it quite
right and insert after "Where," "it appears
to a court of summary jurisdiction that" and
leave out "apparently."
The CHAIRMAN: That will be a separate
amendment.
a
Amendment
(to insert "it appears to
court of summary jurisdiction that")
agreed to.
Amendment (to leave out "apparently")
agreed to.
Clause 92, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 93 (Proof of attendance at school).
Where an education authority are, by
reason of the default of the managers or
proprietor of a school, unable to ascertain
whether a child or young person, who is
resident within the education area of that
authority and attends that school, attends
school in conformity with this Act or any
by-laws, orders or other instruments made
thereunder, it shall lie on the defendant in
any proceeding under this Act to show that
the child or young person has attended
school in conformity with this Act or with
those by-laws, orders or instruments.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave out
Clause 93. I suggest that this clause is not
strictly necessary. It is pretty well covered
by 97, and in any case it is not a clause that
is very attractive. On the face of things it
is putting on the parent the responsibility
for the default of those who are not his
servants - the managers and keepers of
schools. Although I am quite willing to
consent to anything in this way that will
really assist in school attendance and things
of that sort, still there are, I think, some
things we ought not to do. We ought not
to insert in this Bill a clause of this stamp,
especially as all the good of it is, I think,
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie.]
given to us by Section 97. Therefore, I beg
to move the omission of Clause 93.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The last thing I would like to do is to
impose any hardship on parents or to have
some wording in the Bill that might offend
their susceptibilities in any way. The hon.
Member considers that Clause 93 is covered
by Clause 97 and that it may inflict hardship
on parents. I cannot say that I agree with
him in his suggestion.
Clause 93 relates to "Proof of attendance
at school" and the onus of proof is placed on
the defendant, as it should be. Clause 97 goes
further and puts a limitation on right
to certain defences. The two things are
different and I must oppose the amendment.
There can be no question of hardship on
parents. It must be borne in mind that
these two clauses become operative at the
stage when "legal proceedings" are taken.
Before that stage is reached the parent must
have appeared before the education authority; he must have been warned; a school
attendance order must have been issued and
he must have failed to comply with the order.
If the hon. Member will consider these points
I think he will see that Clause 93 is to a
very large extent necessary, and I feel that
the Bill might suffer if we followed his
suggestion and omitted the clause. I hope
he will allow the clause to stand.
Mr. LESLIE: I withdraw the amendment,
as the noble Marquess is satisfied that the
retention of the clause will serve a useful
purpose.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 94 (Certificate of teacher as to
attendance) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 95 (Appearance of education
authority in legal proceedings).
An education authority may appear in all
legal proceedings by their clerk, or by some
member of the authority authorised by
resolution of the authority, and every such
resolution shall appear upon the minutes
of the proceedings of the Authority, but
every such resolution shall, until the contrary is proved, be deemed in any legal
proceedings to appear upon those minutes.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 95,
page 61, line 16, to leave out the word "legal"
("appear in all legal proceedings") and in
line 17, after the word "proceedings" to insert the words ("before a court of summary
jurisdiction")
a
This again is
section copied from the
English Act, but I think it has gone too far.
I
The object is to enable the education authority
to save expense by sending their clerk to the
local police court instead of employing a
lawyer. I suggest that we should leave out
the word "legal" and put in the words
"before a court of summary jurisdiction.”
think that is the intention of the section, and
that is as far as we ought to allow the education authority to go. I do not think we ought
to encourage the education authority to send
its own officers to represent the ratepayers
before any superior court of jurisdiction to the
magistrates.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think the hon. Member has not fully appreciated the words of the clause.
"An education authority may appear in
all legal proceedings by their clerk."
This does not interfere with appearance by
counsel in the ordinary way.
Mr. LESLIE: I must point out that this
gives the clerk to the education authority
power to appear in the High Court.
The MARQUESS OF
There is no harm in that.
LONDONDERRY:
Mr. LESLIE: I do not understand how the
section got in the English Act, but I do not
think it can be disputed that the section as
it is drawn would entitle the clerk of an
education authority, if he had got his resolution, to appear in just the same way as any
ordinary counsel.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think the hon. Member is rather addressing
himself to the very minor point, and he is
not observing that there is nothing in the
clause which interferes with appearance by
counsel in the ordinary way. When we do
proceed to the higher courts it is conceivable
that we should be represented as an education
authority by eminent counsel. There is no
point of disagreement between the hon. Member and myself, and I shall be glad to consider
the matter between now and the Report stage.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I will withdraw the
amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

come into force, they shall be laid before

(3) Regulations made by the Ministry,

within twenty-one days on which that
House has sat next after any such regulations are laid before it, praying that the

so annulled shall forthwith become void,
but without prejudice to the validity of
anything which in the meantime may have
been done thereunder.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 99, page 63, line 8, to
leave out from the word "before" to the word
"
presents" in line 12, and to insert instead
thereof ("both Houses of Parliament and if
during the next ten days on which either
House shall sit, either House ").
This and the following consequential amendment is merely technical, and effects the procedure of laying schemes and regulations
before Parliament. The amendments bring
the procedure into line with the practice in
other cases where Papers have to be laid before Parliament.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 99, page 63, line 21, to
leave out the word "twenty-one" (within
twenty-one days) and to insert instead thereof
the word "ten."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSES 100 (General Returns), 101
(Public inquiries by Ministry and
Ministry of Home Affairs, 102 (Service
of notices etc.), 103 (Evidences of

CLAUSE 105 (Publication of notices).
(1) Notices and other matters required to
be published under this Act shall, when no
particular method is provided or indicated,
be published either by advertisement, or by
affixing the same on the doors of churches
and chapels, and other public places, or in
such other manner as the Ministry may
either generally or with respect to any particular area, place, or notice, or class of
area, place, or notices, by regulations determine, as being in their opinion sufficient for
giving information to all parties interested.
(2) If any person wilfully tears down, injures, or defaces any notice affixed in pursuance of this Act, or any order or regulation of the Ministry made thereunder, he
shall be liable in respect of each offence to
a fine not exceeding forty shillings.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 105,
page 65, line 42, to leave out from the word

or" to the word "in" on page 66, line 1
(by affixing the same on the doors of churches
and chapels, and other public places, or). I
think it would be better if we omitted these
words. Although it is the practice in England-and this section again is taken from the
English Act-to deface church doors with
notices of a public character, in Ireland it is
not the custom, and I am not aware that the
State has any right now to affix notices on the
doors of churches. It will not seriously impede the operation of the statute because it
says that notices will be published by advertisement or in such other manner as the
Ministry may provide.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
accept the amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 105,
page 66, line 7, to leave out the words “tears
down" (wilfully tears down) and to insert
instead thereof the word "removes." In this
suggested alteration I am thinking of bills,
etc., that are hanging loose.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 105,
page 66, line 8, to leave out the word
"affixed" (any notice affixed) and to insert
instead thereof the word "exhibited."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.

This Act shall come into operation on the
appointed day, and the appointed day shall
be such day, not being earlier than the first

may by Order in Council appoint, and different days may be appointed for different
purposes and for different provisions of this
Act, for different education areas or parts
of education areas, and for different persons
or classes of persons.

beg to move in Clause 109, page 68, line 19,
to leave out the word "July" (first day of

"October." This amendment refers to the
time when the Act will come into force. I
think hon. Members will see that this is a
necessary amendment, giving longer time.
Amendment agreed to.

of the Bill.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move in the Third Schedule, page
71, line 19, after "shall" ("shall be transferred to the education") to insert except
where the Ministry otherwise directs."
hon. Members are aware the Third Schedule
deals with the transfer to the education
authority of property from councils
committees that are not themselves education
authorities. It does not follow that in each
and every case where powers and duties are
transferred it would be necessary or desirable that the buildings and property should
be handed over. That, of course, will be
the general rule, but the proposed amendment
allows the Ministry to decide each case on

Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY ;
There is another amendment to Schedule 3,
page 72, line 10 ("as service under the
education authority") at the end to add
"3 (1) Any person who is at the appointed
day employed as an officer of a school
attendance committee under the Irish
Education Act, 1892, shall, unless the
Ministry otherwise directs, be transferred
to the employment of the education
authority of the education area in which
he has been employed as such officer, and
shall perform under that authority duties

LLLL
Committee ED
analogous to those which he performed in
his capacity as such officers and the em
ployment of any such officer under the

Ministry.
may be determined by the
(2) The provisions of Section eighty-seven
of the Local Government Ireland Act, 1898,
shall have effect as if the reference to an
officer of a school attendance committee
under the Irish Education Act, 1892, included a reference to an officer of an
education authority under this Act."
This amendment is designed to meet the
special case of officers of school attendance
committees. The school attendance committees were set up under the Irish Education
Act of 1892, one half of the members being
appointed by the local authority and the
remainder by the National Education Commissioners. The school attendance committees
were empowered to appoint and also to remove
officers and to prescribe their duties and,
with the approval of the local authority, to
fix their remuneration. The salaries and
expenses of these officers are defrayed out of
the local rates. But the Act of 1892 disappears, and with it also disappear the school
attendance committees. The duty of enforcing
school attendance is now placed on the new
education authorities. Some of the officers
of the school attendance committees are
whole-time employees. Others of them are
part-time officers only, and some of them
hold other public offices, such as registrars
of births, clerks to unions, and also clerks
to district councils and other appointments.
The proposed amendment is designed to safeguard the interests of these officers, and it
provides that unless the Ministry otherwise
directs the officers shall be transferred subject
to such conditions as to remuneration and
otherwise as may be determined by the
Ministry. The reference to Section 87 of the
Local Government Act, 1898, in the amendment means that in so far as any such
officer has pension rights those rights shall
not be affected by his having to act as school
attendance officer under the education
authority. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*

in any particular case, that question shall
be determined by the Ministry of Home

FOURTH SCHEDULE (Constitution,
duties, procedure, etc., of committees).
Part I. Regional Education Committees.
The constitution, powers, duties and
procedure of a regional education committee of a county and the term of
office of the members of such regional
committee shall, subject to the provisions
of this Act, be such as may be determined
by a scheme framed by the council of the
county and approved by the Ministry.
Provided that the rules hereinafter set
out shall, except to such extent as the
Ministry may otherwise direct, be incorporated in every such scheme, that is
to say-

committee may resign on giving to the

or council one

so to do.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the Fourth
Schedule, page 72, line 38, to leave out from
"resign" ("may resign on giving") to the
end of line 40 ("writing, of his intention
to do sot") and insert "by giving written
notice to the appointing authority or
council." I think it would be better that a
member of the regional committee should be
able to resign simply by giving written
notice to the appointing authority. If we
follow Scotland and provide a month's notice
it may perhaps frighten them from joining the
committee at all, or the member will simply
stay away during his final month. I do not
think this provision about a month's notice
is of any practical service. Therefore, I
suggest this change. I think that that is
the practice on some of the local committees,
but of course the county council is a more
serious thing. A man has to pay a fine if
he resigns. We do not want anything of
that sort, because it would discourage people
from joining.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This is an amendment which I am not sure
is altogether necessary, and if the hon.
Member will allow me I will consider between
now and Report. During the month that the
notice of resignation would be before the
committee the member would probably absent
himself from the meetings. Moreover, by
the amendments made in this Schedule in
the House of Commons these rules may be
varied with the consent of the Ministry. I
will consider the amendment between now
and Report.
و
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: There is another one on this
schedule on page 73, line 11, dealing with the
quorum of the committee. I suggest that
the committee itself should fix its own
quorum, but the number must not be less
than three. I think if you put a compulsory
quorum of one-third the total number you
are likely to have several meetings of the
committee without any result. I have found
that in the war pension sub-committees and
even in the finance committee of the county
council. It is remarkably difficult to get even
three people together. I have also observed
that when people turn up and find that there
is no quorum they are extremely loth to come
back. I therefore think it would be better
to take out the words "one-third of the
total," etc., and insert "such number not less
than three as that committee may appoint."
Perhaps the noble Marquess will consider
this also. I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The amendment which the hon. Member has
moved is not one to which I am particularly
favourable. I believe that it is a deplorable
circumstance that even in these large committees it does happen on occasions that there
are very few members present. A quorum
of three members in a committee of 15 or 21
is very small indeed. A quorum of one-third
may be perhaps fairly large. I do not feel
somehow that it is too large for the objects
we desire to obtain. I feel that the hon.
Member will agree with me that it is
particularly undesirable that the work of the
regional committee, especially if there is
only one committee in the county, should
devolve into the hands of very few people.
The object which I have had in this Bill is
really to associate the people with the requirements of education; and while education
has been faithfully served in the past by a
great many individuals working very often
single-handed still the practice is not one
we want to encourage. If a great many advantages may accrue, there are a great many
disadvantages which may also accrue, from
the whole jurisdiction of education residing
in the hands of one or two or even three
Committee.. 246

people in the locality. Nevertheless I am
not going to say that I do not see the force
of the hon. Member's arguments where this
matter of practice is concerned, and it is
better to rely on three people to carry out
the duties which are comprised in the Education Act than not to get that higher
measure of a larger quorum.
I accept the
invitation to consider this between now and
Report, and to go into the matter of the
pros and also of the cons.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much obliged to the
noble Marquess and withdraw the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Fourth Schedule agreed to.

6. On confirming an order the Ministry
shall forthwith publish notice of the confirmation, and, if within one month after
such publication, a petition against the order
is received by the Ministry signed by not
less than two persons interested in the land,
then (unless the petition is withdrawn within the prescribed period) the order shall be
provisional only and shall not have effect
unless confirmed by Parliament.
“
Mr. LESLIE: There is a small point here.
I beg to move in the Fifth Schedule, page 75,
line 13, to leave out "not less than two"
("signed by not less than two persons") and
insert any." It deals with difficulties that
may arise after an order has been confirmed
by the Ministry, and gives the right to two
people interested in land to petition. But I
do not see why you should limit the right of
petition to cases of two people interested.
What would happen might be that you would
get a piece of land from two people and they
would have a chance of further hearing. If
there is only one single owner he also might
have something to say. Therefore it is perhaps a little hard to close him out. I beg
to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
After having accepted so many and such
helpful amendments from the hon. Member I
regret exceedingly that I should feel bound
to refuse the last one he should move. I
think the hon. Member will see that if this
amendment were accepted it means that every
order might be taken to Parliament. It is
contrary to the intention of the Schedule
itself. I think also he will recognise that
there are numerous precedents for Departmental orders becoming final without the
necessity of Parliamentary confirmation, and/
I feel that when he considers his amendment
in this light he will see that to carry out the S
Fifth Schedule, compulsory acquisition of
land by the education authority, there is
really no necessity for the requirements embodied in his amendment. I hope I shall be
able to prevail upon him to withdraw his
amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I hope I shall never be implacable, especially after receiving so much
at the hands of the noble Marquess. I have
pleasure in asking leave to withdraw the
amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Fifth Schedule agreed to.
SIXTH SCHEDULE agreed to.

INCREASE OF RENT AND MORTGAGE

ANCE) BILL.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Intoxicating Liquor Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Second time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
The PRIME MINISTER (who was received
with general cheers): I first of all must explain to your House that my appearance here
to-day is one intended to be purely of
courtesy, and in no way takes away from my
feeling that the noble Marquess who leads
the House (The Marquess of Londonderry)
and my noble Friend who sits beside me
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard) would
not be able equally well, if not better, tol
give this Bill the send-off it deserves to have
in this Chamber.
I appreciate very muc
personally the opportunity of coming to the
Senate, and I appreciate the courtesy with
which the Senators have received me to-day.
I have no intention, nor do I think there is
any necessity to make a long speech on the
Second Reading of a Bill which has already
passed through the Commons and which has
been commented upon so widely in the public
Press. Its provisions are well known, but at
the same time there may be points here and
there that it would be useful for Members of
this House to understand before examining
the measure in detail in Committee.
I should explain, first of all, that the Bill
arose from a promise I made in our House to
deal with the question of licensing or
intoxicating liquor or temperance, whatever
name one cares to give it. I made a promise
there to deal with the matter this session in
preference to setting up a Commission of
Inquiry into the wide field which is
necessarily covered by any Bill of the kind.
There was a feeling-1 think probably an
exaggerated feeling-that to commit this
matter to a commission would possibly entail
prolonged delay, and that after all it would
end by the Government having to decide
exactly what they would do. I therefore, with
my colleagues, settled down to do our very
best to meet the grave objections that existed
to the present system, and at the same time
to endeavour to hold the scales as evenly as
possible between those who have vested interests in certain directions and the public
generally, who have brought forward such
grave complaints against a certain form of
trading. The Bill is the result. It has passed
through the other place, and although it is
impossible to say it has given universal satisfaction-I do not think that any Bill dealing
with such an intricate subject could possibly
give universal satisfaction-I hold that it is
a proper Bill, and one of which Ulster will
be proud in the future; and I believe it is a
long stride on the road towards those ameliorative measures that this Administration is
pledged and willing to carry out.
Clause 1 abruptly dashes into Sunday
closing, and although one will find it impossible to get unanimity on the subject anywhere, I believe it will be especially to young
Ulster of the future-a very great benefit if
the custom of using Sunday for drinking purposes is abolished and the custom of all
public-houses and hotels being closed on that
day is established. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) I know well that in the early stages
when the Bill was first brought forward the
outcry was "Class legislation." There is no
class legislation in this Bill. It is quite true
that in the first instance we did not include
altogether Sunday closing in the hotels, but
such were the representations made to us that
we felt obliged to amend the Bill in that
251 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
[Prime Minister].
direction in order that the old abuse of the
bona-fide traveller should be swept away; and
under the Bill it is only possible to
any form of intoxicating liquor within the
hotels if the person is bona-fide resident in
the hotel.
secure
Clause 2 deals with a more complicated
matter. It deals with what has been wrongly
described as the spirit grocer's licence. There
is no such thing as a spirit grocer's licence,
and a considerable amount of confusion of
thought has taken place with regard to that
term. For all intents and purposes, so far
as we here are concerned to-day, there are
only two forms of licences that need be considered. One is the on-licence and the other
is the off-licence. There are occasional
licences such as theatre licences, but practically speaking this Bill deals with full onlicences and off-licences. Clause 2 of the Bill
prohibits in future mixed businesses of any
kind, whether groceries or otherwise, having
an off-licence, and this Clause is in order to
cut away the grievous complaints that have
been made that, especially in the city of Belfast, the grocers' licences have been abused,
so that the people as a whole are desirous to
get rid of them altogether. When we considered the question whether they should be
executed-that is to say, swept away altogether, we were up against a difficulty which
I would explain to the House. There will be
no more grocers or any other people with
other businesses entitled to have off-licences,
but where the houses that are at present used
for grocery and other businesses and have
off-licences-where they can be converted properly into an off-licensed business solely and
entirely, it may be carried on under conditions which are laid down in the Bill, that
is to say, all those persons who hitherto have
been purchasing wine in bottle, draught beer
or stout in a jug, can now go into these offlicensed premises, but the regulations that
we are making have been very much tightened
up, so that it will be impossible for any of
them to consume that liquor on the premises.
An effort was made to confine the sale to
bottles sealed, but after cautious inquiry we
discovered that a certain class of custom is
found for the lighter forms of beer, and
especially amongst those English workmen
who come over and reside in our midst, and
if we were to confine it solely to sealed
bottles the cost to the working man would be
considerably increased, and the particular
class of light beer they desire could not be
obtained, so we were informed. Therefore,
we have taken the precaution of allowing the
Licensing Justices in the future to take a review of the whole circumstances of the case
so that they will be able, having due regard
not only to the off-licence but also to the onlicence, to allow a certain number of them
to remain in existence for off-licence purposes
only.
There have been many other points brought
to our notice with regard to this particular
clause-Clause 2-and it is pointed out that
possibly not so many of the licences will be
done away with as might be hoped for. However, we now come to Clause 3. Clause 3 embodies the compensation which will be payable to the various owners of these houses
where for one reason or another the licence is
not renewed. There is also, of course, a
Schedule to the Bill, and there are other
material portions of it that bear to a certain
extent on Clause 3. But Clause 3 is the Compensation Clause, and it explains very fully
under what conditions compensation can be
obtained. In the first instance the wiping
away of these licences was to be followed by
the grant of compensation based on three
years' purchase, but since then, by, I think,
more or less general agreement, it has been
raised to five years, and five years also for
those interested in the premises, being the
difference between the value of the premises
whilst in possession of the licence and the
value of the premises after the licence has
gone.
Then with regard to Clause 4, it deals with
methylated spirits. I do not think it requires
many words from me to commend to the
House a clause which does away with such
an evil in our midst. In my official position
I have had brought before me many serious
crimes committed under the influence
of this methylated spirit drinking. 1
can only say that in the clause we
have done our very utmost to stamp
it out. We have 60 arranged that it
now practically is synonymous with poison,
and the most definite information must be
given before it can be supplied to any one
under the age of 18. The label has to be addressed to the person to whom it is sold, and
the name of the seller of the methylated
spirit has to appear upon the vessel. I have
had a suggestion put before me that it might
be possible to add to methylated spirits some
form of coloured matter that would be very
noxious and therefore in a practical way prevent anyone drinking it. However, I understand that this matter has been very
cautiously considered by the Home Office in
London or by their advisers, and so far it
has been found impossible to adulterate
spirits without interfering with their utility
in certain classes of trade. Consequently at
the moment we have to satisfy ourselves with
the stringent regulations laid down in
Clause 4.
Clause 5 I may call the Lady Astor Clause.
She recently in the Imperial Parliament got
a Bill through, I believe, all its stages, but
at all events I think she got it through two
stages-the first and second reading-by a
large majority, and that Bill raised the age
from 14 to 18 years. That is to say, no
young person under the age of 18 can be
253 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—
Second Reading. 254

:

F
L
served in future in any of these licensed
premises. We have gone a step further in
this Clause by forbidding the right of any
female to assist in the selling or delivery of
intoxicating liquor, because we felt that if it
was wrong to permit girls under 18 years of
age to actually enter the shop for the purpose
of purchasing liquor it was even more wrong
that they should be allowed to serve in it.
The only comment that may possibly be
made in this regard is, "why does the
Government discriminate between boys and
girls in this particular clause?" The feeling
that we had, after very long and cautious
deliberation, was this: that certain owners
of licensed premises have boys who they
intend should follow in the business, and as
the compulsory age for attendance at school
under the Bill of the noble Marquess (The
Marquess of Londonderry) is 14, we felt that
it would be an unnecessary intrusion, and,
therefore, would introduce irritation into the
life of the family, to say that if the owner
was going to train his son to carry on the
trade that he and perhaps his grandfather
was in he must send the boy about his
business from the time he was 14 until he
was 18 before he was allowed to take any
part in the business. But we have safeguarded girls under 18 by prohibiting them
from performing similar work.
Clause 6 deals with the on-licence as against
the off-licence that is to say, the mixed
licence. These mixed houses are perhaps more
prevalent in the country than in the towns.
One knows the hardware shop with the
counter for supplying liquor in one corner,
and one knows the grocery shop with a similar arrangement, and it was felt when we
were considering the question that it would
be an anomaly to allow the mixed licences
to remain in one part of the country and take
them away in another part. It was also felt
very strongly that had the licensing laws
been properly administered at the time
licences would never have been granted to
shops carrying on the grocery or hardware
business. Consequently, in providing under
this clause for the complete separation of
the sale of spirituous liquors from the other
trade carried
felt that
meeting the general wishes of the public. It
was brought to our notice that in some instances the premises were so small that if the
licensing portion
set aside by the
erection of a new wall or other structure,
the licence would lapse
on account
of the very meagre rating left to that portion
of the premises, and to safeguard people who
would lose their licences in such a way we
have brought them under Clause 6 and thus
under the compensation which is payable to
off-licence holders who lose their licences
under similar conditions in the City of
Belfast.
on we
was
we were
I need hardly trouble the House with some
of the other clauses, which are readily understandable when read, and which I think are
practically supplemental to what I have said,
but I would like to touch on Clause 12, which
deals with Pharamaceutical Chemists, Druggists, and Registered Druggists, three
different sets of people who sell the ordinary
medicines which we are sometimes unfortunately accustomed to take. I may say
that the words, "Registered Druggists" will
be inserted in the Bill by an amendment in
this House if the Members approve, but at
the moment we are dealing with Pharmaceutical Chemists and Druggists. The others
are practically the same, but there is some
technical difference which must be covered.
With regard to this clause, it has to be
pointed out that a class of business grew
up in these chemists shops which was never
anticipated. They had their ordinary
business of selling drugs and medicines and
so on on a doctor's order or prescription, but
gradually-I will not say slowly, because
sometimes very rapidly it was discovered
that by getting an off-licence they were able
to have practically the same class of business
so much objected to in the form of spirit
grocers' licences. It was practically a spirit
chemist's licence, and very much abuse has
been brought to our notice. Therefore, we
determined that at the same time as we were
dealing with the other matters we would take
away from chemists the power to sell drink
for consumption off the premises-unfortunately not always off the premises, but
actually on the premises-which caused so
much mischief in our midst. But if we do
that, here again we must pick them up for
compensation, because in all these cases it has
to be borne in mind that the fault does not
lie entirely with the licencees but with the
lie entirely cith the licensees but with the
past.
essence
I would not like to use the expression that
the law or the authorities were winking at
these abuses, but undoubtedly had the old
R.I.C. been instructed to enforce the law of
the land as it then was, one-third of the
abuses to which I am referring would never
have cropped up. The
of this
measure is that under our new administration
it should be faithfully carried out, and that
the public should be invited to co-operate
with the Government in seeing that it is
properly carried out. If the police are vigilant and the Home Office does its duty, as I
have no doubt it will, I believe that a great
many of the scandals that have been lying at
the root of our civil life will be swept away
for ever. I would like to point out that a
great many of these abuses have become a
habit, almost a local custom, and necessarily,
in order to smash up a habit or break down
a local custom, a considerable amount of discontent must make itself apparent here and
there, but I am fortified by the fact that in

regulations have been laid down the storm
that arises in the first instance
natural death after a year or two.
fore I have no hesitation in saying that in
the times to come we will be thanked as a
Parliament-I will not say as a Government
-for having passed this law, and we will be
thanked even more if Parliament sees that
the Government carries that law out faithfully and well.

Therea
con-
Then I come to Clause 11, which I passed
over for the moment. Clause 11 deals with
the Tribunal, which we have altered
siderably compared with the existing Court,
One of the abuses most strongly brought to
our notice, and by both sides, -by those interested in the trade as well as those keenly
interested in temperance reform-was the
way the habit, or custom had sprung up of
packing the bench as soon as a licensing
case came on, and that it was very hard
to get strict justice done. Under Clause 11
we
propose that the Licensing Court-
Court of Summary Jurisdiction, I think, is
the proper term-shall consist of two resident
magistrates-we have increased it from one
to two-who shall try all cases under the
Act. But I want to point out one small
matter. The noble Lord, the Leader of the
House, will introduce in the Committee
stage a few words which allows the present
system to continue so far as the ordinary
charge of drunkenness is concerned. We discovered that we had made it obligatory upon
the two resident magistrates to hear all these
petty cases, and it was pointed out that that
was overloading their duties and at the same
time placing upon them work which could
properly be done by the Bench in the
ordinary way. Therefore you will understand when the Clause is amended to that
extent that it simply means that those
ordinary cases that are brought up before a
Court will continue to be tried by the Bench,
and all cases that arise under the Act itself
will be tried before two resident magistrates
sitting together.
I do not think that I can be considered
discourteous if I refrain from going further
into the Bill. It would really be anticipating
the Committee stage discussion, and I think
I have as far as possible covered the main
provisions of the Bill as it affects the public
outside. The only other point I would like
to make before I resume my seat is this: That
up to the last moment in the other
House we were not quite decided
as to whether one Clause that we
proposed to introduce would meet with the
approval of the Imperial authorities.
had an experience in connection with the
clause dealing with the illicit distillation of
We
spirits, poteen, and so on, which was included in the Bill in the first instance, and
which the British Government considered, and
I believe quite rightly considered, was ultra
vires for this Parliament to legislate about,
as it was a Reserved Service-Customs and
Excise. But our Attorney-General and other
advisers went over to consult the authorities
on the other side, with the gratifying result
that the Finance Bill which is at present before the British House of Commons will have
embodied in it a clause which we should have
included in this Bill had it not proved to be
ultra vires.
There is one other clause proposed to be
added to the Bill dealing with the strength
of spirits, in which there is a differentiation
at the present moment as between Scotland,
England, aud ourselves. The authorities in
England are prepared that we should come
into line with them so that in future there
will be no differentiation between the strength
of Irish and Scotch whisky. This is a matter
which I think ought to satisfy the trade on
the one hand, and certainly it ought to satisfy
the temperance reformers on the other, because it is in the downward direction of
strength and not in the upward direction,
from 25 to 35. However, that amendment,
which I think is the only amendment of any
real importance, will be moved by the noble
Viscount (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard)
when we come to the Committee stage. Otherwise the Bill is practically as it left our
House. There is to be a slight alteration in
the title for the reason I have mentioned, that
we did not know up to the last moment
whether we would have to include a clause
regarding the illicit distillation of spirits or
not, and we left the title to cover that in case
we had to embody a clause. With these remarks I think I have covered practically the
whole of the ground, and I can only say myself that after hearing vast hordes-I repeat
it, vast hordes of deputations on the one
side and on the other, and along with my
colleagues listening patiently and carefully to
all the arguments in favour of one course of
action and of another, I can confidently say
that the Bill is a fair Bill all round.
Temperance party admit that we have gone
further than our pledge. The pledge was for
Sunday closing, for the abolition of the spirit
grocer's licence, and for the abolition of the
bona-fide traveller, whereas we have in this
Bill gone rather further in clearing up some
anomalies and some, as I say, serious difficulties that have been experienced in the past.
But I would not like to finish without saying
that in all those vast hordes on both sides
that met my colleagues and myself we met
certainly with nothing but the utmost courtesy, and they were most helpful all through
