?
what is a critical business, the framing of a
Bill dealing with such a delicate matter as
temperance reform. I pay a tribute to both
the trade and the Temperance party for the
consistent way in which they helped us as far
as they possibly could and as far as it was
not in opposition to their particular feelings
in the matter all along the line. I believe
when the Bill has had a reasonable chance on
the Statute Book, for a year or a year and a
half, the people as a whole will be satisfied
that it is a Measure of great benefit to the
people of Ulster. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
a
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I think
the advantages of a system which allows
Cabinet Minister to address both Houses have
been very forcibly brought before our minds
in listening to the lucid explanation of this
Bill by the right hon. Gentleman, the Prime
Minister. I think, also, the House will feel
it is an honour that the first Minister, not
being a Member of this House, to address us
should be our Prime Minister. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) That some reform in the
licensing laws was necessary will be conceded
by everybody. We are all anxious to see
Ulster temperate as well as well-educated,
and it is fitting therefore that education and
temperance should go hand in hand. I think
also they have been brought into this House
in the right order, because I believe that
broadly speaking, education is the best means
of producing temperance, and that the most
effective and lasting temperance reformer is
the schoolmaster. He appeals to the pupils'
reason and not to force, and the temperance
he produces is voluntary, is self-reliant, is
not compulsory and not liable to break down
if the policeman happens to be round the
corner. The Government, no doubt, in tackling this question were faced with a great
many difficulties. On the one hand, there
were those who were so obsessed by the evils
of the liquor trade that they were blind, or
at any rate rather short-sighted, towards
some other evils. The trade were naturally
watchful and suspicious of any measure that
might affect their business, and there was
the great body of citizens who, I am sure,
are anxious to see this country sober, but
who are jealous of their liberties and wish
to act fairly to any body of men who have
carried on their trade, which has hitherto
been sanctioned by every Government, and
from the taxation of whom every section, including the prohibitionists, have reaped
pecuniary advantage. Into
these crosscurrents the Government had to dive.
I am not
that they made the
plunge exactly at the right moment.
am not quite
that they made
the plunge at the moment which they
themselves would have selected. I
I
sure
sure
cannot help thinking that they received a
push from some hon. Members in another
place and their followers who could never
resist urging all their friends to the water.
At any rate, whether it was voluntary or not
we are very glad to see that the right hon.
Gentleman is a strong swimmer, and our
duty is only to point out where in our opinion
he will find the best landing place. One thing
I must say, I do believe that if the Government had stuck to their original decision and
had a Royal Commission, before which all
parties and all sections of opinion could
have ventilated their views and which would
have educated the country as to the merits
of the rival interests, it would have saved
a great deal of bitterness and much of the
expenditure which has gone on. There is
much in the Bill that will commend itself
to all parties. It is what is behind the Bill
which, I think, may cause some legitmate
apprehension in our minds. The section of
the population who forced this Bill at the
time they did can only take it as a step
in the right direction, or what they consider
the right direction, and if they are supported
there is no shadow of doubt, in my mind at
any rate, that the legitimate liberty of the
individual citizen will be sacrificed and the
cause of real temperance will not be promoted.
The increasing penalties for the distillation
and sale of poteen, the substitution of resident magistrates for the ordinary bench, the
sale of drink to children, the sale of methylated spirits-all that, I believe, is thoroughly
sound legislation. It is probably right that
spirit grocers should go. I am glad to hear
the right hon. Gentleman say that they are
to get five years' compensation, but I do not
think that even yet they are getting treated
on the same basis as the spirit grocers in
England and Scotland. I certainly think
they are entitled to be treated as generously
as if they lived in England or Scotland. I
am not much in sympathy myself with the
objection to Sunday closing in Belfast, because Belfast was an isolated case and I do
not see why it should be privileged. I am
not at all certain that some houses should
not be opened in every town where
they can be properly watched and
where it would be possible to get a glass of
beer on Sunday afternoon if one wants it, but
I do think the entire abolition of the bonafide traveller is a hardship which I hope very
much may be altered in Committee. Of
course, if the Government decide they will
not alter it, it is hardly worth our while to
push it. What I do think is this, if I
bicycled or motored on a Sunday afternoon to
an hon. Senator's house, I have not the
slightest doubt that he would offer
drink. I probably would not take it, and if
he gave me a chance of a cup of tea I cerme a
R
259 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—

THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
[Colonel Perceval-Maxwell]
tainly would prefer it, but I do think that if
a man bicycles or motors down to Bangor or
Larne and wants a glass of beer, which I
think is a perfectly innocent thing for him to
want, it is a hardship that the Legislature
should prevent him from getting it. I believe there is no part in the Prime Minister's
speech in which he introduced this Measure
in another place which was more universally
approved than that in which he expressed a
hope that he would not have to introduce
any further licensing legislation in his time.
I think we may congratulate the right hon.
Gentleman on being in the fortunate position
of being able to ensure the fulfilment of his
own wish as no one can force him into introducing legislation of which he does not approve. We all hope that he will be spared
for many years to be our Prime Minister.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I am certain
he will have the great mass of the citizens of
Northern Ireland behind him in resisting
every attempt to substitute what I may call
the bondage of fanaticism for what is admitted sometimes to be the slavery of intemperance. I think this Bill, with some amendments, will prove a benefit to the community,
and consequently I have great pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading of the
Measure.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: May I
join with my right hon. and gallant Friend
on the left (Colonel Perceval-Maxwell) in returning our congratulations to the Prime
Minister on his first appearance in this House?
I am sure there is no one in this Senate who is
not wishing to see an increase of temperance
in Northern Ireland, and I am only going to
make a few remarks upon a subject on which
the whole Bill hangs. After all, why have
we a Bill on this subject? I suppose to the
ordinary person, to myself, it seems its object
is to try to reduce the consumption of liquor,
or to keep it within proper bounds. I am
afraid there is one aspect of this Bill that
does not carry that out. We can be just as
intemperate in the house as in the publichouse. I am going to make
few remarks on what have been the results in
another city across the water in regard to
legislation on the same lines. I am confining
myself to the method of Sunday closing. The
reason for that is the knowledge of what is
taking place in the other city. We know
that total closing on Sunday has not had the
desired effect, but has had, or the contrary,
the effect of increasing the sale of liquor.
In this city, which of course is well known
to everybody. I heard a publican the other
day say that he did not want the Sunday
closing repealed because, he said, "I sell more
liquor now on Saturday than I ever did on
Saturday and Sunday combined." What does
a
a
canny
a
A
that mean? It means this, that you are
driving the men from a certain amount of
publicity in the open. We are all human
and we know what goes on. Some of us can
resist it, but some cannot resist it, and these
men who cannot resist it and who get their
drink on Sunday will probably buy a double
quantity on Saturday and consume it in their
own houses. You are making it worse for
the women and families of those men who
will be drunk on Sundays in their own
houses. What I would have liked to have
seen in this Bill is in the English Act. There
is no one here more anxious than I am to see
temperance as it ought to be. It ought to be
in the open, as in the English Act, and so
prevent any of this talk of class legislation.
I should have liked to do away with the bonafide traveller, which to my mind has been
one of the greatest curses, but if publichouses opened a couple of hours, shall we say
from three to five? nobody can complain of
that. May I just, as a small illustration
of what I mean, tell what
Scotsman can do, and this is
matter of my own personal knowledge?
great many years ago when I was soldiering at
Aldershot there were two regiments stationed
there, one Irish and the other Scottish. The
Irishman naturally had his whisky or his
beer, and perhaps took a little too much and
was brought up for minor punishments. The
Scotsman was not brought up much in the
same way. Suddenly one night there was an
alarm of fire at the barracks and the Irish
regiment turned out very well, but "divil"
a man of the Scottish regiment at all. The
reason of that was that it was on a Sunday.
When the matter came to be inquired into it
was found that every Scotsman of the regiment
was drunk because he had taken a bottle of
whisky to bed with him and had drunk it
during the night. This is what will happen.
A man will take drink home and will drink
it in his own home. I know the right hon.
and gallant Member (Col. Perceval-Maxwell)
said there is no use in making amendments
because the Government have made up their
minds to do this, and I do not purpose
moving any amendment, but I do think it
is a hardship, as the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman has said, that legitimately a man
cannot get a drink on Sunday afternoon.
If it could be done, and with proper arrangements I believe it could and that the consumption of drink will be lessened my last
words will be that if the Government find it
so there may be some amendment so that a
man can get a drink on Sunday. I believe
that drinking will not be increased, but as I
say, lessened by
generous Government
making such an amendment.
a
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to join with the two hon. Members who
have spoken in saying how pleased I am to
261 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

12 JUNE 1923
Second Reading.
T


hear the Prime Minister in our House.
Certainly the Bill which he has introduced
is one which is very important. In fact I do
not think there has been any legislation for
a number of years that has created more
interest amongst the people in this city than
the Intoxicating Liquor Bill. It is topic
amongst all classes, particularly the working
classes. Even the great Insurance Act when
it was passed in the Imperial House, and it
really affected the workers quite as much,
was not considered in the same way as this
has been considered. It has, I agree,
For
certainly some good points about it.
instance, the restriction with reference to
methylated spirits is one that is very
necessary because there are some poor, low
down creatures who are even prompted to
make use of methylated spirits, so that even
for such as that it is very necessary. I would
go further and say that even the restriction
on the bona-fide traveller is necessary, because
it was abused, and I am speaking with
experience, because I have moved about
among that select class to which I belong-I
mean the working class. I have moved about
amongst them roughing it on a Saturday night,
on a Sunday, and through the week. I know
exactly their habits. When I am speaking
here I wish to express exactly the views I
have heard from all classes.
The bona-fide traveller was much abused, and
as to the spirit grocers, I have not heard one
complaining of the Government action with
reference to that.
a
Then the
The working classes do
think that they are used for wrong purposes,
and therefore there is no complaint from any
quarter against the Government for dealing
with the spirit grocers. I presume that the
trade may have some complaint to make
against the Government, for removing
luxurious livelihood, for example, because I
believe they made capital profits out of very
small concerns in back streets.
clause dealing with age, or, as the right hon.
the Prime Minister said, the Lady Astor
clause is another thing that is needed, because
everyone of us--no matter how we feel with
reference to intoxicating drink-is most
anxious that children should not be brought
into contact with it too early in life. We
desire that they should be of an age when they
will be able to choose for themselves. I remember very well I had not tasted strong
drink until I had to leave Belfast to seek
employment in Glasgow. It was there I fell
in with company and I found it a great
pleasure in the company I was in to have
a glass of beer on a Saturday night. I am
speaking of Glasgow where there was Sunday closing at that time. I can assure you
there was drink to be had on Sundays,
though I have no personal experience of that.
Undoubtedly the part of the Bill that has
serious consideration
been given most
amongst the working clases is that clause
by which it is intended to close the publichouses on Sunday in Belfast. Now, it is injurious as affecting the working classes. It
has already been said that drink may be
carried in on Saturday night and consumed
amongst the children on Sunday, because in
an ordinary workman's house there is only
one living apartment. What is done by the
father and mother is seen by the younger
members of the family, so that if, as I believe will be the case-because I know it
is done now, even when the houses are open
at two o'clock-a considerable number of
the workers take drink home with them on
Saturday night in order to have an early
drink on Sunday morning, what may we expect in future when the houses are closed
all day. It is very likely that a good number
of them will bring in considerable quantities
into the house, and probably make beasts
of themselves by getting drunk. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
I am certainly strongly opposed to the
opportunity being put in any man's way to
enable him to get drunk. I protest against
drunkenness, and I do not think anyone will
defend excess, but what I am anxious about
is that the freedom of the ordinary working
man shall not be restricted in any way. It
is by this Bill, and you cannot convince the
worker that it is not class legislation. I
do admit that the Government has no intention in that direction at all. (Hon Members: Hear, hear). I know there is no one
better inclined or more anxious for the
welfare of the people of the Six Counties
than the present Members of the Cabinet,
the Prime Minister included. (Hon Members: Hear, hear). I am satisfied on that
score. But if you close the public-houses on
these three hours on Sunday you cannot
convince the workers that it is not a slap
at them. It is entirely a restriction on the
workers, because it affects no one else.
The gentleman in the villa has quite
sufficient in
treat himself and
his friends. The working man has no place
to put it. He can only consume it as quickly
as possible. It certainly is a great pity that
the Government if they have been forced, as
the hon. and gallant Member (Colonel Maxwell) said a few minutes ago, by any of those
who hold such strong views it is a pity, and
I wish even at this last hour that this revising Chamber of ours, where there is so
much intelligence and wisdom-I hope that
even in this Chamber they will be able to do
something to deal with that portion of the
Bill and that particular clause.
to
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Hear,
hear.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Our Prime
Minister has said that young Ulster would in
263 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—
Second Reading. 264
THE SENATE.
a
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
the time to come praise the Government for their action. There may be
time coming when people will
be quite satisfied to have houses closed
on Sundays, but I do hope before that
time comes that the poor people will be better
housed, and if they are required to stay in
their homes they will have decent homes to
stay in. But bear in mind that to-day you
have a considerable number of hovels in the
City of Belfast. Men have to get out somewhere on Sundays. I am quite sure that
Members here will hardly realise that there
are some houses so small that the families
would be crammed together if they all had to
remain in. The worker, the head of the
family, is out on Sunday. He goes for
walk. When he is having a walk through
the country he likes to drop in to have a
Certainly there is
refreshment.
no great
pleasure in having a stroll if you cannot get
refreshed on the way to make you feel comfortable. On the other hand, the workers go
out in the city on Sunday, and if they feel
inclined, go in along with their friends and
have a refreshment.
a
I may say that so far as Belfast is concerned it has not been the conduct of the
people in Belfast that has brought about the
necessity for this legislation. I do not know
any city where the working classes that move
about the centre of the city on Sunday are
better conducted-(hon. Members: Hear, hear)
-or better clothed. No other place is more
respectable. Belfast is really an example for
any other city so far as that is concerned. I
took the opportunity in the city last Sunday
to look round. Of those leaving the publichouses there was not one showing the slightest
symptoms of that dread disease. I do say it
was not the conduct of the people of Belfast
that brought about any necessity for this
legislation. At the same time I will agree
that there are good points in the Bill
which I am strongly in favour of, but
even at the last moment I do hope the House
may be able to do something to delay the
operation of that particular clause until the
time comes when the workers are housed
better and more comfortably, and when they
will not be compelled to go out and look for
a club or some other place where they can sit
down or stand up and have a conversation
and a drink with their friends. I certainly
wish to support the Second Reading, but I
do hope when the Committee stage arrives
there will be something introduced, by the
Government even, to relieve the anxiety of
the workers.
Mr. WOODS: I find I am quite in agreement with a considerable part of what has been
said by the two hon. and gallant Gentlemen
who spoke previously. I claim that I have
the right to speak with regard to the temperance question. I have been an advocate of
temperance all my life, and so far as opening
or closing public-houses is concerned it does
not matter to me if there were none of them
open at all, because if all the people of the
Six Counties were of the same opinion as I
am they would not require any public-houses.
At the same time, I speak for the freedom
of the people, and if a man is inclined to go
into a public-house and get drink he has a
perfect right to do so. That is the attitude
I take up, and I contend rightly or wrongly
that we shall never make sober people by
legislation.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: Hear,
hear
Mr. WOODS: I have said that all along to
the extreme Temperance party. They have
wanted legislation in years gone by, and
they have overstepped themselves on many
occasions, and I have said the only thing to
create a feeling of sobriety is education, as
stated by my right hon. and gallant Friend
on my left (Colonel Perceval-Maxwell). Now
if you close the public houses entirely on
Sunday you will have before you exactly the
position shown by the right hon. Member on
my right (Colonel Sharman Crawford). The
workman is bound to get it and he will get
it.
Unfortunately, he will drink in a place
where he will perhaps get more than that,
and which will be a great deal worse for the
health of coming generations. These are the
things that strike me far more forcibly than
keeping a man from getting drink in a
public house. I say that is one of the things
that will have to be amended in this House
this closing of public-houses on Sunday.
There is a considerable part of the Bill
that I do agree with. Perhaps I would have
gone further than the promoters of the Bill.
I would have abolished spirit grocers entirely.
I would have abolished all public-houses
round what may be called the back streets
or entries. I would have nothing but publichouses on public thoroughfares properly controlled. That is my idea of the licensing
Bill that should have been brought forward
by this Government. I think if we had done
that we would have had very few complaints
from working men. I take it upon me to
say that if a plebiscite vote were taken you
would find a huge majority in favour of
leaving the public-houses open for three
hours on Sunday. You would also find a
huge majority to cater a little for the bonafide traveller. I agree that the bona-fide
traveller has been abused in the past, That
was want of proper administration,
Colonel PERCEVAL - MAXWELL: Hear,
hear.
Second Reading.
Intoxicating Liquor Bill.

Mr. WOODS: A bona-fide traveller going
out a few miles on Sunday might receive one
drink or two drinks in a house. That would
be quite sufficient if the place were properly
administered. Instead of that they were
allowed to get drunk. But if a few people
go to extremes it is not fair that other people,
perfectly able to control themselves, should
be punished on their account. I do trust
that this House will see their way to bring
in amendments to that particular clause.
Mr. McDOWELL: It has given me considerable thought at the moment, particularly
when I heard my two colleagues, to know if
I am a working man or if I live in the midst
of working men. I can assure you that I
approve of every word that the Premier has
said in his introduction of the Bill. I am in
favour of the total closing of public-houses on
Sunday.
Sir JAMES JOHNSTON: Hear, hear.
Mr. MCDOWELL: The only objection I
have to the Bill is leaving any place, hotel
or otherwise, open. I think I have travelled
as much and seen as much of this world
as perhaps most hon. Members of the Senate.
I have been a working man all my life, and
my experience has been that even many of
those who are most addicted to drink wish
that something could really be done to save
them from themselves. It is all very fine
for my two colleagues to say that if a
plebiscite of the Six Counties were taken the
great majority would vote in favour of
Sunday opening. I dispute that point, for in
my opinion 75 per cent. of the working classes
to-day would vote for the Bill in its entirety,
and it is a slur and a shame to impose such
an assertion on the working men, as that
they wish legislation to be enacted to have
the public-houses open.
I am not going to make a great speech. I
have lived in a district-in one street aloneperhaps 17 or 18 years where I could not have
gone out of that house to any part of the city
without passing either public-house or spirit
grocery, and I have seen a young man taken
into a public-house where he spent all his
wages and then was kicked out for doing it,
and to think that working men should be in
favour of such legislation as that! I cannot
tolerate the thing for a moment. I am not
going to detain the House so far as this Bill
is concerned, only to say that while I belong
to no temperance organisation, nor am Ia
fanatic so far as temperance reform is concerned, I am in favour of some well thought
out scheme that would enable men to protect
themselves. We have a right to come to their
assistance and help them to help themselves.
Talk about the freedom of the working man!
It is very strange that with all their freedom
once they come outside a public-house and
make the slightest stagger there is always
one big stalwart man ready to grab them by
the shoulder and take them down to the police
court where they get fined 40s. If you have
freedom to imbibe, why does the law see that
you get punished? But you say he has taken
in excess. We are not all blessed with that
great, strong determination and power of will
that we can resist. Consequently, we have
to take these poor fellows into consideration.
I am thoroughly in favour of the Bill. I say
"God bless the Bill." I believe the Senate
will give a royal reception to it and pass it
unanimously when it comes to the Third
Reading.
So
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
From the interesting debate to which we
have just listened and in which Members
from all sides have spoken I think I may say
that the Bill has been favourably received.
The right hon. and gallant Member, Colonel
Maxwell, in his speech referred to the great
pleasure and honour it is to have my right
hon. Friend, the Prime Minister, to support
the Second Reading of this Bill in this House,
and I need only say that I know that every
Member cordially agrees with what the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman has said.
far as I have gathered from the debate it
appears to me that most, of the criticism of
this Bill has been directed to Clause 1, which
deals with the total closing of public-houses
on Sunday. Senator Joseph Cunningham and
Senator Woods spoke very strongly on this
matter. Senator Cunningham held that the
closing of public-houses on Sunday would lead
to more drink being taken home on Saturday
night for consumption on Sunday, and the
criticism was also made that the closing of
the public-house on Sunday was interfering
with what I may call the liberty of the
subject. I think that point was made by
Senator Woods. I need hardly tell this
House that nearly all laws are an infringement of the liberty of the subject, and the
question is whether the laws that the Government frame and pass are for the benefit of
the community as a whole or only for the
benefit of the few. If a law is only for the
benefit of the few then it is a bad law.
But the Government maintain that Sunday
closing is undoubtedly for the benefit of the
community at large. I would like to remind
hon, Members that one of the most democratic countries in the world has got Sunday
closing, and that country is Australia, which
has a Labour Government now and has naa
a Labour Government for many years,
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: There are
no greater tyrants than some of the Labour
men.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I may also mention that the Isle of Man has
recently passed an Act closing public-houses
267 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Rent Restrictions Bill.
THE SENATE.

If
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
on Sunday. The hon. Member, Senator
McDowell, who also represents the Labour
interests in this House, spoke wholly and
very forcibly in favour of Sunday closing,
so I think I am perfectly justified in saying
that the Labour representatives in this House
are not unanimous on this question of the
total closing of public-houses on Sunday.
There is another point, and it is this.
you open the public-house on Sunday for even
a few hours it will entail Sunday labour for
those who have been working all the week,
and I do think it would be a distinct blessing
to all those assistants, barmen and others,
who work right through the week if they
could get Sunday off like everybody else. I
think it was Senator Joseph Cunningham who
expressed the hope that during the course of
the Bill through this House some amendment
would be introduced whereby the publichouses would be open for a certain period on
Sunday. I must tell the hon. Member that
such an amendment would cut clean across
the whole principle of the Bill. The title of
the Bill is
"To make provision for the closing on
Sunday and Christmas Day of certain premises licensed for the sale of intoxicating
liquor,"
and if the Government were to agree to any
such amendment it might as well not proceed
with the Bill. I believe that Sunday closing,
which has been in operation in the Six
Counties for something like 25 years but not
in Belfast, will be of great benent to every-
Lody, and, although I am not a prophet, I Go
not think that all these evils of extra drinking
on Sunday in the home will result from this
Bill. On the other hand, I believe it wili
result in the people getting accustomed to
not going to the public house on Sunday, and
that in the end Ulster will be thankful that
this Parliament introduced this Bill and,
as I hope, passed it. As regards the point
raised about the bona-fide traveller, there is
no doubt that in the past the true bona-fide
traveller, if he existed at all, was a very rare
person, and we do not desire to allow the
abuse of that privilege to be continued any
longer. One hon. Member, I think Senator
Joseph Cunningham, alleged that this was
class legislation. I cannot agree with him.
We have no desire to introduce in this Parliament any legislation that could be called class
legislation. We give the same privileges to
all clubs, whether they are working men's
clubs or clubs for people better off. They are
all treated exactly alike, and we treat the
big hotel and the small hotel exactly alike.
Therefore I cannot admit that this is in
any sense class legislation. Certain other
points were raised which I think were really
Committee points, and I trust that in Committee we shall discuss this Bill, as we have
done to-day, in a calm spirit and in no sense
in a spirit of antagonism. Although we may
make some small amendments in Committee
I hope the House will pass the Bill practically as it has been introduced in this House.
On Question, Motion, "That this Bill be
now read the Second time," agreed to.
Bill accordingly read the Second time and

Increase of Rent and Mortgage
Interest Restrictions (Continuance)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading, read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move the Second Reading of this
Bill. The effect of the Bill is to continue
the existing Act of 1920 for the period of six
months. As hon. Members are aware, the
present Act expires on the 24th of the present
month.
As has already been explained in
another place, the Government of Northern
Ireland have to all intents and purposes
accepted the principle that it would be
desirable to allow the Act which exists at
the present moment to lapse on that date,
and a Committee has been set up so as to
recommend what modifications, if any are
necessary, should be brought into effect in
continuing the Act. As hon. Members are
aware, the Committee is at present engaged
on this work, and they have already submitted to the Minister of Home Affairs an
Interim Report, in which they express the
opinion that while the control of rents should
be removed at the earliest possible date, it
is not wise or expedient that that control
should be removed at the present moment.
This Committee have pointed out also that
they would not be able to make a report
which could be termed in any sense a complete
report for some time to come. I think I may
say we are satisfied that even if we got the
report of the Committee by the end of the
present month it would not be possible for
the Government or for Parliament to pass a
Bill on the lines of the recommendations of
the Committee before the end of the present
Session. Therefore, hon. Members will see
that it will be necessary for us to wait until
the Autumn Session for the introduction of
the final Bill, and we shall then be able in
the light of all the interests concerned, and
Scutch Mills Bill-
Second Reading.
12 JUNE 1923

DE
优

having had the benefit of the Committee's
report, to produce a Bill which we hope will
be satisfactory to all the parties concerned.
One important matter is that the Rent
Restrictions Bill is not an encouragement to
build. The Committee which I have referred
to have in their Interim Report laid very
special stress on the great importance of
encouraging the building of houses in
Northern Ireland, and I think it is right that
I should state for the information of the
House that this matter has been engaging
the attention of the Minister of Home Affairs
and also the Minister of Finance. There are
certain inquiries and investigations being
conducted which remain as yet to be completed which refer to the extent and the form
of encouragement that the Government of
Northern Ireland can provide, and what
would be best suited to the needs and circumstances of private and other builders.
should like to say that we do most sincerely
hope that in a very short time the Government will have completed its consideration
of this matter and will be in a position to
announce the terms it is able to offer. In
any event I think I may safely say now for
the information of those persons in Northern
Ireland who are anxious to build-and this
has already been said by my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs, in
another place that assistance will be offered
to public authorities and also to private
builders for the building of houses that will
I
be suitable for the working classes. I am also
Fauthorised to give this assurance-that when
we bring before this House in the Autumn
Session a Bill for continuing the Act of 1920.
to such a date as may be advisable, and with
such amendments as may seem desirable, we
shall also be able to lay before Parliament
a Bill that will deal with housing. I think
I am entitled to express the hope that builders
in Northern Ireland will take the fullest
possible advantage of our proposals, and I
do sincerely trust that during the next year
we shall see a very rapid development of the
building of the smaller houses which will,
to a very large extent we hope, meet the requirements and needs of the working classes.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill accordingly read the Second time, and
committed to a Committee of the whole House
for to-morrow.
Scutch Mills Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD :
I beg to move the Second Reading of this
Bill. It is a short and a simple Bill, and
one which, I think I may say, passed through
the other House with very little criticism.
The main object of the Bill is to prevent
scutch mill owners from keeping tow as part
payment of the charges for scutching flax,
and to provide that mill owners shall not
purchase, or offer to purchase, any tow until
the flax has been scutched, and then only by
weight. The Bill also requires every scutch
mill owner to declare whether or not he has
insured the flax or tow of his customers
against fire.
The system whereby flax mill owners purchased tow or accepted it as payment or part
payment for scutching flax dates back to
something like 35 years ago, when, as a result of the depression in the flax industry,
mill owners turned their attention to tow as
an additional source of profit.
The result
was that this system became established, so
that in most districts all tow at the mill was
left to the mill owners to scutch it or dispose
of the product in bulk. In this way the mill
owners obtained complete control over tow
for a considerable time.
Previous to the war farmers became dissatisfied with this method under which tow
was retained by the mill owners. They were
unable to ascertain the amount of tow
scutched from their flax, and they had no
means of finding out its value, while a suspicion existed that the mill owners were
making undue profits out of tow and that due
care was not taken to prevent wasteful
scutching. It was also believed that the
systém tended to affect adversely the millers'
interest in the provision of improved machinery for the production of flax. This
irritation, owing to the method I have just
stated, spread very widely amongst growers,
so that in December, 1917, a Departmental
Committee was appointed to inquire into this
matter. The committee reported that the
system under which the mill owner is in a
position to exercise an option was pernicious
and wrong in principle. The committee recommended that the practice of accepting tow
in payment or part payment for scutching and
the purchasing or negotiating for the purchase of such tow until it is scutched is
prohibited An order giving effect to this
recommendation was made under the Defence
of the Realm Act and was in force in August,
1922, when this and other Orders made during
the war lapsed. The Department of Agriculture and Technical Instruction in September,
1919, advised the Chief Secretary that the
provisions of the Order should be made permanent by Act of Parliament. Pressure of
business, however, in the Imperial Parliament prevented this suggestion from being
adopted. Since the Ministry commenced
to function continuous pressure has been
brought to bear to have legislation on the
subject introduced in the Northern Parliament.
After careful consideration, the
present Bill was prepared and, as I said
before. may be regarded as an agreed
measure. It has been approved by the in-
271 Belfast Corporation Bill.

THE SENATE.
Adjournment.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
terests concerned, namely, The Ulster Farmers' Union, The Scutch Mill Owners'
Association, and The Ulster Agricultural
Organisation Society. It has also been approved by the Ministry's Flax Advisory
Committee, on which the above interests and
also the flax spinners and the Ministry are
represented. This Bill has been drafted so
as to ensure the attainment of the objects in
view with the minimum of official supervision.
It is in fact an attempt to obtain increased
efficiency in scutch mills while at the same
time carefully avoiding the system of wartime control under which, as stated, every
scutch mill was required to be licensed. I
beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
BELFAST CORPORATION BILL.
Brought from the Commons with the
amendments made by Joint Committee,
agreed to without amendment.
Adjournment.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): The first amendment
I have to move is in Clause 8, page 14, line
34, after the word "may" ("authority may
discontinue"), to insert the words "subject
to the provisions of this Act."
an
The object of this and the next amendment
is to make it clear that
education
authority may discontinue or change the site
of a school transferred to them if the transferred school is unnecessary or if a change of
site is desirable. If there is an absolute and
unconditional conveyance to the education
authority the school becomes in effect, a
provided school; but in dealing with the cases
of transferred schools the education authority
must have regard to the proviso to Clause 9
and comply with the terms and conditions on
which the transfer has been effected.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move in Clause 8, page 14, line 35,
after the word "by" (provided by them) to
insert the words "or transferred to."
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 13 (Co-operation with voluntary
agencies).
In the exercise of their powers in relation
to the supply or provision of nursery schools
or means of recreation, or the health,
nourishment and physical welfare of children attending nursery schools, the education authority may encourage and assist
the establishment or continuance of voluntary agencies, and may associate with
themselves representatives of voluntary
associations for the purpose, and no expenditure shall be incurred by the education authority in the exercise of those
powers unless they can show to the satisfaction of the Ministry that the activities
of voluntary agencies are inadequate.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The next amendment which I beg to move
occurs in Clause 13, page 16, line 10, after the
word "or" (or the health) to insert the
words "in relation to."
This is merely a drafting amendment
making for clearness in expression. There is
no change whatsoever in the sense of the
clause.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 32 (Power of education authority
to make school attendance orders).
If it appears to the education authority
that any parent, who is required by or
under this Act to cause his child to attend
school, has neglected or failed to perform
his duty in that respect, the education
authority shall have power, after due warning to the parent, to summon him to appear before them with or without the child,
and to require from him information and
explanation as to his neglect or failure of
duty. If the parent, or some person on his
behalf, either does not appear, or appears
and does not satisfy the education authority
that he has not neglected or failed in his
duty, the education authority may make an
order in writing (in this Act referred to as
an "attendance order") to the effect that
the child shall attend some school willing
to receive him and named in the attendance
order, being either such school as the parent
may select or, if he does not select a school,
then such school as the education authority
think expedient, and the child shall attend
that school in such regular manner as is
specified in the attendance order. The
education authority shall cause a copy of
the attendance order to be served upon the
parent by registered letter, or in such manmay be prescribed by by-law
of the education authority, and a certificate
under the hand of an officer of or other
person duly authorised by the education
authority, to the effect that an attendance
order has been made and served upon the
parent, shall be received as evidence of
what is stated therein.
ner as

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :

the words "duly qualified.'' This is

CLAUSE 38 (Restriction on employment of
children to prejudice of health, etc.).
(1) The education authority, if they are
satisfied by a report of a registered medical
practitioner or otherwise that any child
under the age of fourteen is being employed
in such a manner as to be prejudicial to
his health or physical development, or to
render him unfit to obtain the proper benefit
from his education, may either prohibit, or
attach such conditions as they think fit,
to his employment in that or any other
manner, notwithstanding that the employment may be authorised under the other
provisions of this Act or any other enactment.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 38, page 33, line 38,
to leave out the word "registered" (registered
medical practitioner) and to insert instead
thereof the words duly qualified." This
refers to the same point as the last amendment.

Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 42 (Power of officer of education
authority to enter place of employment).
(1) If it appear to any justice of the
peace, on the complaint of an officer of the
education authority acting under this Part
of this Act, that there is reasonable cause
to believe that a child or young person is
employed in contravention of the foregoing
provisions of this Part of this Act in any
place, whether a building or not, the justice
may by order under his hand empower an
officer of the education authority to enter
such place at any reasonable time, within
forty-eight hours from the date of the order
and examine such place and any person
therein touching the employment of any
child

or young person therein.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 42, page 36, line 4,
to leave out the words er young person."
As hon. Members are aware, the object of
Clause 42 is to secure right of entry for the
education authority through the officers to
any place where there is reason to suppose
that a child is employed in contravention of
the Act. The term "young person" is defined
in Clause 108 of the Bill as a person under
sixteen who is no longer a child," and "child"
means a child of compulsory school age, i.e..
up to 14, except in the case of afflicted children. Street trading for young persons between the ages of 14 and 16 is to be regulated
by the education authority, but as street
trading" is carried on in "streets or public
places" there is no need for right of entry
in such cases. I think the point is an obvious
one.
Amendment agreed to.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 43, page 36, line 20, to

I think this is an amendment which has
escaped the eye of my hon. Friend opposite

means in this case "physical development or
education," and it is now proposed that these
words should be inserted in the Bill.

The following amendment stood in the name
of Mr. Leslie: -Clause 54, page 42, line 5, leave
out lines 5 to 9 inclusive and insert:
"(1) Blind, deaf and mute children, that
is to say, children who are too blind to be
able to read the ordinary books used by
the children, or are too deaf, or too indistinct in speech, to be taught in a class of
ordinary children, in an ordinary elementary
school."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move. The noble
Marquess has kindly expressed the same idea
in better language.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am obliged to the hon. Member for what he
has said, and I should like to say we are grateful to him for having drawn our attention to
this very important point. The amendment
dealing with the matter which the Government hope to insert in the Bill is, in Clause
54, page 42, line 5, to leave out the word
and," and to insert after the word "deaf,"
the words "and dumb." There is another
amendment on the same subject, in line 7.
These amendments are designed to meet the
case of children who may be dumb and yet
not deaf. Such cases are extremely rareso rare in fact that the English Acts make no
reference to them. But our proposed amendment of the clause (adjusted after reference
to the Chief Medical Officer of the Ministry
of Home Affairs) is in better form than
Senator Leslie's amendment. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 54, page 42, line 7,
to leave out from "children" to the end of
line 9 ("or are too deaf to be taught in a
class of hearing children in an ordinary
elementary school") and insert" and children
who by reason of being deaf or dumb are
incapable of being taught in the ordinary
classes of an elementary school."
Amendment agreed to.

CLAUSE 103 (Evidence of certificates, etc.,
issued by education authorities).
All orders, certificates, notices, requirements, and documents of an education
authority under this Act, if purporting to
be signed by the clerk of the education
authority or by any officer of the education
authority authorised to sign in this behalf
shall, until the contrary is proved, be
deemed to be made by the authority and to
have been so signed, and may be proved
by the production of a copy thereof purporting to have been so signed.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 103, page 66, line 14,
to leave out from the first "authority" to
"shall" in line 15 (" or by any officer of the
education authority authorised to sign on his
behalf ").
The words proposed to be deleted are not
necessary in view of Clause 108 (16) which
says that "the expression 'clerk' includes in
relation to an education authority any duly
authorised officer of that authority."
Amendment agreed to.

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 14th June, 1923.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Intoxicating Liquor Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House be now put into
Committee on the said Bill. (The Marquess
of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The Marquess of DUFFERIN AND AVA
in the Chair).
Ordered: That CLAUSE 1 (Closing of
licensed premises on Sunday and Christmas
Day) stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LONG: With regard to Clause 1,
page 1-
The CHAIRMAN: I am afraid we have
passed that.
CLAUSE 2 (Abolition of certain offlicences).
Mr. ARMSTRONG: It appears to me that
Clause 2 would read better if on page 2, line
9, the words "in any case" ("but shall not
in any case continue in force") were omitted.
It says " in any case" and immediately afterwards "unless renewed in accordance with
the provisions of this Act."
I
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I do not quite follow the hon. Senator, and
I really do not see the necessity for omitting
these words. I think they are needed.
hope the hon. Member will not press the

Mr. LESLIE: Clause 2 appears to be the
appropriate place for raising the question of
the railway refreshment room, which I am
quite prepared to believe is adequately
covered by the Bill, but it is a little hard
to follow how it is protected. The railway refreshment room appears to require protection
both in connection with Clause 2 and in connection with Clause 6, because the ordinary
railway refreshment room as we know is a
place where bona-fide travellers-real bonafide travellers-can obtain intoxicating liquor
either to drink at the time with or without
refreshment, or they can--
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I am sorry to interrupt the hon. Member.
but there is an amendment to Clause 6 with

CLAUSE 3 (Provisions as to claims in
respect of abolition of off-licences).
(1) Compensation shall be payable, in
accordance with the provisions hereinafter
in this section contained and out of the
fund in this section mentioned, in any case
where, by reason of the foregoing provisions
of this Act with respect to the abolition of
certain off-licences, a licence for the sale
by retail of intoxicating liquor for consumption off the premises is not renewed
at the annual licensing sessions held next
after the passing of this Act, "and in any
case where, by reason of inability to comply
with the requirements of this Act with
respect to the structural separation of the
part of such premises in which the sale of
intoxicating liquor is carried on from the
remainder of those premises, a licence for
the sale by retail of intoxicating liquor for
consumption on the premises is not renewed
at the annual licensing sessions held next
after the date when such requirements come
into operation."
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(5) The Ministry of Finance may make
rules for carrying into effect the provisions of this section and may, in particular, by those rules prescribe the procedure
in connection with applications for claims,
the time and manner of making payments
in respect of claims and the manner in
which the accounts of the Claims Fund are
to be kept and audited.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in Clause 3, page 4, line 11,
to leave out "such" ("separation of the part
of such premises") and insert "any"; in line
13 to leave out "remainder" ("the remainder
of those premises") and insert "part"; in
line-
The CHAIRMAN: Would the noble Viscount take each amendment separately?
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move to leave out "such" and insert
"any." This is really a drafting amendment
to make it quite clear that compensation
can only be claimed where structural alteration: takes place.
Amendment agreed to.
285 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
14 JUNE 1923

Committee.
+


CLAUSE 4 (Restrictions on sale of methylated spirits).
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in Clause 3, page 4, line 13, to
leave out "remainder" and insert "part."
This also is a drafting amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
a
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in Clause 3, page 4, line 13,
after "premises" ("of those premises,
licence for the sale") insert "in which any
other business is carried on." This is also a
drafting amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I have an amendment which is not down on
the Paper. It is in Clause 3, page 4, line 17,
at end ("into operation") to add:
"A licence for the sale by retail of intoxicating liquor for consumption off the
premises which, by reason of the provisions
of Section eight of the Finance Act, 1917,
is, at the date of the annual licensing
sessions held next after the passing of this
Act, to be treated as suspended shall, for
the purposes of this section, but for no
other purpose, be deemed to be a licence
which is not renewed at the said sessions;
and where in such case a claim for compensation is made, the period of the
suspension of the licence shall terminate
as from the date of the said sessions."
The object of this amendment is to make
it quite clear that compensation to an offlicence holder shall not be prejudiced by the
fact that his licensed premises have been
damaged or destroyed during the disturbances of the last few years. The compensation
of such persons has already received recognition in the Finance Act of 1917 to the
extent of keeping the licence alive although
the business is in suspension through the
damage or destruction of the premises. This
amendment provides that cases of this kind
recognised by the Finance Act of 1917 shall
be on the same footing as cases where the
business is in being but the licence is refused
under the provisions of the Bill.
I beg
to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in Clause 3, page 5, line 34,
at end ("kept and audited") add "and regulate the payment of the expenses of the
Claims Tribunals in connection with the
exercise of their powers or the performance
of their duties."
This is only to make it perfectly clear that
the Ministry of Finance can control the
amount of expenses of administration as
distinct from the claim.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered: That Clause 3, as amended, stand
part of the Bill.
(1) It shall not be lawful for a retailer
of methylated spirits to sell such spirits
to any person under the age of eighteen
years or, without prejudice to the foregoing prohibition to sell methylated spirits
to any person unknown to the seller, unless
such person is introduced by some person
known to the seller; and on every sale by
retail of methylated spirits in a quantity
not exceeding one gallon, the seller shall,
before delivery, make or cause to be made
an entry in a book to be kept for that
purpose, stating-
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 4.
a
pago 5, lines 41 and 42, leave out "in
quantity not exceeding one gallon" ("sale by
retail of methylated spirits in a quantity
not exceeding one gallon").
Clause 4 is a clause which deals with
methylated spirits. As the right hon. Gentleman, the Prime Minister, told us the other
day methylated spirits are practically a
poison, and it is proposed to treat them as
such. Consequently, regulations are brought
in in this Bill, and with methylated spirits
it is necessary to incorporate methylated
ether. The whole intention of Clause 4 is,
as I understand it, to put methylated ether
and spirits on exactly the same footing, but
I have some difficulty in the matter. I must
admit freely that the difficulty may be
entirely in my own mind, but in extenuation
of that I must plead the shortness of time
one has had to get one's ideas into proper
shape. It seems to me in taking the Bill as
a whole we have not succeeded in bringing
methylated ether in every case into the same
category as methylated spirits, and it seems
to me also that we are in that difficulty with
methylated spirits when we refer to sales by
retail, because so far as I have been able to
ascertain-I may be quite wrong on this
point-the sale of spirits by retail means a
sale of spirits in a quantity less than two
gallons. This is the first of three or four
amendments which will be necessary if the
amendment I now propose is considered. We
are dealing here with the sale of methylated
spirits in a quantity not exceeding one gallon. The intention is, as I understand it,
that the expression "sale by retail of methylated spirits" shall in future mean a sale of
methylated spirits in a quantity less than
one gallon. That is clearly expressed in the
words which for the moment I propose to
omit. But we seem to get into difficulties
further on in the Bill when we simply say
"sale by retail." Therefore, we have to get
rid of certain words, and finally to insert
another small clause at the end of the section
-an amendment to which effect I will in due
Intoxicating Liquor Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
course propose that all the provisions of the
Acts which apply to methylated spirits shall
apply also to methylated ether, and that the
expression "by retail," where applied to
methylated spirits, in this Act shall mean the
sale of any quantity not exceeding one gallon.
These are two propositions which I suggest.
I beg to move the first amendment, which
would be the omission at the bottom of page
5 of the words "in a quantity not exceeding
one gallon."
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The words in the clause which the hon.
Member has drawn attention to and wishes to
omit were really put in more or less after
consultation with wholesale dealers, who
agreed that small quantities should be allowed
to be sold, and yet not too big. It was decided
to put in the words "in a quantity not exceeding one gallon."
I think the hon.
Member in his speech referred to the provisions of certain Acts. I am not prepared at
the moment to say whether the hon. Member
is right or is wrong, but I am inclined at
present to think he is labouring under a
misapprehension. I will look into this matter
before the Report stage, and if necessary let
him know whether we will accept his words
or not.
Mr. LESLIE: In that case it would be
better to withdraw this amendment, and with
the leave of the noble Viscount I will not
move the other three which are more or less
consequential.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I am grateful to the hon. Member for his
consideration. These all hinge on the first
amendment, and it would be much better
to leave the matter to the Report stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Ordered: That Clause 4 stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Extension of Provisions of 1
Edw. 7, c. 27).
"
6"
The Intoxicating Liquors (Sale to Children) Act, 1901, shall have effect as if in
section two the words under the age of
eighteen years were substituted for the
words "under the age of fourteen years."
Notwithstanding anything in the said Act,
no female under the age of eighteen years
shall be employed in the sale or delivery of
intoxicating liquors.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move in Clause 5, page 7, lines 20
and 21, leave out "notwithstanding anything
in the said Act."
This is another amendment which I am
sorry to say is not on the Paper. The reason
for the necessity of omitting these words
is that the second sentence in Clause 5 is
not accurate as it stands, because the Intoxicating Liquors (Sale to Children) Act,
1901, as amended by the first part of the
clause, does not deal with the case of persons
under the age of 18 years selling intoxicating
liquor. This second sentence of the clause
under discussion was inserted in another
place on the motion of a private Member,
apparently not only in order to prevent any
female under the age of 18 years from coming
within Section 5 of the Act of 1901-that is,
being permitted to deliver intoxicating liquor
--but also from being concerned in the sale
of such liquors. That was entirely outside
this Act of 1901. Consequently, the words,
"notwithstanding anything in the said Act,"
should be omitted if this double intention
is to have effect. If, however, it was intended
to confine the Act of 1901 the words "sale
or" would have to be omitted. The former
course ought to be adopted, and consequently
the amendment has been drafted as it now
stands on the Paper.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered: That Clause 5, as amended, stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Structural arrangement of
licensed premises).
(1) Where any business, other than the
sale of intoxicating liquor for consumption
on the premises, is carried on in any
premises licensed for such sale as aforesaid (not being premises structurally
adapted for use and bona-fide used exclusively as a hotel as hereinbefore in this
Act defined, or as a refreshment house or
restaurant), the part of the premises in
which the sale of intoxicating liquor is
carried on shall be structurally separated
from, and have no internal communication
with, the remainder of the building, and
nothing shall be sold or offered for sale
in such part of the premises as aforesaid
except intoxicating liquor, tobacco, table
waters, and such food as may be supplied
for consumption on the premises.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move in Clause 6, page 7, line 29,
after the word "house" ("a refreshment
house or restaurant") insert railway
refreshment room."
"
I think that these words will meet the
objection that my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie)
has, that is to say, that railway refreshment
rooms ought to be exempted from the operation of Clause 6 as to structural alterations,
etc., otherwise they would come under the
head of mixed business. This amendment
will exempt refreshment rooms.
Mr. LESLIE: I am grateful to the noble
Viscount for looking into the matter and
for inserting the words he has proposed. The
only point that seems to remain open is
whether it is now unnecessary to add any-
289 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

14 JUNE 1923
Committee.

D


J
S
thing towards the end of the same section to
cover the case of matches and boxes of sweets,
which are commonly sold in railway refreshment rooms. The intention I understand
now is to put a railway refreshment room
in a separate category and where it properly
belongs. As we know in railway refreshment
rooms you can obtain sandwiches or buns
either to eat there and then or take away
with you, and you can also have tobacco,
cigarettes and matches. It is especially
important to the travelling public that
matches should be obtainable. You can also
obtain boxes of chocolates and things of that
sort which are commonly given to children
on long journeys. I would like the noble
Viscount to assure me that none of these
articles are in any way affected.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I may point out to my hon. Friend that the
effect of the amendment enables the sale of
these articles. The words really meet the
point which he has raised.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered: That Clause 6, as amended, stand
part of the Bill.
Ordered: That CLAUSE 7 (Amendment and
extension of existing law as to licensed
premises, clubs, etc.), stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Forfeiture of licence and recording of convictions for certain offences)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Restrictions as to the issue of
new licences).
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
or
(2) Where, by reason of the expiration of
a lease, a licence for the sale of intoxicating
liquor for consumption on the premises comprised in the lease is extinguished
surrendered, the licensing authority may,
notwithstanding anything in this section,
grant a licence for suitable premises in the
immediate vicinity of the premises to which
the licence so extinguished or surrendered
was attached.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in Clause 9, page 10, line 37,
at end to add: "Provided that no such licence
shall be granted to any person who is entitled,
under Section five of the Town Tenants (Ireland) Act, 1906, to compensation for the loss
of goodwill in respect of a tenancy of the
premises to which the licence so extinguished
or surrendered was attached."
The object of this amendment is to ensure
that, where a licensee has his licence extinguished or surrendered and obtains compensation under the Town Tenants Act, he
should not also be entitled to a licence. 1
think the amendment explains itself clearly.
I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Control of licensed premises
on election days).
The provisions of this Act with respect to
the closing of licensed premises on Sunday
and Christmas Day may be applied by
order of the Minister of Home Affairs so as
to have effect, either throughout Northern
Ireland or in any specified area therein, and
subject to any conditions which may be
specified in the order, on the day of election
or on the day of the poll (or on both of those
days), in connection with any Parliamentary election, whether for the Parliament of
Northern Ireland or for the Parliament of
the United Kingdom.
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to move, in Clause 10,
page 11, line 43, at end add "or Local Government election."
The reason I desire this amendment is
that sometimes local feeling runs quite as
high at an urban or county council election
as it does at a Parliamentary election. I do
not suggest for one moment that the word

may" should be altered to "shall." The
rest of the clause would remain as it is. It
would be in the hands of the Minister of
Home Affairs in the case, perhaps, of a
Border town like Newry, if excitement ran
high, to apply this clause to a Local Government election as well as to an election for
the Parliament of Northern Ireland or the
Imperial Parliament. If Larkin and his
party arrived in such a Border town the
liberty of the subject, which we are So
anxious to protect, might be interfered with.
Therefore, I move that these words be added
to the Bill.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In reply to the hon. Senator (Mr. Dickson)
I would like to point out that justices have
now the power, if a disturbance should take
place, to close licensed premises. I think it
would be making this clause a little too
drastic to add, even with the word "may,"
the power for the Minister of Home Affairs
to close all licensed houses on days of Local
Government elections. I am sorry I cannot
accept the amendment.
Mr. DICKSON: On the understanding that
the power is in the hands of the justices at
present I beg to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

T
291 Intoxicating Liquor Bill-

THE
Committee.
SENATE.
t
CLAUSE 11 (Proceedings, Constitution of
Courts and Penalties under the Act and
Licensing Acts).
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(3) A court of summary jurisdiction
when trying an offence either under any
Act regulating the sale of intoxicating
liquor by retail or under this Act, and when
hearing any matter required by this Act
to be determined by such court, shall be
constituted of two or more resident magistrates; and a court of quarter sessions,
when hearing and determining an appeal
from such court of summary jurisdiction,
shall be constituted of the county court
judge or recorder sitting alone.
No justice of the peace shall have any
jurisdiction to try any offence, or to hear
or determine any matter mentioned in this
sub-section, except the persons specified
therein.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in Clause 11, page 12, line 14,
to leave out the word "Act" ("under any
Act") and insert instead thereof the word
"enactment."

There are two other amendments whicli
hinge on this matter. The object of putting
in the word enactment" and leaving out
the word "Act" is to provide that cases of
simple drunkenness will still be tried by the
ordinary justices at petty sessions. We do
not want to throw upon this Court of Summary Jurisdiction, consisting of two resident
magistrates, the onus of having to try
ordinary cases of simple drunkenness. Therefore, this word "enactment" is to be inserted and the word "Act" left out.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 11, page 12,
line 15, after "retail" ("sale of intoxicating
liquor by retail") insert " (except Section
twelve of the Licensing Act, 1872)."
This is really consequential upon the first
amendment. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD :
I beg to move, in Clause 11, page 12.
line 24, to leave out the words "mentioned
in this sub-section" ("any matter mentioned
in this sub-section") and insert instead
thereof the words "to which this sub-section
applies."
This also follows on the first amendment.
I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Moved: "That the clause, as amended, stand
part of the Bill." (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
Mr. LESLIE: This seems to me to be the
appropriate place to draw attention to Subsection (6) of this clause. Sub-section (6),
which provides for the power of appeal for
the police authorities in the event of the
decision of the Court of Summary Jurisdiction
being against them, is one which has provoked
a good deal of comment. It has been suggested that the giving of the right of appeal in
such cases to the prosecution is contrary to
the usual practice of our law. I am not, of
course, going as far as that, because, as the
House knows, in a really serious case there is
always the power of getting beyond the magistrates by means of the right of the Attorney-
General to send up a Bill to Assizes, irrespective of what the magistrates may do. But in
this particular case we are establishing by
this Bill a new form of tribunal, consisting,
not of two ordinary magistrates, but two
Resident Magistrates, men presumably of
sufficient knowledge of the law and of the
world, and it is being provided that a police
officer or constable, who has appeared before
them and has had judgment against him,
may have an appeal. It is perfectly obvious
that such a right is not intended to be freely
exercised. I venture to think that it is extremely improbable that it will ever be
exercised at all, and if it is exercised it will
only be exercised on the authority of a very
superior officer of the police. As the matter
has been commented on, as I say, somewhat
freely, I think it would not be out of place
to ask the Government to give us a few words
of explanation of the extent to which in their
judgment the power is likely to be used.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I hope the hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie), who has
raised this somewhat important point, is not
going to move an amendment. I admit it is
somewhat drastic to ask that it should be put
in this Bill that any police officer or constable has a right of appeal, but I can assure
the hon. Senator that this power of appeal
will be used in very, very few cases, and very
great care will be taken to see that no frivolous appeal or an appeal which obviously is
not warranted will be allowed. As the hon.
Member will know, there are sometimes cases
in a summary Court where judgment is decided possibly on some technicality, and in
the interests of everyone it is necessary that
an appeal should be provided. I can assure
this House that the right of appeal will not
be used in any vindictive or unnecessary
manner, and I trust the occasion will very
seldom arise when such an appeal will be
necessary.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in Clause 13, page 15, line 4,
at end to add: "The expression 'chemist and
druggist includes registered druggist."
The necessity for inserting these words is
that the registered druggists are a distinct
class in the trade. The chemist and druggist
is a man who was not in the trade at the
time of the passing of the Pharmacy (Ireland)
Act of 1875 and amending Acts, while
registered druggist is a druggist who becomes
registered under that Act.
Amendment agreed to.
a
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 14 (Short Title) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
FIRST SCHEDULE.
PART II.
PROVISIONS APPLICABLE TO CONSI-
DERATION OF CLAIMS.
In cases where the licence in respect of
any premises ceases, by virtue of this Act,
to be renewable, the following provisions
shall apply as respects the payment of compensation to persons interested in the
premises :-
(1) Compensation shall be paid in respect
of the depreciation in the value of the
premises to an amount not exceeding five
times the difference between the amount at
which the premises were valued under the
Irish Valuation Acts immediately before
they ceased to be licensed premises, and
the sum which the Commissioner of
Valuation for Northern Ireland certifies
to be their annual value immediately after
they ceased to be licensed.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(7) The Claims Tribunal shall, in cases
where several persons are interested in
the same licensed premises, have power
to apportion the amount of compensation
determined under this part of the Schedule
amongst those persons according to the
several interests held or enjoyed by them.
*
*
*
*
*
*
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in the First Schedule, Part
II, page 16, line 31, after the word "certifies"
("certifies to be their annual value") to
insert the words "in such manner as may
be prescribed by rules made by the Ministry
of Finance."
I regret to say that this is also an amendment which is not on the Amendment Paper.
This explanation has only been handed to
me.
The CHAIRMAN: I think that I may say
that this shows how very inconvenient it
is to have amendments coming in late and
not appearing on the Paper. As Chairman
I am responsible for the correct conduct of
the business, and it cannot be properly conducted if amendments are put in like this
at the last moment. It is not in my power,
unfortunately, to prevent it; but I hope that
in future this will not be done.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD :
I beg to withdraw the amendment. I will
put it down on the Report stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD.
I beg to move, in the First Schedule, page 17,
line 35, at the end to add:
"and shall have power, in any case in
which the Claims Tribunal thinks it just
so to do, to declare that a lease of licensed
premises shall be to be surrendered as from
such date as may be specified in the
declaration."
This amendment is to enable the unexpired
portion of a lease to be surrendered in a case
where the licence is not renewed and the
rent is in excess of what the premises ought
to fetch when no longer licensed.
Amendment agreed to.
*
*
SECOND SCHEDULE.
*
*
*

6. The following provisions shall have
effect with respect to offences in connection
with premises licensed for the sale of
intoxicating liquor by retail :-
(a) Any police officer or constable may
at all times enter any premises in respect
of which a licence is is force for the
sale of intoxicating liquor by retail, and
every person who by himself, or by any
person in his employment or acting with
his knowledge and consent, refuses, fails
or unreasonably delays to admit any such
officer or constable demanding to enter
in pursuance of this paragraph, shall be
guilty of an offence under this Act.

*
*

THE SENATE.

(e) It shall not be lawful for any
person to consume, or permit the consumption of, intoxicating liquor on any
premises licensed only for the sale of
intoxicating liquor by retail for consumption off the premises, and it shall
not be lawful for a licensed person to
keep or use any drinking vessels on any
such premises. This provision shall, as
respects any premises, have effect as
from the date when the licence for such
sale as aforesaid is first renewed after the
passing of this Act.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in the Second Schedule, page

This is intended to make it quite clear that
there shall be no unreasonable interruption
of meetings legitimately taking place on
licensed premises.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD.
I beg to move, in the Second Schedule, page
20, lines 45 and 46, to leave out the words

instead thereof the words "in any portion of
such premises which is set apart for such
sale of intoxicating liquor."
This is merely a drafting amendment.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in the Second Schedule, page
21, line 16, at the end to add:
an
81. ("Strength of spirits.) In determining
whether
offence has been committed
under the enactments relating to the sale
of food and drugs by selling, to the prejudice of the purchaser, whisky, brandy,
rum or gin not adulterated otherwise than
by any admixture of water, it shall be a
good defence to prove that such admixture
has not reduced the spirit more than thirtyfive degrees under proof, and Section six of
the Sale of Food and Drugs Act, 1879, is
hereby repealed."
The object of this amendment is to extend
to Northern Ireland a provision which is
already in force in Great Britain under
Section 10 of the Licensing Act of 1921.
Amendment agreed to.
