TITLE. A Bill to make provision for the
closing on Sunday and Christmas Day of
certain premises licensed for the sale of intoxicating liquor, the abolition of certain
off-licences, and the settlement of claims
for compensation arising thereout; to impose restrictions on the sale of methylated
spirits; to extend the law with respect
to illicit distillation of spirits; and to
amend the law relating to the sale and
supply of intoxicating liquor by retail; and
for purposes connected therewith.
Moved: That this be the Title to the Bill.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move, in the Title, page 1, lines
6 and 7, to leave out the words "to extend
the law with respect to the illicit distillation
of spirits."
This is consequential upon the omission
of a clause relating to illicit distillation,
which was considered to be in part outside
the powers of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland, and which is now being adopted
in the Imperial Finance Bill.
Amendment agreed to.
Title, as amended, agreed to.
Preamble agreed to.
Third Reading.
Education Bill-

14 JUNE 1923
T
L
Ordered: That the Bill be reported with
amendments to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported, with amendments.
Ordered: That the Report of Amendments
be received to-morrow.
Education Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move, "That the Education Bill be
now read a Third time."
I should not think of trespassing on the
attention of the House by making a long
speech in connection with the final stage of
the passage of this Bill through Parliament.
I should only like to say that as far as
Farliament is concerned a very great task
has been finished. As hon. Members are
aware, this Bill was introduced in accordance
with an undertaking of the Government that
it would be one of the first measures which
they would propose in this Parliament.
need hardly say that I sincerely hope that the
measure as it has been passed through the
House is one which will lay the foundation of
a system of education which will redound to
the credit of Parliament and to the benefit of
the Six Counties.
I
I should like to congratulate the Senate on
the work which they have done in connection
with this Bill. My thanks are also due to
my hon. Friend who sits opposite, Senator
Leslie, for the very careful manner in which
he has considered the Bill, which has resulted
in his introducing amendments which have
benefited the measure to a very large extent.
I should like further to express my thanks
to the Lynn Committee who were responsible
for the preliminary consideration of all these
matters, and who laid the foundation of the
Bill which I hope will shortly become an Act.
If hon. Members will permit the digression,
it may interest them to know that we are
passing into law a system which was called
for in this country 300 years ago. A quotation
which has been handed to me is a description
of the state of Ireland in the reign of James
I, entitled, "Advertisements for Ireland," the
manuscript of which from the Sterne collection in the library of Trinity College, Dublin,
has recently been published by the Royal
It runs
Society of Antiquaries of Ireland.
as follows:-
"Schools in the island be very scarce,
which causes so many unlettered there. If,
therefore, in the shire towns and the like
convenient places, free schools were erected
and every impropriator" —
That is, the landed proprietor of our day.
"and others their like would contribute
yearly to the maintenance thereof somewhat
proportionable to the value and impropriation of their lands (pro rata) it would advance much learning there suddenly, which
is the nurse and mother of virtue, civility,
order and perfection."
I do not think we could follow better the
moral which is contained in these few words.
I beg to move the Third Reading of this Bill.
Before doing so, I must apologise to the
House as it is necessary for me to move an
amendment on the Third Reading, which is
as follows-
Mr. SPEAKER: The noble Marquess would
perhaps move it after the Third Reading has
been carried.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move: "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: It is quite impossible for
any of us to part with this Bill without
expressing our congratulations to the noble
Marquess for the manner in which he has
piloted what really is a monumental measure
not only through this House but also through
perhaps more troubled waters elsewhere. We
are all deeply indebted to him for the consideration with which he dealt with us all
during the several debates, and for myself
I must express my gratitude for the kind
remarks which he has been good enough to
make concerning me. Perhaps I may assure
him that any little assistance I was able to
give afforded me the greatest possible
pleasure.
Mr. DICKSON: If I am in order I would
like to add a word to what has been said
by the hon. Gentleman opposite (Mr. Leslie).
The noble Marquess, the Leader of this
House, has congratulated the Senate on the
manner in which this Bill has got through.
I think the credit of the Education Bill is
due to the Marquess of Londonderry, and
generations yet unborn will know more of
the advantages he has conferred on the
rising generation of this Province than we
do to-day. He has given over a year of the
best of his time to this Bill without stint
and without any desire for praise. The
community at large owe
deep debt of
gratitude to him, and to let the Bill go
through the Third Reading without letting
him know I think would be wanting on our
part. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
a
Colonel SINCLAIR: May I be allowed to
associate myself in a few words with the
sentiments which have fallen from the hon.
Gentleman opposite (Mr. Leslie), and from
my hon. Colleague on this side (Mr
Dickson)? I had the honour a short time
ago of seconding in a short speech the
Address to the Throne, and I expressed the
Education Bill-
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[Colonel Sinclair.]
hope that the noble Marquess would have
an opportunity of carrying through what
bas been described as a monumental measure,
in stabilising the education of this Province.
We may heartily thank him for the satisfactory accomplishment of that great object,
and I beg to offer him my personal congratulations. We hope in another sphere to place
a very distinct recognition of the noble
Marquess's educational work by electing him
in a few days Chancellor of the local University. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) We felt
that not only his exceptional talents but the
great services which he has rendered in the
preparation of this Bill entitled him to this
distinction. We hope he will honour us
by accepting the Chancellorship of the local
University. In electing him, as in some
degree in recognition of his work as an
educationist, we feel we have done
thing of great public utility.
some-
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 95 (Appearance of education
authority in legal proceedings).
An education authority may appear in
all legal proceedings by their clerk, or by
some member of the authority authorised
by resolution of the authority; and every
such resolution shall appear upon the
minutes of the proceedings of the
authority, but every such resolution shall,
until the contrary is proved, be deemed in
any legal proceedings to appear upon those
minutes.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move in Clause 95, page 62, line 8, to
leave out "all legal" ("may appear in all
legal") and insert "any summary."
This amendment is one which will appeal
to my hon. Friend opposite (Mr. Leslie). It
is a point which he raised and naturally we
considered the point very carefully. It expressly allows the appearance of the Clerk
in summary jurisdiction proceedings, which
satisfies the point which he raised on both
stages of the Bill.
Amendment agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."-
(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons to
acquaint them that the Senate agree to the
Education Bill with amendments, to which
they desire the concurrence of the Commons.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
The time at which it will be necessary for
hon. Members to be in their places tomorrow is 2-45 o'clock.

Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive Minutes after Four of the
Clock, till a Quarter before Three
of the Clock to-morrow.

Friday, 15th June, 1923.
The Senate met at a quarter before Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.

Ministry of Home Affairs: Royal Ulster
Constabulary Pensions Order, 1993.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act)
and ordered to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.

considered in order to its being suspended

Belfast Corporation Bill.
for Amendment on Third Read-
Mr. SPEAKER: I beg to move that Standing Order No. 172 be considered in order
to its being dispensed with, and that this
Bill, as amended by the Joint Committee,
be now considered. In moving that Standing Order No. 172 be dispensed with I think
I should explain to the House that this Order
lays down that all local Bills as amended
in Committee shall be deposited in Government Departments, both here and in London,
two clear days before being considered in
this House. Owing to opposition
Tuesday last the promoters of this Bill were
unable to make the deposit before Wednesday. Wednesday to Friday is not really two
clear days but the infraction of the Standing Order is very small, and I would ask the
House to allow the Order to be dispensed
with.
on
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That said Standing Order be
dispensed with in respect to the said Bill.

considered.
to.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I have an amendment to move which
is as follows: First schedule, page 16, line 31,
after ("certified") to insert ("in such a
manner as may be prescribed by rules made
by the Ministry of Finance under this Act.")
Article one of part two of the First Schedule lays down that compensation shall be
paid in respect of five times the difference
between the value of the premises when they
were licensed, and the value immediately
after they ceased to be licensed. It has been
pointed out that as the valuation stands at
the moment there is a danger that the
difference between the rateable value of the
premises licensed and unlicensed would not
afford a proper basis for compensation. In
many cases the fact of the premises being
licensed has not appreciably raised the rateable value. It has also been suggested that
this article one, part 2, schedule 1 in its
present form implies that the Commissioner
of Valuation has to ascertain the difference
between the actual valuations made in
accordance with the entire procedure of the
Irish Valuation Acts. The amendment which
I have to move has been drafted so as to
meet both these points, by making it clear
that the proceedings of the Commissioner of
Valuation are to be special proceedings
governed by rules made by the Ministry of
Finance under Clause 3 of the Bill. I beg
to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Moved: that this Bill be now read the
Third time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved that this Bill do now pass.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Public Records Bill.
Adjournment

And a message was sent to the Commons to
acquaint them that the Senate agree to the
said Bill, with some amendments, to which
they desire the concurrence of the Commons.

during pleasure.-(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
Adjourned accordingly during pleasure.

to insert "subject to any liabilities affecting the same."
Preamble, page 1, line 8, after "certify"
insert "amongst other things."

the Commons in said amendments. (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them therewith.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Commons agree to the Public Records
Bill with an amendment to which they desire
the concurrence of the Senate.
They agree to the Land Trust (Powers) Bill
with amendments to which they desire the
concurrence of the Senate.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Commons agree to the amendments
made by the Senate to the Education Bill and
to the Intoxicating Liquor Bill without
amendment.
They agree to the amendments made by the
Senate to the Belfast Corporation Bill without amendments.
Public Records Bill (S).

amendments from the other House.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Commons amendment:
CLAUSE 2, page 3. line 9, at end insert
as a new sub-section:-
(5) There shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament to the said Deputy-
Keeper of the Records and to the Officers,
clerks and servants appointed in pursuance
of this Section, such annual salaries or remuneration as the Minister of Finance may
determine.
Moved: That the Senate doth agree with
the Commons in said amendment.-(Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them therewith.
Land Trust (Powers) Bill.

amendments from the House of Commons. —
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
ROYAL ASSENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am authorised by the Prime Minister on
his behalf to inform the Senate that His
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland in
the name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King has been pleased to give his assent to
the following Bill:
Intoxicating Liquor.
ADJOURNMENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
I do not feel that there is any necessity for
me to say anything on the adjournment. W
are all well aware that the Session has been
prolific in legislation and I think that we
can feel that Acts have been placed on the
Statute Book which will operate for the
benefit of the people of the Six Counties.
The date of our next meeting will be some
time in the autumn. I would suggest that
we adjourn until such date as it shall please
you, Mr. Speaker, my Lord, to call us together again. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Four of
the Clock until such date as the
Speaker shall appoint.

Adjournment.
THE LATE MR. BONAR LAW.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
Before moving the Adjournment and in the
unavoidable absence of the noble Marquess the
Leader of this House, I feel sure I shall be
voicing the sentiments of every Member of
the Senate if I pay a tribute to the Right
Hon. Andrew Bonar Law, the great statesman
who passed away a week ago to-day.
(Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) Yesterday in Westminster Abbey amidst universal scenes of
mourning and grief the Empire paid its last
and greatest tribute to the man who served
her so well. Other illustrious statesmen lie
buried in that historic Abbey, but I doubt
if any one of them ever commanded a greater
degree of affection than Mr. Bonar Law held
in the hearts of all classes throughout the
Empire.
We in Ulster bewail his loss with poignant
regret.
We had a great love for him, for
he understood us as no British Prime Minister of his time ever did. He was a wise
counsellor and a steadfast and true friend
of Ulster all through those years of trial and
uncertainty now happily past. We can never
forget what he did for us. I cannot hope
to say in a few words what we in Ulster feel
about Mr. Bonar Law. We loved him for
his simplicity, his courage, his integrity, and
his humility. Suffice it for me to say that
our hearts to-day go out in sympathy to the
bereaved members of his family, one of whom,
his brother, still lives amongst us. Ulster
will never forget the man of whom it might
be truthfully said that he gave his life for
his country. We in Ulster will keep his
memory green in our hearts for evermore.
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn until Thursday next.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: May I
add a few words of my own to what Lord
Massereene has said about Mr. Bonar Law.
I do so only because Mr. Bonar Law was a
great personal friend of my own, and I had
the honour of sitting with him in the Imperial House of Commons. He was a man
of the greatest ability, and it was wonderful
to see how he controlled the wildest and
extremest of any section of the House. If
there had been any disturbance in the House
and he was called in, it was immediately
soothed and passed away. In fact it would
seem to me, and to others who have spoken
309 Notices Summoning Members. 6 NOVEMBER 1923.

to me, that there was no Prime Minister-1
do not know how far back, but certainly not
in modern times, who got the respect of the
House as Mr. Bonar Law did. The reason
why was apparent to everyone; it was due
to his perfect sincerity and honesty. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) We mourn him in
that respect and also a point that was
greatly missed-because, although he was not
born in Ulster, after all, he was a great
Ulsterman.
The Question was about to be put when
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: There
is a small point which perhaps I might bring
forward as I would like to nave it cleared up.
There seems to be a little conflict of authority as to summoning Members of the Senate
to this House. Before I went away I got a
notice signed by Lord Massereene summoning
us here for 3-15. Afterwards I got a notice
for a meeting of the Northern Ireland Senate
and it was not signed by anybody. I unfortunately did not read it but threw it into
the wastepaper basket, because I had already
entered the previous notice in my little book
of engagements. When I came here at 3-15
I was told that the House did not meet until
3-30 and was given the Orders of the Day.
It is only a smail matter, but it is better
that we should really know what time this
House actually meets.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
In reply to the right hon. and gallant Gentleman, I am afraid I must say it was my
fault that the Whip sent out stated that
this House would meet at 3-15. The Notice the
right hon. and gallant Gentleman has got
and which lies on the Table-
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: That is
not my Notice. I threw mine away.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD :
It is the official Notice. It is the one officially summoning the House and stating the
business. Mine was sent from the Whip's
office. It is my fault that the hour was
stated wrongly in that Notice, and I humbly
apologise to the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman and to any other Member of the
House who was inconvenienced by it.
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps I might add that
the reason why the hour was altered was
because we thought we might get some Bills
down from the other House in time to have
a First Reading. It really was not a mistake
exactly.
We thought that hour would be
convenient, as it turned out, for we have got
these Bills.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am not
finding fault at all. I was only speaking in
the hope that it would not happen again.
The Clerk at the Table is probably more conversant with the matter than anyone else.
As far as I remember in the Imperial House
of Commons unless there is a special Whip
on summoning us for a certain time after
dinner we do not get the Whips at all. We
do not get this Order Paper. Is it necessary
-I do not know-to send a Whip up for this
kind of thing? What I pointed out was
that I got the other Notice first. I thought
the second one was only a duplicate. I never
looked at it afterwards. It does not matter
very much to-day, but some day it might.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the answer really
is that the Order Paper is the official Notice.
It is what hon. Members should really go by.
It is not liable to be altered later in the day.
The Whip is sent out some time before it is
proposed that the House should meet.
official paper is the Order Paper.
The
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: In my
ignorance I thought Lord Massereene's note
was the more important.
The Order of the
Day bears no signature.
I did not know
where it came from or who sent it out.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I moved that this House do adjourn until
Thursday of this week. I venture to say now
that I think it would be more convenient if
the House were to meet to-morrow instead of
Thursday to give a First Reading to some
Bills which we may expect to get from another
place. I beg to move that this House do
now adjourn until to-morrow.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before Four of
the Clock.

That they have passed the Labourers Bill,

ΑΡΡΟΙΝΤMENT OF INSPECTORS OF
WEIGHTS AND MEASURES.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
Mr. WOODS: There is a matter I want to
bring before this House. Some time ago we
passed a temporary measure for the appointment of temporary Inspectors of Weights
and Measures. When that Bill was before
the House we were led to believe that these
appointments were only temporary-until
such time as we should have men in the
R.U.C. capable of taking over the positions.
Now I understand it has been reported to
me-that some of the young men at present
members of the Constabulary have made
application to their officers for information
as to how they should go about entering for
the examination for these appointments.
They have been told that these jobs are to
be kept over for old members of that Constabulary. If that is so, I personally do
not think that it is right. I think every
facility should be given to the young members of the Royal Ulster Constabulary to
obtain these positions where they are capable of passing the examination. I hope the
people who are in authority in this matter
will see that something is done to give
these young men every facility for passing
these examinations.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I have to inform the hon. Member that not
having had notice of this question, I am not
in a position at the moment to reply to him.
I would suggest to the hon. Member that it
would be better that notice of a question of
this sort should be handed in to the Clerk
of the Senate to be put down on the Orders
of the Day so that the Government could be
prepared to give an answer. However, I will
look into the question and endeavour to give
the hon. Member an answer as soon as
possible.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Of course
I quite agree with the noble Viscount who is
leading the House about notice. An answer
cannot be expected to a question of that kind
without notice having been given. At the
same time, I, as chairman of a county council,
under which Inspectors of Weights and Measures come, hope that when he does make
inquiries he will find that the old system of
the Royal Irish Constabulary being appointed
Inspectors in their own districts can still be
carried out in the new R.U.C., because it
worked very satisfactorily. I am greatly
opposed to-I do not know whether it is the
intention in making these appointmentsmaking a new job for somebody else. I quite
agree that it would be much better to give
notice of these questions. We have got into
a lax habit-I am a culprit myself of putting
these interlocutory questions. If we really
want to get any valuable information I hope
that I myself and other Members of the
Senate will give notice of the questions so
that we can get a proper answer.
On Question, Motion ("That this House do
now adjourn") agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes past
Three of the Clock.

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 8th November, 1923.
The Senate met at a Quarter past Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Electricity and Gas (Expenses)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of
the Electricity and Gas (Expenses) Bill I
should like to inform the House that this
Bill was introduced in another place by my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Commerce,
for the purpose of merging certain funds
known as "The Electricity Fund" and "The
Gas Fund" in the general accounts of the
Ministry of Commerce and incidentally for
adjusting the basis of contribution to the
Electricity Fund, from units generated to
units sold. This Bill is really a measure for
simplifying accounts.
upon
The Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, placed
authorised undertakers, that had
statutory powers to supply electricity, the
cost of the administration by the Electricity
Commissioners of electricity supply, and the
Electricity Fund was set up to receive moneys
payable to the Commissioners. In Great
Britain the Electricity Commissioners are
a semi-independent body. Here in Northern
Ireland they are officials of the Ministry of
Commerce. There are three officers appointed
and they perform this work in addition to
their other duties without any extra remuneration. It is considered that no useful
purpose would be served by keeping a separate
establishment for these Commissioners and
their staff. Consequently, for as long as these
conditions apply in Northern Ireland, it is
considered advisable that the income and the
expenditure of the Fund should be merged
in the General Accounts of the Ministry of
Commerce. I may, perhaps, inform the House
that there are only four undertakings which
contribute. They are Belfast, Londonderry,
Portrush, and Dunmurry, and, as I said
before, they contribute on the basis of units
of electricity sold and not units of electricity
generated.
With regard to the Gas Fund, the Gas
Regulation Act, 1920, places upon gas undertakings the cost of the salaries, remuneration.
pensions and gratuities of the chief gas
examiner and the gas referees and their staff
that come under the Gas Regulation Act,
1920. That Act deals mainly with the change
that was made in the basis of charge.
Formerly the charge for gas was per 1,000
cubic feet of gas of a certain illuminating
power. It has now been changed to what
is known as the calorific value of the gas,
commonly known as the charge per therm.
The income of the Fund is only derived from
those gas undertakings that have adopted the
thermal basis of charge. I may say that
in Northern Ireland at the moment there are
three undertakings that contribute-Omagh,
Strabane and Londonderry. The annual
contribution is very small and is limited
to 3s. per million cubic feet of gas sold.
Here again it is considered that no useful
purpose will be served by keeping up a
separate Fund, and this Bill provides that
the Gas Fund shall be merged in the General
Accounts of the Ministry of Commerce; and
that the contributions from the respective
undertakings which would otherwise be paid
into the Fund shall now be brought to
account as Appropriations in Aid of moneys
provided by Parliament to defray the expenses
of the Ministry of Commerce. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read the Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the whole
House, the House to be in Committee on
Tuesday next.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): This Bill continues
temporarily certain enactments whether of
the Parliament of the United Kingdom or of
the Parliament of Northern Ireland, which
may be regarded as being still in the experimental stage or liable to revision. A single
date is fixed
their continuance,
the last day
"
cf
for
and that date is
the year-31st December, 1924. In this
connection, however, it will be noticed
that the continuance is to be, unless
Parliament otherwise determines." These
words were inserted so as to meet cases in
which amending legislation might possibly
be undertaken before the new lease of life
of the Act is due to expire, as in the case of
the Rent Restriction of Workmen's Compensation. The Bill has been framed so as to
fit in with the corresponding Imperial Act
of the present year which applies to Northern
Ireland. In so far as it relates to a subject
with respect to which the Parliament of
Northern Ireland has no power to make laws,
the present Bill supplements this provision
by dealing with enactments which relate to
Second Reading.
Expiring Laws Bill-

THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
subjects with respect to which the Northern
Parliament has legislative powers. These
two measures taken together deal with all
temporary laws applicable to Northern Ireland according as they come within the
power of one or other of the two Parliaments.
I beg to move.
Mr. WOODS: I wish to make a reference
to one of the Acts included in the Expiring
Laws Continuance Bili. I refer to the prolongation of the National Health Insurance
Act. I am in entire sympathy with the prolongation of this Act. I deeply regret that
industrial conditions remain in such a position as to necessitate a prolongation of the
Act of 1923. What I want to get at is that I
object to the method of administration of the
Act. The method is not at all satisfactory
in this way. The Act is to assist those who
are genuinely unemployed. Now the slack
way in which the Act is administered gives
opportunities to employers who wish to avoid
the payment of contributions towards that
Act to evade them, and at the end of the
contribution year to pay a fifth of what they
should be paying throughout the whole contribution year. I consider we are not doing
right in encouraging people who wish to
evade any Act of Parliament. The approved
societies are quite well aware that these
things take place. Unfortunately they cannot report to the Ministry for fear of
victimising some of their members who are
in the employment of these people.
Now this matter that I refer to is not so
prevalent in the industrial centres in the
Northern area as throughout the agricultural districts. I do not know whether the
method of inspection in National Health
Insurance that was in operation before we
took over the transfer to Northern Ireland
is still in existence, but if it is not I think
the Ministry would be well advised to refer
back to that method of inspection, because
prior to the transfer they had the National
Health Insurance Act pretty well directed
all over the Northern Province. I do not
wish it to be presumed that I can give the
Ministry any information as to what they
should do with regard to the administration
of the Act. I respectfully suggest that the
Ministry of Labour should issue a form to
approved societies for all members who
are on the unemployment register. I should
say a form of that description is issued by
the Ministry of Pensions to approved
societies, and therefore keeps the approved
societies in touch with every one of their
members whether suffering from disability
or illness. I do not see why the Ministry of
Labour should not revert to the same system
and issue a form to approved societies in
respect of members unemployed, so that no
one should receive any benefit from the prolongation of the Act but those for whom it
was intended and people genuinely unemployed.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
have listened with interest to the remarks
which have fallen from the hon. Senator,
and I regret that the duty of replying for all
departments falls upon my humble self. I
may say to him that I shall put his representations before the Ministry of Labour,
and I am sure the advice which he has given
will be carefully considered. I feel sure the
suggestions he has made, if they are practicable, will be put into effect as far as administration goes. I think the hon. Member
is really referring to the administration of
the Act rather than to any alteration in its
statutory provisions. I expect he will have
an opportunity of addressing himself to the
Minister of Labour, but I shall certainly
mention the matter to the right hon. Gentleman myself.
a
Mr. LESLIE: There are two Acts
included in these expiring
laws to
which I want to draw attention for
few minutes. The first of them I
really put forward as a matter of inquiry.
It is the Act that deals with the extension
of the Grey Seals Protection Act. I have no
animosity whatever against the grey seals. I
am not at all sure that I have ever seen
one, or that they frequent our coasts in any
considerable numbers. The question that
arises in my mind is whether the grey seal
is an animal or whether the taking of grey
seals is not really a fishery in the same way
as taking whales is a fishery, and if so,
whether it is strictly within our competency
to deal with. Fortunately it makes no difference whatever to the grey seal whether we
put in this Act without power or not. It is
simply taken from the English Act. The
point is really whether it is a fish and
whether if that is so we ought to include
it.
The other point is a rather more serious
one, and that is the proposed extension till
next year of the Summer Time Act. The
whole question of the Summer Time Act has
always been open to very widely different
views in Ireland. I think it has never been
popular. The agricultural interest particularly is opposed to it. Others maintain that
it is not in the interests of the children of
the town. That the difficulties of setting up
a distinction in practice between England
and Northern Ireland have been very much
over-stated is evident as regards railway and
steamboat time-tables, for we managed to exist for very many years when our official
time differed from that of Great Britain by
five and twenty minutes. We managed to
317 Expiring Laws Bill-
Second Reading.
8 NOVEMBER 1923.
.

Act
to
en
Act
e
ете
E
江
alter our watches and nothing very serious
seemed to result. The main point really is
the complaint of the agricultural community. I do not think it was expected that
the Summer Time Act, which was originally
a war-time measure, would be made permanent especially in Northern Ireland. It was
practically necessary for us to accept it last
year because time did not admit of going
into the question. I may say I should prefer if the question should be discussed that it
should come before us as a separate measure
early next year rather than as a clause in the
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill. If this
clause could be struck out now, or rather at
a later stage, it would give time for the opponents of the proposed extension to formulate a case and I think great satisfaction
might ensue.
There is nothing else in the Bill which I
think calls for attention and nothing in the
later schedules that will occasion any surprise or to which any exception can be taken.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon Senator has raised two points to
which I hope I may be able to give him replies that will satisfy him. The first matter
he alluded to was the question of the grey
seal-more from the technical point of view
than from any interest he takes in that particular animal. I venture to say it is
quite true that the Imperial Expiring Laws
Act, 1923, does extend the Grey Seals Act as
respects Northern Ireland so far as it is
within the jurisdiction of Northern Ireland,
that is to say, so far as it is a fishery matter.
I think the hon. Member is aware that the
court which deals with an offence under the
Grey Seals Act is a summary court under the
jurisdiction of Northern Ireland. As the Act
therefore creates an offence to be tried by a
court which comes under the jurisdiction of
the Province it seems advisable that the Expiring Laws Continuance Act of Northern
Ireland should continue the Grey Seals Protection Act. I think that is a logical deduction with which the hon. Member will
agree.
I might point out to the hon. Member
that this is not the only case of an Act
covered by both the Imperial Expiring Laws
Continuance Act and our own Act. The
labourers (Ireland) Act, 1883 (No. 4 in Part
1 of the Schedule to the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill) has already been continued
also by the Imperial Expiring Laws Act of
this year. The object of the legislation-and
I know the hon. Member is fully in accord
with the object is to protect the Grey Seal,
and there is apparently no valid objection to
legislation protecting the animal from both
sides.
The other matter which the hon. Member
raised is in regard to summer time. I am
well aware, as he has pointed out, that there
is a certain amount of differing opinion with
regard to the wisdom of establishing summer
time. I certainly have in my recollection of
the House of Commons that this was a measure which very few considered in a practical
sense at all. I remember the point put forward
that if any individual liked to put his clock
forward he could do so, and so forth. But
since that time I do not know whether
civilisation has increased in wisdom or not
-it has been enacted that there should be
summer time. I feel sure that while the
hon. Member has every reason and every
right to put forward objections, on behalf
of the agricultural industry, and on behalf
of the children in the towns, when we consider the question as a whole he will see
that whatever inconveniences may arise on
these two heads they are swallowed up in
the principle which we passionately hold
here, and that is, that we should do whatever
we can to place ourselves on the same footing
as Great Britain.
It is quite true that we existed for years
with a difference in time of 25 minutes, and
whereas it did cause a certain amount of
inconvenience-I think the hon. Member will
agree with me that it did cause a certain
amount of inconvenience, having different
time tables and matters of that sort it was
unfortunate that for that very small space
of time, 25 minutes, there should be these
difficulties which certainly did arise. For the
time being that has been done away with,
and 12 o'clock mid-day at Greenwich is the
same 12 o'clock mid-day in the Province of
Ulster. I should not really be prepared to
say that the agricultural industry are being
ill-used when they are asked to be 25 minutes
wrong by the sun actually. The point which
I would like to press upon the hon. Member
is the point which was so eloquently made
by the Prime Minister in the House of
Commons. He said that the bulk of the
population of the Six Counties resides practically in the great towns. There is a large
preponderance of the population residing in
Belfast, Ballymena, Ballymoney-in fact in
the big towns, and the prosperity of agriculture depends upon the prosperity of the
great towns. The more the great towns are
in a prosperous condition the more their
requirements are, and the better for agriculture and the better for the spread of
prosperity amongst the agricultural community. And SO when one realises that
summer time is the time which is convenient
and suitable for the population of the great
towns, by reason of the fact that the time
is identical with that of Great Britain, with
whom they co-operate in every possible way,
I do not feel somehow that we are calling
upon the agricultural community to make
a great sacrifice in coming into line in the
Second Reading.
Labourers Bill-

THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
opinions which we hold about summer time.
I think the hon. Member will agree with me
that summer time is a provision which has
come to stay on the other side. There is a
large preponderance of opinion throughout
the country that summer time has more
advantages than disadvantages, and whereas
the hon. Member is entitled and justified,
fully justified, in voicing the complaints of
a minority, still I think that when the matter
has been fully considered in the light of the
desire which we all have of keeping as close
to Great Britain as we possibly can, summer
time, while not the most important link
between the two is one of those which it
would be unfortunate to dissolve. On these
grounds I would ask the hon. Member to
reconsider the points put forward, and to
use his powers of persuasion, which are of
no mean order, amongst those who do not
agree with summer time as it is at the present
moment.
On Question, Motion (That the Expiring
Laws Continuance Bill be now read the Second
time) agreed to.
a
Bill read the Second time accordingly and
committed to

House, the House to be in Committee on
Tuesday next.
Labourers Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The Bill which is before the House at the
present moment deals with the assistance
provided from public funds in connection with
the erection of labourers' cottages under the
Labourers Acts. I would venture to explain
at once that there is no new charge involved,
and the Bill can be classed among other
measures which have been introduced to the
House with the object of simplifying our
financial system as it is at the present
moment. I propose to explain the mode by
which the assistance was given in the past,
and that which it is proposed to grant in
the future. The schemes for the provision
of labourers' cottages under the Labourers
(Ireland) Acts, 1883-1919 were subsidised by
the State in two ways according as the schemes
related to the period prior to the passing of
the 1906 Act, or the period subsequent to
the passing of that Act.
In the case of pre-1906 schemes a portion
of the Exchequer contribution provided under
the Purchase of Land (Ireland) Act, 1891, and
paid through the Local Taxation Account,
was definitely allocated for the purpose of
subventions to rural district councils in connection with labourers' cottages schemes.
The subsidy took the form of a fixed sum
to be divided among these local authorities
on the basis of the number of cottages provided, and by a departmental arrangement
was applied towards the reduction of the
amount of the instalments due to the Board
of Works in repayment of loans made by
that department for the erection of the
cottages. On this basis at the appointed day
a fixed sum of £1,619 was distributed among
rural district councils in Northern Ireland.
This contribution is the equivalent of a
Government contribution of 17 per cent. of
the annuities payable by rural district
councils under these schemes, and as the
House is aware this assistance has been voted
during the last two years, owing to the
machinery of the Local Taxation Account
having disappeared.
In the case of post-1906 schemes, the assistance was similarly given by means of a
Government contribution towards the annuities repayable in respect of moneys borrowed
for the purposes of the Labourers Cottages
Acts.
These moneys were previously advanced by the Irish Land Commission under
the Land Purchase Acts, and I am glad to
say the Imperial Government have agreed to
continue to make similar advances in
Northern Ireland for the future through the
intermediary of the Northern Ireland Land
Purchase Commission. The measure of State
assistance was formerly fixed by statute at
36 per cent. of the annuities so payable. The
House will understand the complicated
system in operation in regard to the sources
from which this 36 per cent. relief was obtained, when I explain that the relief was
comprised of two parts, 16 per cent. and 20
per cent. The 16 per cent. was defrayed
each year out of the income of the Labourers'
Cottages Fund, supplemented by an annual
sum of £9,000 voted by Parliament, and an
annual sum of 26,000 payable out of the
Local Taxation Fund. The 20 per cent. was
originally to be paid out of the Ireland
Development Grant, but owing to subsequent
deficiencies in that Fund, it was found necessary for the Imperial Parliament to vote
annually the sums required to meet these
payments.
It will be seen, therefore, that the 36 per
cent. relief was derived from no fewer than
four sources. The Government of Ireland
Act, and the Orders in Council issued thereunder rendered nugatory the provisions relating to the Exchequer contribution, from
which the pre-1906 houses were subsidised,
and the provisions relating to the Local
Taxation Account, and the Irish Development Grant, while finally the House of Commons decided first by Votes and afterwards
confirmed it by the terms of the Exchequer
Labourers Bill.
Standing Orders.

and Financial Provisions Act passed last
Session, that the Labourers' Cottages Fund,
among others, should not be continued as a
separate fund, but should be merged in the
general Exchequer. The bill is therefore a
necessary corollary to this Act to which I
have just referred, and will in fact give a continuing effect to the provision which has
already been made for this service in full in
the Votes. Some Members may inquire why,
if the money has already been voted, any
further legislative action is necessary. The
reason is in order that the rural district
councils may have a guarantee that the
assistance given will not be dependent upon
an annual vote which might at any time be
omitted, but may feel sure that the assistance will be forthcoming regularly every
ycar for the period of the loan.
I wish to emphasise that the Government
have considered themselves under a general
or moral obligation to continue the degree
of State assistance afforded to local authorities in respect of loans issued to them in the
past under the Labourers Acts, and accordivingly the State subsidy in relation to pre-
1906 schemes has been fixed at 17 per cent.
which I think hon. Members will see, represents the measure of the Government's liability for annuities payable by rural district
councils at the appointed day, while in regard to loans issued between the passing of
the 1906 Act and the appointed day, recoupment from the Northern Exchequer of 36 per
cent. of the annuities will be continued.
Moreover, as regards future loans, the
Government is prepared to continue the same
measure of assistance, viz., 36 per cent., with
the proviso, however, that the assistance will
not be given in respect of any capital sum
in excess of £350 per cottage. This limit
has been imposed so as to ensure that the
rural district councils build as economically
as possible. I beg to move.

THE AMENDMENT OF STANDING
ORDERS.
Moved that this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday next. (The Marquess of Londonderry).
Mr. GREER: I observe at the end of the
Paper that notice is given of some amendments of the Standing Orders of the House of
Parliament. I happen to be interested in a
Bill which may be re-deposited in this session.
I would like to ask whether the present Standing Orders will remain in full force and effect
during this session, or if not what time any
alterations that may be made will take effect.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As there is no day named as yet for the
Standing Orders relating to local Bills, the
Standing Orders which are in existence at the
present moment are the ones that will rule
our procedure.
Mr. GREER: That is to say, the present
Standing Orders are the ones which will be
in force to the end of the session.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I think that question should be addressed
to the Speaker.
Mr. GREER: I beg to ask-
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member asks this
question. These amendments will be taken
next week, and of course we cannot say
whether they will be accepted or not. If they
are accepted the Standing Orders will be
altered from that date.
Perhaps the hon.
Member will wait until the committee has
met to consider this.
Mr. GREER: I can postpone my question
and ask it again.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
Put it down for Tuesday.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not know whether on
the same question I might ask for a little information as to how we are to deal with
questions that arise in the way of amendments to Standing Orders. As I make it out
our position differs from that at Westminster
as to these rules relating to local Bills. In
England as I understand it the two Chambers
have each their own Standing Orders. Here
as we combine for local Bills any alterations
in Standing Orders are presumed to receive
the support and concurrence of both Houses.
I wonder how we should proceed in order to
obtain that joint support, whether the Orders
will be treated somewhat after the manner of
a Bill and be sent from one House to the
other or whether they will be referred to a
Joint Committee on what course will be
taken.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the answer to the
hon. Member's question is that that question
has never arisen yet. I suppose Parliament
will have to decide how, if the two Houses
X

differ, the matter will be settled.
that is the only reply I can give.

Mr. SPEAKER: If the hon Member gives

Mr. LESLIE: I give notice to raise the

5., C. 22.
time Act,
1922.
a
matter
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in the Schedule, Page 5, to leave out lines 8 to 10
inclusive (The Summer Time Act, 1922). This
Amendment which I have the honour to propose this afternoon deals with
which I mentioned on the Second Reading.
The object of the Amendment is to leave out
of the present Expiring Laws Bill the reference to the extension of the summer time to
Northern Ireland. When I spoke the other
day I alluded to the previous history of the
Summer Time measure, and in reply the
noble Marquess who was supporting the application of summer time to Northern Ireland
referred to summer time as an accepted principle across the water. He said that it had
come to stay. There are various kinds of
visits. There is the week-end visit and there
is the annual visit. I do not think that it
is the general desire in Northern Ireland-in
the country districts at all events-that
summer time should become an annual infliction. Also, in the course of his speech, the
noble Marquess quoted the right hon. Gentleman the Prime Minister as having stated
elsewhere that the majority of the inhabitants of Northern Ireland live in the towns
rather than in the rural districts. I confess
that I felt rather disquieted at hearing that
statement on such an authority, because it
pointed to a large transference of population
within comparatively recent times. According to the figures of the Census of 1911 the
preponderance was in favour of the rural
districts, and the preponderance amounted to
75.000. I take these figures-the figures as
between town and country-from the figures
appended to the Lynn Report on Education,
which happened to be the means of reference
I had nearest at hand. Therefore the statistics which the right hon. Gentleman quoted
must be of recent date. I can only say that,
for I do not for a moment challenge their
accuracy. Still I do not think that it is
desired that the population should shift from
the country to the towns.
The right hon. Gentleman, as quoted,
pointed out that the prosperity of the country
Expiring Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
is dependent on the prosperity of the towns.
He said that if the one is prosperous the
other would be prosperous. I did not observe
from the speech which the noble Marquess
quoted any indication of how the prosperity
of the towns was dependent upon the use of
summer time in the towns. The noble Marquess spoke of the inconveniences resulting
from the old system-the old system of having
our time five and twenty minutes behind the
time of Great Britain; and again I must say
that the roble Marquess did not indicate very
clearly what these inconveniences
actually
were. The noble Marquess was a Member of
the House of Commons at Westminster, I
think, during the whole of the period when
Mr. Willet's Daylight Saving proposals were
unden discussion. The proposals were derided
in Mr. Willett's lifetime but they were
accepted after his decease. Therefore the
noble Marquess will be fully equipped with
all the arguments for and against, and in
fact he will know more about it than the
majority of us.
The great objection to summer time--the
only formulated objection really-comes from
the agricultural and rural districts. They
drew attention to the fact that though Parliament may alter the time of the clock by directing that the hands be moved backwards
or forwards. Parliament can do that, but
Parliament cannot control the sun. Parliament cannot arrange that the dew shall be
off the grass or off the hay or the moisture
off the corn stacks, and the agriculturists
also points out that the cow, one of the most
methodical of creatures, objects to any interference with her natural habits. The general
agriculturist puts up the case that summer
time is actually of no benefit to him, and is a
positive nuisance. His men, as a rule, still work
by what is locally called God's time. It is a
distinct interference with the rural districts,
that they should work by one time while everything else is done by another. Their case in
fact is that we are too far west of Great
Britain for it to be reasonably practicable for
us in this instance to conform to the English
practice. They point out that Ireland is an
island west of Great Britain, some of it a
good deal west, and in doing that they are
not oblivious, I think, of the fact that there
are small districts of Ireland that are no more
west than parts of Scotland, and as you get
further west so does the difference in time
get more and more oppressive.
we
As regards the inconveniences that existed
in the days when we had to alter our
watches by twenty-five minutes when
crossed the Channel, I do not think the inconvenience was so very great. The railway
time tables were printed in the ordinary
way. There was usually a foot-note saying
that this was Irish time or English time,
as the case may be. There was also the
necessity imposed on the Post Office of recording the despatch of telegrams in accordance with English time. I think that was
all. Regarding the telegrams, it made no
difference to the public. I do not think the
alteration of the watch made any difference,
for indeed the alteration as between England and Ireland is no more, if I remember
aright, than between French time and Central European time. The alteration of the
watch seems to be a necessary concomitant of
travel. The inconvenience of the watches
and telegrams is no more than that of the
difference in the tides. The tides will be
ebbing and flowing as usual. The tides have
more influence on us than railway timetables. Also, I remember during the old
period the train which connects with the
English service at the station near where I
live started 20 minutes later than it does at
the present time. I cannot agree that there
was any serious inconvenience under the old
system.
The agriculturist would, I think, prefer if
summer time is to be imposed that it should
come before Parliament as a separate measure, which could, of course, be done in the
spring of next year. He thinks that that
would give him more time by himself, or by
his associations, to approach members elsewhere of both Houses generally, and put his
side of the question plainly before them. 1
think the agriculturist has some right to ask
for a separate Bill, because this question has
been raised before. It was raised elsewhere
in March of last year by means of a question addressed to the right hon. Gentleman,
the Minister of Agriculture. The Minister of
Agriculture stated-I will quote from page
75 of "Hansard" of that date:-
While (he himself was) in entire sympathy
with the whole agricultural community in
their antagonism to summer time.
That was the statement of the Minister of
Agriculture, and as the Minister of Commerce did not express any disagreement with
it, I think you may conclude that the Minister of Agriculture and the Minister of Commerce were at one.
Now as regards the towns, it is claimed
for the towns that summer time increases
the period available for leisure and exercise
in the open air, and that that is a great advantage to the dwellers in the towns. Nobody
would wish to deprive them of such opportunities for open-air exercise as they can get.
but I must point out that it has never yet
been established that the entire population
of the town makes use of the facilities
afforded. Clerks and shop-boys, and indeed
to some extent the workers in the shipyards
are able to exercise themselves in the open
329 Expiring Laws Bill-

13 NOVEMBER 1923.
Committee.

ear

fram
me
fir
rf
E
Dere
air more conveniently than before, but I do
not think that the women living in the
smaller back streets of Belfast find summer
time at all to their convenience. I think it
simply lengthens the period of their toils,
and increases their difficulty in getting the
children to bed and up again in the morning.
I do not think it can be contended that the
alteration is good for the children. I very
much doubt whether summer time is really
for the physical advantage of the bulk of the
inhabitants of the towns.
There is one advantage which the towns
might expect to reap if we returned to prewar practice and did not adopt here the
time which is adopted in Great Britain.
Commercial letters for Belfast would arrive
apparently one hour earlier from London.
The time of transit, of course, would not be
affected, but according to our time letters
would be delivered one hour earlier, because
trains in London could not be altered without losing such advantage as there may be
in summer time in that town. I do not say
of course, that the Belfast man would have
any longer time in which to answer his
letters, because the time of departure would
necessarily have to be adjusted. But the
Belfastman would apparently get his letters
one hour earlier--in fact an hour and twenty
five minutes if you do not lose the 25 minutes
in addition to the hour.
On the whole, I think it would be better
if any alteration is to be made in order
that there may be more time for open-air
exercise for the dwellers in towns, that the
alteration should be effected artificially
rather than in this way. It is perfectly
possible if the will is there, for the businesses
to open earlier and to close earlier. We are
told, of course, that it would be impossible.
We were told exactly the same thing when
the early closing of shops was first mooted,
and when the weekly half-holiday was first
suggested. We were told all these things.
Both the early closing of shops and the
weekly half holiday have come to have
general acceptance. I do not think there is
any insurmountable difficulty in varying
artificially hours in towns, whereas it is
absolutely impossible to change the actual
facts of real time in the country. I hope
that there will be a free expression of
opinion on this matter, and in fact that in
the end the House will consent to leave this
particular Bill out of the ambit of the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill. That will leave
the hands of the Government perfectly free
to introduce a proposal next year as a
separate measure.
Mr. LONG: I should like to express the
very strong dissatisfaction of the agricultural
community at the proposal of the Government to continue summer time for another
term.
We considered that it was only a
war time measure. In the interests of agriculture the Government should have some consideration for their position in the matter
and their views. As the hon. Senator who
has just sat down pointed out they form a
very large majority of the population, and
a very considerable proportion of the remaining population are dependent upon agriculture. I think, therefore, the least the
Government could do would be to give some
consideration to their views in the matter.
It seems to me to be a case of the tail wagging the dog for the towns to dictate to the
country in this matter.
I had intended to speak on the question
of the children, but as the hon. Senator has
exhaustively dealt with the matter, it is
not necessary. I would just wish to protest
against slavishly following everything
British. I think in minor matters like this
it is not necessary at all that we should
follow in every particular. Our loyalty
would not be in the least questioned, I am
sure, if we acted differently in the matter of
summer time from what they do in England.
Our hearts are not regulated by our watches.
I think the loyalty of Ulster has been
sufficiently demonstrated to prove to all that
we are heart and soul with the Empire. I
should like if the Government could see
their way to accede to the wishes of the
hon. Senator in this matter. He has reminded the House that Mr. Willett did not
live to see the reform for which he pleaded
carried into effect. I trust a better fate, as
far as Northern Ireland is concerned, will
befall the hon. Senator, and that he will
live to see the change for which he so eloquently pleaded carried into effect. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Amendment
of the hon. Senator.
no
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I have
doubt the attitude adopted by the
Government on this question in another place
is prompted by what they consider is the
wishes of the majority of the people of
Northern Ireland. But I cannot help feeling
that the opponents of my hon. Friend's (Mr.
Leslie) amendment, whether they are in or
whether they are outside of the Government,
do not realize the heavy additional burden
they are placing on agriculture, which is oneperhaps the most important one-of
our industries. The Northern Irish
farmer suffers from some disabilities
which his English and Scotch competitors do not suffer from. He is further
from his market, because our market is really
the large industrial centres in England. He
is continually subject to have his-the whole
of his export trade in cattle and sheep held
up for uncertain periods by any supposed case
Expiring Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Colonel Perceval-Maxwell]
of infectious disease, perhaps 200 miles from
his farm or 300 miles, whereas in England,
unless you are unfortunate enough to be
within 15 miles of it, you are all right. The
Northern agriculturist has a climate to deal
with which makes the harvesting of crops
always an anxious, late and uncertain proceeding. Providence has made Ireland 25 minutes
behind Great Britain by the sun. No Act of
Parliament can alter this, but we have, I
think, a right to expect that our own Parliament will not add to our anxieties and diffculties by putting another hour to our disadvantage. Arbitrarily making us call it six
o'clock when it is really about 4-30 forces us
to lose the best hour and a half in the day.
For every farmer knows that on many days
in the late autumn it is impossible to do anything in the way of saving crops until after
the dinner hour on account of the dew.
I notice that the noble Marquess the Leader
of this House, speaking on the Second reading, said it was a passion with us to place
ourselves in the same position as Great
Britain. That is a sentiment which all the
loyal and industrious farmers of the Six
Counties will thoroughly endorse. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) There are some disabilities in which it is not in the power of
the Government to help us.
They cannot
change the fact that we are surrounded by
sea, but I would ask the noble Marquess to
do something towards placing us in the same
position as Britain by using his influence with
the Cabinet to re-consider their attitude on
this question, and place us, as far as possible,
in the same position as the British farmer.
I think that in doing so we will not be asking
the Government to depart from a policy which
they have already adopted. This Parliament
was received by the people of Ulster on the
advice of their leaders, many of them in the
present Government. I do not think it was
ever meant merely as a machine for registering what had happened in Westminster,
whether it was good or bad for Ulster. The
noble Marquess has himself brought in
Education Bill somewhat different, and I am
sure, in spite of criticisms, he at any rate
considers it a better Bill and a better system
than that introduced at Westminster. We
have had a Licensing Bill not identical with
the Licensing Law in England. If none of
these Acts disturb the close attachment which
we have to Great Britain, and which I hope
will always continue, why on earth should we
cripple this great industry for the sake of
conformity with the law of time in England?
There is another argument which I have seen
used. It is said the majority are in the
towns, and the towns want summer time. I
am not quite sure about that. I heard only
an
last week complaints on the part of working
men, that it kept the children up too late.
But supposing there is a majority in favour
of a long summer evening, we realise that
minorities must suffer when serious material
interests clash. But you are placing a very
great burden on an industry vital to the
whole community in order that the townsman
may have an hour's more amusement. That
is really what you are doing. The townsman,
the shopkeeper, or the manufacturer can close
his works and say, "I will begin at such and
such a time to-morrow." The farmer's business never ceases, day or night. The farmer
does not have half the holidays, his business
is always running; nature is always running;
his anxieties are always running on. I would
very strongly appeal to the Government to
reconsider this question, and accept my hon.
Friend's amendment, and if they think, after
careful consideration, that it is necessary to
bring in this summer time, let them bring in
a separate Bll next year. I heartily support
the amendment proposed by my hon. Friend.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to consider this amendment so far as it
affects the majority of the people of the Six
Counties. It appears to me that those who
have already spoken are considering the
interests of those among whom they are
situated.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: No, no.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The farmer
may have some little disadvantage, but at
the same time there seems to be considerable
advantage to the citizen, even in the small
towns as well as in the large cities. I think
there is a considerable amount of imagination
about the grievances that the Summer Time
Act has brought to the farmer We are told
that the cow would be affected by the change.
Well, I have not heard it said whether the
hen lays as regularly or not. But how the
cow, or even the laying of the hen, could be
interfered with is more than I can understand. There are many more things to be
taken into account in this matter. It has
been stated repeatedly by one or two hon.
Senators that children cannot be got in at
night or got to bed. I quite believe that,
even a child knows what is really good for it
and when it is happy. Undoubtedly the
lengthened evening and good sunshine enable
the child to have a longer time to enjoy
itself before bedtime. It is quite true that
it is impossible almost to get children into
their homes at a reasonable hour in summer
time. It is not only the children-they must
not be neglected-but you must take into
consideration those people who are tied up in
thousands in the works in the City of Belfast
and in towns such as Lisburn, Portadown and
Expiring Laws Bill-
Committee.

other places-women who are closed up inside
four walls from a quarter to eight in the
morning probably, and from a quarter to one
or two o'clock until six o'clock in the evening.
Is it not necessary to give these people as
much daylight as possible and as much leisure
as possible? This Daylight Saving Bill has
undoubtedly brought about tremendous
change for the benefit of the worker. These
men in the shipyards value the lengthened
evening. It enables them to get out into the
country to see the green fields. I must say
that so far as I am concerned I cannot vote
for the amendment to have this Daylight
Saving Bill deleted. I think while I would
be in favour of making some little sacrifice
in the interests of the farming class, the
sacrifice would be too great in a case such
as this. I must certainly vote against the
amendment and in favour of the maintenance
of the Dayling Saving Bill.
I
Mr. GREER: I do not often find myself in
disagreement with my hon. Friend who has
moved this Amendment (Mr. Leslie), but on
the present occasion I certainly differ very
strongly. He was professedly speaking on
behalf of the agricultural community.
take issue with him there. I think he is only
entitled to speak on behalf of the farmers
themselves. I live within some 15 miles of
the sea, and I am often delighted to see
hundreds of farm labourers going on their
bicycles down to the sea in the evenings,
delighted to have the opportunity. I think
it is a splendid thing for them to have a
few hours to enjoy the fine sea breeze. It
must be good for them and enable them to
do the work on the farm much better than
if they had nowhere to go, but just to lounge
about their homes.
My Friend also spoke in the interests of
the cows. I do not understand how he was
instructed by them. I think the cows very
readily conform to the
system.
I
new
should not be very much guided
in my opinion or judge the merits of this
Act by its effect upon the cows. He also referred to the children in the slums. Well,
after all, there are some naughty children
who will not go to sleep when they ought
to. I suppose their parents have some way
of dealing with them. I do not think we
should hang up a Bill of this importance
because there are some children in the slums
who cannot sleep.
I cannot help feeling that the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) perhaps omitted one of the
most important considerations in regard to
his Bill, and that is that it might require
one to rise one hour earlier in the morning.
That matter might possibly-I do not know
whether it does or not affect his judgment
to some extent. I can only say for my part
that with the exception of very few people
everyone I have spoken to is delighted with
the long evenings and the arrangements for
the observance of summer time. I cannot
see why the farmers if it is too early to deal
with the labour at the earlier hour, they
cannot change the hour and let their
labourers come an hour later and stay an
hour longer. We have heard in general
terms that it is crippling the great agricultural industry. I would like some of my
friends to give a concrete example of how
it cripples the industry. It seems to me
that the farmer can very easily arrange so
that it would not do them any harm at all.
Apart from the farmers, who I believe are
few in number, their families, the labourers,
and certainly all the dwellers in the towns
and villages would be very much in favour
of this Bill being continued, and for that
reason I would be very sorry to see it dropped.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: May I
give my hon. Friend an example of how it
cripples agriculture? I make arrangements
personally as he suggests that you should
work late, and I have had men leave me
because they wanted to get away before the
shops closed. That is one way it cripples
us. Also he seems to think that there is
not a general demand. I have a resolution
here which I will not trouble you by reading
unless you wish it. It is from the Executive
Committee of the Farmers' Union, representing all the farmers in the whole of the
Six Counties, and expressing their opposition
to summer time.
Mr. WOODS: I would just like to add my
voice against the amendment, because I
believe the Summer Time Bill is a very useful
Bill in industrial centres, not only in allowing us to have an hour extra at night for
amusement but for the great saving it is
in industrial cities and towns in light. That
is a considerable advantage to the working
people for saving in taxation. I quite agree
with my hon. Friend, Senator Cunningham,
that there is a great deal of imagination
amongst farmers in regard to this great
disability. I go sometimes into the country
-I cannot afford to live there and I find
farmers raising objections to the Summer
Time Bill, but when you really get down
to argument with them and ask for concrete
facts about it you will find that they can
give you nothing concrete. I have found
that in some parts of the country the farmers
do not use summer time at all. They change
one clock specially to keep the time of trains,
but the other clocks on the farm are kept
at the usual time and they carry on their
work in the usual way. I do not see any
great disadvantage and I certainly see very
great advantage to the industrial centres.
That is the reason I am in favour of the
Summer Time Bill passing again.
Expiring Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I think that based on the amendment proposed by my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) we
have had a very interesting debate, and I
am sure that no one will quarrel with him
for having raised this question again. It
seems to me that the arguments we have
heard for and against are certainly arguments which anyone has a right to put
forward. The matter which we have to
consider as a Senate is, what is best for the
community as a whole. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) I was anxious at one moment
of the debate lest it might develop into an
argument between the agricultural interest
and the industrial interest, and that is a
controversy which, like all my hon. Friends
here, we expressly deprecate. The community
of Ulster is made up of a splendid agricultural population and an equally efficient and
courageous industrial population, and one
without the other cannot exist. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) It seems to me of
the highest importance that both these great
wheels should work together so as to make
our Province the place which we desire it
to be. The particular question of summer
time is one which my hon. Friend rightly
says came up in the House of Commons at
Westminister, and when he spoke of the
debates which used to occur in that assembly
I began rather to tremble and to wonder
whether he might not have unearthed some
speech which I might have delivered on a
Friday afternoon in the House of Commons.
Mr. LESLIE: Oh, no.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
As he assures me he did not I will not
pursue the matter further. In respect of
that I think we should recognise that we
are a very conservative people. Probably
the agricultural community are more conservative than other sections of the population,
and when an innovation is brought in they
will believe longer than perhaps any other
section of the community that it is not conducive to the national welfare. I do feel
that this perhaps is a case in point, and that
when summer time has been exploited for
some years the agricultural community no
doubt reluctantly will settle down to the
changed state of affairs and will find that
their prophecies and prognostications as to
the disasters that would ensue have not
actually taken place. I think my hon. Friend
will agree with me that this practically
occurred across the water. I may be wrong,
but I have not heard, recently at all events,
that there is a strong body of opinion in
Great Britain against the re-enactment every
year of summer time. I somehow feel that
this state of affairs will come about in
Ulster, and that the agricultural population
will realise that on the whole the advantages
accruing from summer time in the direction
of assisting the industrial population are on
the whole best for the community. My hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie) knows more about the
gyrations of the sun and probably about the
idiosyncracies of the cow than I do, but I
hardly feel that those arguments are sufficiently cogent to induce the Government to
reconsider the position they have taken up.
I am willing, however, to say this to the
hon. Member (Mr. Leslie), that there is no
reason why if he does not agree with me,
if he thinks my arguments are not as cogent
as his-there is no reason why he should
not in our next Session follow in the
steps of Mr. Willett on the other side and
introduce a private Member's Bill for the
purpose of reverting to the time that he
desires, or propose another time than the
one ordained by the sun. The point which I
would venture to make particularly is the
advantages accruing as a whole to the community. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I
believe that when we consider it from that
point of view, the difficulties in this direction
which surround the farmer are really less
than the difficulties which if we change,
would surround the industrial community.
My hon. Friend has made a point of what
I said and what the Prime Minister said in
another place as regards the actual figures
of the two populations. I feel unwilling to
speak of two populations as if they were
entirely separate. It is quite true that we
were originally an agricultural people, but
with the passing of time and the march of
centuries we have developed industry, and
while we can certainly say that the agricultural community is of longer antiquity than
the industrial community I think we
must realise that the prosperity of the great
industrial centres is perhaps the bed-rock of
the prosperity of the country. The more the
industrial centres prosper the greater is the
demand which they will make upon the
agricultural community for the purpose of
supplying them with their requirements. So
that it does seem to me that we should concentrate on making the industrial centres
additionally successful, and I feel that it can
be argued that summer time gives them many
advantages. My right hon. and gallant
Friend (Colonel Perceval-Maxwell) spoke of
the leisure which it gave to the townsman.
Now leisure is not a thing to be decried, and
it is of the greatest importance to those who
work in our shipyards and in our factories
that they should have an ample amount of
leisure, because we know quite well that no
good work can be performed unless there is
leisure also. The present shortness of hours
as compared with working hours formerly is
not really a concession extracted by militant
trade unionists out of unwilling employers.
Not a bit. Employers have realised that the
best work is obtained by observing these con-
