generally been imposed on poor weak women.
The trade unions representing most oссираtions would not tolerate fines. I am very
glad that there is no attempt made to alter
this clause. It is a protection for the weakest and poorest, and certainly above anything
else in this Bill likely to benefit the lower
dog and the poorer women.
Ordered: That Clause 15, as amended,
stand part of the Bill.
Ordered: That Clauses 16 (Recovery of
sums due to workers), 17 (Constitution and
proceedings of Trade Boards), 18 (Establishment of District Trade Committees), and 19
(Appointment of officers) stand part of the
Bill.
Clause 20 (Powers of officers).
Sub-clause (I) —
(e) to examine, either alone or in pre
sence of any other person, as he thinks
fit, with respect to any matters under
this Act, any person whom he finds in
any such place as aforesaid, or whom he
has reasonable cause to believe to be or
to have been a worker in any trade
within the jurisdiction of a Trade Board,
and to require every such person to be so
examined and to sign a declaration of the
truth of the matters in respect of which
he is so examined.
(2) If any person fails to furnish the
means required by an officer as necessary
for any entry or inspection or the exercise
of his powers under this section, or if any
person hinders or molests any officer in the
exercise of the powers given by this section,
or refuses to produce any document or give
any information which any officer is entitled to require him to produce or give
under the powers given by this section,
that person shall be liab'e on summary
conviction in respect of each offence to a
fine not exceeding five pounds.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 20,
page 15, line 20, to leave out from
"examine" ("to examine, either alone") to
"with" in line 21 (thinks fit, with respect
to"). The amendment which stands in my
name on the Paper suggests omitting the
words indicated in Sub-section (e). I am in
a difficulty, to start with, because I must,
of course, admit that the words I want to
leave out from this present Bill were put in
by the Parliament of the United Kingdom
in the 1918 Act. They were put in, or
smuggled in, for they are found on the last
page of the Schedule at the back. Anyhow,
there they are. However, this Bill purports
seems
to
me
to
be
too
to be not only a Bill to consolidate the law
relating to Trade Boards but also to amend
the law. It is generally understood, I think,
that consolidation and amendment do not run
very well together. It is the more usual
practice, I think, in England, certainly of
later years, to amend the law first and consolidate it afterwards. The most outstanding
example that occurs to me at the minute is
the Companies Act. Everybody has heard
of the Companies Act of 1908. That
Act, Chapter 69, of the particular year,
not only repealed the Companies Act,
1907, but the Companies Act, 1908, chapter 12.
However, if we do mix amendment with consolidation I do not see why we should not
have it both ways. I do not think these particular words are desirable words to leave in.
The objection
that
rather putting are
you
much power in the hands of the
inspector. I do not see why it should be for
the inspector to decide whether the person he
is examining is to be examined alone or not.
It may very well be that if the person examined is an employee the employer might
wish to be present and to ask questions. It
might very well be that the employee himself
or herself might wish-particularly if it is a
girl or young woman-that young woman
might wish to have her mother present.
Whether it is a woman or not they might
wish to have some adviser present. I do not
see why they should not be permitted to do
SO. I do not see that the words are necessary,
and I do not think that the inspector should
be given such strong power. I do not think
that in practice he really wants it, because
I think a tactful inspector-and I hope the
right hon. Gentleman who is paying us
another welcome visit will never employ an
inspector who is not thoroughly tactful-1
think a tactful inspector can get on very
well without this additional power. I hope
the Government will consent to omit these
words.
Mr. ANDREWS: I feel it is right that the
inspectors should have this power, whether
they use it or not. Under the Factory Acts
they have the power, and under the existing
Trade Board law, that is under the 1918 Act,
as the Senator has said, we have the power.
I do not think the power has been abused.
and I hope never will be abused; but you will
understand that an inspector has a very
delicate duty to perform, and when he wishes
to cross-question
worker about certain
matters he may wish to have some other employee of the firm present, because of course
if proceedings are taken afterwards in Court
a worker may make a very good witness or a
very bad witness. I understand a good witness is a witness who swears in the Courts
to what he or she previously said in private.
If it is simply left to a worker, who may
a
Trade Boards Bill-
Committee.
THE SUNATE.

[Mr. Andrews.]
have had a good deal of pressure put upon
him in the interval to retract in Court what
he said in private, and if an inspector has
not the power to call in another witness,
owing to the fact that no one heard the previous conversation. I think the object of the
law very often may be defeated. I take it the
object of the law is to bring wrongdoers to
justice. I think no impediment should be
puz in the way of the law to bring wrongdoers to justice. I regret, therefore, that I
connot, on behalf of the Government, accept
the amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: As these words are part of
the Imperial Act I will not offer any opposition, but I wish the words were not in this
Bill. I beg to ask the Committee for leave
to withdraw the amendment.
Mr. ANDREWS: Thank you.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
The CHAIRMAN: The next amendment is
a manuscript one. Clause 20, sub-clause 2, at
Page 15, leave out lines 30 to 32 inclusive.
("If any person fails to furnish the means
required by an officer as necessary for any
entry or inspection or the exercise of his
powers under this Section or").
at
a
Mr. LESLIE: I must apologise for not
giving in the Amendment in time to be put
on the Order paper, but it will perhaps be
remembered that when speaking on the
Second reading of the Bill I drew attention
to the extraordinary speed with which this
House was expected to legislate. I reminded
the House that the Bill was in our hands at
ten o'clock on Saturday morning, that it was
to be discussed
quarter past
three o'clock on Monday afternoon,
and that it was to be taken in
Committee on Wednesday, and I asked for
a little further time. However, we have not
had that further time given, and I cannot but
think that those who listened to my hon.
Friend (Mr. Greer) will be impressed with
the necessity there was for giving ample time
to consider this Bill. Coming to the particular Amendment, I do not in the first place
understand the words as printed in the Bill,
but again I am brought up against the fact
that these words occur in the Imperial Actthe Act of 1909, not, as in the last case, the
Act of 1918. The first three words seem to
be the trouble. The Sub-clause says:
If any person fails to furnish the means
required by an officer.
"Any person." Anybody moving along the
street is presumably, if required by an inspector to run away and fetch a ladder to take
him up to a loft where he suspects something
is wrong. I do not think that can be the
intention of Parliament. The Factory and
Workshops Act, 1901, I observe, seems to have
been used a good deal in the preparation of
this Bill. Sections and passages appear to
have been taken at times, and rejected at
other times. In the Factory and Workshops
Act the expression used for things of this
sort is:
"The occupier of any factory or workshop, his agent and servants, must furnish
the means required."
There would be no objection, I think, to
amending this Sub-section so as to incorporate those words, but to leave it as it is
is ridiculous. Moreover, I do not think that
the words really serve any useful purpose.
The Sub-clause may perfectly well begin:
If any person hinders or molests any
officer in the exercise of the powers
That would catch such a case as the legendary one of the sheriff's officer referred to in
one of Mr. Charles Lever's novels. Everybody remembers the story. The sheriff's
officer insisted on seeing what was inside a
loft. A ladder had to be brought up. He
went up the ladder, and then found the loft
was empty. When he turned round, the ladder was gone, and, according to the story, the
ladder remained away a very long time. That
sheriff's officer was molested or hindered in
the exercise of his duties. I hope it will not
happen again. At all events, anything of
that sort is sufficiently covered by the remaining words of the Sub-clause. I do not think
really that this Committee should pass the
Clause as it is, even although it has been
passed in England. I do not think we require
it, and I therefore beg to move.
Mr. ANDREWS: This Clause is so worded
that we may bring both classes of wrongdoers
to justice. There are those who actively
impede an inspector or officer of the Department in the discharge of his duty, and there
are those who may passively do it. My hon.
Friend has laid great emphasis upon the
words "If any person," but I would like to
lay emphasis on the words "Required by an
officer as necessary "-that is, an officer of the
Department. My hon. Friend said that he
would be sorry to impute folly to an Act. I
am sure he would be equally sorry to impute
folly to the administration of an Act, and I
do not think any responsible officer of the
Department would be likely to exceed his
duty in this respect. It is all important, in
my opinion, that inspectors should possess
considerable powers, otherwise the intention
of the law would be defeated in many instances. On behalf of the Government, I
regret that we cannot accept this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think it is worth
while pursuing the Amendment. I am afraid
I cannot say that I agree with my right hon.

Friend's answer, but these words again are in

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 20,
page 16, line 10, leave out lines 10 to 17 inclusive:
(3) A person examined under this Section
shall not be excused from answering any
question on the ground that the answer
thereto may criminate or tend to criminate
himself; but any statement made by a
person in answer to a question put to him
on an examination under this Section shall
not, except in the case of any criminal
proceeding for perjury, be admissible in
evidence against him in any proceeding,
civil or criminal.
I am afraid that Clause 20 has occasioned
trouble to the Committee somewhat often.
On this Clause I stated on the Second Reading
that powers were sought which were not
recommended in the Report of July, 1922, and
that these powers were not included in either
the English Statute of 1909 or that of 1918.
They are powers which the Ministry have
not got at present, and which I do not think
they ought to have. It seems to me that we
have to examine this Clause very carefully.
It says:
A person examined under this Section
That person may be, apparently, an employer
or an employee, and the examination presumably refers to the examination by an
inspector, which may have been made
privately or may have been made, as the
right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Andrews) indicated a few minutes ago, by an inspector in
the presence of a witness so that he may
bear witness hereafter if necessary to what
had been said. The right hon. Gentleman
told us himself that when the inspector is
examining a man he should take with him a
witness. The Clause goes on:
A person examined under this Section
shall not be excused from answering any
question on the ground that the answer
thereto may criminate or tend to criminate
himself.
I object absolutely to such a power being
given to anyone. There is an old Latin
maxim. I am sorry to quote Latin in this
Committee; but the maxim is very well
known. I see that the noble Marquess (the
Marquess of Londonderry) remembers it. The
maxim is: "Nemo debet se accusare nisi
coram Deo" (nobody ought to accuse himself except in the presence of God). That is
a foundation of British law and justice, and
this Clause violates it, as I say, without the
excuse of a recommendation in a Report, and
without the excuse of having had it included
in two English Statutes, and with the additional disadvantage, as it seems to me, that
it is going practically counter to the Factories
and Workshops Act of 1901, which expressly
provides that a person shall not be required
to criminate himself. The principle sought
to be introduced here is entirely new, and to
my mind thoroughly objectionable. The
Sub-Clause goes on to the apparent
mitigation. It says:
Any statement made by a person in
answer to a question put to him on
examination under this Sectionan
the examination under this section being, I
conclude, still the examination by the
inspector, with or without his private witnessshall not, except in the case of any criminal
proceeding for perjury, be admissible in
evidence against him in any proceeding,
civil or criminal.
That is the apparent mitigation. A man's
statement is not to be used before any Court.
Fortunately, even if that was not there, the
question whether a statement was admissible
would not be for the Ministry of Labour or
the inspector to decide. It would be a ques-.
tion for the judge. Happily that particular
protection of the subject is not sought to be
taken away. Moreover, that protection, such
as it is, does not go far enough. The mere
fact that you may not use a statement which
you have extracted in an undesirable manner
does not take away from the objection to
what may come by means of questions
which have been asked. In this way the
inspector may build up his case; that is,
although he may not use the direct answer
he may on that direct answer found a case.
He may be indirectly using the statement.
It must not be imagined, and we cannot
allow it to go forth, that because an
Inspector has occasion to interrogate anybody, whether an employer or an employe,
an offence has necessarily been committed.
It is not for an Inspector to decide those
things. No Inspector ought to have power
to require a statement from an ordinary
British subject if the ordinary British subject does not desire to answer on the ground
that his reply may incriminate, or tend to
incriminate, himself or might be so used
as the foundation of a case against him.
It must be remembered that the power of
questioning prisoners who have actually been
arrested by policemen is limited most strictly.
Neither a magistrate nor a judge is entitled
to ask anybody charged before him any
question. The only way in which an accused
person can be interrogated in a Court of
Justice is under various Acts, of which the
principal is the last one we adorted only
Trade Boards Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
this Session--when a person is voluntarily in
the witness-box. In such a case he can be
asked a question which may tend to incriminate him as regards a particular offence.
but he has had the advantage of legal advice
as to whether he should or should not submit himself to examination. I do not think
that when this section was allowed to come
before us its full meaning could have been
appreciated. But we have to deal with it as
it is, and for my own part I cannot consent to
such a section being allowed to stand. I beg
to move that the whole of Sub-Section 3 be
expunged.
Mr. ANDREWS: My hon. Friend rather
apologised to the Committee for quoting
Latin. There is an old English quotation which I venture to place before the
Committee, and that is: "Doctors differ and
patients die." It is rather a curious coincidence that this sub-section was added to the
Bill in the other House in order to please
an hon. and learned Member, a very distinguished man in the legal profession in
our House. The hon. Member has pointed to
an apparent safeguard. I think it is a real
safeguard. I think I ought to read the words
to the Committee.
But any statement made by a person in
answer to a question put to him on an
examination under this section shall not
be used against him except in case of
criminal proceedings.
My hon. Friend said the real protection
lay with the Judge and that this clause did
not take away that real protection. Therefore I think we can safery leave it with the
Judge, and I do not think it is necessary for
the Government to accept the amendment.
I think it is desirable that this power should
be retained, and I can only repeat what I
previously said, that in administration it will
be reasonably used. It is desirable that
those who have placed upon them the very
responsible task of seeing that Acts are complied with should not be deprived of the
powers necessary to see that there is compliance. It is an essential feature of the
administration of this Act that there should
be compliance with it, for the reason that if
one firm is obliged to adhere to a statutory
rate and another firm is by device able to
escape that statutory rate the honest firm is
placed at a great disadvantage by the unscrupulous person who is trying to avoid
complying with the statutory rate. Again,
the worker of the unscrupulous employer is
left in a very hopeless position. For all these
reasons, and for a great many others I could
mention, I feel it is essential that we should
leave the Inspectors ample powers to discharge their duties faithfully and well in the
interests of the entire community. I may say
that at headquarters we are most particular
to see that inspection is carefully and
properly performed, and I am glad to say that
in more than two years' experience I have
never had a single complaint from either
an employer or an employee in our area that
inspectors had exceeded their duties or acted
in any other way than civilly and carefully
while discharging the onerous duties which
the State has placed upon them.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid that the root
principle of the right hon. Gentleman's reply
is that in order to prevent one injustice he
wishes to run the risk of another.
Mr. ANDREWS: I cannot go so far as that,
but if the hon. Member is satisfied to meet
me half-way I will say it is the choice of two
evils, and I am choosing the lesser.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite prepared to go on
that ground and I can see no necessity for
introducing any evil at all. I am afraid that
in this case, much as I regret it, I am not
able to withdraw the amendment. The proposal is that we should give to the Inspector
power which is not given to any judge or
magistrate. I am very sorry, but if it is the
opinion of the Committee that this section
should be inserted then the House must take
the responsibility. I cannot.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Probably
we can get some more information on this
point before we are called upon to vote. I
must admit that so far as law is concerned
I am very innocent. I have not even had
the opportunity of being arrested to learn
that much about it. It might be said that
if this sub-section does no harm it will do no
good, but I cannot see the necessity for it.
Is there going to be any compulsion put on
a person to give evidence? If a person desires to remain silent what are you going to
do? The section says a person shall not be
excused, but if he remains silent are you
going to put pressure on him? I have known
case of workers who have not cared to commit themselves so far as to accept responsibility. They would prefer to remain silent,
but if a person is forced by pressure of some
kind to give evidence, what is the position?
It is a rather serious matter from the
worker's point of view.
Mr. ANDREWS: I may say in that connection that if a worker or an employer refuses absolutely to give evidence I think that
would be an end of it. Of course, it would
show a weakness, especially if an employer
refused to give evidence. It would look as
if he was not in a position to clear himself.
I will go as far as this to-day. I will again
have this matter carefully inquired into if
it will meet my hon. Friend's case, and on
the Report stage I will be prepared either to
amend this clause or give a further explanation as to why I think it ought to be adhered
to.
Mr. LESLIE: Of course, I do not wish to
press anything unduly, and if I can be of
any little assistance, not in draftsmanship.

There is a precedent in the Factory and
Workshops Act, 1901, where an Inspector of
the Board of Trade had to be authorised by
the Secretary of State. I do not think it is
a desirable section. It is not fair to the Bar
and it is not fair to the solicitors to take
from them a possible source of revenue. We
do it in some cases. In the case of the Education Act we gave power to the school board
officer to do very trivial things which he
could do well enough, but it is not fair to
go on whittling away the public work of a
solicitor or a barrister. It is very detrimental
to the Bar-it is very hard lines on the junior
Bar-to take away from them the possibilities
of employment.
but in giving an indication of what I should
say on the Report stage, I shall only be too
happy to do so. I hope I am to interpret
the right hon. Gentleman as meaning that
there will be very material alterations in
the clause. A man must not be under any
compulsion to do any injury to himself in
The earlier
possible future proceedings.
words of this section give power to these
inspectors to require any person to give information. The person to be examined
under this section is bound, or will be bound,
to give information to these inspectors.
Therefore, I am afraid I am a little at sea
as to what the position is at the moment.
I have moved my amendment, and I have
not withdrawn it. The right hon. Gentleman has promised to further consider the
matter.
Mr. ANDREWS: May I say at this point
that if my hon. Friend is good enough to
spare the time and will arrange to meet me
at any place and time that may be conve
nient for him I will be very pleased to consult him on the matter and endeavour to so
alter the clause that it will meet his wishes
and save us having to divide the House
to-day on what, after all, is not an essential
clause of this most important measure.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that meets the case
for the moment, and, therefore, if the House
will permit me, for the time being, I will
withdraw the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 21 (Officers to produce certificates
when required), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 22 (Power to take and conduct
proceedings).
*
*
*
*
*
*
Sub clause (2) Any officer appointed by the
Ministry under this Act and the secretary of
a Trade Board, or any officer of a Trade
Board authorised for the purpose, may, although not a barrister-at-law or solicitor,
prosecute or conduct before a court of
summary jurisdiction any proceedings arising
under this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 22,
page 16, to leave out sub-clause 2. I am not
quite sure what the first two lines mean. It
surely does not mean that an officer is to be
jointly appointed by the Ministry and the
Secretary of the Trade Board. That is really
what it says. Or does it mean an officer appointed by the Ministry or the Secretary of a
Trade Board or any officer of a Trade Board
so authorised. If it is the officer authorised
we are not told who is to authorise him.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
They are like a trade union.
I
Mr. LESLIE: As the noble Marquess says,
they are a trade union. I am surprised to
find the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister
of Labour, bringing in such a proposal.
am afraid there are several objections to this
clause besides that of doing a solicitor out
of his job. Cases under this Act will come
in some instances before a bench of magistrates.
A bench of magistrates, like any
other court, is entitled to the assistance of
competent advocacy. It is not fair to any
bench to put prosecutions into amateur
hands. Neither is it fair to witnesses that
they should be examined and cross-examined
by incompetent people. Furthermore, you
have got to remember that if your prosecutor
is an officer of the Ministry, instead of being
a counsel or a solicitor, he is, being only
human, very much more apt to unduly press
his case. He is, I think, more apt than a
trained lawyer would be to forget that he is
only there to assist the court and as representing the Crown. On all those grounds I
think this part of the work and I hope it
will not be much-would be very much better
in the hands of a trained lawyer; but, if it is
decided that this clause should stand in some
form or other. I must refer to what I said
at the beginning, and suggest some trivial
alterations. At the moment, however, I beg
to move and I hope it will be carried-that
this sub-clause be excised altogether.
Mr. ANDREWS: With regard to the alteration in the wording, I am quite willing to
meet the point of my hon. Friend(Mr. Leslie)
and substitute the word "or" for "and," but
that, I presume, is a minor objection to the
clause. On the grounds of economy, that we
hear a great deal about in what I may call
our House, I am sorry I cannot accept an
amendment to do away with the clause. We
have got to consider economy not only from
the point of view of the State, but also from
the standpoint of the litigant, in other words
the workers' side, and if you are going to
have a case conducted on the one side by a
trained lawyer, it really will be necessary that
1D
Trade Boards Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Andrews.]
the case on the other side should also be conducted by a trained lawyer, and that would
impose hardships on the worker.
Mr. LESLIE: He might get him off.
Mr. ANDREWS: That is perfectly true;
but he might not. You would get the big
guns at it, and I do not know whether you
would get greater justice from two big guns
than from two small guns. It seems to me it
would be simply reinforcing the attack, and
at the same time it would be reinforcing the
defence. I think on the grounds of economy
it would not be desirable to delete this clause,
especially as it is the existing law not only as
regards the Trade Boards, but as regards the
Factory Acts, and I would ask
hon.
Friend not to press the amendment.
my
Mr. LESLIE: As the right hon. Gentleman
is anxious to rob Peter to pay Paul, and
nobody else seems anxious to prevent him,
I beg to withdraw the amendment. As
regards the verbal alteration, I presume it
will be brought up by the Government on
the Report stage.
Mr. ANDREWS: Yes, on the Report stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Moved: That Clause 23 (Provisions as to
Legal Proceedings) stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE. In Clause 23 there is something of which I have not had an opportunity
of giving notice. It relates to Sub-Section 2,
which states:
(2) A Court of Summary Jurisdiction,
when hearing and determining any information or complaint in any matter arising
under this Act, shall be constituted of a
resident magistrate appointed under the
Constabulary (Ireland) Act, 1836, and any
enactment amending the same, sitting alone
or with others, or of two or more justices
of the peace sitting in petty sessions at a
place appointed for holding petty sessions.
Before the Clause is ordered to stand part
of the Bill, I would like to ask the right hon.
Gentleman what the intention of the Government is.
Mr. ANDREWS: I did not catch what
the hon. Member said. Is it Clause 26?
Mr. LESLIE: No, Clause 23, at the top of
page 17 of the Bill.
Mr. ANDREWS: Do you wish to get an
answer now?
The CHAIRMAN-I think the hon. Member had better give notice, and bring it up
on Report.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I give notice that ]
will move to expunge Sub-section 2 on the
Report stage, and I will tell the right hon.
Gentleman privately, if I may, my views on
the point. That would be the simplest way.
Mr. ANDREWS: I may be able to satisfy
my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) now. The point
is that the cases can be tried by a Resident
Magistrate acting with an ordinary magistrate, or before two magistrates. The idea
is that if there is no stipendiary magistrate
present the cases can still be heard, but that
if there is a stipendiary magistrate present
he is not being asked to leave the Bench in
order that the cases may be tried before
ordinary magistrates alone. I do not know
whether that meets your point or not. If
the hon. Member prefers to leave it I will
deal with it further. We can have a chat
about it when we meet about the other
Clause to which the hon. Member has referred.
Mr. LESLIE: I suppose I had better leave
it at that for the moment.
The CHAIRMAN: If the hon. Member is
satisfied I think it would meet with the
approval of the House if the matter were
postponed to the Report Stage.
Mr. LESLIE: Very well.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 24 (Regulations as to mode of
giving notice), 25 (Regulations to be laid
before Parliament), 26 (Expenses of carrying
Act into effect), and 27 (Repeal) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 28 (Short Title).
This Act may be cited as the Trade
Boards Act (Northern Ireland), 1923.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page 20,
line 21, to insert at the end the following new
Sub-Section:
(2) This Act shall come into operation on
the thirty-first day of March, nineteen
hundred and twenty-four.
I propose as a short addition here to insert
the date when the Act shall come into operation. If we do not put in a date it will
presumably come into immediate operation,
and that is not what was done in either of the
English Acts, 1909 and 1918. I see that the
first Act, that of 1909, received the Royal
Assent on the 20th October, and came into
operation on the 1st January following. The
later Act received the Royal Assent on 8th
August, and came into operation on the 1st
October. As we know, Acts do not necessarily get the Royal Assent very quickly. It
often happens that Bills stand over till the
end of the Session, and a good many assents
are given simultaneously. So it may very
well be that a Bill has gone through all the
Trade Boards Bill-
Committee.

:
other stages and been before the public for
some little time before it receives the Royal
Assent. That will not be so in this case, I
imagine, because if this Bill survives its
final stages here, and is approved, it will not
be long before it is on the Statute Book. It
occurred to me that the proper date to insert
would be perhaps the 31st March next, which
happens to be a Monday, and, therefore, I
did not select the 1st April. (Laughter.) If
the right hon. Gentleman agrees in principle
that some little time should be given, because of men having to get these books, records, and so on, and if he does not think
the date I have suggested a suitable one, I
should be quite happy to amend it.
I
Mr. ANDREWS: It is quite true that the
other Acts were not brought into operation
as quickly as is proposed in this case, but the
position was entirely different. You know Mr.
Speaker, my Lord, that when this matter was
being very thoroughly inquired into by the
Committee over which you presided, both the
cmployers and the workers were dissatisfied
with the existing legislation, and it would be
a pity to postpone, further than is necessary,
the bringing of this Act into operation.
think it will satisfy my hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie) and this House when I say that I am
in a position to state that both employers and
workers desire that this Act should be
brought into operation at as early a date as
possible. I do not say that either employers
or workers are entirely satisfied with the Act
as it stands. Probably one section of the
community considers that it goes too far,
while another section of the community considers that it does not go far enough; but I
am gratified to be able to state that both
sections of the industrial community prefer
this Bill to existing Acts, and I think, therefore, when I am able to give the House that
assurance that hon. Members will agree that
the sooner it becomes not only the law of the
land but is made operative the better.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I hope
Senator Leslie will withdraw this Amendwent as he has withdrawn the others. The
date he suggests for bringing the Act into
operation involves too long a delay, and I
certainly could not vote for the hon.
Member's addition. I hope the Act will come
into operation by the 1st January at the
latest.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not understand that
the right hon. Gentleman said he would agree
to the 1st January. I am perfectly content
with the 1st January.
Mr. ANDREWS: I do not think it is desirable to postpone it for even a day longer
than is necessary. I think I am right in
saying that it comes into operation as soon
as it receives the Royal Assent, and it would
be better to leave it at that, for the reason I
have already stated, namely, that both employers and workers desire that it should
come into operation immediately. After all,
if they are agreed on a matter of this kind I
do not see that there is any reason why we
should not act in accordance with their
wishes.
Mr. LESLIE: After that deliberate statement by the Minister I hope the House will
permit me to withdraw the amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

Colonel SINCLAIR: May I ask the Noble
Marquess whether he can assure us that
where the words superannuation allowance occur in this Clause such allowances
will be calculated not upon the statutory
salaries, which may date from a very very
remote period, but on such emoluments as
have been granted in more recent times?
Some of the statutory salaries go back to the
time of William IV. and are altogether inadequate under present-day conditions.
I
should like to be assured that, in dealing with
the question alluded to, where it says "superannuation allowance" it really means
salaries and emoluments. May I take this
opportunity of congratulating the Minister of
Home Affairs upon introducing this Bill at
the present time? For five and twenty years
and more every one has been agreed that
Poor Law reform has been called for. It is
left in the hands of the County Councils to
modernise the hospitals of their counties.
423 Local Government Bill-

THE SENATE.
Committee.
The CHAIRMAN: I am afraid the hon.
and gallant Member (Colonel Sinclair) is
making what is really more a second reading
than a Committee speech.
The MARQUESS of LONDONDERRY: I
can quite briefly assure the hon. and gallant
Member on the point he has raised. The
Minister of Home Affairs in another place
has already stated that the salaries on which
superannuation is based do include emoluments.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 2 (Provision as to Constitution
of Towns and Urban Districts Pending the
Taking of a Census); and 3 (Limitation on
Power of Auditor) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
NEW CLAUSE (Amendment of 61 and 62
Vict. c. 37, s. 3 (2).)
Sub-section (2) of Section three of the Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1898, shall have
effect as if the words "not more than five"
were substituted for the words "one or two."
Brought up and read a first time.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move that the
clause be now read a second time. I thought
that after Clause 3 was the appropriate
place to put in this new clause which I
suggest. My suggestion is that we should
give the County Councils power, if they so
desire, to co-opt additional Councillors to
the number of not more than five. At present
our County Councils are limited by statute
to one or two co-opted members, and it
would be desirable to give them a power,
which they need not use unless they desire to
do so, to co-opt up to five. Of course they
need not co-opt them at once. The co-option
of five need not follow immediately at the
first sitting of the Council. The reasons I
think it is desirable to increase this power
of co-option are partly because of the heavy
duties now cast on County Councils and more
particularly with a view to the appointment
of Regional Committees under the Education
Act. Also it is sometimes found that the
power of co-option is of great value in balancing the various interests. The electors are
sometimes a little neglectful of various interests, and it is very advisable to have a
kind of check elsewhere. Moreover, if you
allow more members to be co-opted you
strengthen the County Councils by inducing
men to come forward whose time will not
permit of their serving with a constituency
to attend to. A man might very well serve
occasionally on a County Council, but he may
not feel able to attend often enough to support the needs of a particular constituency.
It seems to me that the time is opportune for
the change, because the constituencies are
about to be materially altered. It also seems
to me that this House is singularly well
qualified to discuss and express an opinion on
a matter of this kind because of the number
of its Members who serve upon County
Councils. If it is thought that my suggested
amendment offends the rule about legislation
by reference it will be easy to add the words
which relate "to the co-option of additional
Councillors," in the appropriate place, but
I do not think that that is necessary. I beg
to move.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I am very glad to
support this amendment. From my experience of a County Council the co-opted members were among the most valuable and
independent members of that body, and they
were so much respected and valued that of
the four Chairmen of the Council who have
been in office since the Act of 1898 three have
been selected from the co-opted members.
I think that no harm can be done by this
Amendment, because it is simply giving the
County Council the option. If they consider
their people are not available to fill these
offices, or if it is undesirable to have so many
who have not been elected direct by the
people, they need not take any action on the
matter. I think a great deal is to be said
for the view that in the coming administration of the Education Act an addition
of this description to the County Council
will be of very great value. For that reason
I support the Amendment of the hon.
Member (Mr. Leslie).
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The Amendment which my hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie) has moved is certainly one that will
bave an important effect, if carried. He is
quite right in saying that this House is a
competent authority for discussing these
matters. I think he is aware that the present Bill has been introduced by the Government either in consultation with local
authorities or after representations have been
received from them. This is a proposal
which is now introduced for the first time,
and is, I feel, of very far-reaching effect, and
it certainly has not been the subject of representations either by any County Council
or by ratepayers. It might very easily be
held to be an infringement somewhat on the
prerogative of the ratepayers as increasing
the proportion of non-elected members on each
County Council. The Amendment is to coopt up to five members, instead of up to a
limit of two, which is at present prescribed
by the Local Government Act of 1898. The
Government cannot accept this amendment,
and I would venture to suggest to the hon.
Member to withdraw it, not only for the
reason I have given him, that it has not been
brought forward by any of the County
Councils or by any body of ratepayers, but
also on the grounds that my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs, has
already given an undertaking that this whole
question of Local Government administration
will form the subject of a special inquiry by

Mr. LESLIE. The subject having been
ventilated, and having obtained the consent
of my hon. Friend (Mr. Armstrong), who did
me the honour of supporting me, I have
much pleasure in asking the House to allow
me to withdraw the Amendment.

Ordered: That Clauses 4 (Scale of Expenses
at elections of Urban District Councillors),

(Fees for holding inquiries) stand part of the

Clause 7 (Expenses of Local Authorities in
connection with war memorials).
*
(1) The Council of any County, County
Borough, or Urban District may incur'
reasonable expenditure in the maintenance,
repair, and protection of any war memorial
within their county, borough, or district
which may be vested in them.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 7,
page 5, line 4, after "borough" ("of any
county, county borough") to insert
"
rural."
I want to give here the same power to a
Rural District Council as is given to an
Urban District Council to repair and maintain war memorials. There are numerous
small war memorials in our country districts,
and if nobody takes charge of them sooner or
later they will fall into decay, and they will
be a disgrace to the community. I am not
aware of any objection to allowing Rural
District Councils the same power of incurring
reasonable expenditure in the maintenance,
repair, and protection of any war memorial
within their district as will be enjoyed by
the County Councils or the Urban District
Councils. I do not think I need say anything
more to commend this slight alteration to
the Committee. It necessitates just the insertion of the word "rural" here and a consequential amendment of two lines further
on, which I will move in due course.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
These Amendments must be taken together.
The object as I see it of the Amendments is to
giant power to the Rural District Councils
to maintain war memorials which have been
vested in them. I may perhaps venture to
point out to the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie)
that the Bill as it now stands invests County
Councils with the necessary powers. The
County Councils, under the Local Government Act of 1898, are already empowered to
keep in repair all ancient monuments. Fur-
I may perhaps draw the hon.
thermore,
Member's attention to the fact that County
Councils have at their disposal a staff of
qualified engineers and surveyors who are
competent to report on the condition of these
monuments and memorials, and can submit
detailed proposals as to the necessity for,
and the cost of, the repairs which would
have to be made. The Rural District Councils, as a general rule, have no qualified
engineers in their employment. It is for this
reason that the Government are unable to
accept these Amendments, and I would ask
my hon. Friend to withdraw his Amendment.
We naturally feel with him that it is of the
highest importance that these memorials
should be kept in a highly efficient state of
repair. I hardly think he need have any
qualms on the subject by reason of the fact
that the larger body, the County Council,
will be called upon to take up this duty when
those who are more closely connected with
the memorials themselves will not be the
actual authority for the carrying out of
whatever repairs will be necessary.
I do
suggest that my hon. Friend need have no
qualms on that point. The Rural District
Councils will always be in a position to make
the representations necessary to the County
Council, and their own representative on the
County Council will be certainly wanting in
his duty if he does not make the necessary
recommendations and gain the assent of the
County Council for the carrying out of the
work which would otherwise be done, according to my hon. Friend's suggestion, by the
Rural District Council. I hope I have been
able to satisfy my hon. Friend in this matter.
and that he will see fit to withdraw the
Amendment which he has put down on the
Paper.
T
Mr. LESLIE: My point, of course, is the
question of the rate. I quite agree with
everything the noble Marquess has said.
would much rather see the responsibility for
all these rural district war memorials placed
on the County Council; but the expenses of
maintaining them according to the Bill will
have to be borne by a rate levied over the
whole county, and that is where I foresee
difficulty. If you leave it a rate levied over
the district concerned I think it would be
better. That is really my only objection to the
course proposed, but if the noble Marquess
having inquired into this matter, is satisfied
that on the whole this is the best method, I
have no objestion to withdrawing the
Amendment. That is my point, the question
of rate. I do not know if there is anything
further the noble Marquess has to say.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
certainly am given to understand by my right
427 Local Government Bill.

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
a
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
hon. Friend the Home Secretary, who
naturally has gone very closely into this
matter that the proper authority is the
county council for dealing with these matters.
The question of rates, whether these rates
will be levied on the whole county and the
whole county will be responsible for the maintenance of war memorials is naturally
matter which he has fully considered. I cannot share the fear which the hon. Member
(Mr. Leslie) has in his mind. It seems to
me that the war memorials are property of
the county. In fact they are the property of
even a wider body. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) I cannot believe that there will be
any question for the sums of money which
will be required for the keeping of these war
memorials of which we are all so naturally
proud, and which are more of
national
heritage than a district heritage. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I cannot help feeling that
there will be none of the difficulties which the
hon. Member foreshadows, and that the time
when these difficulties will arise, if they do
arise, will be so far in the distant future that
this House need not concern itself with them.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

THE MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND

CLAUSE 1 (Temporary Suspension of Age
Limit for Certain Appointments to Royal
Ulster Constabulary).
(1) So long as this Act continues in force,
a special constable, belonging to any such
class of special constabulary and having
such service as may be specified by the
Minister of Home Affairs for Northern Ireland, may, notwithstanding any limitation
of age imposed by any enactment, be lawfully appointed as an officer or constable of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary.
(2) This Act shall continue in force
until the thirty-first day of December, nineteen hundred and twenty-six, and no longer.
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to move in Clause 1,
Sub-Clause 1, page 1, line 11, after the word
"Constabulary" ("officer or constable of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary") to insert the
words "provided that the age of such officer
or constable shall not exceed forty years at
the date of his appointment."
I sat on the Committee which was formed to
make recommendations as to the formation
of the new police force, and the age limit was
then fixed at 35 for all classes-Special Constabulary and others. Now I see there is an
unlimited age given to members who have
served in the Special Constabulary. The
Committee considered this matter in all its
details, and got evidence from every quarter. For the Inspector-General or the
Minister of Home Affairs to tell us now that
in the Six Counties he cannot get 3,000 men
of five feet eight inches in height is a tremendous slur on the physique of the young
men of the Six Counties. Whether it is that
they have failed in their education, or in
their chest measurement, or in their height I
cannot tell, but to say that we cannot get
3,000 men for a force of this kind up to the
standards that are fixed seems to me to be
an unreasonable suggestion.
The chief objection I have to the clause is
the giving of unlimited power to the
Inspector-General with regard to the age
limit. I know of no service, neither the
Army nor the Civil Service, in which an unlimited power is given to appoint an officer
Constabulary Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Dickson.]
or a man to a pensionable position. We do
not wish to see the old jobbery of Dublin
Castle carried out in our Northern Government. We have every confidence in our
present Home Office officials and our
Inspector-General, but we have no guarantee
that these 250 vacancies will not be filled
with men beyond the age who will either
receive a gratuity or a pension at the end of
their service. I have no fault in the wide
world to find with the Special Constabulary.
Under very distressing circumstances they
rendered good service, but the training they
got as Special Constabulary for three or four
years was not such as would make them
good peace officers as policemen. I beg to
move the amendment which stands in my
name.
Mr. MCDOWELL: I just want to say a few
words in reference to this particular clause.
The amendment moved by the hon. Member
(Senator Hill Dickson) is certainly an improvement on the clause in the Bill, yet to
my mind it is putting the cart before the
horse. I think in no circumstances should
the age limit be extended. Every person who
knows anything about military discipline
knows that in the Army when a regiment is
full up and they want no more recruits they
advance their stature or their chest measurement. If there is a scarcity of recruits the
last thing in the world they fly to is the age
limit, but they reduce their standards so as
to try to get the younger men into the Army.
I think the same thing shold apply to the
Constabulary Force. I cannot follow the
reasoning that says that in taking men inte
the Constabulary a man of forty or forty-five
is as good as a man of thirty-five. It seems to
me that with all the physical exercises you
can give him he may perhaps shall I say?-
come to be muscle-bound or something like
that. It comes to be a regular law, and the
physical exercise does such a man more injury than good. In the case of a young man,
although he may be below the stature, you
certainly have an opportunity of improving
him both as to height and chest measurement, and consequently making him a very
efficient man. The hon. Member (Senator
Dickson) says that probably the shortage of
men may be due to lack of education.
I
think that speaks very badly indeed for the
education system of the present day.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: It
has not got into working order yet.
Mr. MCDOWELL: I have great hope that
it will be a great success when it does get
into order.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr. Andrews): Hear, hear.
Mr. MCDOWELL: I do not wish to embarrass the Government in any way, and I
some
feel there is
legitimate reason for
adopting this Bill, notwithstanding that 1
cannot get away from the fact that it gives
the authorities unlimited power, which 1
think no other Government would ask for.
Of course I know that so far as we Northeners are concerned, we are a very give-andtake sort of people, and consequently if
some substantial reason can be put forward
why we should do this, and an assurance
given that the authority will not be abused,
we may accede to it. I certainly would be
in favour of the Government either withdrawing the Bill altogether or else falling
in with the amendment, bad and all as it
is, of making the age limit forty years.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: 1 have a
great deal of sympathy with my hon. Friend
opposite (Mr. Dickson) up to this point, that
he does not want Parliament to delegate ita
powers in this way and lose its power of
decision.
Mr. DICKSON: Hear, hear.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I agree
with him that we have every confidence both
in the Ministry of Home Affairs and in the
Inspector-General, and I differ from iny hon.
Friend behind (Mr. McDowell) because he
seems to think that after 35 people lose their
usefulness to a certain extent. I am inclined
to think there are many things a policeman
'has to do which he is quite as well able to
do, and perhaps has more discretion in doing,
at 40 or 45 than he would have at 35 or 25, but
I do think it is asking a good deal of Parliament to allow any officer or any Minister
simply to take a man into a pensionable office
at any age that he likes. Of course, I quite
realise that this is a temporary Measure.
I would suggest at
rate that if
any
the Government could see their way to
add the approval of the Treasury to that
of the Minister of Home Affairs it would
probably meet the objection of my hon.
Friend (Mr. Dickson), because I am certain
the Treasury would take very good care that
there were not very many people appointed
to pensionable offices who were too near the
age. I only throw that out as a suggestion;
I do not know whether the Leader of the
House could consider it.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Senator who moved this
amendment (Mr. Dickson) lays stress on the
fact that he assumes we cannot find 3,000
men in Northern Ireland to fill up the ranks
of our Constabulary. I disagree with him
on that point. The object of this Bil', which
I think I stated on the Second Reading, was
to give to the Special Constabulary, to those
who had, as we all know, done their duty,
and a very arduous duty it was, under very
difficult and trying circumstances, some
Constabulary Bill-
Committee.

ble f

small privilege, and at the same time this
Bill was introduced to enable the responsible
Minister, who is my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Home Affairs, to avail
himself of eminently suitable men of the
Special Constabulary who are over this statutory age limit. The hon. Senator, Mr.
McDowell, objects on the ground that he
thinks if any little privilege is given it
should be on the ground of height and chest
measurement. The height and chest measurement as compared with the Royal Irish Constabulary are reduced. In the Royal Irish
Constabulary the height was 5ft. 9ins., in
the Royal Ulster Constabulary our height is
5ft. 8ins., and I do not think it advisable
that we should in any way reduce that standard. It seems to me that the hon. Member who moved this amendment (Mr. Dickson) is very apprehensive as to what the
effect of this will be. It must be obvious
that there is no intention on the part of the
Government to make use of this Bill to fill
the Force with a lot of middle-aged men. It
would only be used in very exceptional circumstances, and any man who was approved
by the Minister of Home Affairs and the
Inspector-General of Constabulary would have
to conform to all the other qualifications that
are required, and, as I said on the Second
Reading, would have to be equal in physical
capacity, and so on, to any younger member
who would put himself forward for the Royal
Ulster Constabulary.
This Bill is limited to three years, and I
must say I cannot anticipate, nor would it be
reasonable for any Member of this House to
anticipate, that during those three years the
Force would have a considerable number of
men over the statutory age. It is obvious
the first consideration of my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs, is to
have the Police Force, which is responsible
for order in the Province, in a highly efficient and satisfactory condition. It is obviously unlikely that men would be appointed
who were of an age at which they would not
be fitted to perform their duties properly.
Personally, I think it would be a mistake,
and I trust the House will agree with me
that it would be a mistake, to lay down any
hard-and-fast rule as suggested in the amendment. I trust the hon. Member (Mr. Dickson) will not press his amendment because,
on behalf of the Government, I should feel
bound to resist it.
Mr. DICKSON: I am prepared to withdraw the amendment if the Government will
give us any guarantee as to age. Otherwise
I shall press it to a division,
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member who moved this amendment (Mr. Dickson) has made a condition
that if the Government will give some guarantee as to age he will withdraw the amendment, and I do not know that I feel inclined
really to resist a division. We can have
a division in this House, and I am not
sure it will not be more satisfactory to the
Members, if they so desire, to have a division. I am not sure my hon. Friend really
wants a division on this point. He is quite
rightly watching with vigilance the public
service, and in his capacity as Senator it is
his duty to do so. I am sure all of us welcome the expressions he has used, which
show that he has the interest of the public
service at heart and that he is apprehensive
that the provisions which are incorporated in
this Bill may in the future be liable to
abuse. I think my hon. Friend who sits beside me (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard)
has covered the ground very adequately. This
Bill will be in force for three years from
now, and I feel sure the hon. Member (Mr.
Dickson) cannot entertain any fears that in
those three years the Royal Ulster Constabulary will be filled up with a lot of individuals who are even older than we Senators,
and perhaps even less capable of doing their
share of muscle-work in protecting the Province by the strength of their right arm. I
do not think that the hon. Member need
anticipate a gloomy picture of that description. The Minister of Home Affairs, in his
own interest, and the Inspector-General are
not likely to fill up the Force with men who
are physically incapable of carrying out their
duties.
At this moment, when we are setting up
a new Force-I am not speaking of any incident that I know of-I believe it might be
desirable, as my hon. Friend opposite has
said, to have a person of a higher age than
that which he desires to incorporate in the
Bill. If that was so the Minister of Home
Affairs would be entirely unable to use his
discretion for putting a man older than the
age of 40 into a responsible position. In a
few years to come there will be men of that
age in the Force who will undertake these
higher and rather more administrative posts,
in which the duties are other than those
which require all the strength of their own
right arms. I have not considered the question in conjunction with the Minister of
Home Affairs, but I am quite sure that if
the hon. Member (Mr. Dickson) desires
guarantee of some age older than 40 he would
be prepared to give it to him, but I hardly
feel it is necessary at this moment to incor-
1E
a
Report Stage.
Constabulary Bill-

THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
porate any actual age in the Bill, for the
simple reason that I do not anticipate that
many of those older than 40 are likely to be
called upon to discharge these duties. That
is the practical side of the question, if I may
call it that. I think what is the root of the
matter and what is really uppermost in the
mind of the hon. Member (Mr. Dickson) is
that this latitude in the Bill is liable to
abuse, but I cannot help thinking that the
limitation placed in the Bill at the present
moment of its operation extending for only
three years is a sufficient safeguard, and the
hon. Member, who is very well versed in
public affairs and has the opportunity of
making all the investigations he requires into
what the Ministry of Home Affairs are doing.
need not be unduly apprehensive on this
matter, and therefore if I may be allowed I
would suggest that he accept the assurance
given by my hon. Friend on my left (Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard) and withdraw the
amendment which he has put down on the
paper.
Mr. DICKSON: As the Government are
not prepared to give me a guarantee as to
any age, I cannot see my way to withdraw
the amendment.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: Might I
suggest that it seems my hon. Friend (Mr.
Dickson) is really fighting for a principle?
Mr. DICKSON: That is it.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL:
He is
fighting for the control of Parliament over
public expenses, and I think he is fighting
for a very great principle although I do not
mean to say that he, or I, or anybody else,
fears the Government will abuse it, but if
the Government would give a guarantee, he
has already said he would withdraw the
amendment. I think it is a pity to go to a
division over this, and I would again ask the
noble Marquess if they cannot associate the
Treasury with the Ministry of Home Affairs
in this case. I believe it would satisfy my hon.
Friend (Mr. Dickson), although I have not
had any conversation with him about it.
The Government seems to be rather hard on
this question.
Mr. LESLIE: I had not proposed to intervene in this debate, but a question has been
raised as regards the control of Parliament.
That matter, of course, is a principle of firstclass importance. I cannot refrain from
submitting to the House that what we have
here before us is an absolute submission on
the part of the Government to Parliament,
because what they are doing here is, they
are asking Parliament to give them power to
do something which, but for the special
power which they ask, they cannot do.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY.
Hear, hear.
a
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that the
production of the Bill is
very sufficient
recognition of the power of Parliament, and
therefore on that ground there is no need for
uneasiness on the part of my hon. Friend
(Mr. Dickson) or on the part of any member
of this House.
Amendment negatived.

out from “(3)” [“(3)” A person examined
under this"] to "any" in line 13 ("himself;
but any statement')-(The Marquess of
Londonderry.)
Mr. ANDREWS: This Amendment is
moved by the Government for the purpose
of meeting a very important point which was
raised by Senator Leslie yesterday during
the Committee stage. On further considering
the matter we feel that our inspectors will
have sufficient powers for all the duties they
are called upon to perform if this portion
of this clause is left out, and I hope that
this will meet Senator Leslie's point of view
and that the clause as amended will be
allowed to stand.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that I can only say
that the right hon. Gentleman is as generous
in his treatment of amendments as he is
sympathetic in his attitude towards the interests committed to his charge. I asked
yesterday afternoon that the whole nostrum
might be taken away. The right hon.
Gentleman has done something better; he
has taken away the powder and left the jam.
I think the clause as it now stands is one to
which nobody can possibly take exception.
I am very glad that the alteration has been
made. I should like, if I may, to thank the
right hon. Gentleman for the courtesy he
displayed to me yesterday afternoon in giving
me a short discussion on the matter. I cordially support the clause as it now stands.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. ANDREWS: This was another point
raised yesterday by Senator Leslie. It is an
Amendment agreed to.
Clause 23 (Provisions as to legal proceedings).
The following provisions shall have
effect with respect to summary proceedings
under this Act, namely:
(1) All offences under this Act shall be
prosecuted, and all fines thereunder shall
be recovered, in manner provided by the
Summary Jurisdiction Acts;
a
(2) A court of summary jurisdiction when
hearing and determining any information or
complaint in any matter arising under this
Act, shall be constituted of
resident
magistrate appointed under the Constabulary (Ireland) Act, 1836, and any
enactment amending the same, sitting alone
or with others, or of two or more justices
of the peace sitting in petty sessions at a
place appointed for holding petty sessions.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 23,
page 16, line 44, to leave out from "Acts"
("Summary Jurisdiction Acts") to the end of
line 8 on page 17 ("for holding petty
sessions"). The question of subsection (2) of
section 23 was raised yesterday afternoon,
and therefore to put myself in order I am
formally moving that the section to the end
of line 8, page 17 be left out; but the House
will not be troubled with a division on the
subject, because in an interview which I had
last evening the right hon. Gentleman put
me in possession of the reasons which led
him to insert the clause. I am sure the
House will find them as satisfactory when
they hear them from the right hon. Gentleman as I did yesterday. It is a fact, of
course, that this section does not appear in
either of the Trade Boards Acts of 1909 and
1918, but it does appear in another Act to
which the right hon. Gentleman referred me,
and I may say that I have found it in earlier
Acts dealing with cognate subjects as applied
to Ireland, and still further back in 1871, in
the Prevention of Crimes Act, which applied
to all the British Islands, differentiating
particularly in this matter between England and Scotland and Ireland. I beg to
move.
Mr. ANDREWS: I feel that it is scarcely
necessary for me to say anything because
my hon. Friend has so clearly expressed not
only his own views but mine that the
House is in possession of the true facts of
the case. All that I therefore desire to say
Local Government Bill.

THE SENATE.
[Mr. Andrews.]
is that there is absolutely no intention in
any way to supersede in this matter the
local magistrates. The clause leaves it open
for the R.M to sit with the local magistrates
or for the local magistrates to sit by themselves without the R.M.; and when I explained that to my hon. Friend, as he has
already told the House, he was quite satisfied, and so we are in entire agreement, and
the little controversy is ended.
Mr. LESLIE: I must ask leave to withdraw the amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

Business of the House. 22 NOVEMBER 1923. Workmen's Compensation Bill. 442
THE SEΝΑΤΕ.

Friday, 23rd November, 1923.
The Senate met at a Quarter past Three of
the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPERS PRESENTED
Ministry of Commerce-First Annual Report of the Minstry of Commerce on the
Mining and Quarrying Industries in
Northern Ireland, together with the Annual
Report of the Inspector of Mines for the same
period.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act)
and ordered to lie on the Table.
And a message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to the said Bill without amendment.
Constabulary Bill.
Order for Third reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. - (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to the said Bill without amendment.
Business of the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In asking the House to pass the resolution that stands in my name on the
Order Paper to-day, I feel that I am asking
a good deal of indulgence from the House.
(An hon. Member: Hear, hear.) We are ever
jealous in this House of our right as we are
a
Chamber of revision-to criticise and
thoroughly examine any Bill that comes before us. I think the House will know that
this is a very unusual occasion. The Government is very anxious that this Bill should
be passed in this Session of Parliament, and
therefore I crave the indulgence of the House
in asking that Standing Order No. XXV be
considered in order to its being dispensed
with for the purpose of passing the Bill
through all its stages. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that it would
be better to defer any remarks on this
motion until the Bill is actually before us.
I do not think anybody would wish at this
stage to obstruct in any way the proceedings of this House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Standing Order be
suspended for this day's sitting in respect of
the said Bill.
Trade Boards Bill.
Order for Third reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. - (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to the said Bill with some amendment,
to which they desire the concurrence of the
Commons.
The House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Workmen's
Compensation Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
Superannuation Bill.
