The expression "transferred civil servants" means persons who are either transferred Irish officers or specially appointed
officers within the meaning of the Superannuation Act, 1921.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
desire to move to leave ort lines 25 to 28
inclusive, namely,
"The expression transferred civil servants' means persons who are either transferred Irish officers or specially appointed
officers within the meaning of the Superannuation Act, 1921."

These lines, which are a definition of the
expression transferred civil servants," are
no longer necessary as an amendment in
another place removed the expression therein
defined from the Bill. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: Are the numbers of the lines
quoted quite right? In my copy of the Bill
the lines in question are 18 to 22.
seems to be some difference in the copies.

The CHAIRMAN: I am afraid the wrong
copies of the Bill have been placed on the
table. They are prints of the Bill as introduced in the Commons, and the Bill in which
the lines are numbered 25 to 28 is the copy
as printed after it has been subject to the
Commons amendments. I think the best way
out of the difficulty would be for me to read
out the four lines proposed to be left out.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think that will meet the case.

Moved: "That the report be now received."
-(Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That the Bill do now pass."-
(Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the Commons to
acquaint them that the Senate have agreed
to the said Bill with an amendment to which
they desire the concurrence of the
Commons.
Consolidated Fund Bill.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Owing to the fact that we have received the
Consolidated Fund Bill from the Commons,
and that we have suspended Standing Orders
so that this Bill also may go through all its
stages. I beg to move the Second reading.
There have been voted in Committee of
Supply in another place:
(a) the Supplementary Supplies for the
expiring year (£44,062);
(b) the excess on the preceding year
(£1,771) in respect of prisons;
(c) such a substantial sum in respect of
the coming Financial Year as will be sufficient to meet the whole of the public
expenditure on Supply services for the first
few months of that year (£2.277,500).
Secondly, Ways and Means Resolutions have
been passed, authorising the issue out of the
Consolidated Fund of the amounts referred
to above, and these Resolutions are now embodied in this Bill, which, on becoming an
Act of Parliament, endows them with complete legal effect and is the authority for the
Comptroller and Auditor-General to permit
issues from the Exchequer up to the amounts
involved.
Clause 4 of the Bill empowers the Ministry
of Finance to borrow on the credit of the
aforesaid sums, not exceeding in the whole
£1,000,000.
It may easily happen that during certain
periods of the year the expenditure may exceed the revenue, owing to the unequal rate
at which the two sides of the Account mature.
It is necessary, therefore, to obtain power
from Parliament to borrow to meet a temporary deficiency.
Clause 5 allows savings on the Royal Ulster
Constabulary Vote for the current year to
be utilised to meet an excess on the Special
Constabulary Vote. Normally no financial
distinction would be made between these two
Forces and one Vote would be taken to cover
both, but owing to the conditions governing
the Imperial Grant-in-Aid towards the ex-
63 Consolidated Fund Bill.

THE SENATE.
Adjournment.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
penses of Special Constabulary, it has been
necessary to present separate estimates
throughout the year. It has happened, however, that owing to the Royal Ulster Constabulary not being brought up to strength as
quickly as anticipated, the strength of the
Special Constabulary has had to be maintained at a higher figure than estimated
originally, with a consequent expenditure
from the Special Constabulary Vote in excess
of the estimate. The usual remedy would
have been to introduce a further Supplementary Estimate for Special Constabulary,
although the excess is more than set off by
the corresponding saving in the Royal Ulster
Constabulary Vote. To avoid this unnecessary proceeding in relation to two
Votes which for all practical purposes relate
to the same service, it is proposed in this
Clause to adopt the same procedure as is
permitted by the Imperial House in connection with Army Votes, that is to say, with
Treasury approval savings on one Army, Vote
may be used to meet a deficit on another.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That the House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said Bill."-
(Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: "That the Bill be now read the
Third time.'-(Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That the Bill do now pass."
(Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons to
acquaint them that the Senate have agreed
to the said Bill without any amendment.
Adjournment.
Moved: "That this House do now adjourn."
-(Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Fifteen
Minutes after Four o'clock,
Petty Sessions Bill--
Second Reading.
29 MARCH 1923.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 29th March, 1923.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Petty Sessions Fees and
Administration Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to move the
Second Reading of the Petty Sessions Fees
and Administration Bill. This Bill empowers
the Governor to make Orders prescribing the
Fees to be paid on the various forms which are
used in proceedings under the Petty Sessions
Act, These include summonses, warrants, informations, recognizances, orders, and other
details.
The fees were fixed by an Act of 1858, and
have remained unaltered for over 60 years. It
is felt that the time has come when the fees
should represent more adequately the cost of
administering the procedure in Petty Sessions. The great bulk of the fees are paid
in respect of summonses, orders and warrants,
and it can hardly be said that the present fee,
which is usually 6d in each case, is sufficient
charge for the labour of making out a summons, entering the case in the order book,
taking depositions at the hearing, and finally
making out a warrant, all operations which
require a good deal of technical knowledge
and also a certain amount of skill.
Another fee which the Governor is given
power to increase is that payable for certificate of registry on a dog licence. By the
Dog Regulation (Ireland) Act of 1919 the
licence duty for a dog was increased from 2s
to 4s, but the fee of 6d for registering the
licence was not altered, and I think it should
be increased in the same proportion.
expenditure,
sources
Formerly the salaries of Petty Sessions
Clerks and the general expenses of administering the Petty Sessions Acts were paid out of
fines and fees, and for a good many years
the income
sufficient
was
to meet the
but owing to the falling
off in the receipts from these
the expenditure exceeded the receipts,
and it was found
necessary to give
the Lord Lieutenant, as the
then, power to draw upon the receipts from
Dog Licences to meet the deficincy. During
the last few years owing to the state of the
country the income from fines and fees fell
case was
off rapidly. In 1920 the total expenditure of
the Petty Sessions Department in all Ireland was over £84,000, and the amount received from fines and fees and some minor
sources of income was only about £27,000, so
that a sum of about £57,000 would have had
to be taken from the Dog money to make up
the deficiency.
As a matter of fact, in view of the state of
the Petty Sessions Fund, the Lord Lieutenant transferred practically the whole balance
of the Dog money for that year to the Petty
Sessions Fund. The surplus receipts from
Dog Licence duty are payable to the Local
Authorities in proportion to the number of
dogs licensed, and if the fees are increased
and our Northern Petty Sessions Fund put
on a proper basis, it is the Local Authorities
who will be the gainers, and the rates will
be relieved accordingly. The increase will
not benefit the Northern Government, but
will be utilised to safeguard the Petty
Sessions Fund and to lessen the deductions
from the Dog Licence surplus, and thereby
increase the share of the Local Authorities
in the latter.
The second Clause deals with the election
of Petty Sessions Clerks. It is not proposed
to take away the rights of the magistrates of
any district to elect a clerk for that district,
but there have been a large number of conflicting decisions in the superior courts as to
the magistrates who are entitled to vote, and
it is proposed to give the Governor power
to make regulations setting out the
qualifications which entitle a magistrate
to vote. It is also proposed to give him
power to decide disputes as to elections,
and where there is a tie to decide
which candidate shall be declared successful.
This is absolutely necessary if we are to
avoid what has happened in the past, a series
of elections each resulting in a tie, so that
a district is kept in turmoil and without a
clerk for months.
The last Sub-section of this Clause deals
with the appointment of one clerk to serve
two or more districts. There is power to
make an order to this effect at present, but
it is doubtful whether this can be done where
the districts concerned are not in the same
County, and the Sub-section is intended to
clear up this point. It also places the first
appointment in the hands of the Governor,
but enacts that he is to appoint one of the
former clerks unless they are all unfit. It is
most desirable, in my opinion, that steps
should be taken to unite these small districts. At present the salaries of the clerks
of the small districts are very poor, and there
is not nearly sufficient work to keep the clerk
occupied. If we can give him charge of two
or more districts we can afford to pay him a
higher salary and we can make it worth his
while to devote much more attention to his
duties. The present Petty Sessions Clerks, as
E
Roads Bill.
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
a body, discharge their most efficiently, but
with the exception of a small war bonus of
20 per cent. their salaries have not been increased for years, and it is becoming more
difficult to induce the type of man we want
to take up the clerkships of these very sn.all
districts. It must be remembered that we do
not propose to do away with any district,
but merely to make one clerk look after two
or districts, and as he will be bound to
give certain fixed attendances in each district
no inconvenience can be caused to either the
magistrates or the public.
as
It is probable that most of these orders
will be made when
a vacancy occurs
in one of the districts concerned so
to avoid the hardship of compelling one of
the clerks to retire, so that the process of
uniting districts under one clerk will be a
gradual one, and will not cause any hardship
to the parties concerned. I beg to move:
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I merely want to ask
the noble Lord whether I rightly interpret
this Bill, that sub-clause 3 of Clause 2 substitutes nomination by the Governor for the
very cumbersome process that now occasionally takes place. I was summoned the other
day to a meeting of magistrates at Enniskillen
to appoint a Petty Sessions Clerk at which
74 magistrates attended. It is obviously a
most undesirable way of conducting an election. I think that the second clause covers
that, but I should like to be certain whether
that is so that in the case of several districts being united the appointment will fall
to the Governor under this Bill instead of to
the whole body of magistrates in the country.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
understand that the first appointment falls
to the Governor, but after that I will take
steps to find out the information which the
hon. Gentleman requires. At the present
moment the first appointment falls into the
hands. of the Governor, who will appoint one
of the former clerks, unless all these former
clerfs are found to be unfit.
Mr MONTGOMERY: I think that is satisfactory. I hope so.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read the Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the whole
House, the House to be in Committee on
Tuesday, 24th April next.
Local Government Roads Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving the Second Reading of this Eli
I will venture to ask the indulgence of the
House for a few moments, if they will Lear
with me, while I explain to them its provisions. The Roads Bill, which has been read
First time in another place, has been intoduced on the urgent representations of the
Roads Advisory Committee and of the various
county officials concerned with roads administration. Its object is to remove anomalies and
difficulties that exist in the existing Statutes
dealing with roads, particularly in regard to
the classification of roads.
Prior to the passing of the Ministry of
Transport Act, 1919, there was only one system of classification of roads in Ireland. That
system comprised main roads and district
roads. Under that system the expenses of
main roads were borne half by the County at
large and half by the district concerned,
while the district roads were wholly borne by
the districts. The Ministry of Transport Act,
1919, which was an Act primarily intended
for England, introduced a new system of
classification, under which roads in the
United Kingdom have been classified under
three heads, Class I. being the main trunk
roads, Class II. being what are called secondary roads, and Class III. the minor roads.
Following in this classification grants are payable to the Road Authorities out of the Road
Fund on a basis which has, up to the present,
involved 50 per cent. of the expenditure on
the Class I. roads and 25 per cent. of expenditure on the Class II. roads.
Accordingly, in Northern Ireland at present
we have thus two systems of classification imposed on each other, involving considerable
difficulties of accountancy and administration.
Some main roads are Class I., and others are
what are called Class II., and it has been
represented to us that it is nearly impossible
for the Road Authorities to keep and to give
us accurate statistics as regards expenditure
on the three classes of roads. The new system of classification, being based on grants
payable out of the Road Fund, transcends in
importance the old main road system, and
one of the objects of the present Bill is to
abolish the main road system altogether and
to leave only the new system of classification
which I have referred to. As this involves
the abolition of the old system of chargeability of expenses as between the County at
large and the District, a new system is essential, and Clause 1 (1) of the Bill gives effect
to this.
It will be seen that under that Clause the
expenses of Class I roads in the Rural portions of each County will be borne in future
as a county-at-large charge. The Class II
roads will be borne half County at large and
half Rural District, whereas the expenses of
the minor or Class II roads will fall wholly
on the Rural Districts. This is in our judg
ment an equitable and logical arrangement,
particularly when it is remembered that the
State pays half the expenses of the Class I
roads and 25 per cent. of the expenses of the
Class II roads.
Roads Bill
Second Reading.
29 MARCH 1923
Sub-section 2 of Clause 1 gives effect to the
desired object, to which I have referred, of
the abolition of the main road system.
a
Sub-section 3 of Clause 1 is a very important section and involves wholly new
principle in Road Administration. Under
existing law every proposal in regard to roads
must emanate from the Rural District Coun
eil in the first instance, it then passes up and
is approved by the County Council and becomes effective. The result of that system is
that a County which desires to have good
trunk roads is at the mercy of the Rural District Councils through which these trunk
roads pass, and experience has shown that,
while in some cases the Rural District Councils concerned deal with their trunk or Class
I roads in an enlightened manner, their
efforts are neutralised by the parsimony or
lack of interest shown by other Rural District Councils in the County, the result being
that the whole object of having trunk roads
uniform in construction and condition
throughout is entirely defeated. Motorists
have complained bitterly of this, and this
difficulty has been strongly represented to the
Ministry by the Roads Advisory Committee,
who urge that it will not be possible to obtain
a proper system of trunk roads until these
roads are wholly in the charge of the County
Councils concerned.
I have no doubt that there will be a certain
amount of opposition to this proposal on the
part of the Rural District Councils concerned.
who will represent that their privileges are
being curtailed, but, in addition to the arguments given above, it should be remembered
that, under the new scheme, the charges for
the Class I trunk roads will be borne by the
whole County, and that of these charges the
State itself will pay half the cost.
Sub-section 4 of the Bill provides that
Urban District Councils will have complete
control of their own roads, and, in order to
carry this principle to its logical conclusion,
a provision is made later on in the Bill in
Clause 2. Section 1, providing that the
Ministry shall have power to deal direct with
the Urban District Councils in regard to
grants from the Road Fund.
Hitherto, under the main road system, in
some cases the County Councils undertook the
repair of the main roads in the Urban Districts, and there were financial adjustments
between the Urban District Councils and the
County Councils regarding expenses so incurred. Urban District Councils, have, however, strongly represented that, as they are
Road Authorities, they should deal with us
direct, and their attitude in this respect cannot be controverted.
Under the system laid down in the Bill
the Urban District Councils will pay for their
own roads only and will not be responsible
for any charges in connection with the roads
in the Rural portion of the County.
This
will give them an added interest in their own
roads, but the Bill as it stands will not prevent them from entering into financial arrangements with the County Councils concerned for the maintenance and improvement
of their roads, under the supervision of the
County Surveyor. In fact, express provision
is made in the Bill, in the proviso to Subsection 4 to Clause 1, empowering the Ministry of Home Affairs, after inquiry, to determine the financial adjustments between the
County Councils and the Urban District
Councils.
Sub-section 2 of Clause 2 calls for little
comment. At the passing of the Local Government Act, 1898, it was felt that, in order
to relieve the anxiety of ratepayers, some check
should be placed on extravagance in regard to
road maintenance, and a provision was made
in Section 7 (2) of the Act restricting, save
with the approval of the Local Government
Board, the expenditure in each Rural District to a certain standard amount in each
case. This provision has now become wholly
unnecessary and out of date, having regard to
the new conditions that prevail and the enhanced grants from State sources, and the
applications made by Local Authorities for increasing the limit of expenditure are approved as a matter of form. On the urgent
representation of County Surveyors, supported
by the Roads Advisory Committee, it has been
decided to remove these restrictions and to
empower the Local Authorities concerned to
make such financial proposal in regard to
their roads as they may deem fit.
Clause 3 is inserted at the urgent request
of County Surveyors, supported by the Roads
Advisory Committee, in order to enable the
procedure of Local Authorities in connection
with Road Administration to be somewhat
more elastic than at present. The procedure
of Local Authorities is governed at present
by the Procedure of Councils Order, 1889,
which is of statutory effect, and that procedure has generally proved satisfactory, but in
certain respects will, under the new arrangements, be found to be inconvenient, particularly in connection with schemes for construction and maintenance of roads by Direct
Labour. The formalities prescribed in this
respect are of a detailed and lengthy character
and, in many cases, have been found to be
unworkable. This Clause gives power to the
Ministry of Home Affairs to vary the Procedure of Councils Order to meet the new
conditions.
Clause 4 calls for little comment, as it
deals with the title, construction, and
commencement of the Act, but attention is
direced to the proviso to Sub-section 3.
It is essential that the Act should come
into operation as soon as possible, and the
date has been fixed for the 1st April, 1923,
Roads Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
but if that date were fixed without qualification, the whole system of chargeability, upon
which the budgets of the various roads
authorities have been framed for the service
of the coming financial year, would be upset,
and consequently, the provision in question,
which, in effect, brings the Act into operation on the 1st April, 1923, but which leaves
the financial arrangements regarding chargeability of expenses undisturbed until the 1st
April of that year.
The general effect will be that the County
Councils will take over charge of the Class I
roads immediately, but the expenses in connection with those roads will be charged as
heretofore on the old basis during the coming year. Similarly the Urban District
Councils will now be in direct touch with us
from the 1st April, 1923, and so obviate the
confusion and delay which has hitherto been
caused owing to their having to communicate
with us through the County Surveyors. I beg
to move.
Mr. LONG: I think there will be general
agreement that many of our first-class roads
are in a deplorable condition, and I am glad
to say there is almost a universal desire that
such a state of affairs should not be allowed
to continue. I can assure the Government
that they may count on the whole-hearted
and loyal support of County Councils in
having the present state of affairs remedied,
and our roads, which are at present retarding
trade and hampering industry, made fit and
proper for the work they have to do. At the
same time, I think an undeserved and unjust
slight has been cast on many of the Distret
Councillors, who have merited some recognition for the very efficient services they have
rendered in their position as Rural District
Councillors, and for their determination to do
the best they could for every interest concerned.
In the District Council with which I have
been connected since the passing of the Local
Government Act, there were about forty members, and to attend the meetings of the Council it was necessary for me to travel 344
miles by horse vehicle for each meeting. I
attended an average of 26 meetings out of 28
during the past 24 years. It is also necessary
to travel a like distance to attend our weekly
meeting of the Board of Guardians of which
meetings I attended
an average of 47 out
of 52. Many of our Members travelled equally
long distances, and I understand that one or
two traversed even longer distances. But
notwithstanding the difficulty of attendance
we have never had any difficulty in getting
a quorum, and many Members attended better
than I did. Therefore I think some recognition is due to many of our District Councillors for the manner in which they have discharged their duties. At the same time I
quite recognise the undeniable fact that a
number of our Rural District Councils have
failed in their obvious duty to make due provision for the traffic on the roads, but many
of the District Councils which have done
their duty resent the insinuation in this Bill
that they have been remiss in the discharge
of those duties. In our District Council the
rule is that each member inspects all the
roads in his district quarterly, and we find
that this is of immense assistance to us. Their
local knowledge is of inestimable value. From
1889 up to the year 1915 no complaint whatever was received regarding the manner in
which District Councils had discharged their
duty, except possibly from an upstart motorist
or one of the newly-rich, who think they should
complain about something, but in the latter
year mechanical traction took the place of
horse-drawn vehicles, and such an event had
not been foreseen. Perhaps we were to blame
for not foreseeing it, but provision was not
made for it, and the result was disastrous to
our roads.
In a town in the County of Londonderry
with which I am acquainted only one merchant, owing to the high railway rates, gets
his goods by train. They all come from
Derry by motor lorry. All the foodstuffs,
potatoes, grain, etc., are carried by road instead of by rail. You can understand that
our water-bound macadam was not equal to
the strain of bearing such traffic. It was not
intended for that class of traffic, and
certainly did not bear it. Then the War
broke out, and District Councils had great
difficulty in procuring contractors. I am
very pleased to say that many of our road
contractors and farmers sons answered their
country's call, and the result was that we
had an immense difficulty to contend with
in providing material for the roads. Then
again
many contractors found it
more remunerative to grow flax and
provide food for the nation than to enter into
contracts with us. So I think you will understand some of the difficulties we encountered
during that trying period. At that time the
limited expenditure on the Rural District
with which I am connected was £6,750 per
We expended about that sumslightly more occasionally-up to the year
1914, when we gradually increased the
amount, until now it is between £18,000 and
£19,000, exclusive of Road Fund Grants. The
adjoining Derry No. 1 Rural District Council
has also increased its limit of expenditure to
about three times the original amount, and
the Coleraine Rural District Council has done
likewise.
annum.
was
There is no doubt that the remaining Dis
trict Council in the County of Londonderry
Magherafelt which is at present being administered by a Commissioner of the Nor-
Roads Bill-
Second Reading.
29 MARCH 1923.
thern Government, has not come out of the
trying ordeal with flying colours, but part of
the blame is to be attributed to the County
Council. At that time there was a considerprices, and a deputaable
tion
and
slump in
representing
number
a
the Farmers' Union
of other farmers
apevery
peared before the County Council to urge
them to reduce expenditure in
possible way, and so keep the rates within
reasonable bounds. The result was that when
the estimates were received from the various
Rural District Councils in the County they
were remitted to the Rural District Councils,
with an expression of opinion that if practicable they should be reduced. Three of the
Rural District Councils I have enumerated
did not, I am glad to say, reduce their estimates, but the fourth did considerably reduce its estimates wth disastrous results.
At the present moment
small
stretch of first-class road between Derry and
Limavady we are expending, in addition to
the ordinary maintenance contract, a sum of
£3,000 on a supplementary contract, and our
county surveyor has just returned from
Derby, where he purchased a Bitumen boiler
for £998 for the treatment of that road. We
find that water-bound macadam is quite out
of date, and that pot-holes are the result of
treating the roads with that macadam.
Accordingly we have decided to use tar for
surface dressing in the belief that it will be
more practicable
on
a
of
A matter that I dislike is the proposal in
this Bill to place grievous additional burdens
on the County Councils. I feel-and I would
like to emphasise the fact that County
Councils are already overworked. We do not
object to work, but since the passing of the
Local Government Act-and I have been a
member of the County Council since thenthe work has very considerably increased,
notably through the addition of such things
as Tuberculosis, Insurance, Diseases
Animals Act, etc. The outcome of this increase of work is that the business has to be
conducted with a haste and despatch that is
almost indecent-at least, it is indecorousand the result is a want of due consideration
to many of the proposals that come before us.
The noble Marquess, the Leader of the House,
purposes putting additional burdens on us in
connection with the Education Bill. We will
shoulder them loyally, but I am very much
afraid that so strenuous will the work become
in the near future that we will have
difficulty in obtaining the best men to serve
on the County Councils, and we are anxious
to have only the best men for the positions.
My fear, however, is that keen business men
will not sacrifice the very serious loss of time
that will be involved in becoming a member
of the County Council.
I should also point out that under this proposal in the Bill the members of County
Councils will not have the requisite local
knowledge of matters that come before them.
It will be necessary for them to appoint Road
Committees, the members of which cannot
possibly have the same intimate local knowledge as members of Rural District Councils
have of the work, and in my view these Road
Committees will eventually be more objectionable than the Rural District Councils are.
The noble Marquess has mentioned the fact
that uniformity of result has not been
obtained by the present method of treatment.
As one who has had very considerable experience of road maintenance, I would like
to point out that uniformity of treatment
will not secure uniformity of result.
The foundation of roads varies so much in
different places that in a mile of road you
will have a variety of sub-soils requiring different treatment, and no matter what system
you adopt you cannot have a uniform treatment of the roads, rpless reinforced concrete
might meet the case. But, after all, the matters I have mentioned are not serious. I do
not propose to move an Amendment to the
Bill, but I really think it is a wonderful thing
in this age of enlightenment and free education that the Minister of Home Affairs and
his advisers will bring forward a proposal that
I feel is more or less reactionary. The spirit
of the age is not in favour of centralisation.
We will be more or less in the position of the
Grand Juries, and the great outcry against
them was that there was taxation without representation. That system was considered
hateful and detestable, and in future you
will be more or less in the hands of officials.
On the general question there are two things
that I think are obvious. The first is that
our roads require reconstruction and improvement. That has been admitted. The
second is that the taxable capacity of the
ratepayers was never less than it is now, and
they cannot pay more. As chairman of the
Finance Committee of the Londonderry
County Council, it is my duty to keep in
touch with the rate collectors and ascertain
how their collection is going, and, when the
books are presented, to inquire why certain
sums have not been collected. Many of the
reasons that are given for these rates not
being collected would be amusing, if they
were not pathetic. For instance, a rate
collector informed me he had entered the
house of an old man and found that his bed
consisted of a holed-out part in the clay floor.
and his bed-clothing was a second-hand sack.
He pursued the only hen the old man had,
but failed to overtake it. We could not do
anything but allow that rate to be marked
"irrecoverable." In another case the collector seized the only live-stock the ratepayer
Roads Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Long]
had, took it home, but found he could
not get it sold, and as the neighbours
objected he had to release the goat. These
are extreme cases, but I would emphasise the
fact that the ratepayers are not able to pay
any additional rates. In a good many cases
the rates exceed the fair rent, and I would
earnestly appeal to the Government to consider this aspect of the question in any forthcoming legislation.
Might I read the last resolution of the
Roads Advisory Committee on this subject?
It was passed at a meeting held on 20th February, 1923.
I was in the chair, and there
was a full attendance, with the exception of
Colonel the Right Hon. Sharman-Crawford,
who is away for the benefit of his health. As
a basis of discussion the matters that were
put before the Committee were :-
(1) That first-class or trunk roads should be
taken over by the Government of Northern
Ireland and put in charge of the Ministry of
Labour, or other Ministry, for such time as
will be requisite to put them in proper order;
(2) That the Government of Northern Ireland
should finance the scheme;
(3) That the Road Fund Grants should be
retained in part payment of the sum or interest during the continuance of the operations;
(4) That the respective County Councils
should be annually required to contribute an
amount not exceeding the 1914 expenditure
on such roads;
(5) That at the expiration of five years, or
other period, the control of said roads should
again revert to the present Road Authorities.
During the discussion it transpired that in
the past the longest term for which money
could be borrowed was five years, and it appearing that under the reconstruction scheme
a longer term could be obtained, the Committee came to this finding :-
.-
"The Roads Advisory Committee for
Northern Ireland feel it their duty to draw
the attention of the Government of
Northern Ireland to the deplorable condition of the Class I roads in many parts of
Northern Ireland, and to urge that drastic
measures should be taken to remedy this
state of affairs.
In the opinion of the Committee the present method of patching and resurfacing is
uneconomical and inadequate, resulting in
considerable loss and inconvenience to the
owners of motor and other vehicles using
the roads, without commensurate benefits
from the sums spent on the roads out of the
rates and from State sources.
The Committee consider that, in order
that the roads in Northern Ireland may be
brought into a condition suitable for the
traffic now using them, it is absolutely
essential that a comprehensive work of reconstruction and restoration should be
undertaken.
From the experience gained during their
recent tour in Great Britain the Committee
are convinced that by a wholesale reconstruction of these roads a considerable saving would be ultimately effected, as the
initial expenditure involved would be more
than counterbalanced by the greatly reduced maintenance charges required in
future years.
The Committee at the same time realise
the obvious disadvantages of imposing
additional cost on the ratepayers under
present circumstances, and they strongly
recommend that Road Authorities in
Northern Ireland should be granted
facilities for borrowing the necessary sums
required for this work of restoration, that
the Government of Northern Ireland should
undertake to grant loans to these authorities on the most favourable terms possible
for long periods, and that the annual loan
charges for repayment of interest and
principal should qualify for the usual
grants out of the Road Fund,
The Committee feel that, by adopting
this arrangement, the cost to the ratepayers
will not be any heavier than at present,
and, in all probability will be very much
less.
The Committee also desire to represent
that if the local authorities are encouraged
to promote comprehensive schemes of restoration on their Class I roads in the
manner suggested above, very substantial
stimulus to employment will be effected,
which would have most beneficial results at
the present stage when there is so much
unemployment in the Northern Ireland
area.
I trust the Government will favourably consider this recommendation and deal with us
not only justly but generously. We are about
to reconstruct our roads, and we feel that we
are about to reconstruct them for posterity.
Someone has said that posterity never did
anyhing for us, but we feel that at least a
part of this expenditure should be borne by
those who come after vs. I should be very
pleased if the Government could see its way
to obtain a loan for, say, 50 years, at a
moderate rate of interest. I believe that
would be an absolute and final solution of
the whole difficulty.
Mr. DICKSON: I would like to support the
Government in the introduction of this Bill,
which, we hope, will improve our roads and
77.
29 MARCH 1923.
Roads Bill-

Second Reading.
let the privilege go. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). After the lucid manner in which the
noble Lord has explained the Bill, and the
speech of Mr. Long, who followed, I need not
detain you further.
make the procedure very considerably easier
than it has been in the past. I think the
difficulty in connection with road maintenance will never be solved until the heavy
motor traffic is got back to the source from
which it came and where it should continue
to be on the railways. Our roads in the
Northern part of Ireland-we are only speaking for the Six Cornties-were never built to
carry five tons and upwards, and taxation will
continue, no matter what system is carried
out, until the Government can by some means
induce the railway companies to bring the
heavy freight back to the railways. That can
only be done by reducing the railway fares.
My Friend, the previous speaker (Mr. Long),
has spoken of his connection with Derry
County Council and Derry Rural District
Council. I have been a member of the Rural
District Council of Newtownards for the past
quarter of a century, and I agree with him
that the work done there has been well done
in the past, and the members who looked
after the roads did their duty faithfully,
honestly and well. But the main road system has now, owing to motor taxation, gone
into first and second-class roads-first-class
roads of which we get back 50 per cent. and
second-class roads-as the Leader of the
House has told us-of which we get back 25
per cent. He said from the Government, but
I say from motor taxation. It comes entirely
from the owners of motors. The Government
are not putting their hand into any other
pocket except that of the owners of motors.
We in the County of Down have got during
the last year our full share of this.
I am delighted to see by the Bill that firstclass roads are now to be a county-at-large
charge. We will have efficiency; we will
have uniformity in construction; we will have
economy in administration. But why the
Government do not include second-class roads
in that same category I cannot understand.
The mileage of second-class roads in the rural
districts is very small, and it would still
create the difficulty in accountancy to keep
these second-class roads part district and part
county-at-large charges. If it is right to
apply the system to first-class roads, it certainly is right to apply it to second-class
roads, because second-class roads would
run through. perhaps, two or three
rural districts, and the accountancy
difficulties would apply to them, and
you would have a system of three different
accounts to keep. I have given notice of an
amendment that the second-class roads should
be placed in the same category as first-class
roads. We in County Down, perhaps, are
more after economy than after privilege. Mr.
Long has asked for privilege for the District
Councils not to be interfered with. We
would rather see economy and uniformity and
VISCOUNT BANGOR: I cannot speak with
the experience and knowledge and authority
of my hon. Friend who has just sat down, but
I am in entire agreement with every word
which he has said. What I venture to think
is essential in the proper administration of
the roads is a uniform system, and I cannot
see, in the case of second-class roads, if they
are finally put under dual control, how that
uniformity of system can be attained. The
second-class roads, as my hon. Friend who
has just spoken (Mr. Dickson) mentioned,
are not very numerous, and he also mentioned
that some of these roads run out of one rural
district into another, and consequently the
financial responsibility for their upkeep will
devolve on more than one rural district.
This. I think, will lead to a good deal of unnecessary work in the way of correspondence
and office work, will be uneconomical, and
will cause delays when repairs have got to be
carried out to the roads. Second-class roads
will, therefore be going to the bad, and the
users of second-class roads will be the
sufferers.
Now under present day conditions, with
large and heavy motor lorries carrying all
sorts and descriptions of merchandise from
various parts of the country which used to be
carried by the railways, the roads, of course,
suffer accordingly. There is enormous strain
put upon the roads which ten or fifteen years
ago could not have been contemplated, and
for which, as has been mentioned by the hon.
Senator, Mr. Long, these roads were never
designed. We, when we use the roads, feel
this very much, and the destruction of the
surface of the roads is very rapid. I am sure
hon. Members must know that very well,
especially those who, like myself, have to
drive their own motor cars.
Now I think any Measure which is calculated to facilitate the efficient maintenance of
the roads and their prompt repair must commend itself to all hon. Members here. I do
not know really what the general feeling is
amongst the Rural District Councils throughout the Six Counties as to the Sub-section C
of Clause 1 of the Bill, paragraph 5, but I
know, as pointed out by the hon. Member,
Mr. Hill Dickson, that in the County Down
the Rural District Councils are all in favour
of the responsibility for the maintenance of
second-class roads being in the hands of the
County Councils. I can only express the hope
that the Government will give their most
favourable consideration to the suggestion of
my hon. Friend, Mr. Hill Dickson.
Roads Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I do not wish to
prolong these proceedings, but as I have considerably longer experience on road administration than any of the gentlemen who have
spoken, I think, perhaps, it is right that I
should say a few words. I have had 25 years'
experience of Grand Juries and Baronial Sessions before the County Councils and District Councils came into being. As the day
is wearing on I will not say very much, except that it is quite clear to me that the main
Clause of this Bill is excellent and necessary.
In our present circumstances it is quite necessary that these first-class roads should be put
on the County. I am as reluctant as the
Gentleman opposite (Mr. Long) to do anything to affront in any way the Rural District Councils, of one of which I have the
honour to be Chairman. I believe they will
be glad to be relieved of what has become an
exceedingly difficult operation-the maintenance of these first-class roads. They must feel
now that they have had their share of this
work, and what they do is rendered vain if
the next rural district is not managed in the
same way. If, as soon as they get along
their good road going to the county town,
they have to cross another district, not on
their good but on a very bad one, I believe
they will be exceedingly glad to fall in with
a system under which the whole of these
roads will be put under uniform management.
Evidently the various processes that have
been mentioned by Mr. Long with regard to
Government assistance will be far more easily
put into practice in connection with countyat-large roads than if they have to be dealt
with by a number of Rural District
Councils. I am very anxious, on the other
hand, that the local knowledge and
experience, and good sense, I may say,
of the Rural District Councillors should not
be wasted, and therefore I do not wish anything taken out of their hands that may be
left in their hands without injury to the
general road maintenance of the country. I
was therefore at first rather inclined to object
to these B. Roads being taken over. It is
quite clear that these B. roads differ verv
little from A. Roads. The number is
not very large, and therefore they had
better be treated like A. roads. I do not see
any use in this half-way house in this Bill.
Therefore I think that the suggested amendment will be an improvement.
But if the second-class roads are to be
treated on the same footing as first-class
roads, from what I know of them and from
the opinion of others whose opinion
I value, it will be absolutely necessary that
there should be very stringent revision of the
classification. The present classification as
between second-class and third-class roads is
not what it should be, and I am not sure that
the classification as between first-class and
second-class roads is quite what it should be
at any rate. And if this Bill, as it stands,
does not give the Ministry of Home Affairs
ample power to revise the classification of
these roads, I think an amendment should be
introduced by the Government to give them
that power.
Undoubtedly there is a difficulty that has
been touched upon here that the County Councils have too much to do, but I think their
work in other directions may be eased, because after all, it is their proper work to
look after the roads In the old Grand Jury
days we used to spend a whole day going
through the presentments sent up from the
Baronial Sessions. Now the presentmentsor the proposals as they are now called-are
taken up at the end of an adjourned meeting
when there are very few people present, and
they get no proper revision at all. I think
it would be most important to bring the
County Councils back to a certain extent to
the old Grand Jury system and make this
looking after the administration of these
first-class roads the most important part of
their business. Some way must be found to
ease them in other respects.
I do not think I need detain the Senate any
longer. I shall certainly support the Second
reading of this Bill, and when the Committee
stage is taken, provision should be made with
regard to the revision of the classification and
the other matters that I suggested.
I
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am sure the House will agree that we have
listened to a very valuable discussion on this
subject which naturally interests all of us
here. In reference to the remark which the
hon. Senator who has just sat down has made,
I can inform him that we have ample power
to revise the classification, and this classification will be revised as soon as it is desired, and as soon as it is convenient to do so.
I think that will probably relieve the hon.
Gentleman of a certain amount of anxiety.
The debate has ranged over a wide area.
almost feel inclined to say we shall be able
to discuss the great majority of these points
on the Committee stage, as you, my Lord, put
it clause by clause from the Chair. I have no
doubt it will be possible to amend some of the
provisions of the Bill as it stands at the
present moment. I need hardly say I welcome very much the cordial reception that
has been given to the efforts of my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs. I
think I may say there is general agreement
that the Bill itself marks an event that
will have a far-reaching and very beneficial
effect on the Six Counties.
I should like to congratulate Senator Long
on his very able speech. He naturally possesses a great knowledge of all these subjects
with his very wide and varied experience. I
should like, if I might, at once to remove
from his mind the doubts he expressed that
Roads Bill
Second Reading.
29 MARCH 1923.

in the Bill there was an insinuation against
the Rural District Councils for the manner
in which they have performed their duties. I
can assure him that any intention of this description was entirely absent from the mind
of my right hon. Friend, as it is from that
of every Member of the Government. I feel
that we, like the rest of the community, owe
to the local bodies an immense debt of gratitude, for the amount of work they have done
all over the country is something for which
we have every reason to be proud. Moreover,
when we realise the many duties which they
are called upon to perform-duties on which
the whole life of the nation depends-we have
every reason to feel we are very adequately
served by the local bodies which exist
in this particular part of the United Kingdom.
My hon. Friend suggests that the County
Councils are being overburdened; that the
duties which are being put on them are be
yond their strength, that they have too much
to do. I am not sure that if I moved a resolution in this House as to whether any of us
have too much to do-in fact, that all of us
have too much to do-it would not be carried
by a large majority. Nevertheless, these
burdens have got to be carried by some one,
and there are no more adequate bodies to discharge these duties than the County Councils
which are in existence in the Six Counties.
I hope my hon. Friend's fears are unfoundedthat business men will not come forward to
take part in the local administration. That,
indeed, would be a calamity. I sincerely
hope, and I believe there are enough
public-spirited people amongst our friends
in this part of the country to put
that fear on one side altogether. The
hon. Member talks of uniformity, and of
course the main provision of this Bill is the
desire for uniformity on this question.
Hitherto the administration of the roads has
not gone at all in the direction of uniformity.
One does feel that when the roads are put
to a far greater strain than they have ever
been put to in the past they will be best administered by the central authority of the
County Council. But while the administration will be uniform that does not mean that
the treatment of the roads will be uniform.
I feel sure that in these details as to different
sub-soils, and everything connected with the
making of the roads, the treatment of each
portion of the roads will be the best to which
that portion is entitled. I hope the hon.
Member will have no qualms on that point.
He went on to deal with the question of
finance. Of course this is the most important
subject that confronts all of us, especially at
this time. I cannot say I was disappointed
by what he said. I would venture to suggest
to him that in his position as Senator
he is probably much more the guardian
of the overburdened and harassed taxpayer
than he is of the ratepayer. Whereas he has
pleaded a great case for the ratepayer and
brought forward many harrowing examples
of inability to pay rates, if he is desirous of
these improvements the money has got to
come from somewhere, and if it does not come
from the ratepayer, it has got to come from
the taxpayer. It is quite true that although
we are members of an august body who
are not deemed to be capable
actually dealing with finance, there is
reason why we should not express Our
opinion on this important subject. He speaks
also of a loan, and as coming from the Chairman of the Finance Committee of the
Londonderry County Council, I think that
that is a matter that should be treated with
greater respect.
of
no
But speaking for myself, I am not sure, in
these days when the country is burdened with
loans as it is at the present moment,
that it is possible for the Government to
embark very lightly on further loans in any
direction. He is anxious, as he said, that
these charges should be laid on posterity who
have done nothing for us. His last suggestion is correct, but as to whether or not
posterity should bear this burden I do not
feel myself competent at this moment to say.
I will say this to him, that this is a matter
that has not escaped the attention of the
Government. They are considering it from
every point of view, and will no doubt come
to a decision in a very short time from now.
The hon. Member who followed him, Senator
Hill Dickson, spoke of second-class roads, and
told us that he has given notice of an amendment in this connection. I believe that if he
has done anything of the kind he is out of
order, because it is impossible to give notice
of an amendment until after the
Second reading has been accomplished.
However, that is a minor detail, and I can
assure him that when the proper time comes
and the amendment is handed in to the
Clerk at the Table and comes forward on the
Committee stage, I shall be very happy to
give very favourable consideration to it, be--
cause the Government think his suggestion is
a most excellent one. I do not feel that I
should be entitled to detain the House much
longer on this most interesting discussion.
The general operation of the Bill will come
into force forthwith, but the final date, which,
I think, I gave to the House as April, 1923,
is, as I am sure hon. Members are aware,
1st April, 1924. I thank the House very
much for the reception they have given to the
Bill, and I hope that on the Committee stage
we shall be able to make it into a perfect
measure that will suit the requirements of the
Six Counties.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Before I move that this Bill be now passed,
I desire to move an amendment on the Third
reading. The House will remember that I
promised Mr. Senator Greer in Committee to
move the amendment on the Order Paper
to-day in place of the suggested amendment
which he brought forward on the Committee
stage. I think that the point which these
amendments are designed to meet was fully
debated on that occasion. I will not, therefore, trespass further on the indulgence of the
House. The amendment occurs in Clause 5,
page 4, lines 15 and 16, leave out "and every
director and manager thereof." I beg to
move.
On Question, Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
On line 22 I would move, at end insert (" and
every director and manager of the Company
who knowingly and wilfully authorises or permits the default shall be liable on summary
conviction to the like penalty.")
On Question, Amendment agreed to.

Royal Assent.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am authorised by the Prime Minister, on
his behalf, to inform the Senate that his
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland, in
the name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his assent to
the following Bills:-
Canals (Continuance of Charging Powers).
Inspectors of Weights and Measures.
Consolidated Fund and Appropriation.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that the House do now adjourn.
I do not believe that it will be necessary for
the House to meet again after the Easter
recess until Tuesday, 24th April. I hope that
date will be for the convenience of hon. Members. I beg to move.
On Question, motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Five of the
Clock, until Tuesday, 24th
April next, at a quarter after
Three of the Clock.

Criminal Law Amendment Bill.

itself into Committee on the said Bill.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

In Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.)
Clause I. (Consent of young person to be
no defence) ordered to stand part of the Bill.

of 48 and 49 Vict., c. 69, as to defence of

Reasonable cause to believe that a girl
was of or above the age of sixteen years
shall not be a defence to a charge under
Sub-section (1) of Section five or Section
six of the Criminal Law Amendment Act,
the prin-
1885 (in this Act referred to as
cipal Act"). The limit of time mentioned
in the second proviso to Section five of the

twenty-seven of the Prevention of Cruelty

as
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I have a small Amendment here which, I regret to say, the House has not been informed
of, but it is merely an Amendment to make
the Bill a little more explicit. The Amendment is as follows: Clause 2, page 3, line 11,
after the word "or" insert the word "under."
It will then read: "under Sub-section (1) of
Section 5 or under Section 6," and so forth.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
Clause III. (Amendment of penalties under
Section 13 of the principal Act) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I may admit at once that the
amendment I have to move is of the conversational order rather than the controversial,
because, although I think myself that the
proposal is a proper one, I do not think that
it is a proposal which ought to be pressed if
the opinion of the Government is against it,
either because of its substance or because of
the opportunity taken for suggesting it.
The
The reason I invite the House to take notice
of the matter to-day is that we have before us
an amending Bill which brings under review
a not very recent Statute of the Imperial Parliament, and I submit that in such a case it
behoves us to look carefully to see whether
there are any discrepancies between the law
of England and the law of Ireland which
ought to be altered here; that is, if it is considered worth while to alter them, and if it
is thought that the time is suitable.
proposal is directed to Section 17 of the principal Act, the Act of 1885. This is the section
which, after applying the Vexatious Indictments Act of 1859 to all misdemeanours under
the Act of 1885, both in England and in Ireland, goes on to say-"No indictment under
the provisions of this Act shall in England be
tried by any Court of Quarter Sessions." I
do not know why the distinction was made, in
the latter part of the section, between England and Ireland. It is possible that it may
have been due to a consideration of the lack
of travelling facilities in Ireland at that
time. It is more probable that it was due to
some other case. I cannot express any jositive opinion, for the debates of 1885 are not
yet included in our library. But, negatively,
having regard to the privilege of bail, I do
not think that any question of keeping prisoners too long in jail can have been involved.
As a
In England the arrangements as regards the
jurisdiction of Quarter Sessions differ very
much, as the House knows, from those prevailing in Ireland, because the Quarter Sessions Act does not apply to Ireland.
consequence, we have at present no statutory
regulation at all. The discretion of magistrates is controlled only by the circulars
which have been issued from Dublin Castle
and by the right of the Attorney-General
to apply to Sessions to remit to Assizes. The
Attorney-General also has the power, so to
speak, of short-circuiting Sessions by not
sending up bills there. I am not sure that
that state of things is altogether desirable,
but I recognise that very possibly it might
be better to deal with the subject, it may be,
some day perhaps when we are dealing with
relief of jury service, and considering
Quarter Sessions practice as a whole, and it
may be possible that we may feel justified
Criminal Law Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
in abolishing Quarter Sessions jurisdiction
altogether except so far as there are Recorder's Courts. Modern tendency is rather
to extend than to curtail Quarter Sessions.
But I can find no indication of any likelihood of a change in practice as regards
offences of the character with which this Bill
is concerned. On the contary, there are two
modern Acts closely related by reference to
this Act of 1885. I mean the Punishment of
Incest Act, 1908, which applies to Ireland,
and the Mental Deficiency Act, which does
not so apply. Both these Acts expressly
exclude the offences they relate to from the
purview of Quarter Sessions, and I do not
think that there is any cause for suggesting
that changing the practice as regards these
cases would result in any undue delay. We
may remember that the City Commission sits
four times during the year, and that all the
counties have three Assizes-Spring, Summer, and Winter.
a
The advantages that would appear to follow
from this amendment are (first) the substitution of trial at Assizes before a Judge of
the High Court for trial at Quarter Sessions,
where the Chairman of the Sessions, unless
he is sitting as Recorder, has not undivided
responsibility. The change would make, I
think, for uniformity, and it may be for improvement both in procedure and in practice,
and it would, perhaps, make for better coordination in the matter of the apportionment of sentences. Secondly, it would no
longer be necessary to consider whether
particular case should go to the Assizes or to
Sessions. Further, lady magistrates who
may be attending Quarter Sessions would be
spared hearing repulsive cases. Also some
check, by presenting such Bills only at the
Assizes, might be put on the attendance at
the trial of these drawn to Court by no
motive other than a prurient curiosity.
Lastly, there would be a further assimilation of the practice that obtains in Englan 1,
which to my mind is of considerable value.
I do not want to be understood as suggesting
that the English arrangements are theoretically perfect. I do not think they are. In
practice they work well enough.
It may
well be that the same opinion is entertained
as to the practice in Northern Ireland.
beg to move.
As the hon.
and for the following reason.
Senator said, Section 17 of the Criminal Law
Amendment Act provides that no indictment
under the provisions of the Act shall in
England be tried at any Court of Quarter
Sessions. In Ireland this provision does not
apply.
Prima facie, it might seem as if the insertion in the Criminal Law Amendment
Bill of such a provision would have the
effect of assimilating the law as between
England and Ireland. In fact, however, this
is not so, because-firstly, there are numerous other cases, some twenty or thirty,
where indictments cannot be tried at Quarter
Sessions in England but can be so tried in
Ireland. Secondly, the object of the Section
can be and is, in Ireland, achieved by the
action of the Attorney-General on behalf of
the Crown, either by arranging that a pri
soner is not to be brought up at Quarter
Sessions or, if necessary, by entering a Nolle
Prosequi at Quarter Sessions and proceeding
afresh. Therefore the amendment cannot be
accepted.

Clause 4 (Calling of wife or husband in
certain cases).
(1) The wife or husband of a person
charged with an offence under any enactment mentioned in the Schedule to this
Act may be called as a witness either for
the prosecution or defence and without the
consent of the person charged.
(2) Nothing in this Act shall affect a case
where the wife or husband of a person
charged with an offence may at common
law be called as a witness without the consent of that person,

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
The amendment I have to move is in the
first schedule, page 6 leave out lines 1 and 2
in the third column of the schedule, and insert "Sections 48, 52, 53, 54, and 57." The
amendment
as to the inclusion of the
Offences Against the Person Act of 1861,
which are enumerated in the First Schedule
is proposed for two reasons. Firstly, it is
more accurate than that in the text. The
text reads: "Sections 48 to 55," and as
sections 49 to 51 have been repealed, and

Act. Secondly, it will bring the Irish law
91 Criminal Evidence Bill-

THE SENATE.

[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
into line with the English law. The object
of section 28. sub-section 3 of the Criminal
Justice Administration Act of 1914, was to
enable the wife or husband of the person
charged with bigamy to be called as witness
either for the prosecution or for the defence,
and without the consent of the person
charged. The effect of this sub-section was
to add bigamy to the Schedule of the 1918 Act.
A number of the provisions of the Act of 1914
did not extend to Ireland, because the 1918
Act did not so extend. With a view therefore of assimilating the general law by the
present Bill, it would seem consistent to extend the English provisions to Ireland which
can best be done by adding section 57.
beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
VISCOUNT
MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: There are three other amendments
to the First Schedule standing in my name
which I propose, with the leave of the House,
to move en bloc. They are "After line 16.

These amendments are put forward by way
of consolidating the law. These three enactments which I have mentioned were all
passed during the period when the law of
criminal evidence in Ireland differed from
that of England. These enactments enabled
the husband or wife to give evidence. The
necessity is dispensed with by Clause 4 of the
present Bill. I beg to move.

The Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1885-
Section 20. This amendment proposes to repeal section 20 of the Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1885, superseded by the provision
of the present Bill, except in the one respect in which it is inconsistent with the
present Bill, that is to say, where it prevents
the husband or wife from giving evidence in
a prosecution before the Grand Jury. This
is inconsistent with Clause 4 of the Bill.
therefore it requires express repeal. In regard to the remaining amendments, these are
to repeal those provisions whose place is now
taken by Clause 4, and the First Schedule of
the present Bill. I beg to move.
