to usurers, probably an auctioneer or moneylender, and in such a case the peasant proprietor will not be a success.
I had better not go into the question of
the indebtedness of the peasantry, because it
would lead me too far, but what I specially
wish to emphasise is that immediate attention
should be given to the question of making
adequate arrangements at the Land Registry
for Northern Ireland, and that whoever
superintends it should not for a moment
lose sight of the fact that when the arrears
are overtaken it is absolutely necessary that
the investigation of these equities should be
undertaken. Then, if the system of regis
tration of title is to work here as usefully
as it does abroad, some arrangement must
be made to put at the disposal of the small
owners those facilities in the way of credit
which decentralised and small districts
abroad afford. I think Senator Greer was
wrong when he said the system existed in
France. The reason people think the system
exists in France is that the system they
have there for the registration of mortgages
is for very small districts; and the bad
system there works better than the good
system does here, simply because the area
is smaller.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry I must again
interrupt the hon. Member, but he is going
far too much into this question. If the hon.
Member wishes to say anything more I must
reques him to put down a motion on the
Paper.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: After the exhaustive
addresses we have listened to from my hon.
Friends (Senator Greer and Senator Montgomery) opposite. I feel almost inclined, first
of all, to apologise to the House for making
any remarks. I wish to limit myself to the
fourth part of the question, namely that of
providing proper staffing for the local offices.
According to my view, and the view of those
with whom I have discussed the matter, if
the local offices were more strongly staffed
the congestion at the head office would
largely disappear. We think that the importance of these local offices can hardly be
exaggerated. One of my hon Friends referred
to the desirability of having more than one
office in a county. Of course the counties
vary very much in size, but in the
county I know best we find one office pretty
well sufficient. Already there are 15,000 registrations, and we feel that if this were developed it would be sufficient, and, as I have
said, relieve the congestion, because upwards
of 95 per cent. of the cases go through as a
matter of course, and there is no necessity
to refer to the central office at all, except in
cases of difficulty such as the construction
of a will, or if there is an application to discharge equities. I know this as a matter of
fact, because on one occasion, partly from
good nature and partly from a desire to see
how the thing worked, I put through a case
myself, and it did not take more than five
minutes. There is nothing to prevent any
simple title going through without reference
to head office at all. Of course a note of it
has to be sent up to the head office, but it
can be perfectly well dealt with by the local
office. That was the original intention, as
the Act of 1891 shows, because it was the
Local Registration of Title Act. I hope, in
whatever way the matter is dealt with, sight
will not be lost of this particular point.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The question which has been put down by
the hon. Member (Senator Greer) is on the
Paper divided into four parts and the debate
has ranged round those four sections in a
very interesting manner, though I regret
that my hon. Friends have transgressed,
or in a measure transgressed, the
ruling which you, Sir, have laid down from
the Chair. This is a question of the greatest
possible importance, and I should like to
take this opportunity of thanking my two
hon. Friends for their great courtesy in furnishing me with an outline of the remarks
which they proposed to address to the House
this afternoon. The debate, as I have said, has
been an interesting one, but it was of more
or less an academic nature because all matters arising out of the registration of titles
belong to what we call a reserved service,
and any question relating thereto should be
properly put in the Imperial Parliament. I
need hardly say that, while this places us
all in exactly the same position, and we who
are Members of the Government have really
little power to do anything more than the other
Members of this House, still when we have
two such high authorities on the question
of land as my two hon. Friends' opposite to me,
it is certainly a matter of interest, and it is
consistent with the debates which we desire
to have in this House that they should come
forward and put down questions of this description so that the matter should be debated in a proper way.
I hope that when my hon. Friend (Senator
Greer) desires to place a question of this
kind on the paper he will make it of a fuller
nature, if I may use the expression, and I
am sure it would be possible to give a day to
a discussion of this description when we
363 Reconstitution of Records.

THE SENATE.
Housing Bill-
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
have not so much public business to transact.
On those grounds I would venture to ask the
hon. Members not to expect a long answer
from me to-day, because, while I need hardly
say that this is a matter that must interest
all of us, by reason of it being a reserved service I can add very little of authority which
would be of assistance.
The reconstruction of folios and maps destroyed in the Four Courts, Dublin, naturally
is a very tedious and very lengthy process,
but steps are in progress to carry out this
reconstruction as cases requiring this arise,
and I think my hon. Friends know that it is
intended gradually to institute a full reconstruction of all the damage which was
occasioned by the fire in the Four Courts.
With regard to current registration, I am
aware that delays have occurred but with the
provision of adequate accommodation
for the central staff which is
now in hand and the appointment
of a full staff much greater expedition
is expected, though it must be realised that
arrears must first be overtaken, and of course
there are a great many arrears. Improvements in the position and status of officers
in the local offices are also about to be made.
To some extent the delays which have
occurred in these offices have been the outcome of the unsatisfactory position of the
central office.
Whether it is desirable that the service
should be transferred to the Northern Government, is a matter entirely within the discretion of the Imperial Government, but I
note that my hon. Friend (Senator Greer) is
of opinion that it should be transferred, and
it is impossible for me to say what may
happen in the near future.
With regard to that portion of the question
which deals with ordnance survey maps, I
am able to say that the original surveys have
been completed, and the staff of the Ordnance
Survey is now engaged on the continuous revision of the 25-inch sheets. Since the transfer of the service to the Government of Northern Ireland revision of the county of
Antrim has been completed on the ground,
and progress has been made with the revision
of County Londonderry. These are the
specific replies to the questions which my
hon Friend (Senator Greer) has put down,
and, while he has raised a subject, or rather
many subjects, of the greatest interest, I feel
that it is impossible for me to go more fully
into the matter than I have been able to do
in the few minutes for which I have occupied
the time of the House.
Mr. GREER: I have to thank the noble
Marquess
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member (Senator
Greer) cannot address the House again.
Housing Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In asking the House to give a Second reading to the Housing Bill, I am well aware that
I am entering upon a subject which with unemployment has been since the war the great
Hifficulty of every administration. The
Housing Act of 1919 was an attempt to deal
with the problem through undertakings by
local authorities and provided a subsidy of
twenty-five shillings from State sources for
every twenty shillings of rent collected by
the local authority for houses built under
the Act. Those terms may seem generous
enough at the present time, but in those days
they did not receive the entire approval of
the local authorities. Moreover, these
authorities were required by the administration of the day to build to specifications
which did not make for the provision of the
class of houses most urgently required. They
resulted in the erection of houses quite unsuited to the need of the working classes
whom the Act was designed to benefit, houses
which could not be let to the working man
at an economic rent at all. Some of them
cost upwards of twelve hundred pounds to
build, and in consequence could not possibly
be made available for him except at a ruinous cost to the general body of the ratepayers,
even with the assistance of the state subsidy.
So far as Northern Ireland is concerned,
the Government regard that Act as a failure.
It is true a certain number of houses were
built under it by the Belfast Corporation,
but outside Belfast, as a rule, the local
authorities were deterred by the high costs
and this problem of economic rents from
taking any but a very slight advantage of
the Act. Even in Belfast, where alone the
shortage of houses is estimated at between
eight and ten thousand, the number erected,
amounting to some four hundred and sixty
houses, could have but little effect
the solution of the great problem.
towards
Under the Housing (Additional Powers)
Act of 1919 some five hundred and thirty
further houses were built by private builders,
while some advance has been made under
the grants of 60 per cent. of the labour costs
from the Ministry of Labour, whereby housing has been stimulated and unemployment
relieved at the same time. By this means
nearly sixteen hundred artisans' dwellings
have been put in hand by the Belfast Corporation, and a further two hundred and
eighty have been started by private enterprise in the city itself, while outside some
sixty more have been commenced. But the
bulk of the work still remains to be undertaken. High building costs and the difficulty
as to rents, to which I have already alluded.
have militated to discourage private enter-
Housing Bill.
Second Reading.
19 NOVEMBER 1923.
prise. In this field also the restriction of
rents has served to deter the private
builder. It is the intention of the Government, as hon. Members know, to introduce a
measure dealing with this aspect of the
question-the
restriction of rents-next
Session, and I will, therefore, confine myself
to-day strictly to the problem of the provision of houses.
I may say at once that it is the considered
view of the Government that the solution of
our problem lies along the line of giving encouragement to the private builder, to whose
energy and enterprise the people of Northern
Ireland were indebted for the very satisfactory
pre-war position as to houses
throughout the Province. In our opinion, it
is only by the resuscitation of private enterprise that we can hope to meet the present
situation, and this is the underlying principle of the Bill, though we certainly do not
propose to discourage local authorities from
assisting in the emergency. But, in the
main, as I have said, it is to the private individual that we look so to take courage as
a result of the terms we offer under the present Bill, as once more to be led to build as
a profitable and attractive means of investing
his capital. A bridge was discovered to bring
the landowner and the tenant together for
the purpose of effecting Land Purchase for
the general benefit of the community, and a
form of state assistance seems not unsuitable or unpromising to span the distance between the rent which makes building a
reasonable proposition and that which the
working man is reasonably expected to pay in
rent. I do not feel that I need go very fully
into the clauses of the Bill itself. The Committee stage will be taken in a very short
time, and I will therefore move the Second
reading of this Bill. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: It is, I think, a happy thing
that the first visit our Friend the right hon.
Gentleman the Minister of Labour (Mr.
Andrews) has paid to this House in his
official capacity should coincide with the
introduction of a measure in which, I am
sure, he takes the deepest interest. The Bill
itself gives us an opportunity of doing that
which we are always very ready to do. It
will give us the chance of doing something
to assist the working classes. The greater
part of the Bill deals with finance, and as
such does not particularly affect this House,
but I cannot pass over the Bill without congratulating those responsible for it on the
great improvement made in it since it was
first introduced in another place. When the
Bill first saw the light it contained what
seemed to me a most objectionable provision
as regards building houses and allowing
them to remain rates free for a period. That
has now gone, and a bridge, to use the words
of the noble Marquess, has been substituted
which takes the form of a grant, or perhaps
of a loan. That applies to the rural districts
in which I am particularly interested. 1
desire also to congratulate the Government
on extending to industrial centres in rural
districts benefits which they have not
hitherto enjoyed.
I should like to invite the attention of the
House to the wording of the section dealing
with industrial centres, in order to make
sure that everybody is satisfied that it really
meets its purpose. The particular section,
which sustained a good deal of attack in its
original state and has in my judgment been
very materially improved, catches as it
stands rather difficult cases in an industrial
centre cases not within one mile and a half
from the boundary of the area of a local
authority. Such cases do exist, and give a
certain amount of trouble. Very wisely the
Government has abstained from defining
industrial centres. I note that the grant
under the sub-section, which I am alluding
to, in the case of the rural areas is to be at
a rate of not exceeding £60 instead of a flat
rate of £60 as in the urban districts. I am
quite sure that the Government will be able
to give us an assurance that that safeguard
will be liberally interpreted. I am not in
the least disposed to object to the wording.
One knows from experience that building in
rural districts can sometimes be effected
more cheaply than in the urban areas. It
is not invariably so, but it is sometimes the
case that you can get building done in the
country more economically than in the town.
When we come to a later stage of the Bill 1
shall venture to ask for some explanation
of the sub-section which is inserted towards
the end requiring that the consent of the
county council shall no longer be required
to certain proceedings in urban districts.
I do not press that matter to-day, and I do
not intend to put forward any amendment.
There is nothing in the Bill I want to cut
out. I think it is an excellent Bill, and we
have every reason to hope that it will help
very materially all concerned.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill be
now read a Second time "), agreed to.
Trade Boards Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
feel somewhat diffident in the presence of
my right hon. Friend (Mr. Andrews) in
introducing the Bill which he had the privilege of introducing in another place, but I
am sure I am voicing the opinions of everyone present here to-day in giving him a
Trade Boards Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
welcome to our deliberations (hon. Members: Hear, hear)--and saying how glad we
are that by the Rules of our Houses of Parliament we are superior to other Houses of
Parliament not far distant from here in
allowing Ministers to sit in both Houses,
and in having in each House the benefit of
their experience and the knowledge they
have on those matters which appertain to
their offices. I hope the right hon. Gentleman will make a practice of coming amongst
us and give us that assistance which no one
is better qualified to give than he is.
The Bill which I have the honour to bring
forward for Second Reading will, I am sure,
receive a sympathetic consideration in this
House, because it is so largely the result
of your own labours, Mr. Speaker, and those
of the Committee on Trade Boards, over
which you presided with so much distinction
during the close of the year 1921 and the
first half of last year. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) The measure before the House
is founded upon the conclusions which were
arrived at by your Committee after very
exhaustive inquiry, and which were embodied
in their admirable report issued last year.
Indeed, for a full understanding of the whole
subject of this Bill, I cannot too strongly
recommend to Members of this House a close
study of that report; and it is because I
feel sure that that valuable document which
has already been in your hands and which
has already received your attention that I
will not trespass upon the time of the House
with any lengthy introductory remarks.
Trade Boards came into existence with the
Trade Boards Act of 1909, as the direct result
of many years of public agitation against
sweating in industries, agitation which was
carried on by many devoted men and women
on behalf of those sections of labour which
were ill-organised, or altogether unorganised,
and, therefore, quite unable to give expression
to their sufferings, amongst whom, perhaps,
the name of Mary McArthur is the most
remembered. That Act, the Act of 1909,
achieved three great changes: firstly,
the enforcement of a compulsory minimum
rate under statute; secondly, the establishment in the trades concerned of bodies called
Trade Boards to fix the rate, bodies equally
representativo of employers and employed,
with a third element of members specially
appointed by the Board of Trade; thirdly, the
Act provided that each trade should be dealt
with as a separate entity and that no flat
rate applicable to all the trades should be
imposed. This Act operated in those trades
where rates, as compared with the rates in
industries generally, were considered by the
Board of Trade to be unduly depressed. Herein, I think, they found a true criterion of the
necessity, or otherwise, of a Trade Board
to a particular trade.
The orders of the Ministry of Munitions
during the War governed wages which were
established far in advance of the statutory
minimum scales. The activities of the Trade
Boards were, as a result, more or less in
suspension during that period. But it was
foreseen that the close of the War would
render the Trade Boards' machinery more
necessary than ever to protect ill-organised
workers in industries which had arisen or
extended owing to the stress of war. The
Trade Boards Act of 1918 was, therefore,
enacted so as to provide for the further
extension of the Trade Boards Act of 1909,
and that Act authorised the Minister of
Labour to extend the provisions to apply to
trades in which, in his opinion, there was
no adequate machinery for the effective
regulation of wages throughout the trade,
and where, having regard to the rates of
wages prevailing in the trade, or any part
of the trade," it seemed to him, "expedient
that the principal Act should apply to that
trade." Further, the Act of 1918 provided
that the Minister of Labour could apply the
Acts by special Order, thus avoiding the
delay attendant upon procedure through
Provisional Orders which, as hon. Members
recognise, necessitated confirmation by
Parliament.
Moreover, this Act enabled Trade Boards
to extend their powers to fixing guaranteed
time rates, piecework-basis time rates,
and overtime rates. The administration of
these Acts passed to the Ministry of Labour
with the transfer of services, and the present Bill, therefore, is an attempt to consolidate existing legislation and amend it
in so far as it applies to the conditions of
trade and industry in Northern Ireland.
These are a few remarks which I desire
to make in introducing this Bill. Hon.
Members have the Bill in their hands, and
we shall have an opportunity of further
discussing it on the Committee stage. There
are many matters of great importance, and
I feel that this is not perhaps the stage in
which hon. Members would desire that I
should make an exhaustive review of all
the clauses of the Bill. The measure itself
is one of consolidation and amendment of
existing Trade Board legislation so far as
it affects our Province. The name of my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Labour,
has already been connected with many
measures dealing with the relief of unemployment and the amelioration of the conditions under which the labouring classes
of the Province live. No one is more sympathetic with the working classes of the
country than my right hon. Friend, and I
feel that we are fortunate indeed in having
a Minister of Labour who understands these
matters as he does, and who is governed by
that great quality of sympathy with those
whom he desires to benefit. I beg to move.
Trade Boards Bill-
Second Reading.
19 NOVEMBER 1923.
Mr. GREER: I had not very much time to
look into the Trade Boards Bill, but so far
as I can see this Bill is certainly an improvement on the last, and, with the exception of a few clauses, I approve of it.
think it right to say that when the Bill
comes up in Committee I hope to refer to
one or two clauses that I think might be
improved. The clauses I refer to are Clause
8. Clause 12, Clause 15, Sub-section 1 and
Sub-section 8. Perhaps I may have an opportunity of seeing the Minister for a few
minutes about those points. I do not propose
to detain the House further with any remarks
at present.
Mr. WOODS: I rise to support the Bill
that has been so ably put forward by the
noble Marquess. I would like to compliment
him upon the admirable way he has presented this measure. It is not the first time
I have been struck with the great knowledge
the noble Marquess possesses of the labouring
class. I would also like to compliment the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Labour,
upon the great courage he displayed in introducing this Bill to the Commons, and on the
interest he takes in all industrial matters.
I have read the Bill through, I have followed
with interest the debates in another place,
and I am perfectly satisfied that the Bill as
presented in this House will be welcomed
not only by the employees, but by the great
number of employers, especially the good employers, throughout the Northern area, and
we have good employers in the Northern
area. It will be welcomed by the employees
because it will go a long way to set up
machinery which will do away with disputes
and strikes, which have been a great bone
of contention in the past. It will also ensure
that the employees, especially those who are
unorganised, will receive a wage which will
at least enable them to live. On the other
hand it will be welcomed by the good employers who in the past have endeavoured to
pay fairly good wages, but were threatened
by employers of the sweating type who endeavoured to undercut them, with the result
that they were compelled to reduce the
wages they paid accordingly. Those people
will welcome the Bill. I would like the House
-I do not suppose it is possible-to pass this
Bill right through without the delay associated with the various stages of Committee
and Third reading. I feel so strongly in support of the Bill that if it were possible I
would advise the House to do that. At any
rate I voice my opinion very strongly in support of the measure.
Mr. LESLIE: I believe that the right
Parliamentary method of disposing finally of
a measure which does not meet with the
entire approbation of the House for one cause
or another is to move that it be read a Second
time this day six months, and it seems to me
that that is a fate which might very well
overtake this particular measure.
Mr. WOODS: No, no.
for one
Mr. LESLIE: I said just now
cause or another," and the reason why I
think this House ought not to pass, and
ought not to be asked to pass, this Bill
to-day is because this House has not had
time to consider it. The history of the
matter to some extent is well-known. There
was a Committee over which you, Mr. Speaker,
my Lord, presided, and that Committee reported in the month of July, 1922. This
measure was referred to in the Gracious
Speech from the Throne at the opening of
this Session. Now, within the last ten days
of the Session, we have a Bill of 23 pages
thrown at us, and we know that there are
only eight or nine days of the Session unexpired. The Bill was read the First time
in this House on Friday. It was in the
hands of those Members of the House who
live in the country about 10 o'clock on Saturday morning. We are discussing it at halfpast four on Monday afternoon. If we are
to consider the Bill and to discuss it we
have, so to speak, to collate it, not only
with the Report to which reference has
already been made-I had read it before,
and I read it through as hastily as I could
again, but you cannot read it under an hour
-but also with the two Imperial Acts of
1909 and 1918. The Imperial Act of 1918 was
passed, I think, when the noble Marquess
was not there. It is a most puzzling Act;
it takes you backwards and forwards, and in
that way adds very much to the time which
you require to study the matter.
I should like to be informed in what way
the passing of this Bill to-day is going to
assist unemployment, because the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Labour, could
tell us it is not for us to tell him-that
that is the crying evil at the moment.
Indeed I have a note that it was stated in
the Report that it appeared to a good many
people that when trade was bad and unemployment was rife, it was time to allow
questions as between employer and employed
to settle themselves. I should like to know
in what way it is thought that this Bill
will assist unemployment. I should like to
know what the cost of administering it is
likely to be, and the extent of the staff
which will be required to work it. I should
also like to know what is the reason for
the hurry with which this Bill is now being
put through. Why cannot the Bill be left
over until next Session? Is there any grave
urgency, and if so, what is the urgency?
We are told this is a Bill which will largely
assist in the suppression of sweating, with
1A
THE SENATE.
Trade Boards Bill-
Second Reading.

business is not going on, and people are not
being employed.
[Mr. Leslie]
that
I
object,
am
sure,
we are all
you
are
not
in sympathy; but, if you do not pass
this Bill,
will still have the
existing measures of 1909 and 1918. They
still there;
will
those Acts
disappear from the Statute Book merely
because you do not add another one to them.
I am certainly of the opinion that it would
be better to defer action on this matter.
Looking through this Bill of 23 pages as
hastily as I had to, I admit there is a great
deal of detail which need not be mentioned
this afternoon, and on which I certainly am
not in a position to offer an opinion of any
value; but I find in the Bill certain clauses
dealing with administration which would
appear to give to the inspectors employed
by the right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Andrews)
powers which they have not now got, powers
which are not given in either of the Imperial
Statutes passed in England, powers which are
not set out in the Report of your Committee,
Mr. Speaker, my Lord, and powers which to
my mind they ought not to have.
There is one provision in the Act which
really roused in my mind something approaching disgust. I could scarcely believe that it
would be gravely proposed in the twentieth
century to insert such a clause in an Act
of Parliament. If the House decides to
pass this Bill, I hope my hon. Friend on
my left (Mr. Greer) will include that clause
amongst those which he proposes to excise
at a later stage.
We have to consider in dealing with this
matter not only the interests of the City
of Belfast, where the Labour problems are
necessarily most acute, and where unemployment is probably more rife. The right hon.
Gentleman (Mr. Andrews) will correct me
if I am wrong, but I am afraid the rate of
unemployment in Belfast is still higher than
it is in the British Isles, or is it only in
the Six Counties? It is very high at all
events. There is a very large number of
unemployed in or about the Northern area;
there is a certain amount of it in the rural
areas also and in the small towns. A certain
number may be observed drawing their unemployment money; they may not, perhaps,
be observed actually seeking for work.
There used to be rural industries. I remember the time when the tannery and the
ropework were not confined to the large
towns, but those rural industries do not seem
to be thriving very much at the moment.
An attempt was made in the town near which
I live to occupy a disused workhouse as an
embroidery factory. A rather large supply
of sewing machines and other things were
set up in the building. Endeavours were
made to secure labour; labour was secured,
and then suddenly the business stopped. The
sewing machines are still there, but the
am
I
not going to
tell the House why that business was
closed down, because I have not got
the absolute facts, and I do not make any
suggestions. Stories have been told, but I
must leave it at that; I do not make any
definite suggestions. There was practically
a rural industry just on the outskirts of a
small town of 3,100 inhabitants. The thing
was started, and it did not go on. I am very
much afraid that if we introduce more of
these Boards, or in any way extend the
powers of these Trade Boards to the rural
districts, we will strike what may be a blow
at industry. I would very much prefer that
the House should wait, and let this matter
stand over until next Session. It may be
that when we meet next year the prospects of
trade generally may be brighter, for more
than one reason, and I think that we should
have more opportunities for really studying
this matter more completely than it is possible to do between 10 o'clock on Saturday
morning and 4 o'clock on Monday afternoon.
I do not know that anybody really desires a
formal motion for the rejection of the
measure, but if anybody does think that it
really ought not to be read the Second time I
would certainly be in accordance with that
opinion. In any case I hope we shall not be
asked to take the Committee stage for at
least three or four days. I think it is fixed
for the day after to-morrow. It is really
impossible to go into the matter in the time.
The Debates elsewhere on this matter ranged
over a period of three weeks. I am not sure,
but a good deal was said both by way of support and by way of criticism. So far as 1
know the complete Debates are not yet available or if they are it is only just now. We
ought to have plenty of time, and then we
could deal with it properly-not necessarily
unsympathetically. The objection I am trying to voice this afternoon is that we have
not the time to deal with the measure.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews) (who was received with general
cheers): I really did not come here to-day
with the intention of saying anything, but I
do appreciate very much the kind welcome
which I have received in this House to-day,
and I am very grateful to my right hon.
Friend, the Leader of this House, and to other
Members, for the kind references which they
have made to me personally.
My hon Friend opposite (Mr. Leslie) has
asked me a direct question, which I feel I
ought to answer in the first place. He asked
me was it a fact that unemployment in this
great city was greater than in any place in
Great Britain. I am glad to be able to tell
my hon. Friend that that is not true. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) In a great many of
the industrial centres of Great Britain they
19 NOVEMBER 1923.
Trade Boards Bill -

Second Reading.
Boards are a
cure for unemployment, because of course they are not. But on
the figures which I can show to my
hon. Friend he will see that at any rate they
have not been in Northern Ireland a cause
of unemployment.
are oppressed by even a higher rate of unemployment than we have got here. I can quite
appreciate my hon. Friend's desire that
nothing should be done to interfere with or
injure in any way industries scattered
throughout the Six Counties. One of the
reasons why I desire that this measure should
be passed into law without delay is because
it is to permit the Ministry to establish
district committees throughout our Six
County area, so that they may act as an
advisory body to the main Trade Boards, and
make it possible for the main Trade Boards
to know better the industrial conditions
throughout the Six Counties than has been
possible in the past.
There are many other reasons why I think
it is all-important that this Bill should be
passed into law without delay. One is that
the procedure in the past has been so slow.
From the time that a proposal was made to
vary a rate until it was actually made effective about four months elapsed. It will be
possible now to have a rate fixed within a
month or two, at the utmost, of the application being made to have the rate changed.
Hon. Members will appreciate the importance
of that. When trade was bad and, wages
falling employers naturally felt that when
the Trade Board decided that the rate
should be reduced they ought to get the
advantage of the reduction in wages without
delay, because their customers got to know
that a reduction would take place sometime
later, and naturally these customers expected
to have the commodities reduced that were
manufactured in this industry before the
employers got any relief in the cost of production of any kind or shape. Again, the
workers, when it is decided that they are
entitled to receive an advance in wages,
naturally feel it exasperating to be kept
waiting three or four months before they
get the advance of wages that the Board felt
they were entitled to. I could
many other reasons why I think that this
Bill should not be hung up for a
session, but I think I have mentioned a few
of the most important just to show this
House the urgency of the matter.
mention
new
un-
My hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) asked me how
I thought that this Bill would assist
employment. Well, of course, this Bill will
help. Unemployment is a very big problem,
and there are many ways of assisting. It
cannot be expected that every Bill that is
passed by this House or any other House
will assist in this great problem. At any
rate I can give the House this grain of comfort in this connection, that in the Trade
Board Trades in Northern Ireland during
the last two years there has been less
unemployment than in other trades that
have had no Trade Boards at all.
I do not wish to argue from that, that Trade
My hon. Friend asked me to tell the House
something about the cost of administration.
Roughly speaking during the time the
Northern Government have had charge of
administering the Trade Boards in this area
the cost has been about £3,000 per annum.
Before we took over the administration from
Dublin the cost in Northern Ireland was
about £9,000 per annum, so that we have been
able to introduce very considerable economies. It may be in the future that the cost
will rise from £3,000 to double or possibly
even treble that amount, but even so I would
say the money would be well spent-
Mr. WOODS: Hear, hear.
Mr. ANDREWS: Because in Northern Ireland we have not had during the last two
years a strike worth the name in any of our
Trade Board trades, and any prolonged strike
of a serious character in connection with any
of these industries may very readily cost the
community here treble or quadruple this comparatively small sum.
Mr. GREER: Hear, hear.
Mr. ANDREWS: I feel I would not like
to conclude to-day without expressing my
sense of indebtedness to you, Sir, for the
able and painstaking way in which
you presided over the Committee which
investigated this problem SO thoroughly
over a very prolonged period. It was
wholly, as I said in our House of
Commons, due to your tact and due to your
personal popularity that the Committee were
able to present me with a unanimous report.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). This Bill,
which I have the honour to be identified with,
was based on that Report. I believe I am
right in saying that every suggestion contained in that Report is to be found in the
Bill itself. It, at any rate, has been in the
hands of the community a great many
months, and if the Bill itself is being somewhat pressed forward at the end of the
Session I think it should give hon. Members
confidence that they are doing the right
thing in passing it into law, when I can
assure the House, as I have already done,
that every suggestion which your Committee
made has been embodied in the Bill, and
beyond that report we have not gone to any
material extent. I thank this House for the
patient way in which they have listened to
these few remarks and the kind reception
they have given me will encourage me to
come back on another day. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear).
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill be
read the Second time ") agreed to.
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
Local Government Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The Measure at present before the House
has been introduced, not as in any sense of
the word drastically reforming our present
system of administration in matters of local
government. My right hon. Friend (Sir
Dawson Bates) has already stated in another
place that he intends very shortly to set up
a Commission with such terms of reference
as will permit it to investigate the whole
subject of local government administration
in the Province and to make recommendations for the reform of the whole system,
including Poor Law and medical administration. Inasmuch as this inquiry covers a
wide field and will necessarily cecupy a considerable period of time, it has appeared wise
to the Government, on the advice of my right
hon. Friend, to deal forthwith with certain
outstanding anomalies which have been
evident to the Ministry of Home Affairs since
the transfer of services in 1921. That is
mainly the object of the Bill. The removal
of these anomalies, it is hoped, is not a matter of controversy, and it is with the object
of effecting this that the present Bill has
been introduced.
Clause 1 deals with the county infirmaries. For many years past infirmary committees and their officers have been making
representations against the anomalies and
the difficult position in which their infirmaries are placed by reason of the present
restrictive legislation. The law relating to
county infirmaries in Northern Ireland dates
as far back as the year 1765, to the old
County Infirmary Act, which was introduced
in that year. The Local Government Act of
1898 did, indeed, bring the County Infirmary
within the scheme of county council administration but the old restrictions upon county
councils in regard to contributions to those
infirmaries remain limited to the amounts
specified in the Grand Jury Act of 1836. That
limitation was to a sum of £1,400 only, and
though the Local Government Act of 1902
made provision for a refund to the county
infirmary committee from the county council,
if there happened to be a deficit, this was a
very unsatisfactory way of escaping the difficulty, inasmuch as it is obviously better that
the county council should know beforehand
what money it is proposed to spend rather
than that it should be asked to pay a bill
after the expenditure has long been made.
Nobody certainly wishes to do away with
voluntary contributions, or to depreciate the
incentive to voluntary work. County infirmaries occupy a unique position in this respect. They are, to a very substantial degree, supported by voluntary contributions
and their administration is shared between
the county councils and their subscribers. As
the House is aware, one of the most unfortunate results of the war has been the enforced restriction upon private benevolence,
while the vastly diminished purchasing power
of money has made the situation of these institutions very critical indeed. There are
a number of other matters which I will not
trespass upon the indulgence of the House
by enumerating.
Clause 2 is a temporary one and deals
with the conditions that follow upon the fact
that there has been no census in Northern
Ireland since the year 1911. Any towns,
therefore, in Northern Ireland which have
increased their population above 1,500 since
the year 1911 are still subject to the restrictions of the Towns Improvement Act of 1854,
nor can they become urban districts under
the Public Health and Local Government
Acts. This clause makes provision to meet
such cases.
Clause 3 provides the Minister of Home
Affairs with powers analogous to those given
to the Minister of Health in Great Britain
under the Local Authorities (Expenses) Act of
1887. It enables the Minister of Home Affairs
to sanction expenditure on the part of local
authorities which, in his opinion, is justifiable, although liable to surcharge by the
auditor as lacking proper authority for payment. This sanction is limited to expenditure under £100 and is not so wide as that
of the British Minister.
Clause 6 amends the existing law as to
the remuneration of inspectors for holding
Local Government inquiries. The present
limit of three guineas is quite inadequate,
and the present clause makes provision for
increasing this remuneration on the same
lines as in Great Britain under the Fees Increases Act, 1923.
Clause 7 makes provision for the proper
preservation of war memorials by local authorities. The powers given under this clause
run along the same lines as those which are
already possessed by local authorities in regard to ancient monuments.
Clause 8. Under the Health Resorts and
Watering-places (Ireland) Act, of 1909, local
authorities were enabled to spend the proceeds of a penny rate, or less, in advertising
their towns as health resorts. This amount
is now increased to the proceeds of a threepenny rate in the case of urban districts and
towns, but not in the case of rural districts.
Clause 9 is a necessary corollary of the
recent revision of electoral areas in Northern
Ireland. The Poor Relief Act of 1851 enacts
that dispensary districts must comprise complete district electoral divisions. Without
the provision in the present Bill we should
have to revise the whole scheme of dispen-
$77 Local Government Bill-

19 NOVEMBER 1923.
Second Reading.
sary districts. It is, therefore, provided by
this clause that the Minister of Home Affairs
may retain the old districts, while giving him
power to form new divisions where necessary.
Clause 10 is a financial clause, which has
the effect of giving statutory sanction to arrangements already adopted since the transfer
of services for the payment of grants to local
authorities. All doubt as to the legality of
such payments is thus removed.
I beg to move that the Bill be read a
Second time.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I merely wish to
say that I have been for many years a
Governor of two county infirmaries-Tyrone
and Fermanagh-a member of a county council, and chairman of a rural district council,
and in all those capacities I heartily welcome
this Bill and desire to support it.
Mr. LESLIE: I can scarcely say anything
in general to add to what my hon. Friend
(Senator Montgomery) has already said. I
also welcome the Bill, and I believe it will
be a very excellent and useful measure. I
should like to say a word or two as regards
contributions to county infirmaries under
Clause 1. I am sometimes afraid that I
spend my life disagreeing with people.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Hear, hear. We welcome it.
a
Mr. LESLIE: This is an occasion on which
I do not think the noble Marquess and I
agree to disagree. I do disagree with
some of the smaller local bodies in my own
neighbourhood of County Antrim as regards
their attitude to the county infirmary at
Lisburn. It has been a bone of contention
for
long time, and there have
been persistent attempts to reduce the statutory contribution to the Lisburn County Infirmary. Now it will be open to the county
council, if it so desires, to increase that contribution. I think the contribution at present is £600 a year, and £70 for the surgeon. The rateable value of County Antrim
is £738,000, and the product of a halfpenny
rate is £1.812. That is the position. Passing
to the rest of the Bill, everything is on the
permissive side. There is practically nothing
compulsory at all. There is nothing I want
to take out of the Bill, but there are two
things I would like to see put in. I would
like to see the clause referring to war memorials extended to give rural district councils
the same power as the county councils have.
because there are many small war memorials
in the rural districts which would run a very
fair chance in a few years of becoming
neglected and damaged. I do not know of
any objection to the extension. A rural district council is a body corporate just as much
as a county council, and the extension required would only put us to the necessity of
altering one word and a subsidiary sub-section
as to the raising of the rate. That would be
a simple matter.
The second point is a more debatable one.
It occurs to me that it might be worth while
to take this opportunity of increasing the
power of co-option which county councils
possess. County councils in Northern Ireland have not had a very long history, but
in some ways they have had a rather
chequered career. They were the creation of
the Act of 1898, and they were strengthened
during their first term of office by representatives of the old Grand Jury. They were
given power to co-opt one or two persons as
they wished. Everything continued in a
more or less normal manner until during
the war the life of the then county councils
was temporarily extended. Then the constitutencies were altered, and instead of a
one-member constituency you had a three or
four-member constituency. Such county
councils have endured for three years,
and their life has been artificially
extended for another year. They will cease
to exist next June, and there will be a reversion to the one-member constituency. The
constituencies will be altered, an attempt
being made to adjust the new constituency to
the present population.
and it occurs
might be very
clause
a
allowing
Duties have been cast on the county councils, as has so often been said here before,
to an increasing extent in recent years. Probably next year they will have additional work
thrown on them,
to me that it
desirable to insert
county councils, if they so desire, to increase
to a limited extent the number of their coopted members. I propose to put an amendment on the Paper accordingly in order that
the matter may be discussed in this House,
where there are so many members singularly
qualified to express an opinion. One has to
remember, of course, the danger of interfering with the democratic character of county
councils, but I think it might perhaps
be done with advantage and without
detriment to the existing status of county
councils.
A third point I want to suggest to the
Government is that they should do something
for the poor and much-abused motorist. The
motorist pays half the cost of the roads, and
apparently he is not a very popular individual. But here is an opportunity to do something for him. The practice has recently
grown up of erecting what are called curbside pumps for the use of motorists, and a
Local Government Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
decision has just been given to the effect that
the erection of such pumps is an obstruction.
It occurs to me that the Government might
consider whether a clause could not be introduced in this Bill enabling county councils
to authorise the erection of such pumps, subject, if you like, to the approval of the
Ministry. I think good would be done and
no harm would result. It is not a matter on
which I shall press for an answer to-day,
but it is a matter that might be looked into
in order that if the Government is disposed
to adopt the suggestion, they might bring in
an amendment to that effect.
On the whole
I am sure this Bill will be received with
satisfaction, and that the additional powers
which are given will be used for the advantage of the community at large.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill be
read a Second Time," agreed to.
Moved: that this House do now adjourn.--
(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Fifteen
minutes after Five o'clock.
381 Standing Orders (Local Bills)-

20 NOVEMBER 1923.
Amendments.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 20th November, 1923.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Standing Orders (Local Bills).
Mr. SPEAKER: In moving the amendments to Standing Orders I think it would be
better, as they are somewhat numerous, if I
merely tried to explain the more important
ones, and then if hon. Members require any
further explanation I will do my best to
satisfy them. The amendments to which I
wish to allude specially are Standing Order
No. 11, No. 30, No. 65, and No. 105.
With regard to leaving out Standing Order
No. 11, this will relieve promoters from making application to owners, lessees and occupiers as to whether they assent, dissent or
are neuter in respect to taking houses or
lands compulsorily. Notice will, of course,
have still to be given, but at Westminster
it has been found that the replies to the
application have never been made any use of,
and these amendments were made in the
Westminster Standing Orders last year.
Standing Order No. 30: After "commerce"
insert "and at the Office of the Board of
Trade in London." The Board of Trade, as
the custodian of Crown rights in foreshores,
and also as the authority for navigation,
desire to see the plans and sections, and as a
matter of courtesy, if for no other reason,
it is desirable to provide by this amendment
that these plans are so lodged.
Standing Order No. 65: We are leaving out
this Standing Order, which provides that in
the case of a Railway or Tramway Bill authorising the construction of works by a nonstatutory company a declaration may in certain cases take the place of the deposit of
5 per cent. on the amount of the estimate
which is insisted on by Standing Order No.
64. At Westminster this Standing Order has
been found practically useless and has hardly
ever been relied upon, and was struck out
last year.
out
Standing Order No. 105: We are leaving
the words "locally or otherwise
interested," and inserting the words "has
no private interest." This amendment is
framed so as to make it somewhat easier to
nominate a joint committee to consider the
provisions of an opposed Local Bill. The
words We are striking out if interpreted
widely, make it very difficult to pick out a
committee of the two Houses in the case of
a Bill such as the Belfast Corporation Bill
of this year.
The remainder of the amendments are
nearly all of a drafting nature or consequential on the amendments which I have
explained. As I said before I am quite ready
to go further into any of these amendments.
I beg to move.
Mr. GREER: I highly approve of all these
alterations. I think they will simplify the
procedure in some cases and make things
plainer in others. I would like to call
attention to Standing Order No. 105, where
the words, "that he is not legally or otherwise interested" are going to be changed
to "has no private interest.” That is an
improvement, and I approve of that alteration. The idea underlying that Standing
Order is that no person shall take any part
in the settling of a Bill, particularly in Committee, it he has got any interest in the
matters brought before the Committee that
might influence him consciously or ullconsciously. I think the Standing Order was
rather too strict before because, as Was
pointed out just now, it nearly prevented
the possibility of getting a Committee to sit
on the Belfast Corporation Bill in the last
Session. I do not like the word "private,"
although I cannot think of a better word,
because to my mind the word "private"
means something that concerns a man himself, but does not concern his neighbours.
Therefore if this Bill concerned his neighbours as well as himself you might say he
had no private interest. However, I
do not quarrel with it because I cannot
think of any better word, but I wish a better
word could be invented.
Mr. SPEAKER: I will speak to the Chairman of Ways and Means on this question.
He is a man of vast vocabulary, and perhaps he can suggest a better word than
"private."
Mr. GREER: I should not like to move an
amendment. I merely make the suggestion.
STANDING ORDER No. 4.-(Notices to
contain description of termini, names of
boroughs, &c., affected by any work, and to
state place of deposit of plans, &c.)
In cases of Local Bills of the Second
Class and of Local Bills of the First Class
in respect to which plans are required to
be deposited, such notices shall also contain
a description of all the termini, together
with the names of the urban and rural
districts, and, in the case of rural districts,
383 Standing Orders (Local Bills)-

THE SENATE.

the townlands from, in, through, or into
which the work is intended to be made,
maintained, varied, extended, or enlarged,
or in which any land or houses intended to
be taken are situate, and where any common or commonable land is intended to be
taken or used compulsorily, such notice
shall contain the name of such common or
commonable land (if any), and the name
of any townland in which such land is
situate, together with an estimate of the
quantity of such common or commonable
land proposed to be taken, or used compulsorily, and shall state the time and place
of deposit of the plans, sections, books of
reference, and copies of the Gazette notice
respectively, with the clerks of the peace,
with the secretaries of the County Councils,
and also with the clerks of the Urban or
Rural District Councils.

On
or before the fifteenth day of

lands or houses compulsorily or for compulsory user of the same, or for an extension of the time granted by any former

application in writing shall be made to

marked (A).

Separate lists shall be made of the

STANDING ORDER No. 12-(Notice to

On or before the 15th day of December immediately preceding the application
for a Bill for laying down a tramway
notice in writing shall be given to the
owners or reputed owners, lessees or reputed lessees, or occupiers of all houses,
shops or warehouses abutting upon any

of the footpath on either side of the road
and the nearest rail of the tramway, or a

intended to run on the tramway carriages
or trucks adapted for use upon railways.

STANDING ORDER No. 14 (Notices
when it is proposed to abstract water from
any stream).
On or before the fifteenth day of December immediately preceding the application
for a Bill whereby it is proposed to abstract
water from any stream for the purpose of
supplying any cut, canal, reservoir, aqueduct, navigation or waterwork, notice in
writing of such Bill shall be given to the
owners, or reputed owners, lessees or reputed lessees or occupiers, of all mills and
manufactories or other works using the
waters of such stream for a distance of
twenty miles below the point at which such
water is intended to be abstracted, such
distance to be measured along the course
of such stream, unless such waters shall
within a less distance than twenty miles
fall into or unite with any navigable
stream, and then only to the owners or reputed owners, lessees or reputed lessees, of
such mills or manufactories or other
works as aforesaid which shall be situate
between the point at which such water is
proposed to be abstracted and the point at

STANDING ORDER No. 20-(How applications to be made and notices served).
All applications shall be made and
notices served either by delivering the
same personally to the party entitled to
such application or notice, or by leaving the
same at his usual place of abode, or, in his
absence from Northern Ireland, with his
agent, or by forwarding the same by post
in a registered letter, addressed with a sufficient direction to his usual place of abode,
and posted on or before the third day previously to the day required for the delivery
of the same personally, at such places, at
such hours, and according to such regulations as the Postmaster-General shall from
time to time appoint for the posting and
registration of such letters, and shall be
accompanied by a copy of the Standing
Orders which regulate the time and mode
of presenting petitions in opposition to
Bills.

STANDING ORDER No. 21-(Written
acknowledgment of party applied to, and in
case of application by post, Post Office receipt sufficient evidence of application).
In all cases the written acknowledgment
of the party applied to shall, in the absence of other proof, be sufficient evidence
of such application having been made or
notice given; and in case of an application
or notice having been forwarded by a registered letter, the production of the Post
Office receipt for such letter shall be
sufficient evidence of the due delivery of
such letter: Provided it shall appear that
the same was properly and sufficiently
directed, and that the same was not returned by the Post Office as undelivered.

STANDING ORDER No. 22.-(Notices, etc.,
not to be given on Sundays, etc.)
No notice served or application made on
a Sunday, Christmas Day, Good Friday,
Easter Monday, or bank holiday, or before
eight o'clock in the forenoon or after eight
o'clock in the afternoon of any day, shall
be deemed valid, except in the case of delivery of letters by post.

application made.”)

STANDING ORDER No. 105-(Declaration
to be signed by members of Joint Committee).
Each member of a Joint Committee on
opposed Bills shall sign and return to the
Speaker of the House of which he is a
member a Declaration that he is not
locally or otherwise interested in any Bill
or Order referred to such Committee, and
no such Committee shall proceed to business until the said Declaration shall have
been signed by each member.

STANDING ORDER No. 159-(Sale of new
shares of stock of gas companies to be by
public auction or tender.)
In every Bill by which an existing gas
company is authorised to raise additional
capital, provision shall be made for the
offer of such capital in shares or stock to be
paid up within a limited period by public
auction or tender at the best price which
can be obtained, unless the Joint Committee on the Bill shall be of opinion that
such provision ought not to be required for
the interest of the public.

STANDING ORDER No. 160-(Committee
to consider and report on Clauses in reference to various matters affecting Local
Government or Rating.)
In the case of any Bill promoted by or
conferring powers on a Municipal Corporation or Local Board, Improvement Commissioners, Town Commissioners, or other local
authority or public body having powers of
local government or rating, the Joint Committee on the Bill shall consider the clauses
of the Bill with reference to the following
matters:-
And the Committee shall report specially
to the House-
In what manner any clauses relating to
the several matters aforesaid have been
dealt with by the Committee; and
Whether any Report from any Government Department relative to the Bill has
been referred to the Committee; and
If so, in what manner the recommendations in that Report have been dealt
with by the Committee; and
Any other circumstances of which, in
the opinion of the Committee, it is desirable that the House should be informed.
And the Report of the Committee shall
be printed.

PAPER PRESENTED TO PARLIAMENT.
Ministry of Home Affairs. Civil Authorities
(Special Powers) Act (Northern Ireland) 1922.
Amendment to Regulation 23 B, relative to
Internees.
Constabulary Bill. 394
Superannuation Bill.
20 NOVEMBER, 1923.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of
this Bill I do not propose to weary the House
with a speech. It is a purely financial
measure and deals with the issue of a certain
sum of money out of the Consolidated Fund,
and also states how those grants out of the
Fund are to be appropriated for certain
purposes defined in the Schedule of the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill be now committed.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question Motion negatived
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time on Thursday next.
Superannuation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be read
a Second time. It is a small measure containing but three clauses, and it deals with
the pension rights of civil servants. Its
objects are two-fold. The first is to give
legislative effect in Northern Ireland to
arrangements recently made between the
Government and the Treasury with respect to
the superannuation of "specially appointed"
officers, i.e., civil servants, who have come
to the Northern service from that of the
United Kingdom. The second is to make
provision for the preservation of superannuation rights in respect of past service in
the case of civil servants coming to the
Northern service from that of the late Provisional Government of Southern Ireland,
or of the Irish Free State who were originally
Imperial civil servants. Under
Superannuation Act, 1921-22, the position of
those civil servants who came to us from
the Imperial service as "specially appointed"
officers has been safeguarded as regards
superannuation. That is to say, the Imperial
Treasury pays its share and the Government
of Northern Ireland also pays its share for
the period during which those officers have
been in its employment. But the provision
for the continuity of service after the first
transfer of these officers to Northern Ireland
was practically non-existent.
our Own
After negotiation with the British Treasury
it was agreed that no matter which service
the officer eventually retired from an equitable contribution to the total amount of his
pension, etc., will be paid by each employer
having regard to the service rendered and
the emoluments received in each period of
employment, determined in the first instance
by the Treasury, but subject to review, if
asked for by the Northern Government, by
the Joint Exchequer Board. This is provided
for in Clause 2.
I now come to the second object of the
Bill. There are, however, very few officers
who have come to the Northern Government
from the South of Ireland, and these officers
are for the most part civil servants who,
if the Government of Ireland Act had
operated in both South and North, would
have remained in reserved services in the
South and, therefore, under Imperial control.
As the result of the Treaty they were, without
any desire on their part, transferred by the
British Government automatically to the
Free State, and they served for a few
months under the Free State Government.
The Northern Government feel that having
taken these officers into its employment it
is under a moral obligation to ensure that
they shall not lose the benefit of their previous service when the time comes for awarding their superannuation, especially having
regard to the fact that in all cases the whole
of such previous service, save for a few
months, was rendered as Imperial officers.
It is hoped that arrangements will be come
to at a later date with the Free State authorities by which the latter will agree to make
an equitable contribution in respect of these
officers services previous to their transfer
to the Northern Government. The Northern
Government feel that the only right course
at present open to them is to assume full
liability for the superannuation rights of
the officers in respect of their previous service, and to deem them to be civil servants
of the Northern Government, and their previous service to be service under that Government for the purposes of superannuation.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) These are the
two objects of this Bill. I think I have explained the matter sufficiently fully to the
House, and accordingly I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Thursday next.
Constabulary Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second reading of this
Bill, I think it is clear to hon. Members of
this House that the object of the Bill is to
enable members of the Special Constabulary,
Constabulary Bill—
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
who are over the statutory limit of age, to
be admitted to the R.U.C. The limit of age
for candidates joining the R.I.C. was 40
years. The statutory limit of age for the
R.U.C. was fixed at 35 years. Hon. Members
will agree that for recruits for the Constabulary Force there must be some limit
to the age at which a man can be taken for
that Force. I may mention that when the
establishment of the R.U.C. was first formed
it was obviously desirable that as far as
possible the services of experienced men of
the R.I.C. should be availed of, and provision
was accordingly made in the Constabulary
(Northern Ireland) Act for the suspension
of the limitation of age for one year, after
which the age limit was to be reduced, as
I have already stated, to 35 years. It was
then expected that the establishment of the
Force would have been completed before the
expiration of the period of twelve months,
but this hope has not been fully realised.
There are at the moment, however, a number
of otherwise perfectly suitable candidates,
ruembers of the Special Constabulary, who
cannot be accepted as they are not within
the statutory age limit, and it is possible
from time to time other candidates, members
of the Special Constabulary in the same position, may come forward.
I think the House will agree with me that
it would be a pity if these men, who may be
regarded to a large extent as trained policemen of well known character and reliability,
were to be lost to the service simply because
they happened to be something over the
statutory age limit. I also trust the House
will agree with me when I say that I think
it is only fair that members of the Special
Constabulary-a Force which we all know
has carried out so well the dangerous and
difficult duties which have fallen to its share,
should be allowed this small privilege in
regard to the conditions of recruitment
which it is now proposed to give them. It
is hardly necessary to inform this House
that no man over the age of 35 will be
selected until the authorities are satisfied
that he is physically and in every other
respect fully qualified, and that his qualifications generally justify his being given a
preference over candidates of a younger age.
I beg to move.
Mr. DICKSON: I would just like to ask
the House if it is the intention to give the
Inspector-General power to appoint a man
from the Special Constabulary, no matter
what his age may be, to a pensionable office?
I understood from what I saw of the debate
in another place that the age limit was increased from 35 to 40 years for members of
the Special Constabulary who had been well
trained in police duties and were efficient
men. That we understood, from the Press reports of the debate in another place, was all
that the Bill intended, but according to the
Bill that is now presented to us it gives the
Inspector-General unlimited power as
age:
to
A special constable . . . may, notwithstanding any limitations of age imposed
by any enactment, be lawfully appointed as
an officer or constable of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary.
If this is the intention of the Government
I will be compelled to move an amendment
in the Committee stage that the Inspector-
General should not have the power to appoint
to a pensionable office a man nearing the
age of pension. We understood that the
height limit was not altered, and that the
age limit was increased from 35 to 40 years.
I would like the Leader of the House to make
that point clear.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the questions put to
me by the hon. Senator on my left, it is
quite true that in this Bill power is given
to appoint a member of the Special Constabulary to the R.U.C. at any age. I cannot
speak personally for my right hon. Friend.
the Minister of Home Affairs, but I am
perfectly sure it is not the intention to
appoint members of the Special Constabulary
to the ranks of the R.U.C. at an age when
it is obvious that they would not be suitable,
and when they would be nearing the time
when they would be liable to leave the Force
owing to arriving at a pensionable age. If
the hon. Member wishes to put down an
amendment for the Committee stage I will
consult my right hon. Friend before then
and give him a definite reply.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I do not see that
there is any advantage in making the condition as to age so very strict. If my hon.
Friend (Mr. Dickson) proposes an amendment
and I am here I shall have to vote against
him. You may have a Special constable at
over 45 years of age who may be a better man
than another who is 35 years of age, and I
think it would be objectionable under the
circumstances to prevent his appointment.
You
want to man this Force with the
very best men you can get, and if you
have a man who is a thoroughly good
man over 40 years I think it would be a
great mistake to prevent him coming in.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I for one
would be strongly in favour of making every
effort to induce the Special Constabulary to
become members of the R.U.C., and to encourage the Home Office to do everything
possible to take as many of them into the
R.U.C. as possible, but in this case it is
not a case of whether it shall be 45 or 40
or 35 years. It is giving the Home Office
unlimited power.
397 Constabulary Bill.

20 NOVEMBER 1923.
Adjournment.
Mr. DICKSON: Hear, hear.
Mr. CUNNINGHAM: They may take over
an officer at 60 years of age. They may also
take over an officer at 65 who would shortly
come under pension before the Northern
Government has received much value from
that officer. While I believe that it is with
the best intentions that the Home Office
makes this alteration, at the same time I
think there should be some undertaking or
some assurance given to the House before the
Bill is passed that it is not intended to exceed
a certain limit. I think if it were extended
from 35 to 40 or 45 years and an undertaking
is given to the House that it would not be
exceeded it would satisfy the House. It is
hardly to be expected that Members of the
House would allow the Bill to pass and
leave such power in the hands of the
Inspector-General, who may have some one
whom he desires to place in a certain position.
I think there should be something done in
respect to this, and I do hope that, without
dividing the House upon it, in the Committee
stage some assurance will be given that it
is not intended to exceed a certain limit.
If that is the case I will be very well satisfied.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Thursday next.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
In moving the Third Reading of the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill, there is one
amendment which I would venture to put
before the House. That amendment is in
the Schedule, page 4, to leave out the Workmen's Compensation (War Additions) Act,
1917. As hon, Members are aware the Government have introduced a Workmen's Compensation Bill in another place, and it is proposed that this Bill should go through both
Houses before the close of the present Session
of Parliament. The repeal of the two Acts
which are mentioned in the Schedule of the
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill, namely the
Workmen's Compensation (War Additions)
Act, 1917, and the Amending Act of 1919, is
consequent upon the passage of the new Bill
to which I have referred. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask whether the Bill
to which the noble Marquess has referred
is yet in print? If not it seems to me that
in adopting this amendment, which I am
sure is only brought forward with the
strongest reasons, and which we would probably all be willing to support, we are
excluding these Acts from the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill, gambling, so to speak, on
the fact that this other Bill will be passed,
and as this other Bill is one which we have
not yet seen we are creating a very dangerous
precedent. I do not know much of the
detail of the Act, which was passed in Great
Britain, but it occurs to me that there may
perhaps arise two questions which it might
be worth while in the special circumstances
of the case to mention now. One of them is
the date that the Act would come into operation, and the other is whether the existing
arrangements of people who are protected
by policies of insurance against payment of
compensation would be in any way affected.
I do not know whether the noble Marquess
can give us any information on those points,
but if so I should be glad to hear it.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The Bill is already in print, and will be
circulated to both Houses by to-morrow
morning. I think I may assure the hon.
Member (Senator Leslie) that he need have
no doubt on those questions about which his
mind is exercised. The Bill is in accordance
with the Bill on the other side, and I think
it is one that, if necessary, should be passed
through all its stages by the end of the present Session. I cannot say more than that,
but the Bill will be in the hands of the hon.
Member (Senator Leslie), and I have every
reason to believe that when he has read it
he will see that what he terms a gamble
will have turned out to be a very safe investment.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill be now read a
Third time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill be now passed.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Clerk do carry the Bill
to the Commons, and acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without
amendment.

read.

I beg to move that the House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.

AVA in the Chair.)

The Small Dwellings Acquisition (Ireland)
Acts, 1899 and 1919, shall have effect subject
to the following amendments :-
(a) An advance under those Acts may
be made to a person intending to construct a house, and in such case the
limitation in those Acts requiring that
the person to whom the advance is made
must be resident in the house, shall be
construed as requiring that the person
should be a person intending to reside
in the house when constructed;

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 5,
page 9, line 3, to leave out the word "should"
("the person should be a person intending
to reside") and insert instead thereof the
word "shall."

I think this amendment explains itself. At
first reading the section seems to suggest that
the more imperative word shall" should
be used in place of the word "should." 1,
therefore, put down the amendment in order
that the question may be decided. It is a
very simple matter. I beg to move.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The amendment which has been moved by
the hon. Member (Senator Leslie) is that the
word should" be left out and the word
"shall" inserted. I feel very diffident in
venturing to disagree with such a noted
purist as my hon. Friend, but I venture very
respectfully to put forward--and this is the
advice which has been given to my right
hon. Friends-that the word "should" in
this connection conveys the desired attention,
and I am also advised that from a grammatical point of view it is even more correct
than the word shall." It is also to be
noticed that this clause follows the lines of
the English Act, in which the word "should"
does appear in this connection. While the
Government would not have been disposed to
offer any objection to the amendment if it
had been made in the earlier stages of the
Bill, I would venture to suggest to the hon.
Member (Senator Leslie) that if any amendment were made in the Senate now it would
naturally delay the passage of the Bill, and,
therefore, hold up the question of housing.
I, therefore, hope that when the hon.
Member (Senator Leslie) realises how the
Bill stands, and that it certainly does fulfil
the object he has in view, he will see his way
to withdraw the amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I have great pleasure in
asking to be permitted to withdraw the
amendment. The noble Marquess has really
conveyed the answer I wanted, and which I
was unable to get for myself, namely the
reference to the preceding Act.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered That the Bill be reported without
amendment to the House.

The House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment to the

Ordered
That Clauses 1 (Application of
the Act), 2 (Provisions as to Provisional
Orders), 3 (Establishment of Trade Boards in
trades to which Act applies), 4 (Duties and
powers of Trade Boards as to reports and
recommendations), 5 (Obligation of Trade
Boards to fix general minimum time rates and
piecework-basis time rates), 6 (Powers and
